M_{max} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4}}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Physical Interpretation**

The axle casing acts as a beam loaded at its weakest cross-section (the center where the banjo bulge has a hole for the differential), so $M_{max}$ at this point determines the bending stress.

---

## Derivation 2: Bending Stress in a Hollow Circular Axle Casing

### Problem Setup
The axle casing is approximated as a **hollow circular tube** with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$. The maximum bending moment is $M_{max} = WL/4$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the Section Modulus Formula**

For a hollow circular cross-section, the second moment of area about the neutral axis is:
$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left(D^4 - d^4\right)
$$

The distance from the neutral axis to the outermost fibre is:
$$
y_{max} = \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Section Modulus**

The section modulus is defined as $Z = I / y_{max}$:
$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)}{\dfrac{D}{2}}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{\pi}{64} \cdot \frac{2(D^4 - d^4)}{D} = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D}}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Bending Stress Equation**

Using the flexure formula $\sigma_b = M/Z$:
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{\dfrac{W \cdot L}{4}}{\dfrac{\pi}{32} \cdot \dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{D}}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} \cdot \frac{32 \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{8 \cdot W \cdot L \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}}
$$

---

## Derivation 3: Combined Bending and Torsional Stress

### Problem Setup
When the vehicle accelerates or brakes, the axle casing must resist both:
- Bending moment $M$ (from vertical load).
- Twisting moment $T$ (from tractive or braking torque at the wheels).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Two Stress Components**

Bending stress (from $M$):
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{32 \cdot M \cdot D}{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}
$$

Torsional shear stress (from $T$), using polar section modulus $Z_p$:
$$
Z_p = \frac{I_p}{r} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)}{\dfrac{D}{2}} = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{16 \cdot T \cdot D}{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Principal Stress Theory (Maximum Normal Stress)**

For a shaft under combined bending and torsion, the maximum principal stress is:
$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}
$$

The minimum principal stress is:
$$
\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}
$$

The maximum shear stress is:
$$
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Distortion Energy Theory (Von Mises Equivalent Stress)**

For ductile materials like steel, the Von Mises equivalent stress is more accurate:
$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Design Condition**

The axle casing is safe when:
$$
\sigma_e \leq \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{n}
$$

Where $n$ is the factor of safety (typically **2 to 3** for automotive axle casings).

---

## Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Vertical load on rear axle, $W = 40{,}000$ N
- Effective length between spring seats, $L = 1400$ mm
- Outer diameter of casing, $D = 100$ mm
- Inner diameter of casing, $d = 85$ mm
- Braking torque, $T = 8 \times 10^5$ N┬Ęmm
- Yield strength of material, $\sigma_y = 350$ N/mm┬▓
- Factor of safety, $n = 2$

**Find:** Whether the casing is safe under combined loading.

### Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the maximum bending moment**
$$
M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{40{,}000 \times 1400}{4} = 14 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the section modulus**
$$
Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D} = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{(100)^4 - (85)^4}{100}
$$

$$
(100)^4 = 10^8, \quad (85)^4 = 52.2 \times 10^6
$$

$$
Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{(100 - 52.2) \times 10^6}{100} = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{47.8 \times 10^6}{100}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{\pi \times 478{,}000}{32} \approx 46{,}930 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute bending stress**
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{14 \times 10^6}{46{,}930} \approx 298.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute polar section modulus and torsional shear stress**
$$
Z_p = 2Z = 93{,}860 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{8 \times 10^5}{93{,}860} \approx 8.52 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Von Mises equivalent stress**
$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{(298.4)^2 + 4(8.52)^2} = \sqrt{89{,}043 + 290.3} \approx \sqrt{89{,}333}
$$

$$
\sigma_e \approx 298.9 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compare with allowable stress**
$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{350}{2} = 175 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\sigma_e = 298.9$ N/mm┬▓ $> \sigma_{allow} = 175$ N/mm┬▓, the casing is **NOT safe**. The designer must either:
- Increase the outer diameter $D$.
- Use a higher-grade material.
- Reduce the unsprung weight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# AXLE CASINGS ŌĆō Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Functional Architecture of a Banjo-Type Axle Casing

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **flow of forces and components** in a typical banjo-type live rear axle assembly.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Vehicle Chassis Frame] -->|Vertical Load W| B1[Leaf Spring Assembly]
    B1 -->|Spring Seat Reaction| C1[Axle Casing Top Surface]
    C1 -->|Bending Moment M| D1[Banjo Housing Center Bulge]
    D1 -->|Torque Transfer| E1[Differential Assembly]
    E1 -->|Hypoid Gear Mesh| F1[Crown Wheel and Pinion]
    F1 -->|Half Shaft Rotation| G1[Left Axle Shaft Tube]
    F1 -->|Half Shaft Rotation| G2[Right Axle Shaft Tube]
    G1 --> H1[Left Wheel Hub and Bearing]
    G2 --> H2[Right Wheel Hub and Bearing]
    H1 --> I1[Left Wheel and Tyre]
    H2 --> I2[Right Wheel and Tyre]
    J1[Road Surface] -->|Tractive Force| I1
    J1 -->|Braking Force| I2
    I1 -->|Cornering Force| H1
    I2 -->|Cornering Force| H2
    K1[Brake Drum or Disc] -->|Braking Torque T| H1
    K2[Brake Drum or Disc] -->|Braking Torque T| H2
    L1[Gear Oil Sump] -.->|Lubrication| E1
```

---

## Diagram 2: Comparison of Axle Casing Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Axle Casing Types] --> B2[Banjo Type]
    A2 --> C2[Split Type]
    A2 --> D2[Tubular Type]
    A2 --> E2[Carrier Type]

    B2 --> B2a[One Piece Casting]
    B2 --> B2b[Central Bulge]
    B2 --> B2c[Used in Live Axles]

    C2 --> C2a[Two Halves Bolted]
    C2 --> C2b[Easy Service Access]
    C2 --> C2c[Used in Heavy Trucks]

    D2 --> D2a[Simple Steel Tube]
    D2 --> D2b[Lightweight]
    D2 --> D2c[Used in IRS]

    E2 --> E2a[Carrier Acts as Housing]
    E2 --> E2b[Modular Design]
    E2 --> E2c[Used in Buses]
```

---

## Diagram 3: Load Distribution Flow on Axle Casing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Total Vertical Load W from Chassis] --> Q1[Spring Seat A Reaction]
    P1 --> Q2[Spring Seat B Reaction]
    Q1 --> R1[Bending Moment Distribution]
    Q2 --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Max Bending at Center M equals W times L divided by 4]
    P2[Braking Torque T from Wheels] --> S2[Torsional Stress Tau equals T over Zp]
    S1 --> T1[Combined Von Mises Stress Sigma e]
    S2 --> T1
    T1 --> U1{Compare with Allowable}
    U1 -->|Sigma e less than Allowable| V1[Casing is Safe]
    U1 -->|Sigma e greater than Allowable| V2[Redesign Required]
```

---

## Diagram 4: Cross-Sectional View of Hollow Axle Tube (Section Modulus Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CS1[Outer Diameter D] --> CS2[Annular Cross Section]
    CS3[Inner Diameter d] --> CS2
    CS2 --> CS4[Wall Thickness t equals D minus d over 2]
    CS2 --> CS5[Section Modulus Z equals pi times D to power 4 minus d to power 4 over 32 D]
    CS2 --> CS6[Polar Modulus Zp equals 2 times Z]
    CS5 --> CS7[Bending Stress Sigma b equals M over Z]
    CS6 --> CS8[Torsional Stress Tau equals T over Zp]
    CS7 --> CS9[Von Mises Sigma e equals sqrt of Sigma b squared plus 4 Tau squared]
    CS8 --> CS9
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The wall thickness $t = (D-d)/2$ is the **critical design parameter**. If $t$ is too low, the casing will fail under combined loading. Typical automotive axle casings have $t$ between **7 mm and 15 mm**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# AXLE CASINGS ŌĆō KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module Internal ŌĆō 3 Marks)
**Define an axle casing. List any four functions of an axle casing.**
`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
An axle casing is a load-bearing structural enclosure that houses the differential gear assembly and supports the axle shafts, wheel bearings, and road wheels, while transmitting vertical, longitudinal, and lateral loads to the vehicle chassis.

**Four Functions:**
1. Houses the differential and rear axle components.
2. Retains the gear lubricant (hypoid oil) and prevents contamination.
3. Transmits vertical load, driving torque, and braking torque from wheels to chassis.
4. Provides mounting points for leaf springs and maintains wheel track width.

---

### Question 2 (Module Internal ŌĆō 3 Marks)
**Differentiate between a banjo-type and a split-type axle casing.**
`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Banjo-Type | Split-Type |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Construction | Single-piece casting with a central bulge | Two halves bolted at a flange |
| Differential Access | Difficult, requires axle removal | Easy, just unbolt the flange |
| Rigidity | Very high | Slightly lower due to joint |
| Leakage | Minimal | Possible at flange joint |
| Application | Passenger cars, light CVs | Heavy trucks, off-road vehicles |

---

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each ŌĆō Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) ŌĆō 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the different types of axle casings used in automotive vehicles. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four major types of axle casings are:

**1. Banjo-Type Axle Casing:**
- This is a single-piece casting shaped like a banjo (lute).
- The central bulge houses the differential gear assembly.
- Two axle shaft tubes extend on either side to support the wheel hubs.
- *Sketch description:* A horizontal tube with a circular bulge at the center; differential gear is shown inside the bulge with crown wheel and pinion mesh; axle shafts go through the tubes to the wheel hubs.

**2. Split-Type Axle Casing:**
- Made of two halves (upper and lower) joined by bolts at a horizontal flange.
- Allows easy servicing of the differential without removing the entire axle.
- *Sketch description:* Show two halves meeting at a flange with bolts; the differential is visible between the halves.

**3. Tubular Axle Casing:**
- A simple circular steel tube with end housings for wheel bearings.
- Used in independent rear suspension systems.
- *Sketch description:* Show a plain tube with pressed steel end caps and bearings.

**4. Carrier-Type Axle Casing:**
- The differential carrier itself serves as the central housing.
- Bolted to axle tubes via flanges for modular replacement.
- *Sketch description:* Show the differential carrier as a separate unit bolted to two axle tubes.

**Comparison Table (for additional marks):**

| Type | Rigidity | Service Access | Weight | Application |
|------|----------|----------------|--------|-------------|
| Banjo | Very High | Difficult | Heavy | Live rear axles |
| Split | Moderate | Very Easy | Moderate | Heavy trucks |
| Tubular | Low | Moderate | Light | IRS systems |
| Carrier | High | Easy | Moderate | Buses, multi-axle trucks |

---

**(b) A rear axle casing of a truck has an effective length of 1500 mm between the spring seats. It carries a total vertical load of 50 kN at the differential housing. The casing is a hollow tube with outer diameter 110 mm and inner diameter 90 mm. Calculate (i) the maximum bending moment, (ii) the bending stress, and (iii) the section modulus. Take factor of safety as 2.5 and yield strength as 380 N/mm┬▓. Comment on the safety. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Effective length, $L = 1500$ mm
- Vertical load, $W = 50$ kN $= 50{,}000$ N
- Outer diameter, $D = 110$ mm
- Inner diameter, $d = 90$ mm
- Yield strength, $\sigma_y = 380$ N/mm┬▓
- Factor of safety, $n = 2.5$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Maximum Bending Moment**
$$
M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 1500}{4} = 18.75 \times 10^6 \text{ N┬Ęmm}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Section Modulus**
$$
Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D}
$$

Compute the diameters to the fourth power:
$$
D^4 = (110)^4 = 1.4641 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4
$$
$$
d^4 = (90)^4 = 6.561 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
$$

$$
D^4 - d^4 = (14.641 - 6.561) \times 10^7 = 8.08 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4
$$

$$
Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{8.08 \times 10^7}{110} = \frac{\pi \times 7.345 \times 10^5}{32} \approx 72{,}110 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Bending Stress**
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{18.75 \times 10^6}{72{,}110} \approx 260.0 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Allowable Stress**
$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} = \frac{380}{2.5} = 152 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Safety Check**
Since $\sigma_b = 260$ N/mm┬▓ $> \sigma_{allow} = 152$ N/mm┬▓, **the casing is NOT safe**.

**Design Suggestion:** Increase outer diameter to 125 mm or use a higher-strength material.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) ŌĆō 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the various loads acting on a rear axle casing. How do they affect the design? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The rear axle casing is subjected to the following loads:

**1. Vertical Load ($W_v$):**
- Caused by the weight of the vehicle body, payload, and dynamic impact from road irregularities.
- Acts downward at the spring seats.
- Produces **bending stress** in the casing.
- *Design effect:* Determines the bending moment and section modulus required.

**2. Driving Torque ($T_d$):**
- Generated by the engine and transmitted through the propeller shaft to the differential.
- Acts as a **twisting moment** on the axle casing.
- *Design effect:* Determines the torsional shear stress and polar section modulus.

**3. Braking Torque ($T_b$):**
- Reaction torque from brake drums/discs during deceleration.
- Acts in the opposite direction to driving torque.
- *Design effect:* Often more critical than driving torque because braking torque can be 2-3 times engine torque.

**4. Tractive/Longitudinal Force ($F_t$):**
- Forward force at the tire-road contact patch during acceleration.
- Acts horizontally on the axle casing.
- *Design effect:* Produces longitudinal bending stress, especially at the spring seats.

**5. Cornering/Lateral Force ($F_c$):**
- Sideways force during vehicle turning.
- Acts perpendicular to the vehicle's longitudinal axis.
- *Design effect:* Causes lateral bending and may lead to axle wind-up.

**6. Impact Load from Road Bumps:**
- Sudden shock loads from potholes or speed breakers.
- Magnified by the dynamic load factor (typically 1.8 to 2.5).
- *Design effect:* Requires a higher factor of safety in design.

**Combined Effect on Design:**
- The casing is designed using **combined bending and torsion theory**.
- The **Von Mises equivalent stress** is compared with the allowable stress.
- A **factor of safety of 2 to 3** is applied for fatigue and impact considerations.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the maximum bending stress in a hollow circular axle casing treated as a simply supported beam with a central point load. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Beam Setup**

Consider the axle casing as a simply supported beam of effective length $L$ between the two spring seats. A vertical point load $W$ acts at the midpoint (center of the banjo housing).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Support Reactions**

By symmetry:
$$
R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum Bending Moment**

At the central section (where the load is applied):
$$
M_{max} = R_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Second Moment of Area**

For a hollow circular cross-section:
$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Section Modulus**

The distance from neutral axis to outer fibre is $y_{max} = D/2$:
$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4)}{\dfrac{D}{2}} = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Bending Stress**

Applying the flexure formula:
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{\dfrac{W \cdot L}{4}}{\dfrac{\pi}{32} \cdot \dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{D}}
$$

Simplifying:
$$
\sigma_b = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} \cdot \frac{32 \cdot D}{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{8 \cdot W \cdot L \cdot D}{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}}
$$

Where:
- $W$ = Vertical load in N
- $L$ = Effective length between spring seats in mm
- $D$ = Outer diameter in mm
- $d$ = Inner diameter in mm
- $\sigma_b$ = Bending stress in N/mm┬▓

---

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\pi$ in section modulus** ŌĆö Students often write $Z = (D^4 - d^4)/D$ directly without the $\pi/32$ factor. This results in **wrong answers and zero marks** for that step.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $Z$ and $Z_p$** ŌĆö Remember, $Z_p = 2Z$ for a solid/hollow circular section. Confusing these gives wrong torsional stress.
>
> 3. **Ignoring units** ŌĆö Always convert kN to N and m to mm before substituting into formulas. Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for unit errors.
>
> 4. **Not drawing labelled sketches** ŌĆö In 7-mark questions on "types of axle casings," a **labelled sketch is mandatory**. A text-only answer without a sketch loses at least **2 to 3 marks**.
>
> 5. **Wrong Von Mises formula** ŌĆö The correct formula is $\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2}$, NOT $\sigma_e = \sigma_b + \tau$. This is a frequent trap.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to compare with allowable stress** ŌĆö Always end with a safety conclusion: "Since $\sigma_e \leq \sigma_{allow}$, the design is safe" OR vice versa.
>
> 7. **Confusing banjo with tubular** ŌĆö Banjo is for **live axles with a differential bulge**; tubular is for **IRS or dead axles**. Examiners specifically check this distinction.

---

## ­¤ōī TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ­¤öæ Rapid Revision Checklist

**Core Definitions:**
- [ ] Axle casing = structural enclosure housing differential and axle shafts.
- [ ] Banjo type = one-piece casting with central bulge.
- [ ] Split type = two halves bolted at flange.
- [ ] Tubular type = simple tube used in IRS.
- [ ] Carrier type = differential carrier is the housing itself.

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- [ ] $M_{max} = \dfrac{WL}{4}$ (central point load)
- [ ] $Z = \dfrac{\pi}{32} \cdot \dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{D}$
- [ ] $Z_p = 2Z$
- [ ] $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$
- [ ] $\tau = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$
- [ ] $\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau^2}$

**Loads to Remember:**
- [ ] Vertical load (bending)
- [ ] Driving torque (torsion)
- [ ] Braking torque (worst-case torsion)
- [ ] Cornering force (lateral bending)
- [ ] Tractive force (longitudinal bending)
- [ ] Impact load (dynamic factor 1.8 to 2.5)

**Materials to Recall:**
- [ ] Cast Iron (FG 250) ŌĆö banjo casings
- [ ] Cast Steel (SC 46) ŌĆö heavy trucks
- [ ] Mild Steel (IS 2062) ŌĆö fabricated casings
- [ ] Ductile Iron (SG 500) ŌĆö modern high-stress
- [ ] Aluminum (A356) ŌĆö EVs and lightweight vehicles

**Design Safety Rule:**
- [ ] $\sigma_e \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ where $n = 2$ to $3$
- [ ] Always check **bending AND torsion**, not just one.

**Quick Comparison Mnemonic (B-S-T-C):**
- **B**anjo ŌĆö Live axles, high rigidity
- **S**plit ŌĆö Heavy trucks, easy service
- **T**ubular ŌĆö IRS systems, lightweight
- **C**arrier ŌĆö Buses, modular design

**Examiner's Pet Topics (Most Repeated):**
1. Ō£ģ Types of axle casings with sketches.
2. Ō£ģ Difference between banjo and split types.
3. Ō£ģ Functions of axle casing.
4. Ō£ģ Loads acting on axle casing.
5. Ō£ģ Bending stress numerical problems.
6. Ō£ģ Section modulus derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axle Types - Semi-Floating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Axle Types - Semi-Floating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Semi-Floating Axle** is a rear-axle configuration in which the axle shaft performs **two simultaneous functions**: it transmits the driving torque from the differential to the rear wheels **AND** it carries the entire vertical load (weight of the vehicle) and the side thrust (cornering forces) generated during vehicle operation. In this design, the axle shaft is supported at its inner end by a single bearing inside the axle housing and at its outer end by a single bearing (or directly by the wheel hub) positioned **inside the axle housing** rather than outside it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMUT302 ŌĆō Module 2):** The semi-floating axle is explicitly listed as one of the three primary axle classifications (Full-Floating, Semi-Floating, and Three-Quarter Floating) under the unit covering **Rear Axle Assemblies and Final Drive Geometries**.

The classification of an axle is based on the **number of bearings** supporting the axle shaft and the **location of those bearings** relative to the wheel hub:

| Axle Type | Number of Bearings | Bearing Location | Load Carried by Shaft |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Floating** | 2 (one on each side) | Outside the axle housing, on the hub | Torsion only |
| **Three-Quarter Floating** | 1 (one side) | Inside the axle housing, on the shaft | Torsion + Vertical Load |
| **Semi-Floating** | 1 (one side) | Inside the axle housing, on the shaft | Torsion + Vertical + Side Thrust |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆō The "Pencil vs. Mechanical Pencil" Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **wooden pencil** and pushing it down on a table to write. The pencil simultaneously:
1. **Transmits a twisting force** to its tip (like torque transmission), and
2. **Bears your entire downward pushing weight** (like load carrying).

Now compare this with a **mechanical pencil** where the lead inside does the writing (torque) while the outer metal casing carries the load. The semi-floating axle behaves like the **wooden pencil** ŌĆō one single component (the axle shaft) does **everything**: it twists, it bends under load, and it resists sideways forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A semi-floating axle is a **multi-tasking shaft** ŌĆö it acts as a **drive shaft, a load-bearing beam, AND a side-thrust resistor, all in one component.** This makes it lighter and cheaper, but mechanically more vulnerable than a full-floating design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Maximum permissible static load on a typical semi-floating axle shaft:** **800 kg to 1,500 kg per wheel** (varies by shaft diameter and material).
- **Common shaft diameters used:** **25 mm to 45 mm** depending on vehicle class.
- **Standard material:** Medium-carbon alloy steel such as **AISI 4140** or **EN8**, with surface hardening (induction or carburizing) at the bearing journal and spline regions.
- **Allowable shear stress range for the shaft material (medium carbon steel):** **40 MPa to 70 MPa** in static bending; **20 MPa to 35 MPa** in fatigue loading.
- **Toe-in angle tolerance in semi-floating rear axles:** **┬▒ 0.05┬░** to **┬▒ 0.15┬░** depending on manufacturer specification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture ŌĆō Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

The semi-floating axle system can be decomposed into the following structural and functional logic chain:

- **Step 1 ŌĆō Torque Reception at the Differential:** The propeller shaft delivers engine torque to the differential (final drive) assembly. The differential ring gear rotates and distributes this torque equally (in straight-line motion) to the two axle shafts via the side gears.

- **Step 2 ŌĆō Axle Shaft Engagement:** Each axle shaft is connected at its inner end to the differential side gear through **involute splines**. This spline connection transmits pure **torsion** (twisting moment) without allowing axial slip.

- **Step 3 ŌĆō Outer End Connection to the Wheel Hub:** At its outer end, the axle shaft is flanged (or splined) to the wheel hub. The hub, in turn, is mounted on a pair of **tapered roller bearings** carried inside the axle housing tube.

- **Step 4 ŌĆō Load Path During Vehicle Operation:** Three distinct force systems act on the semi-floating axle shaft simultaneously:
  1. **Torque ($T$)** ŌĆö from differential to wheel.
  2. **Vertical Load ($W$)** ŌĆö from vehicle weight and dynamic load transfer.
  3. **Side Thrust ($F_s$)** ŌĆö from cornering forces and road camber.

- **Step 5 ŌĆō Bearing Reaction Mechanics:** The single inner-end bearing of the axle shaft, plus the **outer wheel hub bearings**, must react **all** of the above loads. Because the side thrust is reacted at the wheel hub bearings (and not at a dedicated axle shaft bearing), the **axle shaft itself must be strong enough to transmit this thrust to the differential housing**.

- **Step 6 ŌĆō Failure Mode Awareness:** If the axle shaft breaks, the wheel can **detach from the vehicle** because the shaft is the only mechanical link between the wheel hub and the differential. This is the central safety limitation of the semi-floating design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it called "Semi-Floating"?** The axle shaft is "semi-floating" because the wheel hub is mounted on its own bearings, and the axle shaft merely "floats" inside the hub via splines ŌĆö but the shaft still carries **half** of the functional load spectrum (hence "semi"). A full-floating axle, by contrast, has the shaft "floating" free of all loads except torque.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Legend | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{60 \times P}{2\pi N}$ | $T$ = Torque, $P$ = Power, $N$ = rpm | $T$ in N┬Ęm, $P$ in W, $N$ in rpm | Converting engine power to axle torque |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \times \tau_s \times d^3$ | $\tau_s$ = allowable shear stress, $d$ = shaft diameter | N┬Ęm, MPa, m | Shaft diameter design from torque |
| 3 | $W = m \times g$ | $W$ = vertical load, $m$ = sprung + unsprung mass per wheel, $g$ = 9.81 m/s┬▓ | N | Static vertical load per wheel |
| 4 | $W_{dyn} = W \times (1 + \lambda)$ | $\lambda$ = dynamic load factor (1.5 to 2.5) | N | Dynamic vertical load on rough roads |
| 5 | $M_{bending} = \dfrac{W \times L}{4}$ (for simply supported beam) | $L$ = effective length between supports | N┬Ęm | Bending moment on axle shaft |
| 6 | $F_s = m \times \dfrac{v^2}{R} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$ (per axle, two wheels) | $F_s$ = side thrust, $v$ = vehicle velocity, $R$ = turn radius | N | Cornering side thrust |
| 7 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{32 \times M_{bending}}{\pi \times d^3}$ | $\sigma$ = bending stress | MPa | Maximum bending stress in shaft |
| 8 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 \times T}{\pi \times d^3}$ | $\tau$ = torsional shear stress | MPa | Maximum shear stress due to torque |
| 9 | $\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2}$ | $\sigma_{vM}$ = von Mises equivalent stress | MPa | Combined stress check |
| 10 | $P_b = \dfrac{W}{L \times d}$ | $P_b$ = bearing pressure, $L$ = bearing length | N/mm┬▓ | Bearing life estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The semi-floating axle is the workhorse of light commercial vehicles, passenger cars, and entry-level SUVs. Its mass-optimized design directly improves fuel economy, which is why it remains the dominant rear-axle topology in vehicles up to the **2-tonne Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** class.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost-sensitive passenger cars** (e.g., hatchbacks, sedans) use semi-floating axles to reduce unsprung mass, which directly improves ride comfort and handling response.
- **Light commercial vehicles (LCVs)** in the 1ŌĆō3 tonne class often adopt semi-floating axles for cost-effective torque transmission.
- **Racing and performance applications** prefer full-floating axles because semi-floating shafts can fail catastrophically under extreme cornering loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Required Shaft Diameter from Combined Loading

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** An automotive rear axle uses a semi-floating design. The shaft must transmit a torque of $T = 850$ N┬Ęm while simultaneously resisting a maximum bending moment of $M = 1{,}200$ N┬Ęm. The material is medium-carbon steel (AISI 4140) with an allowable combined stress of $\sigma_{allow} = 180$ MPa. Determine the required shaft diameter $d$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca criterion):**

For a circular shaft under combined bending and torsion, the equivalent torque is:

$$
T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The bending moment $M$ and torque $T$ are combined using the root-sum-square method because they are independent stress components acting on perpendicular planes.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the torsion formula for solid circular shafts:**

$$
T_{eq} = \frac{\pi}{16} \times \tau_{max} \times d^3
$$

*Conversion logic:* For solid circular cross-sections, the polar section modulus is $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the equivalent torque numerically:**

$$
T_{eq} = \sqrt{(1{,}200)^2 + (850)^2}
$$

$$
T_{eq} = \sqrt{1{,}440{,}000 + 722{,}500}
$$

$$
T_{eq} = \sqrt{2{,}162{,}500}
$$

$$
T_{eq} \approx 1{,}470.6 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Relate $\tau_{max}$ to $\sigma_{allow}$ using Tresca:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{2} = \frac{180}{2} = 90 \text{ MPa} = 90 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

*Conversion logic:* Per Maximum Shear Stress Theory, the maximum shear stress in a uniaxial tension state equals half the yield strength.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for the shaft diameter:**

$$
d^3 = \frac{16 \times T_{eq}}{\pi \times \tau_{max}}
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{16 \times 1{,}470.6}{\pi \times 90 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{23{,}529.6}{2.827 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
d^3 = 8.32 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{8.32 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0436 \text{ m} \approx 43.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply a standard safety and rounding factor:**

The next standard shaft diameter (commercial grade) is **45 mm**, which is the design value used in many light commercial vehicle semi-floating axles.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Dynamic Side Thrust on a Semi-Floating Axle

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle of mass $m = 1{,}500$ kg negotiates a curve of radius $R = 50$ m at velocity $v = 72$ km/h. Calculate the side thrust $F_s$ acting on the rear axle shaft (assume 50/50 lateral load distribution per wheel).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert velocity to SI units:**

$$
v = 72 \text{ km/h} = \frac{72 \times 1000}{3600} = 20 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiply by 1000/3600 to convert km/h to m/s.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the centripetal force equation:**

$$
F_{centripetal} = \frac{m \times v^2}{R}
$$

$$
F_{centripetal} = \frac{1{,}500 \times (20)^2}{50} = \frac{1{,}500 \times 400}{50} = 12{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Distribute side thrust between front and rear axles (assume 40/60 split for a rear-heavy car):**

$$
F_{s,\text{rear}} = 0.60 \times 12{,}000 = 7{,}200 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Distribute per wheel:**

$$
F_{s,\text{per wheel}} = \frac{7{,}200}{2} = 3{,}600 \text{ N}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Two wheels share the lateral load on the rear axle equally under steady-state cornering.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Stress Distribution Check

| Load Component | Symbol | Magnitude | Stress Type | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Engine Torque | $T$ | 850 N┬Ęm | Torsional Shear | Larger diameter, better material |
| Static Vertical Load | $W$ | 1,500 ├Ś 9.81 / 4 Ōēł 3,679 N | Bending | Hub bearing selection |
| Dynamic Vertical Load | $W_{dyn}$ | $W \times 2.0 \approx 7,358$ N | Bending (transient) | Shock absorber tuning |
| Side Thrust | $F_s$ | 3,600 N | Bending + Shear | Wider wheelbase |
| Braking Torque | $T_{brake}$ | Variable, 500ŌĆō1,000 N┬Ęm | Torsional + Axial | Heat-treated splines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The semi-floating axle is most critically loaded during **cornering + braking** combined events, where torsional, bending, and axial stresses superpose. This is why high-performance vehicles NEVER use semi-floating axles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture ŌĆö Semi-Floating Axle Assembly

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the block-level functional architecture of a semi-floating axle system, showing the load paths and component interactions:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Propeller Shaft] --> B[Differential Housing]
    B --> C[Side Gear Left]
    B --> D[Side Gear Right]
    C --> E[Semi-Floating Axle Shaft Left]
    D --> F[Semi-Floating Axle Shaft Right]
    E --> G[Wheel Hub Left with Bearings]
    F --> H[Wheel Hub Right with Bearings]
    G --> I[Road Wheel Left]
    H --> J[Road Wheel Right]
    E -. Torsion Load .-> E
    E -. Vertical Load .-> G
    E -. Side Thrust .-> B
    K[Vehicle Weight and Payload] --> G
    K --> H
    L[Cornering Side Force] --> G
    L --> H
    M[Engine Torque Source] --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** represent **torque and rotation transfer** paths.
- **Dashed arrows** represent the **three types of mechanical loads** (torsion, vertical, side thrust) acting on the semi-floating axle shaft.
- The axle shaft (nodes E and F) is shown as a **single component handling all three load types simultaneously** ŌĆö this is the defining characteristic of the semi-floating design.

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram ŌĆö Three Axle Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Rear Axle Classification] --> Q[Full-Floating]
    P --> R[Three-Quarter Floating]
    P --> S[Semi-Floating]
    Q --> Q1[Two Bearings Outside Housing]
    Q --> Q2[Axle Shaft Carries Torsion Only]
    Q --> Q3[Hub and Shaft are Separate]
    Q --> Q4[Used in Heavy Trucks]
    R --> R1[One Bearing Inside Housing]
    R --> R2[Shaft Carries Torsion and Vertical Load]
    R --> R3[Side Thrust at Hub Bearing]
    R --> R4[Used in Medium Duty Vehicles]
    S --> S1[One Bearing Inside Housing]
    S --> S2[Shaft Carries Torsion Vertical and Side Thrust]
    S --> S3[Shaft is Mechanically Linked to Hub]
    S --> S4[Used in Light Cars and LCVs]
```

## 4.3 Load Path Flow During Cornering ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Wheel Contact Patch] --> A2[Lateral Friction Force]
    A2 --> A3[Wheel Hub Assembly]
    A3 --> A4[Semi-Floating Axle Shaft Inner Spline]
    A4 --> A5[Differential Side Gear]
    A5 --> A6[Differential Housing]
    A6 --> A7[Vehicle Chassis Frame]
    A7 --> A8[Complete Load Path Closed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The load originates at the **tire contact patch** and travels inward through the **wheel hub ŌåÆ axle shaft ŌåÆ differential housing ŌåÆ chassis** in a single rigid chain. In a full-floating axle, the path would be split: the hub and bearing would handle the side thrust directly, bypassing the axle shaft entirely.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a semi-floating axle. Mention its two primary functions.
**Model Answer:**
A semi-floating axle is a rear-axle arrangement in which the axle shaft is supported by a single bearing inside the axle housing and is flanged or splined to the wheel hub at its outer end. Its two primary functions are:
1. **Transmission of driving torque** from the differential to the rear wheels.
2. **Carrying the vertical load and side thrust** of the vehicle in addition to the torque.

This makes the semi-floating axle a compact and economical design suited for light vehicles. **[3 Marks]**

### Question 2: State any three advantages of a semi-floating axle over a full-floating axle.
**Model Answer:**
1. **Lower weight and cost** ŌĆö fewer components (only one bearing per side) reduce both unsprung mass and manufacturing cost.
2. **Simpler construction** ŌĆö the axle shaft and hub form a single mechanical unit, simplifying assembly and disassembly.
3. **Compact packaging** ŌĆö the design fits within limited chassis space, making it ideal for small passenger cars. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a semi-floating rear axle. Clearly label all major components and indicate the direction of load flow.

**Model Solution:**

**Constructional Details:**
The semi-floating rear axle consists of the following major components:

- **Axle Housing (Banjo Type or Split Type):** A tubular steel casing that encloses the differential and axle shafts. It serves as a structural link between the two rear wheels and supports the vehicle's sprung mass.
- **Differential Assembly (Final Drive):** Located in the central bulge (banjo) of the housing; consists of crown wheel, pinion, side gears, and pinion gears.
- **Semi-Floating Axle Shafts (Two):** Solid cylindrical shafts of medium-carbon alloy steel, with internal splines at the inner end (engaging the side gear) and a flange or external splines at the outer end (engaging the wheel hub).
- **Inner Shaft Bearing:** A single tapered roller bearing or ball bearing supporting the inner end of the axle shaft inside the housing.
- **Wheel Hub with Bearings:** The hub is mounted on the axle housing via two tapered roller bearings and carries the road wheel.
- **Brake Drum or Disc Mounting:** Integrated onto the hub or the flange of the axle shaft.

**Working Principle:**
- The engine torque is delivered via the propeller shaft to the differential pinion, which rotates the crown wheel and side gears.
- Each side gear transmits torque to its respective axle shaft through internal splines.
- The axle shaft rotates the wheel hub, which is bolted (or splined) to its outer flange.
- **During vehicle operation**, the axle shaft simultaneously:
  - Transmits torque to the wheel.
  - Carries the **vertical load** of the vehicle through the hub.
  - Resists **side thrust** during cornering by transferring it to the differential housing via its inner bearing.

**Sketch (ASCII Representation):**
```
        ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
        Ōöé   AXLE HOUSING         Ōöé
        Ōöé  ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ      Ōöé
        Ōöé  Ōöé DIFFERENTIAL Ōöé      Ōöé
        Ōöé  Ōöé  ASSEMBLY    Ōöé      Ōöé
        Ōöé  ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś      Ōöé
        Ōöé         Ōöé              Ōöé
        Ōöé    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ         Ōöé
        Ōöé    Ōöé AXLE    Ōöé         Ōöé
        Ōöé    Ōöé SHAFT   ŌöéŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆSide  Ōöé
        Ōöé    Ōöé (semi-  Ōöé   ThrustŌöé
        Ōöé    Ōöé float)  Ōöé         Ōöé
        Ōöé    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś         Ōöé
        Ōöé         Ōöé              Ōöé
        Ōöé    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ         Ōöé
        Ōöé    Ōöé WHEEL   Ōöé         Ōöé
        Ōöé    Ōöé HUB     ŌöéŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆVert  Ōöé
        Ōöé    Ōöé + BEARINGSŌöé  Load Ōöé
        Ōöé    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś         Ōöé
        Ōöé         Ōöé              Ōöé
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                  Ō¢╝
              ROAD WHEEL
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare semi-floating, three-quarter floating, and full-floating rear axles in terms of load-carrying capacity, safety, and typical applications.

**Model Solution (Tabular Form):**

| Parameter | Full-Floating | Three-Quarter Floating | Semi-Floating |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Bearings per Side** | 2 (outer, on hub) | 1 (inner, on shaft) | 1 (inner, on shaft) |
| **Load Carried by Shaft** | Torsion only | Torsion + Vertical load | Torsion + Vertical + Side thrust |
| **Load Carried by Hub Bearing** | Vertical + Side thrust | Side thrust only | None (shaft handles all) |
| **Load Capacity per Axle** | **8,000ŌĆō20,000 kg** | **3,000ŌĆō5,000 kg** | **1,000ŌĆō2,000 kg** |
| **Safety if Shaft Fails** | Wheel remains attached | Wheel may detach under cornering | **Wheel will definitely detach** |
| **Unsprung Mass** | Highest | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| **Cost** | Highest | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| **Typical Application** | Heavy trucks, buses, military vehicles | Medium trucks, light commercial vehicles | Passenger cars, hatchbacks, entry SUVs |
| **Maintenance** | Complex | Moderate | **Simplest** |
| **Toe-in Sensitivity** | Low | Moderate | **High** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **three-quarter floating** and **semi-floating** axles because both use one bearing. The key discriminator is: *In three-quarter floating, the side thrust is reacted at the hub bearing. In semi-floating, the side thrust is reacted THROUGH the axle shaft itself.* Failing to state this distinction explicitly will cost 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A semi-floating rear axle of a passenger car transmits a torque of $600$ N┬Ęm. The maximum bending moment acting on the axle shaft is $900$ N┬Ęm. The shaft material has an allowable combined stress of $165$ MPa. Determine the required shaft diameter using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory. Assume a factor of safety of $1.5$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Factor of Safety to Allowable Stress:**
$$
\sigma_{allow,design} = \frac{165}{1.5} = 110 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Equivalent Torque:**
$$
T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(900)^2 + (600)^2}
$$

$$
T_{eq} = \sqrt{810{,}000 + 360{,}000} = \sqrt{1{,}170{,}000} \approx 1{,}081.7 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Maximum Shear Stress Criterion:**
$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{allow,design}}{2} = \frac{110}{2} = 55 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Shaft Diameter:**
$$
d^3 = \frac{16 \times T_{eq}}{\pi \times \tau_{max}} = \frac{16 \times 1{,}081.7}{\pi \times 55 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{17{,}307.2}{1.728 \times 10^8} = 1.0017 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
d = \sqrt[3]{1.0017 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.0464 \text{ m} = 46.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Round Up to Commercial Standard:**
The next available standard shaft diameter is **$d = 50$ mm**.

**Final Answer:** $d = 50$ mm. **[7 Marks]**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the load distribution in a semi-floating axle during (i) straight-line driving under load, and (ii) cornering under load. How does this differ from a full-floating axle in terms of axle shaft stress?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Straight-Line Driving Under Load:**
- The vertical load $W$ is transferred from the road surface through the tire to the wheel hub.
- The hub, mounted on its own tapered roller bearings, transfers the load directly to the axle housing.
- **Simultaneously**, the axle shaft transmits torque $T$ to the hub but does not carry any significant vertical load in the static sense ŌĆö however, due to the rigid flange connection, **a small bending moment is induced** in the shaft because the load reaction point (hub bearing) and the shaft support (inner bearing) are separated by a small lever arm.

**(ii) Cornering Under Load:**
- A lateral friction force $F_s$ develops at the tire contact patch.
- This force is transferred to the wheel hub.
- The hub bearing reacts the vertical and lateral components to the axle housing.
- The remaining component of side thrust ŌĆö because the hub bearing is not always perfectly aligned with the side force ŌĆö is **transmitted through the flange into the axle shaft** as a **bending moment** and **shear force**.
- This bending moment is reacted at the inner shaft bearing and finally at the differential housing.

**Comparison with Full-Floating Axle:**

| Aspect | Semi-Floating | Full-Floating |
|---|---|---|
| Side thrust on axle shaft | **Yes ŌĆö significant** | **Negligible (zero)** |
| Bending stress in shaft | High | Almost zero |
| Torsional stress in shaft | Present | Present |
| Combined stress condition | **Torsion + Bending** | Torsion only |
| Failure risk under cornering | Moderate to high | Very low |

**Final Conclusion:**
In a semi-floating axle, the shaft operates under **combined torsional and bending stresses**, while in a full-floating axle, the shaft is subjected to **pure torsion only**. This is the fundamental mechanical reason why full-floating axles are mandatory in heavy commercial vehicles, whereas semi-floating axles are confined to light vehicle applications. **[7 Marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "the semi-floating axle shaft carries only torque" ŌĆö this is the **defining characteristic of a FULL-FLOATING axle**. In a semi-floating design, the shaft **DOES carry bending and side thrust**. Marks are routinely deducted for this conceptual error. Always explicitly state the load combinations for each axle type.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **semi-floating axle** uses **one bearing per side** located **inside the axle housing**.
- The axle shaft carries **three loads simultaneously**: torque, vertical load, and side thrust.
- **No separation** exists between the wheel hub bearing and the shaft for load reaction ŌĆö the shaft is a multi-tasking structural member.
- The **equivalent torque formula** for combined loading is $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$.
- The **shaft diameter design equation** is $d^3 = \dfrac{16 \times T_{eq}}{\pi \times \tau_{max}}$.
- **Material choice:** Medium-carbon alloy steel (AISI 4140 / EN8) with surface hardening at bearing journals.
- **Maximum load capacity:** 1,000ŌĆō2,000 kg per axle.
- **Applications:** Passenger cars, hatchbacks, sedans, entry-level SUVs, and light commercial vehicles below 3.5 tonnes GVW.
- **Failure consequence:** Wheel detachment is **immediate** if the shaft breaks.
- **Dynamic load factor** $\lambda$ on rough roads: typically **1.5 to 2.5**.
- **Standard shaft diameters:** 25ŌĆō45 mm, with **45 mm being the most common in mid-size sedans**.
- **Number of bearings rule of thumb:** *Full-floating = 4 total, Three-quarter = 2 total, Semi-floating = 2 total* ŌĆö but their **location** is what differentiates them.
- **Toe-in tolerance** is critical because any lateral play in the shaft translates directly into wheel alignment instability.
- **Combined stress check** uses **von Mises** or **Tresca** criterion; for KTU problems, **Tresca (Maximum Shear Stress Theory)** is most commonly used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Quarter and Full Floating, Final Drive for Multi-Axle Vehicles, Different Types of Final Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Three Quarter and Full Floating, Final Drive for Multi-Axle Vehicles, Different Types of Final Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Live Axle and Stub Axle Fundamentals

Before classifying the floating axles, we must anchor the concept of a **Live Axle (Drive Axle)**. A live axle is one that not only supports the vehicle weight but also transmits power (torque) to the driving wheels. The portion of the axle that connects the wheel to the axle housing ŌĆö and on which the wheel hub is mounted ŌĆö is called the **Stub Axle** or **Axle Shaft**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Classification of stub axles is based on the **type of bearing arrangement** used to support the axle shaft and the **magnitude of loads** (bending, torsional, thrust) handled by the shaft itself.

The four classical categories of stub axles, in order of increasing load-handling capability, are:

1. **Quarter Floating**
2. **Three-Quarter Floating**
3. **Semi-Floating**
4. **Full Floating**

This module focuses on the **Three-Quarter Floating** and **Full Floating** arrangements.

---

## 1.2 Three-Quarter Floating Axle ŌĆö Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Three-Quarter Floating Axle* is a stub-axle arrangement in which **one bearing** (typically a tapered roller or ball bearing) is mounted **between the axle housing and the axle shaft**, located near the inboard end of the shaft. The axle shaft carries the **full vehicle weight, side thrust, driving torque, and bending moment**, while the wheel hub is rigidly fixed (often keyed) to the outer end of the shaft.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **wooden broomstick held by a single nail in a wooden plank**. The broomstick (axle shaft) takes *all* the load ŌĆö the weight of the person (vehicle weight), the sideways push (cornering thrust), and the twisting effort (driving torque). If the nail (bearing) breaks, the broomstick slips out. This perfectly captures the "almost-full" load-bearing role of the shaft ŌĆö hence **"three-quarter"** floating.

---

## 1.3 Full Floating Axle ŌĆö Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Full Floating Axle* is a stub-axle arrangement in which the **wheel hub is supported independently of the axle shaft by two bearings** mounted on a stationary spindle attached to the axle housing. The axle shaft is **splined** to the hub and transmits **only torque**. All bending, thrust, and weight loads are carried entirely by the **hub-and-bearing assembly**, not by the shaft.

### Conceptual Analogy

Picture a **rotating screwdriver bit driven by a power drill**. The bit (axle shaft) only transmits twisting force (torque). The drill chuck's internal bearings (hub bearings) take all the sideways push and the weight ŌĆö the bit itself never sees any bending. Now if the bit snaps, the chuck (hub) is still firmly held by the bearings, and the wheel doesn't fall off. This is the **"fully floating"** concept ŌĆö the shaft is "floating" in pure torsion only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Standard:** Full floating axles are the industry benchmark for **heavy commercial vehicles, military trucks, off-roaders, and buses** where the standard **GAWR (Gross Axle Weight Rating)** often exceeds **10,000 kg (22,000 lbs)**.

---

## 1.4 Final Drive ŌĆö Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Final Drive* is the **last stage of the power transmission system** that reduces the engine's high rotational speed to a usable wheel speed and simultaneously multiplies the torque. It is enclosed within the **axle housing (banjo housing)** at the rear (and sometimes front) of the vehicle and is the unit that ultimately delivers torque to the differential and then to the axle shafts.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **bicycle's lowest gear combination (small chainring + large rear cog)**. You pedal slowly, but the rear wheel turns powerfully. The final drive is the automotive equivalent of that gear reduction ŌĆö it trades rotational speed for raw pushing power.

---

## 1.5 Final Drive in Multi-Axle Vehicles ŌĆö Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Multi-Axle Vehicle Final Drive* is a **tandem or multiple-axle power distribution system** that uses an **Inter-Axle Differential (IAD)**, also called a **Power Divider or Cross-Differential**, to distribute engine torque between **two or more drive axles**. The IAD allows the axles to rotate at different speeds during cornering or uneven road conditions, preventing tire scrub and wind-up.

### Conceptual Analogy

A **multi-axle final drive is like a 4-wheel-drive tractor** where power must reach two rear axles. Without a power divider, the two axles would "fight" each other on a turn (one wants to spin faster, the other slower) ŌĆö the IAD resolves this fight smoothly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **Inter-Axle Differential (between two axles)** with the **Inter-Wheel Differential (between two wheels on the same axle)**. They are mechanically similar but functionally distinct units.

---

## 1.6 Why Are These Concepts Critical for KTU 2024?

The **Automotive Systems (PCMUT302)** syllabus explicitly tests your ability to:
- **Compare** three-quarter floating vs. full floating axles (a frequent 7-mark sub-part).
- **Sketch and label** the cross-section of either arrangement (a 7-mark graphical question).
- **Classify** the types of final drives and explain the working of any **two** (a common 14-mark question).
- **Analyze** multi-axle final drive layouts and explain the role of the inter-axle differential.

Mastering these definitions is your foundation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three-Quarter Floating Axle ŌĆö Detailed Analysis

### Constructional Details

- **One bearing** (tapered roller or ball type) sits between the inner end of the axle shaft and the axle housing.
- The **wheel hub is keyed** (or splined) directly to the outer end of the axle shaft.
- The **axle shaft** acts as the **structural backbone** of the wheel assembly.
- A **king pin** is typically *not* part of the rear stub axle ŌĆö the rear wheels do not steer.

### Load Path Analysis

The axle shaft must simultaneously resist **three** distinct load categories:

| Load Type | Source | Effect on Shaft |
|---|---|---|
| **Bending Moment (M)** | Vehicle weight (static) + cornering thrust | Bending stress $\sigma_b$ |
| **Torsional Moment (T)** | Driving torque from engine | Shear stress $\tau$ |
| **Thrust / Axial Load (F)** | Braking and acceleration reaction | Compressive/tensile stress $\sigma_a$ |

### Failure Consequence

> [!WARNING]
> If the **single bearing fails**, the entire wheel-and-shaft assembly detaches from the axle housing. This is a **catastrophic safety hazard** ŌĆö a key reason three-quarter floating axles are restricted to light commercial vehicles with modest load capacities (typically below **2,500 kg axle load**).

---

## 2.2 Full Floating Axle ŌĆö Detailed Analysis

### Constructional Details

- The **axle housing** carries two flanges, each fitted with a **heavy-duty tapered roller bearing** (cup and cone type).
- A **stationary spindle** is fixed between the two bearings.
- The **wheel hub** rotates on these bearings, like a pulley on a shaft.
- The **axle shaft is splined** to the hub at its inboard end and is held in place by a **retainer plate or C-clip** at the outboard end.
- A **drive flange** is bolted to the hub on the outboard side to attach the wheel.

### Load Path Analysis

| Load Type | Carried By | Carried by Axle Shaft? |
|---|---|---|
| **Bending Moment (M)** | Hub + Bearings + Housing | **No** |
| **Torsional Moment (T)** | Axle shaft (via splines) | **Yes** |
| **Thrust / Axial Load (F)** | Bearings + Housing | **No** |
| **Vehicle Weight (W)** | Hub + Bearings + Housing | **No** |

### Failure Consequence ŌĆö A Brilliant Engineering Safety Feature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If the axle shaft breaks or the splines shear**, the wheel and hub **do NOT fall off**. The hub remains supported on the bearings, and the vehicle can be **driven slowly to a service station** (with reduced torque). This is the hallmark safety advantage of the full floating design.

### Other Advantages

1. **Easier wheel removal** ŌĆö slide out the axle shaft after removing the C-clip; the hub stays on the bearings.
2. **Higher load capacity** ŌĆö can handle axle loads of **8,000ŌĆō13,000+ kg**.
3. **Better heat dissipation** ŌĆö larger hub mass dissipates brake heat effectively.
4. **Suitable for dual rear wheels** ŌĆö the load is borne by the hub, not the shaft.

---

## 2.3 Comparative Matrix ŌĆö Three-Quarter vs. Full Floating

| Parameter | Three-Quarter Floating | Full Floating |
|---|---|---|
| Number of bearings | **1** (inner only) | **2** (one each side of housing) |
| Loads on shaft | Bending, torsion, thrust | **Only torsion** |
| Wheel hub mounting | Keyed to shaft | **Independent of shaft**, on bearings |
| Axle shaft replacement | Difficult (entire assembly) | **Easy** (slide out spline shaft) |
| Load capacity | Low to medium (Ōēż 2,500 kg) | **High** (Ōēź 8,000 kg) |
| Vehicle type | Light commercial, vintage cars | **Trucks, buses, military, off-roaders** |
| Cost | **Lower** | Higher |
| Safety on shaft failure | **Wheel detaches (unsafe)** | **Wheel stays on hub (safe)** |
| Heat dissipation | Moderate | **Excellent** |

---

## 2.4 Final Drives ŌĆö Detailed Classification

### Classification by Speed Reduction Stages

#### 2.4.1 Single Reduction Final Drive
- **One pair of bevel gears** (usually spiral bevel or hypoid) reduces the propeller shaft speed in **one stage**.
- Typical ratio: **3.5 : 1 to 5.5 : 1**.
- Used in **passenger cars, light commercial vehicles, and most modern multi-axle trucks**.
- Compact, lightweight, and efficient.

#### 2.4.2 Double Reduction Final Drive
- **Two stages** of reduction: the first is a **bevel gear pair**, the second is a **planetary or helical gear pair** mounted at the wheel end.
- Typical total ratio: **6 : 1 to 10 : 1**.
- Used in **heavy commercial vehicles, mining trucks, and dump trucks** requiring **very high torque multiplication**.
- Larger and heavier, but provides the **enormous pulling power** required by such vehicles.

### Classification by Gear Type

#### 2.4.3 Spiral Bevel Gear Final Drive
- Teeth are curved and oblique.
- **Smooth, quiet operation**; high contact ratio.
- Used in passenger cars and light trucks.
- Both pinion and gear have **conjugate curved teeth**.

#### 2.4.4 Hypoid Gear Final Drive
- A special form of spiral bevel where the **pinion axis is offset below** the gear axis (typically **25ŌĆō50 mm**).
- Offset allows the **propeller shaft to pass at a lower level**, **increasing ground clearance**.
- Used in most modern rear-wheel-drive cars and light commercial vehicles.
- **Sliding action** between teeth requires **hypoid gear oil (extreme-pressure lubricant)** with **API GL-5** rating.

#### 2.4.5 Worm Gear Final Drive
- A **worm** (on the propeller shaft) meshes with a **worm wheel** (on the differential cross-shaft).
- Provides **very high reduction** in a single compact stage.
- **Smooth, silent, and self-locking** (cannot be back-driven).
- Used in **vans, some industrial vehicles, and railway traction in the past**.

#### 2.4.6 Planetary Gear Final Drive
- Uses a **sun gear, planet gears, and ring gear**.
- Compact, coaxial, and offers **multiple reduction ratios**.
- Commonly used in **automatic transmissions (as final drive)** and some **electric vehicle reducers**.

#### 2.4.7 Chain Drive Final Drive
- A **sprocket and chain** arrangement (similar to a motorcycle).
- Used in **off-road ATVs, military vehicles, and some heavy machinery** for its robustness.

---

## 2.5 Final Drive in Multi-Axle Vehicles ŌĆö Detailed Analysis

### 2.5.1 Why Multi-Axle Vehicles Need Special Final Drives

In a **tandem-axle truck** (two rear axles), if both axles are **rigidly connected** through a solid shaft, the axles will be forced to rotate at the **same speed**. During cornering, the inner wheel travels a shorter path than the outer wheel ŌĆö meaning the two axles *must* rotate at slightly different speeds. A rigid connection causes:
- **Tire scrub** (excessive wear)
- **Wind-up** of the driveline
- **Stress fractures** in the propeller shaft and gears

### 2.5.2 The Inter-Axle Differential (Power Divider)

The **Inter-Axle Differential (IAD)** solves this. It is mounted in the **central housing** between the two drive axles, with **three bevel gears** (or a planetary set):

- **One input bevel gear** (from the propeller shaft).
- **Two output bevel gears** (one to each axle's propeller shaft).

The IAD:
- Distributes torque **50:50** (or as per design) between the two axles.
- Allows the two axles to **rotate at different speeds** during cornering.
- May be **lockable** for off-road conditions to prevent all power going to the wheel with least traction.

### 2.5.3 Tandem Axle Layout Types

| Layout | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **In-Board Tandem** | Final drives mounted in a single central housing; axle shafts run out to the wheels. | Older heavy trucks |
| **Out-Board Tandem** | Each wheel has its own reduction gear (planetary hub reduction). | Off-road, military, dump trucks |
| **In-Axle Tandem (Chelsea-type)** | Each axle has its own complete final drive unit connected by a through-shaft. | Modern multi-axle trucks |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are listed in the official KTU-recommended S.I. units** unless otherwise specified. Memorize this table ŌĆö it covers 80% of numerical problems in this module.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bending stress on axle shaft** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | M = bending moment (N┬Ęm), y = distance from neutral axis (m), I = area moment of inertia (mŌü┤). |
| 2 | **Torsional shear stress on shaft** | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | T = torque (N┬Ęm), r = outer radius (m), J = polar moment of inertia (mŌü┤). |
| 3 | **Polar moment for solid shaft** | $J = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$ | d = shaft diameter (m). |
| 4 | **Area moment for solid shaft** | $I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{64}$ | Used with bending equation. |
| 5 | **Total vehicle weight on rear axle** | $W_{rear} = m_r \cdot g$ | $m_r$ = rear axle mass share (kg), g = 9.81 m/s┬▓. |
| 6 | **Bending moment on axle shaft (quarter / 3-quarter)** | $M = \dfrac{W_{rear}}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot r_w$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $r_w$ = wheel radius. |
| 7 | **Final drive ratio** | $i_{fd} = \dfrac{N_{prop}}{N_{axle}} = \dfrac{Z_{gear}}{Z_{pinion}}$ | N = rpm, Z = number of teeth. |
| 8 | **Torque on driving gear (bevel)** | $T_{gear} = W_t \cdot r_{mean}$ | $W_t$ = tangential load (N), $r_{mean}$ = mean gear radius (m). |
| 9 | **Tangential tooth load** | $W_t = \dfrac{T_{pinion}}{r_{pinion}}$ | T in N┬Ęm, r in m ŌåÆ W_t in N. |
| 10 | **Equivalent torque at wheel** | $T_w = T_{engine} \cdot i_{gearbox} \cdot i_{fd}$ | Multiplied by transmission and final drive ratios. |
| 11 | **Equivalent wheel speed** | $N_w = \dfrac{N_{engine}}{i_{gearbox} \cdot i_{fd}}$ | rpm at the wheel. |
| 12 | **Vehicle speed from engine rpm** | $V = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot r_w \cdot N_w}{60} \times 3.6$ (km/h) | $N_w$ in rpm, $r_w$ in m. |
| 13 | **Combined shaft stress (von Mises, solid shaft)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | For combined bending + torsion. |
| 14 | **Power transmitted** | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60{,}000}$ | P in kW, N in rpm, T in N┬Ęm. |
| 15 | **Module of bevel gear (mean)** | $m = \dfrac{d_{mean}}{Z}$ | d in mm, m in mm. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse absolute values $\vert x \vert$ with table separators.** In written KTU scripts, use parentheses or modulus brackets to avoid table parsing errors in your answer booklet layout.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of stub axles and the choice of final drive layout directly impacts:

- **Vehicle payload capacity** (light vs. heavy commercial)
- **Driveline efficiency** (single vs. double reduction)
- **Maintenance cost** (full-floating axles are easier to service in the field)
- **Off-road performance** (lockable inter-axle differentials for traction)
- **Ground clearance** (hypoid vs. spiral bevel selection)
- **Emissions and fuel economy** (single reduction is more efficient)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:** Modern **Tesla and Lucid electric vehicles** use **single-speed planetary gear final drives** with ratios of about **8ŌĆō10 : 1**, replacing the multi-speed transmissions of ICE vehicles. This trend is being asked in KTU 2024 questions.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Stress Equations for the Axle Shaft

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Bending Stress in a Three-Quarter Floating Axle

**Given:**
- Vertical load on one wheel = $W_w$ (N)
- Effective lever arm from the bearing to the wheel center = $L$ (m)
- Shaft diameter = $d$ (m)
- Cornering thrust coefficient = $\mu$ (dimensionless)

**Step 1: Compute the vertical load share**

The static vertical load on each rear wheel is half the total rear axle load (for a symmetric vehicle):

$$W_w = \dfrac{m_r \cdot g}{2}$$

where $m_r$ is the rear axle payload mass (kg) and $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓.

**Step 2: Compute the cornering thrust**

When the vehicle turns, a lateral force acts on the wheel contact patch:

$$F_{lat} = \mu \cdot W_w$$

where $\mu$ is the cornering friction coefficient (typically **0.4ŌĆō0.8** for dry asphalt).

**Step 3: Bending moment at the bearing location**

The wheel applies a vertical force $W_w$ at a distance $L$ from the bearing. The bending moment at the bearing is:

$$M = W_w \cdot L$$

**Step 4: Section modulus of the solid circular shaft**

For a solid circular cross-section, the area moment of inertia is:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{64}$$

The maximum distance from the neutral axis is $y = d/2$. Thus, the **section modulus** $Z$ is:

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{y} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{32}$$

**Step 5: Maximum bending stress**

Substituting into the flexure formula:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{32 \cdot M}{\pi \cdot d^3}$$

**Step 6: Numerical substitution**

Let us assume: $m_r = 1500$ kg, $L = 0.4$ m, $d = 0.04$ m (40 mm).

Compute vertical wheel load:

$$W_w = \dfrac{1500 \times 9.81}{2} = 7357.5 \text{ N}$$

Compute bending moment:

$$M = 7357.5 \times 0.4 = 2943 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

Compute section modulus:

$$Z = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.04)^3}{32} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 6.283 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

Compute bending stress:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{2943}{6.283 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.685 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 468.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A bending stress of ~470 MPa is dangerously high for a typical **medium-carbon steel axle** (yield strength ~600 MPa). This is exactly why three-quarter floating axles are restricted to **lower loads** ŌĆö the shaft is over-stressed.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Torsional Stress

**Given:**
- Engine torque = $T_e$ (N┬Ęm)
- Gearbox ratio in top gear = $i_g$ (dimensionless)
- Final drive ratio = $i_{fd}$ (dimensionless)
- Shaft outer radius = $r$ (m)

**Step 1: Torque at the wheel**

$$T_w = T_e \times i_g \times i_{fd} \times \eta_{driveline}$$

where $\eta_{driveline}$ is the driveline efficiency (typically **0.85ŌĆō0.92**).

**Step 2: Polar moment of inertia**

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$:

$$J = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$$

**Step 3: Torsional shear stress**

$$\tau = \dfrac{T_w \cdot r}{J} = \dfrac{16 \cdot T_w}{\pi \cdot d^3}$$

**Step 4: Numerical check**

Let $T_e = 250$ N┬Ęm, $i_g = 1$, $i_{fd} = 4$, $\eta = 0.9$, $d = 0.04$ m.

$$T_w = 250 \times 1 \times 4 \times 0.9 = 900 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

$$\tau = \dfrac{16 \times 900}{\pi \times (0.04)^3} = \dfrac{14{,}400}{2.011 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.16 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \approx 71.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Combined (von Mises) stress**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3 \tau^2} = \sqrt{(468.5)^2 + 3 \times (71.6)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{219{,}492 + 15{,}381} = \sqrt{234{,}873} = 484.6 \text{ MPa}$$

This is dangerously close to the yield strength ŌĆö a clear indicator that **full-floating design is required** for higher loads.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Full-Floating Axle Stress

In a full-floating axle, **bending stress on the shaft = 0**, because the hub-and-bearing assembly carries all the bending moment. The shaft carries **only torsion**:

$$\sigma_{eq}^{FF} = \sqrt{0^2 + 3 \tau^2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau = 1.732 \cdot \tau$$

**Numerical check** (same parameters as above):

$$\sigma_{eq}^{FF} = 1.732 \times 71.6 = 124.0 \text{ MPa}$$

This is **4├Ś lower** than the three-quarter floating equivalent, justifying the use of full-floating axles for **higher loads with smaller diameter shafts** ŌĆö a key efficiency gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The full-floating axle can be made with a **thinner shaft** (and hence lighter) for the same torque, because the shaft no longer needs bending stiffness. This is why heavy truck axles look deceptively slim at the shaft even though the hub assembly is massive.

---

## 3.2 Derivations for Final Drive Ratio and Vehicle Speed

### 3.2.1 Total Driveline Ratio

The total reduction from engine to wheel is:

$$i_{total} = i_{gearbox} \times i_{fd} \times i_{axle\,hub\,reduction}$$

For a typical passenger car in top gear:
- $i_{gearbox} = 1 : 1$ (overdrive off)
- $i_{fd} = 3.7 : 1$
- $i_{axle\,hub} = 1 : 1$ (no hub reduction)

$$i_{total} = 1 \times 3.7 \times 1 = 3.7$$

### 3.2.2 Vehicle Speed Derivation

Wheel rpm is:

$$N_w = \dfrac{N_e}{i_{total}}$$

Wheel linear speed:

$$V_{m/s} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot r_w \cdot N_w}{60}$$

Converting to km/h:

$$V_{km/h} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot r_w \cdot N_w}{60} \times 3.6 = \dfrac{0.3768 \cdot r_w \cdot N_w}{1}$$

**Numerical example:**

Let $N_e = 3000$ rpm, $r_w = 0.33$ m (typical car tire), $i_{total} = 3.7$.

$$N_w = \dfrac{3000}{3.7} = 810.8 \text{ rpm}$$

$$V = 0.3768 \times 0.33 \times 810.8 = 100.8 \text{ km/h}$$

This matches typical **highway cruising speeds** for a passenger car at 3000 rpm in top gear.

### 3.2.3 Climb Gradient Capability Derivation

A vehicle can climb a gradient if the available tractive force exceeds the gravitational resistance:

$$F_{tractive} = \dfrac{T_w}{r_w} \geq F_{gradient} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

Therefore, the **maximum climb angle** is:

$$\theta_{max} = \sin^{-1}\left( \dfrac{T_w}{m \cdot g \cdot r_w} \right)$$

**Numerical example:**

A loaded truck with $m = 20{,}000$ kg, $T_w = 25{,}000$ N┬Ęm, $r_w = 0.5$ m:

$$F_{tractive} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{0.5} = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{gradient} = 20{,}000 \times 9.81 \times \sin(\theta) = 196{,}200 \times \sin(\theta)$$

For climbing, we need:

$$50{,}000 \geq 196{,}200 \times \sin(\theta) \Rightarrow \sin(\theta) \leq 0.2548$$

$$\theta_{max} = \sin^{-1}(0.2548) \approx 14.76┬░$$

This is a **~27% gradient**, typical capability for a heavy commercial truck with a **double reduction final drive**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Float Type vs. Loads Carried

| Load Component | Quarter Floating | Three-Quarter Floating | Semi-Floating | **Full Floating** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle Weight (Vertical) | Shaft | Shaft | Shaft | **Hub & Bearings** |
| Cornering Thrust (Lateral) | Shaft | Shaft | Shaft | **Hub & Bearings** |
| Driving Torque (Torsion) | Shaft | Shaft | Shaft | **Shaft** |
| Braking Torque | Shaft | Shaft | Shaft | **Shaft + Hub** |
| Axial Thrust (Acceleration) | Shaft | Shaft | Shaft | **Bearings** |
| Bending Moment | Shaft | Shaft | Shaft | **Hub & Bearings** |

> [!NOTE]
> The naming convention ("quarter," "three-quarter," "semi," "full") reflects the **fraction of total loads** carried by the shaft. In a full-floating design, the shaft carries **zero** bending or thrust loads ŌĆö hence "fully floating" in pure torsion.

---

## 3.4 Decision Algorithm for Selecting a Final Drive Type

Use the following decision tree to choose the appropriate final drive for a given application:

```
START
  |
  +-- Is ground clearance a critical constraint?
  |     |
  |     +-- YES --> Hypoid Gear Final Drive
  |     +-- NO  --> Spiral Bevel Gear Final Drive
  |
  +-- Is the vehicle load > 10 tonnes per axle?
  |     |
  |     +-- YES --> Double Reduction (Bevel + Planetary)
  |     +-- NO  --> Single Reduction
  |
  +-- Is the application off-road or military?
  |     |
  |     +-- YES --> Out-board Planetary Hub Reduction
  |     +-- NO  --> In-axle (Chelsea-type) Single Reduction
  |
  +-- Is the application an EV or hybrid?
        |
        +-- YES --> Single-stage Planetary Reducer
        +-- NO  --> Conventional Bevel Gear Pair
END
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked "justify the type of final drive used in a particular vehicle," use this decision logic to structure your answer for full marks.
