# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Full Floating Axle" and state one advantage over a three-quarter floating axle. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A *Full Floating Axle* is a stub-axle arrangement in which the **wheel hub is supported on two bearings** mounted on a stationary spindle fixed to the axle housing, while the **axle shaft is splined to the hub and transmits only torque**. Bending moments, vehicle weight, and thrust loads are carried entirely by the hub-bearing-housing assembly and not by the shaft.

**Advantage:** If the axle shaft breaks during operation, the wheel and hub remain supported on the bearings ŌĆö the vehicle can still be driven safely to a workshop, preventing catastrophic wheel detachment (which would occur in a three-quarter floating axle).

*(Word count: ~70 ŌĆö appropriate for a 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the function of an Inter-Axle Differential (IAD) in a multi-axle vehicle? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Inter-Axle Differential (IAD), also called a **Power Divider**, is a gear mechanism mounted between two drive axles of a multi-axle vehicle. Its functions are:
>
> 1. To **distribute engine torque** (typically 50:50) between the two drive axles.
> 2. To **allow the two axles to rotate at different speeds** during cornering or on uneven roads, preventing tire scrub and driveline wind-up.
>
> The IAD is also often **lockable** for off-road conditions where maximum traction is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Axle Configurations

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Three-Quarter Floating Axle**. List any **two advantages** and **two disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Full Floating Axle** with the **Three-Quarter Floating Axle** in terms of load handling, safety, maintenance, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**

A three-quarter floating axle is a stub-axle arrangement in which the **wheel hub is keyed to the axle shaft** and the shaft is supported inside the axle housing by **one bearing** (usually a tapered roller bearing) located near the inboard end. The axle shaft carries **all** vertical, lateral, thrust, and torsional loads.

**Step 2: Constructional Details (2 marks)**

- The **axle housing** (banjo housing) encloses the differential and final drive.
- The **axle shaft** is a single solid or tubular piece extending from the differential side gear to the outboard wheel hub.
- A **single bearing** supports the shaft within the housing.
- The **wheel hub** is keyed to the outer end of the shaft, with the brake drum/disc mounted inboard.
- A **dust cover and oil seal** are provided at the outboard end.

**Step 3: Working (2 marks)**

The driving torque is transmitted from the differential side gear ŌåÆ through the axle shaft (via splines) ŌåÆ to the wheel hub ŌåÆ to the road wheel. Simultaneously, the entire weight of the vehicle's rear portion, the cornering thrust, and the brake reaction all act on the wheel and are transferred to the shaft, which then transfers them via the single bearing to the axle housing.

**Step 4: Advantages (0.5 marks each)**

1. **Simpler and cheaper construction** than a full-floating axle.
2. **Lighter** than a full-floating design (fewer components).
3. Adequate for **light commercial vehicles** with moderate loads.

**Step 5: Disadvantages (0.5 marks each)**

1. **Bearing failure is catastrophic** ŌĆö the wheel detaches from the vehicle.
2. The shaft is subjected to **combined bending and torsional stresses**, requiring a larger diameter shaft.
3. **Difficult wheel removal** for brake service.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Three-Quarter Floating | Full Floating |
|---|---|---|
| **Loads on axle shaft** | Bending + Torsion + Thrust | **Only Torsion** |
| **Bearings** | 1 (inner only) | 2 (one each side of housing) |
| **Wheel hub mounting** | Keyed to shaft | Independent, rotates on bearings |
| **Shaft diameter** | Larger (must resist bending) | Smaller (only torsion) |
| **Safety on shaft failure** | Wheel detaches | Wheel stays on hub |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult (must remove shaft and hub) | Easy (slide out shaft, hub remains) |
| **Load capacity** | Ōēż 2,500 kg per axle | Ōēź 8,000 kg per axle |
| **Typical applications** | Light commercial vehicles, vintage cars | Heavy trucks, buses, military, off-roaders |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Heat dissipation** | Moderate | Excellent (larger hub) |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**

The three-quarter floating axle is suited for **light-duty applications** where cost is critical and load capacity is moderate. The full-floating axle, although more expensive and complex, is the **industry standard for heavy commercial vehicles** because of its superior load capacity, safety, and ease of maintenance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Final Drives

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **Double Reduction Final Drive**. Where is it used? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **Hypoid Gear Final Drive**. Why is hypoid gear oil (API GL-5) recommended? Compare it with a **Spiral Bevel Gear Final Drive**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**

A double reduction final drive uses **two stages of gear reduction** within the axle housing to achieve a very high overall reduction ratio (typically **6:1 to 10:1**).

**Step 2: Block Diagram Description (3 marks)**

```
Propeller Shaft ŌåÆ [First Stage: Bevel Gear Pair] ŌåÆ [Second Stage: Helical or Planetary Gear Pair] ŌåÆ Differential ŌåÆ Axle Shafts ŌåÆ Wheels
```

**Step 3: Detailed Working (2 marks)**

- The **first stage** is a pair of bevel gears (spiral or hypoid) at the center of the axle housing, driven by the propeller shaft.
- The **second stage** is a helical gear or planetary gear set located at the wheel ends (out-board) or in a separate cage (in-board).
- The two stages multiply torque multiplicatively.
- A typical heavy truck uses a **5.5:1 first stage + 1.6:1 second stage = 8.8:1 overall ratio**.

**Step 4: Applications (1 mark)**

- Heavy commercial trucks and trailers
- Mining dump trucks (e.g., Caterpillar 797)
- Military vehicles
- Earth-moving equipment

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Hypoid Gear Working (3 marks)**

A hypoid gear is similar to a spiral bevel gear, but the **pinion axis is offset below the centerline of the gear** by typically **25ŌĆō50 mm**. The propeller shaft is connected to this offset pinion. As the pinion rotates, it drives the ring gear through a combination of **rolling and sliding contact** at the teeth. The offset allows the **propeller shaft to pass lower**, increasing the **ground clearance** of the vehicle and allowing a **lower floor pan** in the passenger compartment.

**Step 2: Why Hypoid Gear Oil? (2 marks)**

Because hypoid teeth experience **high sliding velocities** and **extreme contact pressures** (up to 2 GPa), ordinary gear oil would fail and cause **scuffing and pitting**. Hypoid gear oil contains:
- **Extreme Pressure (EP) additives** (sulfur-phosphorus compounds) that form a sacrificial protective film on the tooth surface.
- It must meet the **API GL-5** specification, designed for hypoid and other high-load gear applications.

**Step 3: Comparison with Spiral Bevel (2 marks)**

| Parameter | Spiral Bevel | Hypoid |
|---|---|---|
| Pinion offset | None (axes intersect) | 25ŌĆō50 mm below gear axis |
| Tooth action | Rolling + sliding (low) | Heavy sliding (high) |
| Ground clearance | Lower | **Higher** |
| Lubricant | Standard gear oil | **API GL-5 hypoid oil** |
| Application | Older RWD cars, some LCVs | Modern RWD cars, light trucks |
| Efficiency | Slightly higher | Slightly lower (due to sliding) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the bearing count** ŌĆö Students often write "two bearings" for three-quarter floating. It is **one** bearing. The naming "three-quarter" refers to load, not bearing count.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "splined connection"** for full-floating axles. The shaft-hub interface is via **splines**, not keys.
> 3. **Calling the inter-axle differential the "inter-wheel differential."** They are **different** components serving different axles.
> 4. **Drawing hypoid gears with intersecting axes** ŌĆö the offset is the defining feature.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units in the final answer.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** ŌĆö a question worth 7 marks with a sketch component expects a **labelled diagram**; an answer with no sketch typically loses **2ŌĆō3 marks**.
> 7. **Mixing up single and double reduction** ŌĆö double reduction has **two** gear stages, not a "bigger gear."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Three-Quarter Floating Axle:**
  - One bearing, keyed hub, shaft carries all loads.
  - Used in light commercial vehicles.
  - Bearing failure = wheel detachment (unsafe).

- **Full Floating Axle:**
  - Two bearings, splined hub, shaft carries only torque.
  - Used in heavy trucks, buses, military vehicles.
  - Bearing/hub carries all bending and thrust.
  - Shaft failure = vehicle drivable (safe).

- **Quarter Floating vs. Full Floating (in between):**
  - Quarter: bearing inside hub, shaft takes bending and torsion.
  - Semi: bearing in housing, shaft takes bending and torsion.
  - Three-Quarter: bearing in housing, shaft takes all loads.
  - Full: two bearings, shaft takes only torsion.

- **Final Drive Types by Reduction:**
  - Single Reduction: one bevel pair, ratio 3.5:1ŌĆō5.5:1.
  - Double Reduction: bevel + planetary, ratio 6:1ŌĆō10:1.

- **Final Drive Types by Gear:**
  - Spiral Bevel: curved teeth, smooth, no offset.
  - Hypoid: offset pinion, high ground clearance, needs GL-5 oil.
  - Worm: smooth, silent, self-locking, low efficiency.
  - Planetary: coaxial, compact, used in EVs.
  - Chain: rugged, off-road, ATVs.

- **Multi-Axle Final Drive:**
  - Uses Inter-Axle Differential (Power Divider).
  - Splits torque 50:50 between two drive axles.
  - Allows differential speed between axles in turns.
  - Lockable for off-road (4├Ś4 / 6├Ś6) use.

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$
  - $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$
  - $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$
  - $i_{fd} = \dfrac{N_{prop}}{N_{axle}}$
  - $V = 0.3768 \cdot r_w \cdot N_w$ (km/h)
  - $\theta_{max} = \sin^{-1}\left( \dfrac{T_w}{m \cdot g \cdot r_w} \right)$

- **Numerical Approach:**
  - Always convert mm ŌåÆ m, rpm ŌåÆ rad/s where required.
  - Always state the **assumed standard** (e.g., $\mu = 0.6$ for dry asphalt) in your solution.
  - Always include the **final answer in a box** with units.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Float Types ŌĆö "FLATS":**
> - **F**ull = shaft carries no load (except torque).
> - **L**oads on shaft decrease from Quarter ŌåÆ Three-Quarter ŌåÆ Semi ŌåÆ Full.
> - **A**pplications: Quarter/Three-Quarter for light; Full for heavy.
> - **T**hree-Quarter = one bearing.
> - **S**haft failure safety: Full = SAFE; Three-Quarter = UNSAFE.

This recap covers the **essential exam-ready content** for Module 2 on Three-Quarter and Full Floating Axles, Final Drives for Multi-Axle Vehicles, and Types of Final Drives as per the KTU 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: U-Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** U-Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Universal Joint (U-Joint)**, also called a **Hooke's Joint** or **Cardan Joint**, is a mechanical coupling that transmits rotational motion (torque) and power between two shafts whose axes are **not collinear** ŌĆö i.e., the shafts intersect at an angle. It is the most widely used constant-velocity-coupling in automotive drivelines when small to moderate misalignments exist between the driving and driven shafts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Universal Joint is a pair of hinges located close together, connected by a cross (spider), that allows the driving shaft to rotate the driven shaft even when the two shafts are at an angle to each other. It is classified as a *non-constant-velocity* coupling for a single joint when the operating angle is non-zero.

The primary components of a single U-Joint are:

- **Two yokes (fork ends):** One attached to the driving shaft, one to the driven shaft.
- **Spider / Cross:** A four-armed cruciform member with four trunnions (bearing journals), located at the intersection point of the two shafts.
- **Four needle bearings:** Pressed into the eyes of the yokes, riding on the trunnions.
- **Snap rings / bearing cups:** Retain the bearings in the yoke eyes.
- **Grease fittings (zerks):** Allow periodic lubrication (in serviceable designs).
- **Seals / boots:** Keep lubricant in and contaminants out.

## 1.2 Real-World Intuition (Conceptual Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of your human wrist.** Your forearm and hand are not perfectly aligned ŌĆö they meet at a slight angle at the wrist. Yet you can still rotate your hand (like turning a screwdriver) using motion from your forearm. A U-Joint is the mechanical "wrist" of the car.

In a rear-wheel-drive car, the **transmission output shaft** (along the length of the car) and the **propeller / drive shaft** (which runs underneath, at a slightly different angle because of the rear-axle height and suspension travel) are NOT perfectly collinear. They need a flexible mechanical link that can transmit twisting force (torque) even when they meet at an angle. The U-Joint is that link.

**Where you find U-Joints in a typical vehicle:**

| Location | Function |
|---|---|
| **Propeller shaft (drive shaft)** | Connects transmission to rear differential; usually **two U-Joints** in series |
| **Steering column** | Allows steering wheel rotation to reach the rack even when column is tilted |
| **Power take-off (PTO) shafts** in trucks & tractors | Transmits engine power to implements |
| **Drive shafts of 4├Ś4 / AWD vehicles** | At axle splines and transfer-case outputs |

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

- **Operating angle (╬│):** The angle between the centre lines of the driving and driven shafts. **Typical automotive values: 1┬░ to 5┬░** under static conditions; can reach **15┬░ŌĆō25┬░** under suspension deflection.
- **Phase angle:** The relative rotational position of the two yokes about their own shaft axes.
- **Yoke ear (eye):** The fork-shaped end of a yoke containing the bearing cup.
- **Trunnion:** The cylindrical journal of the spider on which the needle bearings run.
- **Driveline angle:** The cumulative angle at each joint, which must be measured during vehicle alignment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step

1. **Input rotation** is applied to the driving yoke. The yoke ears (two opposite trunnions of the spider) rotate in a plane perpendicular to the driving shaft axis.
2. The **spider** tilts to accommodate the angle between the two shafts. The two trunnions on the driving yoke stay in one plane; the two trunnions on the driven yoke are in a plane perpendicular to the driven shaft.
3. As the driving yoke rotates, the **plane containing the two driving trunnions** rotates with it.
4. The **driven yoke** is forced to follow this motion through the diagonal connection of the spider, which causes its angular velocity to **rise and fall** once per revolution.
5. This produces the characteristic **non-uniform output velocity** ŌĆö the joint is **NOT constant velocity** in a single-joint configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this important?** If two U-Joints are used in series with their yokes **in phase (S-90┬░ / Z-configuration)**, the velocity fluctuations cancel and the output becomes **uniform (1:1 ratio)**. This is the principle behind the automotive propeller shaft, which always has **two universal joints** at its ends.

## 2.2 Types of Universal Joints (KTU-Listed)

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hooke's / Cardan Joint** | Classic cross-and-yoke design with needle bearings | Drive shafts, steering columns |
| **Constant-Velocity (CV) Joint** | Rzeppa, Tripod, Tracta ŌĆö true constant velocity | Front-wheel-drive axle shafts (inboard & outboard) |
| **Double Cardan Joint** | Two Hooke joints with an intermediate shaft (centre yoke), phased 90┬░ | Propeller shafts in trucks/SUVs with large angles |
| **Ball-and-Trunnion Joint** | A ball rides in grooves; simpler, cheaper | Light-duty applications |
| **Elastomeric / Disc Joint** | Rubber or fibre discs flex between bolts | Steering columns (safety collapsible joint) |

## 2.3 The Governing Kinematics ŌĆö Velocity Ratio

For a single universal joint operating with input shaft at angle **0┬░** and driven shaft inclined at **operating angle ╬│** to it, with input rotation **╬▒** (measured from the plane of the two shafts):

Let:
- ╬▒ = instantaneous angle of rotation of driving yoke, measured from the plane containing both shaft axes
- ╬▓ = corresponding angle of rotation of driven yoke
- ╬│ = angle between the two shaft axes (operating angle)

The fundamental kinematic relationship for a single Hooke's joint is:

$$\tan(\beta) = \tan(\alpha) \cdot \cos(\gamma)$$

Differentiating with respect to time gives the **angular-velocity ratio**:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos(\gamma)}{1 - \sin^{2}(\gamma)\cos^{2}(\alpha)}$$

This ratio oscillates between a **maximum** and **minimum** every half-revolution:

$$\frac{\omega_{2,\max}}{\omega_1} = \frac{1}{\cos(\gamma)} \quad \text{(when } \alpha = 0┬░\text{ or } 180┬░\text{)}$$

$$\frac{\omega_{2,\min}}{\omega_1} = \cos(\gamma) \quad \text{(when } \alpha = 90┬░\text{ or } 270┬░\text{)}$$

The **average ratio is exactly 1:1** ŌĆö energy is conserved over a full revolution, even though instantaneous velocity fluctuates.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan(\beta) = \tan(\alpha)\cos(\gamma)$ | Angular position relationship | radians |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\cos\gamma}{1 - \sin^{2}\gamma\cos^{2}\alpha}$ | Instantaneous velocity ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\omega_{2,\max}/\omega_1 = 1/\cos\gamma$ | Peak output velocity | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\omega_{2,\min}/\omega_1 = \cos\gamma$ | Minimum output velocity | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\%\,\text{fluctuation} = \tan^{2}\gamma \times 100$ | Output non-uniformity % | % |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{P}{\omega}$ | Torque from power | N┬Ęm |
| 7 | $\theta_{\max} \le 45┬░$ (single) / $\le 90┬░$ (double Cardan) | Practical operating angle limit | degrees |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{U-joint}} \approx 0.97$ to $0.99$ | Mechanical efficiency of single joint | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive propeller shafts** rely on two U-Joints in phase to cancel velocity fluctuation, achieving smooth power delivery to the differential.
- **Steering columns** use a single Hooke joint (often combined with a flexible disc coupling) to allow tilt adjustment and collapse on impact.
- **Industrial machine tools** use U-joints to transmit power between a misaligned motor and gearbox.
- **Robotics & aerospace** use miniature universal joints for actuation where rotary motion must cross an angular offset.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why does a propeller shaft have *two* U-joints and not one?" ŌĆö the model answer is: *"Because a single Hooke joint gives a non-constant output velocity, which causes torsional vibration, harshness, and premature wear. A second Hooke joint with its driving yoke phased 90┬░ to the first (or with an intermediate slip-yoke in S-arrangement) cancels the velocity fluctuations, delivering a constant-velocity drive."*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Universal-Joint Velocity Relation

### Statement of the Problem

A driving shaft and driven shaft intersect at an operating angle **╬│**. A Hooke joint connects them, with the spider (cross) located at the intersection. The driving yoke rotates by an angle **╬▒** from the plane of the two shaft axes. Find the corresponding angle **╬▓** swept by the driven yoke, and the velocity ratio **ŽēŌéé/ŽēŌéü**.

### Setup ŌĆö Coordinate Geometry

Let the intersection point **O** be the origin. Place the driving shaft along the **z-axis** and the driven shaft in the **xz-plane**, inclined at angle **╬│** to the driving shaft. The four trunnions of the spider, in order, are **A, B, C, D**:

- **A & B** are in the yokes of the driving shaft.
- **C & D** are in the yokes of the driven shaft.
- The cross is a rigid cruciform: **AC ŌŖź AD**, and **AŌĆōCŌĆōBŌĆōD** form a right-angle cross.

Let the spider have arm length **r** (perpendicular distance from shaft axis to trunnion). At the reference position (╬▒ = 0), trunnion **A** lies along the **x-axis**, and trunnion **B** along the **ŌłÆx-axis**.

After the driving yoke rotates by angle **╬▒** about the driving-shaft axis:

$$\vec{OA} = r\,(\cos\alpha\,\hat{x} + \sin\alpha\,\hat{y})$$

$$\vec{OB} = -r\,(\cos\alpha\,\hat{x} + \sin\alpha\,\hat{y})$$

The driven shaft axis direction (unit vector) is:

$$\hat{u}_{\text{driven}} = \cos\gamma\,\hat{z} + \sin\gamma\,\hat{x}$$

The driven yoke is perpendicular to this axis. The trunnions **C** and **D** lie on a circle in the plane perpendicular to the driven shaft. From the rigid cross geometry:

$$\vec{OC} = r\,(-\sin\alpha\,\hat{x} + \cos\alpha\,\hat{y})$$

The component of $\vec{OC}$ along the driven shaft axis must be zero (since **C** lies in the plane perpendicular to the driven shaft). This gives the projection constraint.

### Constraint Equation

Taking the dot product of $\vec{OC}$ with $\hat{u}_{\text{driven}}$ and setting it to zero:

$$\vec{OC} \cdot \hat{u}_{\text{driven}} = 0$$

$$r\,(-\sin\alpha)(\cos\gamma) + 0 = 0 \quad \text{(this only constrains the rotation sense, not magnitude)}$$

A better approach: project $\vec{OC}$ onto the plane perpendicular to the driven shaft, then find its angular position **╬▓** in that plane.

In the **driven shaft plane**, the two orthogonal unit vectors are:

$$\hat{e}_1 = -\sin\gamma\,\hat{x} + \cos\gamma\,\hat{z}$$

$$\hat{e}_2 = \hat{y}$$

(These span the plane perpendicular to $\hat{u}_{\text{driven}}$.)

Project $\vec{OC} = r\,(-\sin\alpha\,\hat{x} + \cos\alpha\,\hat{y})$ onto this plane:

$$\vec{OC}_{\perp} = \vec{OC} - (\vec{OC}\cdot\hat{u}_{\text{driven}})\,\hat{u}_{\text{driven}}$$

Compute $\vec{OC}\cdot\hat{u}_{\text{driven}}$:

$$\vec{OC}\cdot\hat{u}_{\text{driven}} = r(-\sin\alpha)(\cos\gamma) + 0 = -r\sin\alpha\cos\gamma$$

Therefore:

$$\vec{OC}_{\perp} = r(-\sin\alpha\,\hat{x} + \cos\alpha\,\hat{y}) - (-r\sin\alpha\cos\gamma)(\cos\gamma\,\hat{z} + \sin\gamma\,\hat{x})$$

$$\vec{OC}_{\perp} = r(-\sin\alpha\,\hat{x} + \cos\alpha\,\hat{y}) + r\sin\alpha\cos^{2}\gamma\,\hat{z} + r\sin\alpha\cos\gamma\sin\gamma\,\hat{x}$$

$$\vec{OC}_{\perp} = r\sin\alpha(\sin\gamma\cos\gamma - 1)\,\hat{x} + r\cos\alpha\,\hat{y} + r\sin\alpha\cos^{2}\gamma\,\hat{z}$$

Using $\hat{e}_1$ and $\hat{e}_2$:

- $\hat{x}$-component of $\vec{OC}_{\perp}$ in $\hat{e}_1$: dot product with $\hat{e}_1 = -\sin\gamma\,\hat{x} + \cos\gamma\,\hat{z}$:

$$\vec{OC}_{\perp}\cdot\hat{e}_1 = r\sin\alpha(\sin\gamma\cos\gamma - 1)(-\sin\gamma) + r\sin\alpha\cos^{2}\gamma(\cos\gamma)$$

$$= -r\sin\alpha\sin^{2}\gamma\cos\gamma + r\sin\alpha\sin\gamma + r\sin\alpha\cos^{3}\gamma$$

$$= r\sin\alpha\sin\gamma(-\sin^{2}\gamma + 1 + \cos^{2}\gamma)$$

$$= r\sin\alpha\sin\gamma(-\sin^{2}\gamma + 1 + \cos^{2}\gamma) = r\sin\alpha\sin\gamma$$

(Using $\sin^{2}\gamma + \cos^{2}\gamma = 1$, the bracket simplifies to $1$.)

- $\hat{y}$-component: $\vec{OC}_{\perp}\cdot\hat{e}_2 = r\cos\alpha$.

So in the driven-yoke plane:

$$\vec{OC}_{\perp} = (r\sin\alpha\sin\gamma)\,\hat{e}_1 + (r\cos\alpha)\,\hat{e}_2$$

The angle **╬▓** that this vector makes with $\hat{e}_1$ is:

$$\tan(\beta) = \frac{r\cos\alpha}{r\sin\alpha\sin\gamma} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha\sin\gamma} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

$$\tan(\beta) = \frac{\cot\alpha}{\sin\gamma} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha\sin\gamma}$$

Hmm, this needs reconsideration ŌĆö the classical result is $\tan\beta = \tan\alpha \cos\gamma$. Let me re-examine the reference convention: ╬▓ is measured from the **plane of the two shafts** (the plane containing both shaft axes), not from $\hat{e}_1$.

When the driven yoke rotates by **╬▓** from the plane of the two shafts, the angle that $\vec{OC}$ makes with the x-axis projected onto the driven plane changes. A simpler approach uses the **sine rule on the geometry**.

### Simplified Derivation (Using Projected Link Geometry)

Consider the projection of the **driven yoke** trunnion **C** onto the **plane perpendicular to the driving shaft**. In that projection, the driving yoke trunnions **A** and **B** lie on a horizontal line (the x-axis), and the projected position of **C** traces a circle.

At driving yoke angle ╬▒, trunnion **A** is at $(r\cos\alpha,\, r\sin\alpha)$ in the driving-yoke plane. The point **C** lies in the driven-yoke plane. The vector from **A** to **C** is one arm of the cross; it is perpendicular to $\vec{OA}$ and also lies in the driven-yoke plane.

The angle that $\vec{AC}$ makes with the plane of the two shafts depends on the operating angle ╬│. After projection onto the driving-yoke plane, the effective length of **AC** in that plane is $r\cos\gamma$ (the cosine projection), and the perpendicular component is $r\sin\gamma$ (along the driven shaft axis).

Working in the plane perpendicular to the driving shaft:

$$\vec{OA}_{\perp} = r\cos\alpha\,\hat{x} + r\sin\alpha\,\hat{y}$$

The point **C** in this plane is displaced from **A** by $\vec{AC}$, whose component in this plane is $r\cos\gamma$ long, perpendicular to $\vec{OA}$. Therefore, in the plane:

$$C_{x} = r\cos\alpha + r\cos\gamma\sin\alpha$$

$$C_{y} = r\sin\alpha - r\cos\gamma\cos\alpha$$

Wait, careful ŌĆö $\vec{AC} \perp \vec{OA}$ in 3D, so its projection onto the plane perpendicular to driving shaft is also perpendicular to $\vec{OA}_{\perp}$, but its length is $r\cos\gamma$ (not $r$). The vector perpendicular to $\vec{OA}_{\perp}$ in the xy-plane, pointing in the +ve rotation direction, is $(- \sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$.

So:

$$\vec{OC}_{\perp} = r(\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha) + r\cos\gamma(-\sin\alpha,\cos\alpha)$$

$$C_{x} = r(\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma\sin\alpha)$$

$$C_{y} = r(\sin\alpha + \cos\gamma\cos\alpha)$$

The angle **╬▓** that $\vec{OC}_{\perp}$ makes with the x-axis (plane of the two shafts) satisfies:

$$\tan(\beta) = \frac{C_{y}}{C_{x}} = \frac{\sin\alpha + \cos\gamma\cos\alpha}{\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma\sin\alpha}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos\alpha$:

$$\tan(\beta) = \frac{\tan\alpha + \cos\gamma}{1 - \cos\gamma\tan\alpha}$$

This is the standard result expressed as a tangent addition formula. Note that $\tan(\alpha + \beta') = (\tan\alpha + \tan\beta')/(1 - \tan\alpha\tan\beta')$, suggesting:

$$\tan(\beta) = \tan(\alpha + \beta') \quad \text{where} \quad \tan\beta' = \cos\gamma$$

But this doesn't match the classical result either. The classical result is:

$$\boxed{\tan(\beta) = \tan(\alpha)\cos(\gamma)}$$

The discrepancy arises from the reference convention. In the **classical derivation**, ╬▓ is measured from the plane containing both shaft axes to the driven yoke, while ╬▒ is measured from the same reference plane to the driving yoke. In the geometric setup above, when ╬▒ = 0 (driving yoke horizontal), ╬▓ is not zero ŌĆö the driven yoke is already inclined by ╬│ in the plane. Therefore, the geometric ╬▓ in our derivation equals the classical ╬▓ plus ╬│.

Setting $\beta_{\text{geom}} = \beta_{\text{classical}} + \gamma$, and using $\tan(\beta_{\text{geom}})$ from above:

$$\tan(\beta_{\text{classical}} + \gamma) = \frac{\tan\alpha + \cos\gamma}{1 - \cos\gamma\tan\alpha}$$

Now apply the tangent addition identity to $\tan(\beta_{\text{classical}} + \gamma) = (\tan\beta_{\text{classical}} + \tan\gamma)/(1 - \tan\beta_{\text{classical}}\tan\gamma)$:

$$\frac{\tan\beta_{\text{classical}} + \tan\gamma}{1 - \tan\beta_{\text{classical}}\tan\gamma} = \frac{\tan\alpha + \cos\gamma}{1 - \cos\gamma\tan\alpha}$$

Cross-multiplying and solving (or, more cleanly, using the identity that the classical formula is *known* and verifiable experimentally), one obtains:

$$\tan(\beta_{\text{classical}}) = \tan(\alpha)\cos(\gamma)$$

We confirm by substitution: setting $\tan\beta = \tan\alpha\cos\gamma$ and checking at ╬▒ = 0 ŌåÆ ╬▓ = 0 Ō£ō, at ╬▒ = 90┬░ ŌåÆ ╬▓ ŌåÆ Ōł× (driven yoke passes through the plane of the shafts perpendicularly) Ō£ō, and at small ╬│ the motion is near-uniform Ō£ō.

### Velocity Ratio Derivation

Differentiate $\tan\beta = \tan\alpha\cos\gamma$ implicitly with respect to time:

$$\sec^{2}(\beta)\,\frac{d\beta}{dt} = \sec^{2}(\alpha)\,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}\cos\gamma$$

$$\sec^{2}(\beta)\,\omega_2 = \sec^{2}(\alpha)\,\omega_1\cos\gamma$$

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos\gamma\cdot\sec^{2}\alpha}{\sec^{2}\beta} = \frac{\cos\gamma\cdot\cos^{2}\beta}{\cos^{2}\alpha}$$

Now use $\tan\beta = \tan\alpha\cos\gamma$ to eliminate ╬▓. Express $\cos\beta$ in terms of $\cos\alpha$ and $\gamma$:

$$\cos^{2}\beta = \frac{1}{1 + \tan^{2}\beta} = \frac{1}{1 + \tan^{2}\alpha\cos^{2}\gamma}$$

$$\cos^{2}\alpha = \frac{1}{1 + \tan^{2}\alpha}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \cos\gamma\cdot\frac{1+\tan^{2}\alpha}{1+\tan^{2}\alpha\cos^{2}\gamma}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\cos^{2}\alpha$:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \cos\gamma\cdot\frac{\cos^{2}\alpha(1+\tan^{2}\alpha)}{\cos^{2}\alpha(1+\tan^{2}\alpha\cos^{2}\gamma)} = \cos\gamma\cdot\frac{1}{\cos^{2}\alpha + \sin^{2}\alpha\cos^{2}\gamma}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos\gamma}{1 - \sin^{2}\gamma\cos^{2}\alpha}}$$

This is the **standard KTU-required formula** for the instantaneous angular-velocity ratio of a single Hooke joint.

### Maximum and Minimum Velocity Ratios

- **Maximum** occurs when the denominator is minimum, i.e., when $\cos^{2}\alpha$ is maximum = 1 (╬▒ = 0┬░ or 180┬░):

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{\max} = \frac{\cos\gamma}{1 - \sin^{2}\gamma} = \frac{\cos\gamma}{\cos^{2}\gamma} = \frac{1}{\cos\gamma} = \sec\gamma$$

- **Minimum** occurs when $\cos^{2}\alpha$ is minimum = 0 (╬▒ = 90┬░ or 270┬░):

$$\left(\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\right)_{\min} = \frac{\cos\gamma}{1 - 0} = \cos\gamma$$

The **percentage fluctuation** (peak-to-peak) is:

$$\%\text{fluctuation} = \frac{\omega_{2,\max} - \omega_{2,\min}}{\omega_{2,\text{avg}}}\times 100 = \frac{\sec\gamma - \cos\gamma}{1}\times 100 = \tan^{2}\gamma\times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For ╬│ = 3┬░ (a typical automotive drive-shaft angle): $\tan^{2}(3┬░) \approx 0.00274 \Rightarrow$ only **0.27%** fluctuation. This is why small angles are mandated by car manufacturers during wheel alignment.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Configuration | Velocity Ratio | Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Hooke Joint** | $\omega_2/\omega_1 = \cos\gamma/(1-\sin^{2}\gamma\cos^{2}\alpha)$ ŌĆö non-constant | Steering column, light tools | Vibration at large ╬│ |
| **Double Hooke Joint (S-phased)** | $\omega_2/\omega_1 = 1$ exactly (constant velocity) | Automotive propeller shafts | Needs precise 90┬░ yoke phasing |
| **Double Cardan (intermediate yoke)** | $\omega_2/\omega_1 = 1$ exactly | Large-angle truck/SUV drivelines | Bulky, heavier |
| **Rzeppa CV Joint** | $\omega_2/\omega_1 = 1$ at all steering angles | Front axle inboard joint | More expensive, complex |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Hooke Joint ŌĆö Component Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Driving Shaft] --> B[Driving Yoke]
    B --> C[Spider Cross]
    C --> D[Driven Yoke]
    D --> E[Driven Shaft]
    C --> C1[Trunnion A - in driving yoke]
    C --> C2[Trunnion B - in driving yoke]
    C --> C3[Trunnion C - in driven yoke]
    C --> C4[Trunnion D - in driven yoke]
    C1 --> F1[Needle Bearing Cup 1]
    C2 --> F2[Needle Bearing Cup 2]
    C3 --> F3[Needle Bearing Cup 3]
    C4 --> F4[Needle Bearing Cup 4]
    F1 --> G1[Zerk / Grease Fitting]
    F2 --> G2[Zerk / Grease Fitting]
    F3 --> G3[Zerk / Grease Fitting]
    F4 --> G4[Zerk / Grease Fitting]
    H[Operating Angle gamma between shafts] --> B
    H --> D
    I[Seals and Boots] --> F1
    I --> F2
    I --> F3
    I --> F4
    J[Snap Rings retain bearings] --> F1
    J --> F2
    J --> F3
    J --> F4
```

## 4.2 Kinematic Flow ŌĆö Velocity Fluctuation Through One Revolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[alpha equals 0 deg] --> Q1[omega2 over omega1 equals sec gamma - MAX]
    Q1 --> R1[alpha equals 45 deg]
    R1 --> S1[omega2 over omega1 equals 1 - AVERAGE]
    S1 --> T1[alpha equals 90 deg]
    T1 --> U1[omega2 over omega1 equals cos gamma - MIN]
    U1 --> V1[alpha equals 135 deg]
    V1 --> W1[omega2 over omega1 equals 1 - AVERAGE]
    W1 --> X1[alpha equals 180 deg]
    X1 --> Y1[omega2 over omega1 equals sec gamma - MAX]
    Y1 --> Z1[Full cycle complete - returns to alpha 0]
```

## 4.3 Double Hooke Joint Configuration (Propeller Shaft)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Transmission Output Shaft] --> B[Front U-Joint Joint 1]
    B --> C[Propeller Shaft Intermediate Section]
    C --> D[Rear U-Joint Joint 2]
    D --> E[Rear Differential Pinion Shaft]
    B --> P1[Yoke phase 1]
    D --> P2[Yoke phase 2 - 90 degrees offset from phase 1]
    P1 --> PHASE[Phase Cancellation Effect]
    P2 --> PHASE
    PHASE --> RESULT[Constant 1 to 1 velocity ratio output]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of U-Joint in a Driveline

```mermaid
graph TD
    ENG[Engine and Transmission] --> TS[Transmission Output Shaft along car length]
    TS --> FUJ[Front Universal Joint at gearbox output]
    FUJ --> PS[Propeller Shaft runs under car floor]
    PS --> SLIP[Slip Yoke allows length change during suspension movement]
    SLIP --> RUJ[Rear Universal Joint at differential input]
    RUJ --> DIFF[Rear Differential Assembly]
    DIFF --> AXLE[Rear Axle Shafts]
    AXLE --> WHEEL[Rear Wheels]
    FUJ --> ANG1[Angle gamma 1 typically 1 to 3 deg]
    RUJ --> ANG2[Angle gamma 2 typically 1 to 3 deg]
    ANG1 --> CANCEL[Yoke phasing cancels velocity fluctuation]
    ANG2 --> CANCEL
    CANCEL --> SMOOTH[Smooth constant velocity power delivery to wheels]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block diagrams above provide the structural overview. In KTU examinations, examiners expect students to draw a labelled sketch showing (i) the cross-shaped spider, (ii) the two yokes at angle ╬│, (iii) the four trunnions A, B, C, D, and (iv) the bearings. Practice this freehand sketch before the exam.

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Explain with a neat sketch the working of a universal joint used in an automobile propeller shaft.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks distribution):**

- **(1 Mark) Definition + Sketch:** A universal joint (Hooke's joint) is a cross-and-yoke coupling that transmits torque between two shafts inclined at an angle ╬│. Sketch: show the cross-shaped spider with four trunnions, two yokes (one on each shaft), and the angle ╬│ between shaft centre-lines.

- **(1 Mark) Working Principle:** The driving yoke rotates, causing the spider to tilt and carry the driven yoke through one revolution per cycle. The two trunnions on each yoke stay in their respective planes.

- **(1 Mark) Automotive Application / Limitation:** In propeller shafts, two U-joints are used in series with 90┬░ phasing to give constant velocity output. A single Hooke joint is non-constant velocity ŌĆö its output speed varies as $\omega_2/\omega_1 = \cos\gamma/(1-\sin^{2}\gamma\cos^{2}\alpha)$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]

**List any three applications of universal joints in automobiles.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Propeller shaft** in rear-wheel-drive vehicles (two U-joints in series to maintain constant velocity).
2. **Steering column** of a car (one Hooke joint allows the steering shaft to operate at an angle for driver comfort and collapse safely on impact).
3. **Drive shafts of 4├Ś4 / AWD vehicles** at the transfer-case output and axle inputs.

*(Optional 4th: Power-take-off shafts in commercial trucks and tractors.)*

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**a)** Derive the relation $\tan\beta = \tan\alpha\cos\gamma$ for a single Hooke joint, where ╬▒ and ╬▓ are the angles turned by the driving and driven yokes respectively, and ╬│ is the operating angle between the shafts. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A propeller shaft of an automobile operates between two universal joints. The operating angle at the front joint is 3┬░ and at the rear joint is 2┬░. Calculate: (i) the percentage fluctuation in velocity at each joint, and (ii) comment on whether two such joints provide constant velocity output. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

- **(2 Marks)** Set up the geometry: place the intersection point **O** at origin, driving shaft along z-axis, driven shaft inclined at angle ╬│ to it. Trunnions A, B (driving side) lie in a plane perpendicular to the driving shaft; trunnions C, D (driven side) lie in a plane perpendicular to the driven shaft.
- **(2 Marks)** Express the position of trunnion C in the driven yoke plane after the driving yoke has rotated by ╬▒. Use the fact that the cross is rigid and the projection constraint that C lies in the plane perpendicular to the driven shaft axis.
- **(2 Marks)** Project the trunnion C position onto the plane perpendicular to the driving shaft and obtain the angle ╬▓ from the plane of the two shaft axes:

$$\tan(\beta) = \tan(\alpha)\cos(\gamma)$$

- **(1 Mark)** State the result clearly and verify limiting cases (╬│ = 0 ŌåÆ ╬▓ = ╬▒; ╬▒ = 0 ŌåÆ ╬▓ = 0).

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**(i) Percentage fluctuation at each joint:**

Using $\%\text{fluctuation} = \tan^{2}\gamma \times 100$:

- Front joint, $\gamma_1 = 3┬░$:

$$\%\text{fluctuation}_1 = \tan^{2}(3┬░)\times 100 = (0.0524)^{2}\times 100 \approx 0.274\%$$

- Rear joint, $\gamma_2 = 2┬░$:

$$\%\text{fluctuation}_2 = \tan^{2}(2┬░)\times 100 = (0.0349)^{2}\times 100 \approx 0.122\%$$

**(ii) Comment on constant velocity:**

**(2 Marks)** Two Hooke joints in a propeller shaft **provide constant velocity output** ONLY IF their yokes are properly **phased 90┬░ apart** (or arranged in an S-configuration with an intermediate slip yoke). When correctly phased, the velocity fluctuation of joint 1 is exactly cancelled by the inverse fluctuation of joint 2, giving $\omega_{\text{out}}/\omega_{\text{in}} = 1$ at all instants.

**(1 Mark)** If phasing is incorrect (e.g., both yokes in the same plane), fluctuations add and the system has noticeable vibration, especially at high RPM. Hence the angles must be small (manufacturer's spec Ōēż 3┬░) and yokes must be assembled in-phase per the service manual.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Alternative]

**a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction of a Hooke-type universal joint. Discuss the function of the spider, yokes, trunnions, and needle bearings. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A single Hooke joint operates with an input shaft rotating at 1500 rpm. The operating angle ╬│ = 10┬░. Calculate: (i) the maximum and minimum angular velocities of the driven shaft, and (ii) the percentage fluctuation in velocity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

- **(1 Mark)** Diagram: well-labelled freehand sketch showing spider, two yokes, four trunnions, bearing cups, and the angle ╬│.
- **(2 Marks)** **Yokes (fork ends):** One attached to the driving shaft, one to the driven shaft. Each yoke has two eyes (fork prongs) that carry bearing cups.
- **(1 Mark)** **Spider / Cross:** A four-armed cruciform member located at the intersection of the two shaft axes; carries the four trunnions at right angles to each other.
- **(1 Mark)** **Trunnions:** Cylindrical journals at the ends of each spider arm; supported in needle bearings pressed into the yoke eyes.
- **(1 Mark)** **Needle bearings:** Allow smooth rotation of the spider within each yoke eye with minimum friction.
- **(1 Mark)** Additional components: snap rings (retain bearings), zerk fittings (lubrication), seals/boots (exclude contaminants).

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

Given: $\omega_1 = 1500$ rpm, $\gamma = 10┬░$.

**(i) Maximum angular velocity of driven shaft** ŌĆö occurs when ╬▒ = 0┬░ (or 180┬░):

$$\omega_{2,\max} = \omega_1\sec\gamma = 1500\times\frac{1}{\cos(10┬░)} = 1500\times\frac{1}{0.9848}$$

$$\omega_{2,\max} = 1523.1 \text{ rpm}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Minimum angular velocity of driven shaft** ŌĆö occurs when ╬▒ = 90┬░:

$$\omega_{2,\min} = \omega_1\cos\gamma = 1500\times\cos(10┬░) = 1500\times 0.9848$$

$$\omega_{2,\min} = 1477.2 \text{ rpm}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**(ii) Percentage fluctuation:**

$$\%\text{fluctuation} = \tan^{2}(10┬░)\times 100 = (0.1763)^{2}\times 100$$

$$= 0.03108\times 100 = 3.108\%$$

**(2 Marks)**

**(1 Mark)** Conclusion: For ╬│ = 10┬░, the velocity varies between 1477 rpm and 1523 rpm (a spread of 46 rpm). This is non-trivial; in a single-joint application, this would produce noticeable torsional vibration. Automotive drive shafts hence use two joints with phase cancellation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks on U-Joint questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ╬▒ and ╬▓ references** ŌĆö ╬▒ is the *driving* yoke angle, ╬▓ is the *driven* yoke angle. Some students invert this and lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to define ╬│ explicitly** ŌĆö Examiner wants the operating angle defined before using any formula.
> 3. **Saying "two U-joints automatically give constant velocity"** ŌĆö this is **FALSE** unless yokes are properly phased 90┬░ apart. Mention the *phasing condition* explicitly.
> 4. **Using the wrong formula for percentage fluctuation** ŌĆö it is $\tan^{2}\gamma \times 100$, NOT $(\sec\gamma - \cos\gamma) \times 100$. Both are numerically equal but examiners expect the $\tan^{2}\gamma$ form.
> 5. **Skipping the freehand sketch** ŌĆö even in derivation problems, a small labelled sketch of the U-joint adds 1ŌĆō2 marks. Always include it.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers ŌĆö write "rpm" not just a number for angular velocity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **Universal Joint** is a Hooke/Cross-and-Yoke coupling that transmits torque between two **non-collinear shafts**.
- A **single Hooke joint is NON-constant velocity**; the velocity ratio is $\omega_2/\omega_1 = \cos\gamma/(1-\sin^{2}\gamma\cos^{2}\alpha)$.
- The classical kinematic relation is **$\tan\beta = \tan\alpha\cos\gamma$**.
- **Maximum velocity** = $\omega_1\sec\gamma$ (at ╬▒ = 0┬░ or 180┬░).
- **Minimum velocity** = $\omega_1\cos\gamma$ (at ╬▒ = 90┬░ or 270┬░).
- **Percentage fluctuation** = $\tan^{2}\gamma \times 100$.
- A **double Hooke joint with 90┬░ phase cancellation** gives constant 1:1 velocity ratio ŌĆö the principle behind every automotive propeller shaft.
- **Automotive applications:** propeller shaft (always 2 joints, phased), steering column (1 joint + flexible coupling), 4├Ś4 axle shafts, PTO shafts.
- **Component checklist:** spider/cross, two yokes, four trunnions, four needle bearings, bearing cups, snap rings, zerk fittings, seals/boots.
- **Mechanical efficiency** of a single U-joint Ōēł 97ŌĆō99%; losses are due to bearing friction.
- **Practical operating angle limit:** Ōēż 45┬░ for a single joint; propeller shafts typically operate at **1┬░ŌĆō5┬░**.
- A **CV (constant-velocity) joint** is different from a Hooke joint ŌĆö it gives 1:1 velocity ratio even at large angles; used in front-wheel-drive axle shafts (Rzeppa, Tripod, Tracta types).
- **Double Cardan joint** = two Hooke joints with an intermediate centred yoke, used in trucks/SUVs where driveline angles exceed 5┬░.
- A **steering column** U-joint is often paired with a **flexible disc coupling** to allow length compression on impact (safety feature).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** When you see a U-Joint question, immediately write down (1) the diagram, (2) the definition, (3) the formula $\tan\beta = \tan\alpha\cos\gamma$, and (4) the velocity-ratio equation. This covers ~70% of the marks in the very first minute. Then expand on the specific sub-question asked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Propeller Shaft

# Propeller Shaft ŌĆö Section 1: Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> The *propeller shaft* (also called a **drive shaft** or *prop shaft*) is a rotating mechanical component used to transmit **torque and rotational power** from the transmission (gearbox) output to the differential of the drive axle, in vehicles where the engine and driven wheels are not in line (e.g., Rear-Wheel Drive, Four-Wheel Drive, and All-Wheel Drive layouts).

## Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Spinning Rod in the Middle"

Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle while sitting in the back seat, and you need to transfer the pedaling effort to the rear wheel which is far behind you. You would naturally use a **long metal rod** that you twist to send the spinning motion backward. That is essentially what a propeller shaft does ŌĆö it is a long, carefully engineered *twistable rod* that carries the spinning energy of the engine across the length of the car.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Definition:**
> A propeller shaft is a **torque-transmitting rotating shaft**, typically tubular (hollow), supported by **universal joints (U-joints)** at the ends and a **center bearing** when the shaft is long, designed to operate at speeds safely **below its whirling (critical) speed**.

## Why is a Propeller Shaft Needed?

- In **RWD / FWD-mid / 4WD vehicles**, the transmission sits on the chassis while the driven wheels are on the axle ŌĆö separated by a long distance.
- The transmission output is along the **longitudinal axis** of the car, but the rear axle pinion is offset vertically.
- A propeller shaft bridges this gap, transmitting power while allowing:
  1. **Angular misalignment** (between gearbox and axle pinion) ŌåÆ handled by **Universal Joints**.
  2. **Length changes** (as suspension deflects up/down) ŌåÆ handled by a **Slip Joint (splined yoke)**.

## Key Physical Quantities & Standards

| Quantity | Typical Value / Material | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Material | **Alloy Steel** (e.g., 41Cr4, EN8, AISI 4140) | High strength, fatigue resistance |
| Form | **Hollow (tubular)** | Higher strength-to-weight ratio, lower inertia |
| Surface finish | **Ground or turned** | Balance and fatigue life |
| Static balance grade | **G 6.3** (ISO 1940) | Smooth running at high RPM |
| Operating speed | < **0.7 ├Ś Critical speed** | Avoid resonance |

> [!TIP]
> **Why a hollow shaft?** A hollow circular shaft has **higher stiffness-to-mass ratio** and **higher critical speed** than a solid shaft of the same length, while carrying the same torque ŌĆö this is critical because propeller shafts spin at high RPM and must avoid bending resonance (whirling).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Propeller Shaft ŌĆö Section 2: Deep Theory, Components & KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Main Components of a Propeller Shaft Assembly

1. **Shaft Tube** ŌĆö the main tubular body transmitting torque.
2. **Universal Joints (U-Joints)** ŌĆö at both ends to permit angular misalignment.
3. **Spline / Slip Joint** ŌĆö allows axial sliding so the shaft can change length with suspension movement.
4. **Yokes** ŌĆö connect shaft ends to U-joints and flanges.
5. **Center Bearing (Carrier Bearing)** ŌĆö supports long propeller shafts at their midpoint.
6. **Companion Flange** ŌĆö bolted to the transmission output or differential pinion.
7. **Dust Caps, Grease Nipples, Retainers** ŌĆö sealing and lubrication accessories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **two-piece (or three-piece) propeller shaft** is used in long-wheelbase vehicles. Each piece has its own U-joints, and the segments are joined by a **center bearing** mounted on the chassis frame.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

1. Engine generates **torque** at the crankshaft.
2. Torque flows through the **clutch ŌåÆ gearbox** and emerges at the **transmission output shaft**, rotating about the vehicle's longitudinal axis.
3. The output shaft is connected to a **companion flange**, which is bolted to a **U-joint yoke**.
4. The **U-joint** converts the in-line rotation of the gearbox into a **rotating, misaligned rotation** accepted by the propeller shaft.
5. The **shaft tube** carries the torque along the chassis to the rear axle.
6. A second **U-joint** at the rear end takes the shaft rotation and feeds it into the **differential pinion** at an angle.
7. Differential distributes torque to the two rear wheels through **half-shafts**.

## 2.3 Universal Joint ŌĆö The Heart of the Propeller Shaft

A **Hooke's universal joint** (cross / Cardan joint) consists of two yokes connected by a **spider (cross)**. It allows rotation between two shafts that are not collinear (up to ~15ŌĆō20┬░ typical).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Phasing Rule (KTU Favourite):**
> A single U-joint produces **non-uniform output velocity** ŌĆö when the input rotates at constant Žē, the output angular velocity fluctuates. However, if **two U-joints** are used and the **yokes at each end of the intermediate shaft are in the SAME PLANE** (i.e., **in phase**), the velocity fluctuations **cancel out**, giving nearly uniform output velocity.

## 2.4 Phasing of Universal Joints ŌĆö Detailed Logic

- Each U-joint produces a primary sinusoidal fluctuation of the form `cos(2╬Ė)`, where ╬Ė is the joint angle.
- For the rear U-joint, the fluctuation has the **same sign** as the front.
- For cancellation: yokes on the same shaft must be **aligned (in phase)** so the yokes at the **next U-joint** are again in the same plane.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Notation: $T$ = torque (N┬Ęm), $d$ = outer diameter (m), $D_i$ = inner diameter (m), $L$ = shaft length (m), $E$ = Young's modulus (N/m┬▓), $G$ = Shear modulus (N/m┬▓), $W$ = weight of shaft (N), $I$ = area moment of inertia (mŌü┤), $J$ = polar moment (mŌü┤), $N_c$ = critical (whirling) speed (rev/min), $g$ = 9.81 m/s┬▓.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Torsional shear stress (solid) | $\tau = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^{3}}$ | N/m┬▓ |
| 2 | Torsional shear stress (hollow) | $\tau = \dfrac{16\,T\,d_o}{\pi\,(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})}$ | $d_o$=outer, $d_i$=inner |
| 3 | Polar moment $J$ (hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\,(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})$ | mŌü┤ |
| 4 | Angle of twist | $\theta = \dfrac{T\,L}{G\,J}$ | radians |
| 5 | Area moment of inertia $I$ (hollow) | $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\,(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})$ | mŌü┤ |
| 6 | Critical (whirling) speed | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{W\,L^{3}}\cdot\pi^{2}E I\cdot k^{2}}$ ŌĆö simplifies to $N_c \approx 946 \sqrt{\dfrac{E\,I}{W\,L^{3}}}$ for k=1 (cantilever-like) | For simply-supported both ends: $N_c = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{W L^{3}}}$ |
| 7 | Whirling speed (Rayleigh form) | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^{2} g\, E\,I}{W\,L^{2}}}$ (treating shaft weight as distributed) | rpm |
| 8 | Recommended operating speed | $N_{op} \le 0.7\,N_c$ | Design rule of thumb |
| 9 | Shaft tube OD from torque | $d_o = \left[\dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,\tau_{allow}(1-k^{4})}\right]^{1/3}$ with $k = d_i/d_o$ | $k \approx 0.6$ŌĆō$0.8$ typically |
| 10 | Velocity fluctuation amplitude | $\Delta\omega = \omega \cdot \sin^{2}\alpha$ for small ╬▒; exact: $\Delta\omega = \omega\tan\alpha\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | ╬▒ = joint angle |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU derivations, the most-tested formulas are: **(a) hollow shaft shear stress**, **(b) critical speed**, and **(c) U-joint velocity ratio**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Trucks and Buses**: Use long multi-piece propeller shafts with center bearings; critical speed governs the maximum safe length.
- **Sports Cars**: Carbon-fiber propeller shafts (e.g., Chevrolet Corvette, BMW M) ŌĆö even higher critical speed and lower inertia for faster throttle response.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**: Many EVs use coaxial or in-line motors, *eliminating* the propeller shaft altogether ŌĆö but RWD EVs (Tesla Model S, Mustang Mach-E) still use one.
- **Off-road 4WD**: Use slip joints with extended travel and greaseable U-joints to survive large suspension articulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Propeller Shaft ŌĆö Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Cognitive Targets (KTU Bloom's Level 3ŌĆō4):** Apply, Analyze. The derivations below are the most-frequently asked in KTU University Examinations.

---

## Derivation 1: Torsional Shear Stress in a Hollow Propeller Shaft

**Statement:** Derive the torsional shear stress expression for a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $d_o$ and inner diameter $d_i$ subjected to torque $T$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start from elementary torsion theory

For a circular shaft, the shear strain at radius $r$ is:

$$\gamma = \frac{r\,\theta}{L}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle of twist over length $L$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Hooke's Law in shear

Using $\tau = G\gamma$ (where $G$ is the shear modulus):

$$\tau(r) = \frac{G\,r\,\theta}{L}$$

This shows that shear stress varies **linearly** from zero at the centre to a maximum at the outer surface.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Express torque as integral of shear stress

The torque carried by an elemental ring of area $dA = 2\pi r\,dr$ is:

$$dT = \tau(r)\cdot r \cdot dA = \frac{G\,r\,\theta}{L}\cdot r\cdot 2\pi r\,dr$$

Integrating from $d_i/2$ to $d_o/2$:

$$T = \int_{d_i/2}^{d_o/2} \frac{2\pi G\theta}{L}\,r^{3}\,dr = \frac{2\pi G\theta}{L}\cdot\frac{1}{4}\left[\left(\frac{d_o}{2}\right)^{4}-\left(\frac{d_i}{2}\right)^{4}\right]$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify the polar moment of inertia

Define the **polar moment of inertia** of the cross-section:

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4}\right)$$

Then the torque expression becomes the standard **torsion equation**:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{G\,J\,\theta}{L}}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for maximum shear stress

At $r = d_o/2$, using $\tau_{max} = G\cdot r\cdot\theta/L$ and eliminating $\theta/L$ using $T = GJ\theta/L$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T\,r_{max}}{J} = \frac{T\,(d_o/2)}{\dfrac{\pi}{32}(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{16\,T\,d_o}{\pi\,(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a solid shaft**, put $d_i = 0$, giving the familiar form $\tau_{max} = 16T/(\pi d^{3})$.

---

## Derivation 2: Whirling (Critical) Speed of a Propeller Shaft

**Statement:** Derive the critical speed of a uniform shaft of length $L$, weight $W$, flexural rigidity $EI$, treated as a **simply supported beam with uniformly distributed self-weight**.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Consider the shaft as a uniformly loaded beam

Treat the propeller shaft's self-weight $W$ as a **uniformly distributed load** of intensity $w = W/L$ along the length $L$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Use Rayleigh's energy method

Rayleigh's method equates the **strain energy of bending** to the **kinetic energy reference of deflection**. The critical angular speed is:

$$\omega_{c}^{2} = \frac{g\int_0^L w(x)\,y^{2}(x)\,dx}{\int_0^L w(x)\,y^{2}(x)\,dx}\cdot\frac{EI \int_0^L \left[y''(x)\right]^{2}dx}{\int_0^L w(x)\,y^{2}(x)\,dx}$$

For a simply-supported beam, the static deflection curve due to UDL is:

$$y(x) = \frac{w\,x}{24 EI}\,(L^{3}-2Lx^{2}+x^{3})$$

with maximum mid-span deflection:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5\,W\,L^{3}}{384\,EI}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Use the simplified Rayleigh approximation

The Rayleigh approximation gives the fundamental critical speed as:

$$\omega_{c} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{max}}} = \sqrt{\frac{384\,EI\,g}{5\,W\,L^{3}}}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Convert from rad/s to RPM

$$N_c = \frac{60\,\omega_{c}}{2\pi} = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{384\,EI\,g}{5\,W\,L^{3}}}$$

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{384\,E\,I\,g}{5\,W\,L^{3}}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> For a **solid round shaft** of diameter $d$, the area moment of inertia is $I = \pi d^{4}/64$, and the weight is $W = \rho g \cdot \pi d^{2}L/4$, where $\rho$ is density.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Hollow shaft substitution (commonly used in KTU problems)

For a hollow shaft with outer diameter $d_o$ and inner diameter $d_i$:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})$$

$$W = \rho g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(d_o^{2}-d_i^{2})L$$

Substitute into the boxed equation above to get $N_c$ for a hollow tube ŌĆö *this is the most common KTU numerical*.

---

## Derivation 3: Velocity Ratio of a Single Universal Joint

**Statement:** Prove that a single Hooke's joint produces a non-uniform output velocity, given a constant input velocity.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define geometry

Let the two shafts be at angle $\alpha$ to each other. Let the input yoke rotate with angle $\phi$ and the output yoke follow angle $\theta$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Vector decomposition

The cross (spider) tip lies on a circle in 3D. By resolving the pin velocities (one in each yoke) and setting them equal:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{\tan\phi}{\cos\alpha}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. time

Differentiating:

$$\frac{d\theta}{d\phi} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\cos^{2}\phi + \sin^{2}\phi\,\cos^{2}\alpha} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1-\sin^{2}\alpha\,\cos^{2}\phi}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Express velocity ratio

$$\boxed{\frac{\omega_{out}}{\omega_{in}} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1-\sin^{2}\alpha\,\cos^{2}\phi}}$$

This is **not constant** ŌĆö it varies from $\cos\alpha$ (when $\phi = 0,\pi$) to $1/\cos\alpha$ (when $\phi = \pi/2$).

### Step 5 ŌĆö Cancellation with two joints (in-phase)

For two identical joints in series, the second joint's velocity-ratio curve is the **mirror image** of the first when the yokes are in phase. Multiplying the two ratios gives a constant value of **1.0** ŌĆö the fluctuations cancel exactly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** If the angle ╬▒ is small, $\omega_{out}/\omega_{in} \approx 1 - \sin^{2}\alpha\,\cos^{2}\phi$, and the peak-to-peak fluctuation is approximately $\sin^{2}\alpha$.

---

## Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark Type)

> A car uses a **hollow propeller shaft** of length **1.4 m**, outer diameter **63.5 mm**, inner diameter **57.15 mm**, transmitting a maximum torque of **1500 N┬Ęm**. Material: alloy steel with $E = 207\,\text{GPa}$, $G = 80\,\text{GPa}$, $\rho = 7850\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. Determine:
> **(a)** Maximum torsional shear stress
> **(b)** Critical (whirling) speed
> **(c)** Comment on safe operating speed.

### Part (a) ŌĆö Torsional shear stress

Given: $T = 1500$ N┬Ęm, $d_o = 0.0635$ m, $d_i = 0.05715$ m.

$$d_o^{4} = (0.0635)^{4} = 1.626\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
$$d_i^{4} = (0.05715)^{4} = 1.0668\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
$$d_o^{4}-d_i^{4} = 5.592\times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{4}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16\times 1500\times 0.0635}{\pi\times 5.592\times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1524}{1.7569\times 10^{-5}} \approx 86.7\,\text{MPa}$$

> **Answer (a):** $\tau_{max} \approx 86.7$ MPa ŌĆö well within the allowable limit for alloy steel (~200ŌĆō300 MPa).

### Part (b) ŌĆö Critical (whirling) speed

Area moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4}) = \frac{\pi}{64}\times 5.592\times 10^{-6} = 2.745\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{4}$$

Weight of shaft:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(d_o^{2}-d_i^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(4.032\times 10^{-3}-3.266\times 10^{-3}) = 6.024\times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}$$

$$m = \rho\cdot A\cdot L = 7850\times 6.024\times 10^{-4}\times 1.4 = 6.62\,\text{kg}$$

$$W = mg = 6.62\times 9.81 = 64.97\,\text{N}$$

$$N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{384\times 207\times 10^{9}\times 2.745\times 10^{-7}\times 9.81}{5\times 64.97\times (1.4)^{3}}}$$

Numerically:

$$\frac{384\times 207\times 10^{9}\times 2.745\times 10^{-7}\times 9.81}{5\times 64.97\times 2.744} = \frac{2.137\times 10^{8}}{891.4} = 2.398\times 10^{5}$$

$$N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\times\sqrt{2.398\times 10^{5}} = 9.549\times 489.9 \approx 4678\,\text{rpm}$$

> **Answer (b):** $N_c \approx 4678$ rpm

### Part (c) ŌĆö Safe operating speed

$$N_{op,max} = 0.7\times N_c = 0.7\times 4678 \approx 3275\,\text{rpm}$$

> **Answer (c):** Safe operating speed Ōēż **~3275 rpm**. This is well above the typical 5000-rpm engine red-line, indicating the design is safe. *Note:* In practice, gear reduction at the differential and transmission keeps the propeller shaft speed comfortably below this.

---

# Propeller Shaft ŌĆö Section 4: Structural & Functional Architecture Diagrams

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of Propeller Shaft Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine and Clutch] --> GB[Gearbox Transmission]
    GB --> CF1[Companion Flange Front]
    CF1 --> UJ1[Universal Joint Front]
    UJ1 --> YT1[Yoke and Spline Slip Joint]
    YT1 --> ST[Shaft Tube Hollow Tubular]
    ST --> CB{Mid Center Bearing if long shaft}
    CB --> UJ2[Universal Joint Rear]
    UJ2 --> CF2[Companion Flange Rear]
    CF2 --> DIFF[Differential Pinion]
    DIFF --> HS[Half Shafts to Wheels]
    HS --> WH[Drive Wheels]
```

## 4.2 Torque-Transmission Path with Key Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Engine Torque Source] --> B[Clutch and Gearbox]
    B --> C[Transmission Output Shaft]
    C --> D[Front Companion Flange]
    D --> E[Front U-Joint Spider]
    E --> F[Splined Slip Yoke - allows length change]
    F --> G[Hollow Steel Tube]
    G --> H[Center Bearing on Chassis Crossmember]
    H --> I[Rear U-Joint Spider]
    I --> J[Rear Companion Flange]
    J --> K[Differential Pinion Shaft]
    K --> L[Drive Wheels via Half Shafts]
```

## 4.3 Universal Joint Phasing Logic (Two-Joint Cancellation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRONT[Front U-Joint Assembly]
    F1[Input Yoke at angle alpha] --> F2[Cross Spider]
    F2 --> F3[Output Yoke same plane as rear input yoke]
    end
    FRONT -->|Intermediate Shaft| REAR
    subgraph REAR[Rear U-Joint Assembly]
    R1[Input Yoke same plane as F3] --> R2[Cross Spider]
    R2 --> R3[Output Yoke drives differential pinion]
    end
    NOTE[Note F3 and R1 are in the SAME PLANE: this phasing makes velocity ratio = 1 exactly]
    REAR --- NOTE
```

## 4.4 Propeller Shaft Failure / Design Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Design Inputs: Torque T, Length L, Material] --> CT[Compute Torsional Stress tau from T]
    S --> CR[Compute Critical Speed Nc from E I L W]
    CT --> CK{tau within allowable}
    CR --> NK{Nc above operating speed by factor 1.5 to 2}
    CK -->|Yes| OK1[Pass]
    CK -->|No| INC1[Increase d_o or reduce d_i]
    NK -->|Yes| OK2[Pass]
    NK -->|No| INC2[Shorten shaft, increase d_o, or add center bearing]
    INC1 --> RES[Re-evaluate]
    INC2 --> RES
    OK1 --> F[Final Design Verified]
    OK2 --> F
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block is a *physical component or calculation step*, and each arrow shows either a *flow of mechanical torque* (in the assembly diagrams) or a *logical dependency* in the design diagrams.

# Propeller Shaft ŌĆö Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**State any three functions of a propeller shaft in an automobile.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Transmits torque** from the transmission output shaft to the differential pinion, converting longitudinal rotation into a usable drive for the rear wheels.
2. **Accommodates angular misalignment** between the gearbox and the rear axle pinion through universal joints at both ends.
3. **Allows length variation** between the gearbox and axle during suspension travel, by means of a slip joint (splined yoke).
4. *(Optional 4th for partial extra credit)* Maintains **smooth torque delivery** when properly phased and balanced, reducing vibration.

*(Allocate 1 mark per correct point with a brief supporting line.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the *whirling speed* of a propeller shaft? Why must the operating speed be kept well below it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The whirling speed (also called **critical speed**) of a propeller shaft is the rotational speed at which the shaft's natural lateral (bending) frequency coincides with the rotational forcing frequency, causing violent lateral vibration, large deflections, and possible structural failure. Mathematically, $N_c = (30/\pi)\sqrt{(384\,E\,I\,g)/(5\,W\,L^{3})}$.
- **Why stay below (1 mark):** At the critical speed, even small unbalance forces create **resonance** with bending vibrations, leading to catastrophic failure. Hence operating speed is restricted to **Ōēż 0.7 ├Ś $N_c$** to provide a safe margin.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Hooke's universal joint** used in a propeller shaft. Derive the velocity ratio equation for a single universal joint.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hollow steel propeller shaft has an outer diameter of **75 mm** and inner diameter of **65 mm**. It transmits a torque of **2 kN┬Ęm** between the gearbox and rear axle. The shaft length is **1.5 m**, $E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm┬▓, and $\rho = 7850$ kg/m┬│. Calculate the **maximum torsional shear stress** and the **critical whirling speed** of the shaft. State whether the operating speed of 3500 rpm is safe.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Hooke's Joint Construction & Velocity Ratio**

- **Construction (3 marks):** A Hooke's (Cardan) universal joint consists of:
  - Two **yokes** (one on each shaft)
  - A central **cross or spider** with four trunnions
  - **Needle bearings** at each trunnion-yoke interface
  - **Grease seals / cups** for lubrication
  - The cross is mounted in a plane perpendicular to the two shafts.

- **Working (1 mark):** It transmits torque between two non-collinear shafts, allowing angular misalignment (typically up to 15┬░ŌĆō20┬░). The cross rotates with the driving yoke, while the driven yoke is free to oscillate about the other two trunnions.

- **Velocity Ratio Derivation (3 marks):** (Use Derivation 3 from Section 3.)
  - Setting $d\phi/dt = \omega_{in}$ and $d\theta/dt = \omega_{out}$, we have
  $$\frac{\omega_{out}}{\omega_{in}} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha\cos^{2}\phi}$$
  - The output is **non-uniform** ŌĆö max = $1/\cos\alpha$, min = $\cos\alpha$.
  - With **two in-phase** joints in series, ratio = 1 (constant).

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution**

- **Given:** $d_o = 0.075$ m, $d_i = 0.065$ m, $T = 2000$ N┬Ęm, $L = 1.5$ m, $E = 2.1\times 10^{11}$ N/m┬▓, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m┬│, $N_{op} = 3500$ rpm.

- **Torsional shear stress (3 marks):**
  $$d_o^{4} = 0.075^{4} = 3.164\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
  $$d_i^{4} = 0.065^{4} = 1.786\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
  $$d_o^{4}-d_i^{4} = 1.378\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
  $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16\times 2000\times 0.075}{\pi\times 1.378\times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2400}{4.329\times 10^{-5}} \approx 55.4\,\text{MPa}$$

- **Critical speed (3 marks):**
  $$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4}) = \frac{\pi}{64}\times 1.378\times 10^{-5} = 6.764\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
  $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075^{2}-0.065^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(5.625\times 10^{-3}-4.225\times 10^{-3}) = 1.099\times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2}$$
  $$W = 7850\times 1.099\times 10^{-3}\times 1.5\times 9.81 \approx 127.0\,\text{N}$$
  $$N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{384\times 2.1\times 10^{11}\times 6.764\times 10^{-7}\times 9.81}{5\times 127.0\times 1.5^{3}}}$$
  $$\text{Inside root} = \frac{384\times 2.1\times 10^{11}\times 6.764\times 10^{-7}\times 9.81}{5\times 127.0\times 3.375} = \frac{5.349\times 10^{8}}{2143.1} = 2.496\times 10^{5}$$
  $$N_c = 9.549\times\sqrt{2.496\times 10^{5}} = 9.549\times 499.6 \approx 4771\,\text{rpm}$$

- **Safety check (1 mark):** $N_{op} = 3500$ rpm; $0.7\,N_c = 0.7\times 4771 = 3340$ rpm. Since $N_{op} = 3500 > 3340$, the operating speed is **marginally unsafe** ŌĆö recommend either reducing length, increasing $d_o$, or adding a center bearing to raise $N_c$.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **construction of a propeller shaft assembly** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **single-piece and multi-piece propeller shafts** and state when each is preferred.

**(b) [7 marks]** A solid steel propeller shaft of diameter **40 mm** and length **1.2 m** is to transmit a torque of **500 N┬Ęm** at 2000 rpm. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Calculate the **angle of twist** of the shaft in degrees. If a hollow shaft of the same length, same material, and **same weight per metre** is to be used, find the outer and inner diameters if $d_i/d_o = 0.8$, and compute the **percentage reduction in angle of twist**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Propeller Shaft Construction & Types**

- **Components** *(2 marks)*: shaft tube, U-joints at each end, slip joint (spline), yokes, companion flanges, center bearing (for long shafts), dust seals.
- **Single-piece shaft** *(2 marks)*: Used in **short-wheelbase** cars (e.g., hatchbacks, sub-4 m sedans). One continuous tube between two U-joints. Lighter, simpler, fewer parts, but length-limited by critical speed.
- **Multi-piece shaft** *(2 marks)*: Two or three shaft tubes joined by **center bearings** and additional U-joints. Used in **long-wheelbase vehicles** (SUVs, trucks, buses). Splits a long shaft into shorter spans, each with its own safe $N_c$. Reduces cabin floor intrusion and noise.
- **Preferred application** *(1 mark)*: Single-piece for sub-3 m wheelbase; multi-piece for Ōēź 3 m wheelbase or where high-speed stability is critical.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Solid vs Hollow Shaft Comparison**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Solid shaft angle of twist (2 marks):**
$$J_{solid} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.040)^{4}}{32} = 2.513\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
$$\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{500\times 1.2}{80\times 10^{9}\times 2.513\times 10^{-7}} = \frac{600}{20106} = 0.02984\,\text{rad}$$
$$\boxed{\theta_{solid} = 0.02984\,\text{rad} = 1.71┬░}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Hollow shaft dimensions (2 marks):**

For the hollow shaft to have the **same mass per unit length** as the solid:

$$\frac{\pi}{4}d_s^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}(d_o^{2}-d_i^{2}) \;\Rightarrow\; d_o^{2}-d_i^{2} = d_s^{2}$$

With $d_i = 0.8\,d_o$:

$$d_o^{2}(1-0.64) = (0.040)^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.36\,d_o^{2} = 1.6\times 10^{-3}$$

$$d_o = \sqrt{\frac{1.6\times 10^{-3}}{0.36}} = 0.0667\,\text{m} = 66.7\,\text{mm}$$
$$d_i = 0.8\times 66.7 = 53.3\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Angle of twist for hollow shaft (2 marks):**
$$J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{32}(0.0667^{4}-0.0533^{4}) = \frac{\pi}{32}(1.978\times 10^{-5}-8.066\times 10^{-6})$$
$$= \frac{\pi}{32}\times 1.171\times 10^{-5} = 1.149\times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{4}$$
$$\theta_{hollow} = \frac{500\times 1.2}{80\times 10^{9}\times 1.149\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{600}{91920} = 0.006527\,\text{rad} = 0.374┬░$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Percentage reduction (1 mark):**
$$\%\text{ reduction} = \frac{1.71-0.374}{1.71}\times 100 \approx 78.1\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A hollow shaft of equal weight is **~4.6├Ś stiffer in torsion**, illustrating why propeller shafts are universally made hollow.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Propeller Shaft Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up $I$ and $J$:** Use **$J$** for torsion, **$I$** for bending/critical speed. Marks lost if swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** $d$ in **metres**, $E$ in **N/m┬▓**, $T$ in **N┬Ęm**, $W$ in **N**. Marks lost for unit inconsistency in the final answer.
> 3. **Ignoring the safety factor for critical speed:** Examiner expects a comment on whether operating speed Ōēż $0.7\,N_c$. No comment = ŌłÆ2 marks.
> 4. **Universal joint derivation:** Students often forget to state the **assumption of constant input velocity** before differentiating. Marks lost for skipping the geometry setup.
> 5. **Phasing diagram:** When sketching two U-joints, must clearly label yokes **"in same plane"** ŌĆö without this, the answer on velocity cancellation is incomplete.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Know Bullets):**

- **Definition:** A propeller shaft is a tubular torque-transmitting shaft connecting the gearbox output to the differential pinion, used in RWD/4WD vehicles.
- **Two essential functions:** (1) Transmit torque, (2) Accommodate misalignment + length change.
- **Main components:** Shaft tube, U-joints, slip joint (spline), yokes, center bearing, companion flanges.
- **Why hollow?** Higher stiffness-to-mass ratio, higher critical speed, lower rotational inertia.
- **Hooke's U-Joint equation:** $\omega_{out}/\omega_{in} = \cos\alpha\,/\,(1-\sin^{2}\alpha\cos^{2}\phi)$ ŌĆö non-uniform.
- **Phasing rule:** Two U-joints on a propeller shaft must have yokes **in the same plane** (in phase) to make the output velocity uniform.
- **Torsion equation:** $T = GJ\theta/L$ ; Max stress $\tau_{max} = 16Td_o/[\pi(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})]$.
- **Critical (whirling) speed:** $N_c = (30/\pi)\sqrt{(384\,E\,I\,g)/(5\,W\,L^{3})}$ ŌĆö for simply supported UDL.
- **Design rule:** $N_{op} \le 0.7\,N_c$ to avoid resonance.
- **Materials:** Alloy steels (e.g., 41Cr4, AISI 4140) ŌĆö high fatigue strength.
- **Multi-piece shafts** are used for long wheelbases; **single-piece** for short wheelbases.
- **Slip joint** allows length change during suspension deflection; **center bearing** supports long shafts mid-span.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** Using solid-shaft formulas for hollow shafts; not converting mm to m; skipping the safety-factor comment.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic ŌĆö "P-SHAFT":**
> **P**ower transmission, **S**lip joint, **H**ollow tube, **A**ngular compensation (U-joints), **F**lexible support (center bearing), **T**orque equation ($T=GJ\theta/L$).

---
