topics.title: Differential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Differential # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Differential (Automotive Definition):** > A **differential** is a critical drivetrain component (typically located in the axle assembly between the drive wheels) that performs **three simultaneous engineering functions**: > 1. **Transmits engine torque** from the drive shaft / propeller shaft to the driving wheels. > 2. **Allows the two drive wheels to rotate at different speeds** during cornering (so the inner wheel can rotate slower than the outer wheel). > 3. **Acts as a final speed-reduction gear set**, multiplying engine torque before it reaches the wheels. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for the course **PCMUT302 ŌĆō Automotive Systems**, the differential is classified under the **rear-axle assembly** (or transaxle in front-wheel-drive vehicles) and is treated as a **torque-splitting, speed-equalizing planetary gear mechanism**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Restaurant Tray > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Two Waiters Sharing a Single Tray** > > Imagine two waiters carrying a **single long tray** (this tray represents the propeller shaft delivering torque). They must walk **side-by-side** to the dining table (the axle). On a **straight road**, both walk at the same pace ŌĆö both wheels rotate at the same RPM, perfectly synchronized. > > Now imagine the table is at a **corner**. The outer waiter (outer wheel) has a **longer path** around the corner, while the inner waiter (inner wheel) has a **shorter path**. If both were rigidly bolted to the tray, the inner waiter would be **dragged** or forced to **skid** along the shorter arc ŌĆö exactly what happens to a car without a differential: tire scrub, wear, and poor handling. > > The **differential is the mechanical "give" in the tray** ŌĆö a clever gear arrangement that lets the two ends of the tray turn at *different speeds* while *still transmitting the same torque* from the engine. --- ## 1.3 Why a Differential is Required ŌĆö The Physics of Cornering When a vehicle negotiates a turn, the **center of rotation** lies on an axis perpendicular to the vehicle's longitudinal axis. The wheels trace arcs of **different radii**. | Wheel Position | Path Length per Turn | Required RPM | |----------------|----------------------|--------------| | Outer wheel | **Longer** arc | **Higher** | | Inner wheel | **Shorter** arc | **Lower** | If both wheels were locked to a single shaft (a *solid axle*), one of the following **must happen**: - Tires **slip and scrub** on the road surface. - Drivetrain components suffer **binding stress**, leading to failure. - Steering becomes **heavy and imprecise**. The differential solves this geometric problem elegantly using a **bevel-gear epicyclic (planetary) arrangement**. --- ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Parameters (KTU Notation):** > - **Final Drive Ratio ($i_f$):** Ratio between propeller shaft RPM and wheel RPM ŌĆö typically **3.0 : 1 to 4.5 : 1** for passenger cars. > - **Differential Ratio ($i_d$):** Ratio between crown-wheel RPM and sun/side-gear RPM ŌĆö typically **1.0 : 1** in an open differential (straight-line driving). > - **Lateral Slip Angle ($\alpha$):** Angle between wheel heading and actual travel direction ŌĆö kept near **zero** by a well-functioning differential. > - **Torque Bias Ratio (TBR):** Ratio of torque delivered to the higher-grip wheel vs. lower-grip wheel ŌĆö **1 : 1** for open diff. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Bevel-Gear Differential The **classic automotive differential** consists of the following components: ### A. The Crown Wheel & Pinion (Hypoid or Spiral Bevel) - **Pinion Gear:** Connected to the propeller shaft; delivers input torque at a **90┬░ angle** (or offset angle in hypoid designs). - **Crown Wheel (Ring Gear):** A large bevel gear meshed with the pinion. Rotates about the **axle axis** and forms the input member to the differential case. ### B. The Differential Case (Carrier) - A **rotating housing** that carries: - Two **Side Gears (Sun Gears)** ŌĆö splined to the **inner ends of the two half-shafts**. - Two (or four) **Pinion Gears (Planet Gears)** mounted on a **spider (cross-shaft)** perpendicular to the side gears. ### C. The Half-Shafts (Axle Shafts) - Splined at both ends ŌĆö to the side gears *inside* the differential, and to the wheel hub *outside* the axle housing. --- ## 2.2 Operating Modes ŌĆö The Three Behavioral States > [!NOTE] > The differential exhibits **three behavioral states** depending on driving conditions: ### State 1 ŌĆö Straight-Line Travel (Equal Traction) - Crown wheel, case, both side gears, and both half-shafts rotate as a **single rigid unit**. - No relative motion between the planet (pinion) gears and the side gears. - $N_{left} = N_{right} = N_{crown}$ (all RPMs equal). - Torque is split **50 : 50** to both wheels. ### State 2 ŌĆö Cornering (Unequal Speeds) - The crown wheel + case rotates at an **average speed**. - Planet gears **rotate about their own axis** on the spider, allowing the two side gears to spin at **different rates**. - The constraint equation holds: $$N_{left} + N_{right} = 2 \cdot N_{crown}$$ ### State 3 ŌĆö Slip Condition (One Wheel Lifts / On Ice) - All available torque is sent to the wheel with **least resistance**. - The other wheel receives **zero torque**. - This is the **Achilles' heel** of the open differential. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Reference Table ŌĆö Memorize for KTU Exams** | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|--------------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $N_L + N_R = 2 N_C$ | Sun-gear speed constraint (bevel differential) | All in **RPM** | | 2 | $\omega_C = \dfrac{\omega_L + \omega_R}{2}$ | Angular velocity of crown wheel as the **mean** of side-gear speeds | **rad/s** | | 3 | $\Delta\omega = \omega_O - \omega_I$ | Speed difference between outer & inner wheels during turning | **rad/s** | | 4 | $R = \dfrac{L}{\sin\delta}$ | Turning radius from wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\delta$ | **meters** | | 5 | $\dfrac{\omega_O}{\omega_I} = \dfrac{R_O}{R_I}$ | Wheel angular speeds proportional to turn radii | **dimensionless** | | 6 | $\omega_O = \omega_V \cdot \dfrac{R + \dfrac{t}{2}}{R}$ | Outer wheel angular speed | **rad/s** | | 7 | $\omega_I = \omega_V \cdot \dfrac{R - \dfrac{t}{2}}{R}$ | Inner wheel angular speed | **rad/s** | | 8 | $T_L + T_R = T_{in}$ | Torque equilibrium (with equal traction) | **Nm** | | 9 | $T_L = T_R = \dfrac{T_{in}}{2}$ | Open-diff torque split (equal traction) | **Nm** | | 10 | $T_{wheel} = T_{engine} \cdot i_g \cdot i_f \cdot \eta_d$ | Total torque at wheel | **Nm** | | 11 | $i_{total} = i_g \cdot i_f$ | Overall drivetrain ratio | **dimensionless** | | 12 | $v = \omega_w \cdot r_w$ | Vehicle speed from wheel angular speed | **m/s** | **Symbol Legend (Strict KTU Convention):** - $N_L, N_R, N_C$ ŌĆö RPM of left wheel, right wheel, crown wheel. - $\omega_L, \omega_R, \omega_C$ ŌĆö Corresponding angular velocities (**rad/s**). - $R$ ŌĆö Turning radius of vehicle center; $R_O, R_I$ ŌĆö radii at outer/inner wheels. - $t$ ŌĆö Track width (distance between wheels). - $\omega_V$ ŌĆö Yaw / vehicle angular velocity about turn center. - $T_L, T_R$ ŌĆö Torque on left/right wheels; $T_{in}$ ŌĆö torque into differential case. - $i_g$ ŌĆö Gearbox ratio; $i_f$ ŌĆö final drive ratio. - $\eta_d$ ŌĆö Drivetrain efficiency (typically **0.85 to 0.92** for rear-wheel-drive). - $r_w$ ŌĆö Effective rolling radius of tire (**m**). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where this knowledge is applied in industry:** > - **Passenger Car Design:** Calibration of final-drive ratio to balance acceleration and fuel economy. > - **Off-Road / 4WD Systems:** Designing **locking differentials** and **limited-slip differentials (LSD)** for traction-critical applications. > - **EV Powertrains:** Replacing mechanical differentials with **dual-motor independent drives** ŌĆö a key Modern Automotive engineering shift. > - **Racing / Motorsport:** Use of **torque-vectoring differentials** that actively bias torque to the outer wheel during cornering. > - **ADAS & Stability Control:** Understanding differential behavior is foundational to **Electronic Stability Program (ESP)** algorithms. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö The Fundamental Speed Constraint of a Bevel Differential **Statement:** *In a standard bevel-gear differential, the angular velocity of the crown wheel (case) equals the arithmetic mean of the angular velocities of the two side gears.* ### Given: - Two side gears (sun gears) with angular velocities $\omega_L$ (left) and $\omega_R$ (right). - The planet (pinion) gears are mounted on a **spider** that rotates with the **differential case** at $\omega_C$. - The planet gears **do not slip** at their mesh point with the side gears (pure rolling constraint). ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1:** Define the angular velocity of a planet gear about its **own axis** (relative to the case) as $\omega_{p/c}$. **Step 2:** At the mesh point between the planet gear and the **left side gear**, the no-slip rolling condition gives: $$ \omega_C \cdot r_{planet} + \omega_{p/c} \cdot r_{planet} = \omega_L \cdot r_{sun} $$ Since the side gear and planet gear have the **same pitch radius** at mesh ($r_{planet} = r_{sun}$ in most automotive differentials): $$ \omega_C + \omega_{p/c} = \omega_L $$ **Step 3:** Similarly, at the mesh with the **right side gear**: $$ \omega_C - \omega_{p/c} = \omega_R $$ *(The negative sign appears because the planet rotates in opposite directions at the two mesh points.)* **Step 4:** Adding the two equations to eliminate $\omega_{p/c}$: $$ (\omega_C + \omega_{p/c}) + (\omega_C - \omega_{p/c}) = \omega_L + \omega_R $$ $$ 2\omega_C = \omega_L + \omega_R $$ $$ \boxed{\omega_C = \frac{\omega_L + \omega_R}{2}} $$ **Step 5:** Multiplying by 60 to express in RPM: $$ \boxed{N_C = \frac{N_L + N_R}{2}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The crown wheel always rotates at the **average** of the two side-gear speeds. This is the **governing kinematic equation** of every KTU differential numerical. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Outer & Inner Wheel Speeds During a Turn **Statement:** *Express the angular speeds of the outer and inner wheels in terms of the vehicle's yaw rate, turning radius, and track width.* ### Given: - Vehicle turning radius at its center of gravity: $R$ (m). - Track width (lateral distance between wheel centers): $t$ (m). - Yaw rate of the vehicle: $\omega_V$ (rad/s). - Wheels do not slip sideways (pure rolling assumption). ### Derivation: **Step 1:** The center of each wheel traces a circle about the turn center. For the **outer wheel**, the instantaneous radius is: $$ R_O = R + \frac{t}{2} $$ **Step 2:** For the **inner wheel**, the radius is: $$ R_I = R - \frac{t}{2} $$ **Step 3:** Since the wheels roll without slipping, the linear velocity of each wheel center equals $\omega_V \cdot R_O$ (outer) and $\omega_V \cdot R_I$ (inner). **Step 4:** Equating this linear velocity to $\omega \cdot r_w$ for each wheel: $$ \omega_O \cdot r_w = \omega_V \cdot \left(R + \frac{t}{2}\right) $$ $$ \omega_I \cdot r_w = \omega_V \cdot \left(R - \frac{t}{2}\right) $$ **Step 5:** Solving for the angular speeds: $$ \boxed{\omega_O = \omega_V \cdot \frac{R + \frac{t}{2}}{r_w}} $$ $$ \boxed{\omega_I = \omega_V \cdot \frac{R - \frac{t}{2}}{r_w}} $$ **Step 6:** Ratio of outer to inner wheel speeds: $$ \frac{\omega_O}{\omega_I} = \frac{R + \dfrac{t}{2}}{R - \dfrac{t}{2}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Torque Distribution in an Open Differential **Statement:** *Show that in an open differential with equal traction, the torque is split equally between the two drive wheels.* ### Given: - Input torque from crown wheel to the case: $T_{in}$ (Nm). - Planet gears are mounted on **frictionless bearings** on the spider (idealized assumption). - Equal traction coefficient $\mu$ on both wheels. ### Derivation: **Step 1:** The planet gears act as **idler gears**. An idler does **not** change the magnitude of torque transmitted ŌĆö it only reverses direction. **Step 2:** Therefore, the torque transmitted from the case to each side gear is **identical**: $$ T_{left\,case} = T_{right\,case} = \frac{T_{in}}{2} $$ **Step 3:** This torque is passed directly to the half-shafts and wheels (no further reduction inside the differential case). $$ \boxed{T_L = T_R = \frac{T_{in}}{2}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Trap:** This 50:50 split is **independent of vehicle speed, gear, or cornering** ŌĆö as long as both wheels have equal grip. KTU examiners *love* testing whether students realize that an open differential *cannot* bias torque to the wheel with more grip. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö KTU-Style Problem > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A car with a track width of **1.5 m** takes a turn of radius **30 m** at the center of gravity. The propeller shaft rotates at **1800 RPM**. The final-drive ratio is **3.5 : 1** and the wheel rolling radius is **0.30 m**. > **(a)** Find the RPM of the outer and inner wheels. > **(b)** Verify that the differential case (crown wheel) rotates at the average of the two. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Crown wheel speed from propeller shaft and final-drive ratio: $$ N_C = \frac{N_{prop}}{i_f} = \frac{1800}{3.5} \approx 514.29 \text{ RPM} $$ **Step 2:** Speed difference on a turn of radius $R = 30$ m. For small steer angles, the side-gear speeds deviate equally from $N_C$: $$ \Delta N = N_C \cdot \frac{t}{2R} = 514.29 \cdot \frac{0.75}{30} \approx 12.86 \text{ RPM} $$ *(Derivation of this $\Delta N$ formula follows from the geometry in ┬¦3.2.)* **Step 3:** Outer wheel RPM: $$ \boxed{N_O = N_C + \Delta N \approx 514.29 + 12.86 \approx 527.15 \text{ RPM}} $$ **Step 4:** Inner wheel RPM: $$ \boxed{N_I = N_C - \Delta N \approx 514.29 - 12.86 \approx 501.43 \text{ RPM}} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Verification:** $$ \frac{N_O + N_I}{2} = \frac{527.15 + 501.43}{2} = \frac{1028.58}{2} \approx 514.29 \text{ RPM} = N_C \;\; \checkmark $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö Power Flow Through the Differential The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a rear-axle differential assembly, showing power flow from the engine through to the wheels, and the three operating states of the differential. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ENGINE
Torque Source] --> B[GEARBOX
Variable Ratio] B --> C[PROPELLER SHAFT
Longitudinal Drive] C --> D[PINION GEAR
90 Degree Bevel] D --> E[CROWN WHEEL
Final Reduction] E --> F[DIFFERENTIAL CASE
Rotating Carrier] F --> G1[SPIDER CROSS-SHAFT
Planet Carrier] G1 --> H1[PLANET GEAR L] G1 --> H2[PLANET GEAR R] H1 --> I1[SIDE GEAR LEFT
Half-Shaft Coupling] H2 --> I2[SIDE GEAR RIGHT
Half-Shaft Coupling] I1 --> J1[LEFT HALF-SHAFT] I2 --> J2[RIGHT HALF-SHAFT] J1 --> K1[LEFT WHEEL
Road Contact] J2 --> K2[RIGHT WHEEL
Road Contact] ``` ## 4.2 State Diagram ŌĆö Differential Operating States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> STRAIGHT STRAIGHT --> STRAIGHT: Both wheels same RPM
No relative motion
Torque 50/50 STRAIGHT --> TURNING TURNING --> TURNING: Crown wheel at average RPM
Planet gears spin on spider
Different side gear speeds TURNING --> TRACTION_LOSS TRACTION_LOSS --> TRACTION_LOSS: One wheel slips
All torque to slipping wheel
Stalled traction event TRACTION_LOSS --> STRAIGHT: Driver modulates throttle
Traction recovers TURNING --> STRAIGHT: Steering returns to center
Speeds equalize STRAIGHT --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Component Layout ŌĆö Bevel Differential Cross-Section (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CASE[Differential Case Assembly] direction TB PIN[PINION GEAR
Input from Prop Shaft] CR[CROWN WHEEL
Final Drive] CAS[DIFFERENTIAL CASE
Rotates with Crown Wheel] SP[SPIDER CROSS-SHAFT
Mounted in Case] PL[PLANET PINION LEFT] PR[PLANET PINION RIGHT] SL[SIDE GEAR LEFT
Splined to Left Half-Shaft] SR[SIDE GEAR RIGHT
Splined to Right Half-Shaft] end PIN -->|90 degree mesh| CR CR --> CAS CAS --> SP SP --> PL SP --> PR PL --> SL PR --> SR SL --> LHS[LEFT HALF-SHAFT] SR --> RHS[RIGHT HALF-SHAFT] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Comparison ŌĆö Open vs. Limited-Slip vs. Locking Differential ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OPEND[OPEN DIFFERENTIAL] O1[Torque Always 50/50] --> O2[Lowest Cost] O2 --> O3[Poor in Slip] end subgraph LSD[LIMITED SLIP DIFFERENTIAL] L1[Clutch or Viscous Coupling] --> L2[Biases Torque to Grip] L2 --> L3[Better Traction] end subgraph LOCKD[LOCKING DIFFERENTIAL] K1[Operator Activated Lock] --> K2[100 Percent Both Wheels] K2 --> K3[Off-Road and 4x4 Use] end OPEND -. Performance Upgrade .-> LSD LSD -. Heavy Duty Upgrade .-> LOCKD ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Q: Define a differential in an automobile and state its three main functions.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A differential is a gear mechanism located in the drive axle that connects the propeller shaft to the driving wheels. Its three main functions are: 1. **To transmit torque** from the propeller shaft to the drive wheels. 2. **To allow the two drive wheels to rotate at different speeds** during cornering (so the inner wheel turns slower than the outer wheel). 3. **To provide final speed reduction**, multiplying engine torque before it reaches the wheels through the crown-wheel-and-pinion pair. --- ### Question 2 **Q: Why is a differential necessary in a four-wheeled vehicle? What happens if it is omitted?** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** When a vehicle turns, the inner and outer wheels trace arcs of different radii and hence must rotate at different speeds. Without a differential, both wheels would be locked to a common shaft and forced to rotate at the same angular velocity. This results in: - Tire **scrubbing and slip** on the road surface. - **Binding stresses** in the drivetrain. - **Excessive tire wear** and **poor handling**. - Difficulty in **steering**. A differential provides the necessary speed difference between the wheels while still transmitting torque, solving all these issues. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a bevel-gear type differential used in automobiles. Also derive the relation $N_C = \dfrac{N_L + N_R}{2}$.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 2]` #### Model Solution: **(a) Construction (7 Marks):** A bevel-gear differential consists of the following components: - **Pinion Gear:** Mounted on the propeller shaft, meshes with the crown wheel at 90┬░. - **Crown Wheel (Ring Gear):** Large bevel gear bolted to the differential case; rotates about the axle axis. - **Differential Case:** A rotating housing that carries the spider and the planet pinion gears. - **Spider (Cross-Shaft):** Rigidly mounted in the case, perpendicular to the axle axis; carries two (or four) planet pinion gears. - **Planet Pinion Gears:** Bevel gears mounted loosely on the spider; mesh simultaneously with both side gears. - **Side (Sun) Gears:** Two bevel gears, one splined to each half-shaft; mesh with the planet pinions. - **Half-Shafts:** Drive the wheel hubs. **(b) Working Principle & Derivation (7 Marks):** *Working in Three Modes:* **Mode 1 ŌĆö Straight Road:** The crown wheel, case, spider, and side gears all rotate as a single unit. The planet gears do not rotate on their own axis. Both wheels turn at the same RPM. Torque is split 50:50. **Mode 2 ŌĆö Turning:** As the vehicle turns, one side gear speeds up and the other slows down. The planet pinions rotate on the spider, allowing the speed difference. The crown wheel rotates at the **average** of the two side-gear speeds. **Mode 3 ŌĆö Slip:** If one wheel loses traction, all torque flows to that wheel and the other receives zero. The car may stall. *Derivation of $N_C = (N_L + N_R)/2$:* Let $\omega_C$ = angular velocity of case, $\omega_L, \omega_R$ = angular velocities of left and right side gears, and $\omega_{p/c}$ = angular velocity of the planet gear about its own axis relative to the case. From the no-slip rolling condition at the left mesh: $$ \omega_C + \omega_{p/c} = \omega_L $$ At the right mesh (opposite sense of rotation): $$ \omega_C - \omega_{p/c} = \omega_R $$ Adding: $$ 2\omega_C = \omega_L + \omega_R $$ Converting to RPM (multiply by 60): $$ \boxed{N_C = \frac{N_L + N_R}{2}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $N_C = (N_L - N_R)/2$ or confuse the signs of $\omega_{p/c}$ at the two meshes. Remember: at the *left* mesh the planet surface and the side-gear surface move in the **same direction**, while at the *right* mesh they move in **opposite directions** ŌĆö that's why the signs in the two equations differ. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **Q: Explain the following with reference to an automotive differential:** **(a)** The need for a differential and the geometric justification for different wheel speeds during cornering. Derive expressions for the angular velocities of the outer and inner wheels in terms of the turning radius, track width, and yaw rate. **(b)** A vehicle with wheelbase 2.5 m, track width 1.4 m, and wheel rolling radius 0.28 m takes a turn at a yaw rate of 0.2 rad/s with the center of turn at a radius of 25 m from the vehicle's CG. Find the angular speeds of the outer and inner wheels. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 2]` #### Model Solution: **(a) Geometric Need and Derivation (7 Marks):** When a vehicle turns, the instantaneous center of rotation lies on the normal to the vehicle's longitudinal axis. The inner wheel travels a shorter arc than the outer wheel in the same time, so they must rotate at different angular velocities to avoid tire scrub. From pure-rolling kinematics, with turning radius at CG = $R$, track width = $t$, yaw rate = $\omega_V$: $$ \omega_O = \omega_V \cdot \frac{R + \dfrac{t}{2}}{r_w} $$ $$ \omega_I = \omega_V \cdot \frac{R - \dfrac{t}{2}}{r_w} $$ The ratio of speeds is: $$ \frac{\omega_O}{\omega_I} = \frac{R + \dfrac{t}{2}}{R - \dfrac{t}{2}} $$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** - $t = 1.4$ m, so $t/2 = 0.7$ m - $r_w = 0.28$ m - $\omega_V = 0.2$ rad/s - $R = 25$ m **Step 1:** Outer wheel angular speed: $$ \omega_O = 0.2 \cdot \frac{25 + 0.7}{0.28} = 0.2 \cdot \frac{25.7}{0.28} \approx 0.2 \cdot 91.786 $$ $$ \boxed{\omega_O \approx 18.36 \text{ rad/s}} $$ **Step 2:** Inner wheel angular speed: $$ \omega_I = 0.2 \cdot \frac{25 - 0.7}{0.28} = 0.2 \cdot \frac{24.3}{0.28} \approx 0.2 \cdot 86.786 $$ $$ \boxed{\omega_I \approx 17.36 \text{ rad/s}} $$ **Step 3:** Difference and verification: $$ \Delta\omega = 18.36 - 17.36 = 1.00 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 4:** Conversion to RPM if required: $$ N_O = \omega_O \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 18.36 \cdot 9.549 \approx 175.3 \text{ RPM} $$ $$ N_I = \omega_I \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 165.7 \text{ RPM} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Two common mistakes cost students the full 14 marks: > 1. **Forgetting to use $t/2$** in the formula (using full $t$ instead of half-track width). This gives a 100% error. > 2. **Confusing linear yaw velocity with angular wheel speed** ŌĆö students sometimes equate $\omega_V$ directly to wheel RPM without dividing by $r_w$. The relationship $\omega \cdot r_w = v_{linear}$ must be respected at all times. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Differential (Module 2)** - **Differential = torque transmitter + speed equalizer + final reduction** ŌĆö three roles in one unit. - **Three modes of operation:** Straight (rigid unit), Turning (planets spin on spider), Slip (torque to low-grip wheel). - **Governing equation:** $N_C = (N_L + N_R)/2$ ŌĆö *learn the derivation*, not just the formula. - **Open differential ALWAYS splits torque 50:50** under equal traction ŌĆö *no torque biasing* in standard design. - **Track width $t$** is used as **$t/2$** in turn-radius formulas ŌĆö *most common error*. - **Final-drive ratio $i_f$** is multiplied with the gearbox ratio to get the **overall drivetrain ratio**. - **Wheel torque:** $T_w = T_{engine} \cdot i_g \cdot i_f \cdot \eta_d$ ŌĆö include drivetrain efficiency. - **Limited-Slip Differentials (LSD)** use clutches, viscous couplings, or Torsen gears to bias torque to the higher-grip wheel. - **Locking differentials** mechanically lock the two half-shafts together ŌĆö used in **off-road / 4WD** vehicles. - **Modern trend:** Electric vehicles use **dual-motor independent drive** (no mechanical differential needed) or **electronic torque vectoring**. - **Hypoid gear offset** in the crown-wheel-pinion pair allows the propeller shaft to be **lower than the axle centerline**, increasing ground clearance. - **Always show the sign convention** in derivations ŌĆö opposite signs of $\omega_{p/c}$ at left vs. right meshes is the key step in deriving the speed-constraint equation. - **Units discipline:** Convert RPM Ōåö rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. *KTU examiners mark unit errors strictly.*

topics.title: Constructional Details and Working of Differential Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details and Working of Differential Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Differential Unit** (often called the "Diff") is a critical epicyclic gear train assembly located between the drive axles of a rear-wheel-drive (RWD) or all-wheel-drive (AWD) vehicle. Its primary engineering function is to:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A differential is a device, employing epicyclic gear kinematics, that transmits engine torque from the propeller shaft to the drive wheels while permitting the two driving axles (half-shafts) to rotate at **different angular velocities** during cornering, thereby preventing wheel skidding and tire wear.

It simultaneously performs **three essential functions**:
1. Transmits power from the propeller shaft to the rear axle.
2. Acts as a final speed-reduction stage (typically 3.5:1 to 4.5:1) via the crown wheel and pinion.
3. Permits the inner and outer driving wheels to rotate at different speeds while turning.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Skating Partner" Model

Imagine two ice skaters holding hands and gliding across a rink. When they move in a **straight line**, both walk at exactly the same pace. But the moment they need to **turn a corner**, the skater on the *outside* of the curve has to take **larger, longer steps**, while the skater on the *inside* takes **smaller, shorter steps** ŌĆö otherwise, the inner skater will trip and skid.

A differential does exactly this for the two drive wheels of a car. Without it, turning a corner would cause one wheel to drag, skid, and wear out tires rapidly. The differential "negotiates" the speed difference automatically.

## 1.3 Why a Differential is Mandatory in Automobiles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Different Wheel Speeds Are Needed:**
> When a vehicle turns, the outer wheel covers a **larger radius arc** than the inner wheel in the same time. From the kinematic relationship:
> - Outer wheel path radius = R + (Track Width / 2)
> - Inner wheel path radius = R - (Track Width / 2)
>
> Since both wheels share the same turning time, **V_outer > V_inner**, which directly means **Žē_outer > Žē_inner**. A differential is the only practical mechanical solution to this in a single, compact unit.

## 1.4 Historical and Engineering Context

The automotive differential was patented by **On├®siphore Pecqueur** of France in **1827** and was first used in steam-powered vehicles. Modern automotive differentials are a refined evolution of his original epicyclic design, optimized for high torque loads (often exceeding **3500 N┬Ęm** in heavy commercial vehicles) and silent operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Kinematics ŌĆö The Foundation

The differential is a simple epicyclic gear train. Understanding its kinematics requires mastery of three reference members:

| Member | Common Name | Function |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Sun Gear** | Side Gear (one per drive axle) | Rotates with the half-shaft |
| **Planet Gear** | Pinion (Cross-shaft mounted) | Transfers torque between sun gears |
| **Arm / Carrier** | Differential Case / Cage | Driven by the crown wheel |

The **Willis Equation** (fundamental epicyclic formula) governs all differential motion:

$$\frac{N_c - N_s}{N_p - N_s} = -\frac{T_s}{T_c}$$

Where:
- $N_c$ = Speed of the carrier (differential case)
- $N_s$ = Speed of one side gear (sun)
- $N_p$ = Speed of the other side gear / planet reference
- $T_s$ = Teeth on sun gear
- $T_c$ = Teeth on the equivalent annulus / carrier reference

> [!NOTE]
> In a standard differential, the **cross-shaft pinion** has no fixed reference (it spins freely), making the system a "differential epicyclic" where only the relative ratio matters.

## 2.2 The Master Speed Equation (Willis Result for a Standard Differential)

For a standard symmetric differential where both side gears have **equal teeth** ($T_{s1} = T_{s2}$), the master equation simplifies dramatically to:

$$N_1 + N_2 = 2 \cdot N_c$$

Where:
- $N_1$ = Speed of left driving wheel (rpm)
- $N_2$ = Speed of right driving wheel (rpm)
- $N_c$ = Speed of differential case (= crown wheel speed)

This is the **single most important equation** for any KTU exam question on differentials.

## 2.3 Operating Modes ŌĆö Three Distinct States

### Mode 1: Straight-Line Motion
When the vehicle travels straight, the resistance at both wheels is identical. Therefore:
- No relative motion occurs between the side gears and pinion gears.
- The whole assembly rotates as a solid unit.
- $N_1 = N_2 = N_c$

### Mode 2: Vehicle Turning (Dynamic Differencing)
When cornering, the differential automatically divides the speed:
$$N_2 = 2 N_c - N_1$$

The outer wheel accelerates by exactly the same amount the inner wheel decelerates ŌĆö torque is conserved while speed is freely split.

### Mode 3: One Wheel Spins (Limp Mode)
If one wheel is on ice/mud (zero traction), the entire torque delivered by the engine goes to that wheel. The vehicle gets "stuck" because the gripping wheel receives zero torque. This is the **primary disadvantage** of an open differential.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination ŌĆö Mandatory Formula Table**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$ | Master differential equation | rpm |
| 2 | $N_2 = 2 N_c - N_1$ | Speed of one wheel from the other | rpm |
| 3 | $N_c = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$ | Case speed as arithmetic mean | rpm |
| 4 | $\omega_c = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$ | Angular velocity form | rad/s |
| 5 | $T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N}$ | Torque from Power-P equation | N┬Ęm |
| 6 | $N_{prop} \cdot N_{diff} = N_{wheel}$ | Total drivetrain ratio | rpm |
| 7 | $V = \omega \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Linear velocity of wheel | m/s |
| 8 | $V_1 = r \cdot \omega_1$, $V_2 = r \cdot \omega_2$ | Individual wheel speeds | m/s |
| 9 | $\eta_{diff} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Differential efficiency | % |
| 10 | $T_{1} = T_{2} = \frac{T_c}{2}$ | Torque split (equal) | N┬Ęm |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Differentials are used in:
- **Automotive vehicles** ŌĆö All RWD/AWD cars, trucks, buses.
- **Heavy Earth-Moving Equipment** ŌĆö Bulldozers, wheel loaders, dump trucks.
- **Industrial Robotics** ŌĆö Differential drives for AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles).
- **Locomotive Bogies** ŌĆö For negotiating curved tracks.
- **Helicopter Rotor Heads** ŌĆö Swashplate mechanisms operate on differential principles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Equation: $N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

Let the differential case rotate at $N_c$ rpm carrying the cross-shaft. The two side gears are mounted on the half-shafts. The cross-shaft carries two pinion (planet) gears that mesh with the side gears.

> Each pinion gear has a radius $r_p$ and meshes externally with both side gears of radius $r_s$.

### Step 2: Velocity Analysis at the Pinion Center

When the case rotates, it forces the pinion gear centers to move in a circle of radius equal to the **cross-shaft offset** $r_m$ (distance from case center to pinion center).

The tangential velocity of the pinion *center* (due to case rotation) is:

$$V_{center} = \frac{2 \pi r_m N_c}{60} \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3: Apply the No-Slip Constraint at Mesh Points

If the pinion gear does not spin on its own axis (straight-line motion case), then both side gear surfaces must move at exactly the same tangential speed as the pinion center.

$$V_{left} = V_{right} = V_{center}$$

### Step 4: Introduce Pinion Spin for Turning

When the vehicle turns, the pinion gear *spins* on its cross-shaft at speed $N_p$ (self-rotation). The tangential velocity of the pinion *surface* in contact with each side gear becomes:

$$V_{pinion\,surface} = \frac{2 \pi r_p N_p}{60} \text{ m/s}$$

This added velocity **adds** to the velocity of the side gear on one side and **subtracts** from the velocity on the other side. The key derivation follows.

### Step 5: Write the Two Side Gear Velocities

For the **left** side gear (outer wheel, accelerating):

$$V_1 = V_{center} + V_{pinion\,surface} = \frac{2 \pi r_m N_c}{60} + \frac{2 \pi r_p N_p}{60}$$

For the **right** side gear (inner wheel, decelerating):

$$V_2 = V_{center} - V_{pinion\,surface} = \frac{2 \pi r_m N_c}{60} - \frac{2 \pi r_p N_p}{60}$$

### Step 6: Add the Two Equations

Adding $V_1$ and $V_2$, the pinion spin terms cancel out (one is positive, one is negative):

$$V_1 + V_2 = 2 \cdot V_{center} = 2 \cdot \frac{2 \pi r_m N_c}{60}$$

Since $V = \frac{2 \pi r_{wheel} N}{60}$, and the wheel radius is the same for both wheels (they are the same size tire), we can substitute:

$$\frac{2 \pi r_{wheel} N_1}{60} + \frac{2 \pi r_{wheel} N_2}{60} = 2 \cdot \frac{2 \pi r_{wheel} N_c}{60}$$

### Step 7: Simplify to the Master Equation

Cancelling $\frac{2 \pi r_{wheel}}{60}$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c}$$

This elegant result shows that **the arithmetic mean of the two wheel speeds always equals the case speed**, regardless of how the differential splits the speeds.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example ŌĆö KTU Typical Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Numerical (Model Problem):**
> A car with an open differential is turning right at a moment when the engine is delivering 60 kW to the differential. The crown wheel speed is 1500 rpm. The right wheel (inner to the turn) is rotating at 1200 rpm. The wheel rolling radius is 0.32 m. Find:
> (a) The speed of the left (outer) wheel in rpm.
> (b) The vehicle's turning radius.
> (c) The torque on each half-shaft.

### Solution (a): Speed of Left Wheel

Using the master equation:

$$N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$$
$$N_{left} + 1200 = 2 \times 1500$$
$$N_{left} = 3000 - 1200 = 1800 \text{ rpm}$$

### Solution (b): Turning Radius

Linear velocity of each wheel:

$$V_{left} = \frac{2 \pi r_{wheel} N_{left}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 0.32 \times 1800}{60} = 60.32 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{right} = \frac{2 \pi \times 0.32 \times 1200}{60} = 40.21 \text{ m/s}$$

Using Ackermann-like geometry where the center of turn lies on the perpendicular bisector of the rear axle:

$$R_{left} = R_{right} + t$$

Where $t$ = track width. Let track width be $t = 1.4$ m (typical sedan). Also, since both wheels share the same angular velocity about the turning center $\omega_{turn}$:

$$\frac{V_{left}}{R_{right} + t} = \frac{V_{right}}{R_{right}}$$

$$V_{left} \cdot R_{right} = V_{right} \cdot R_{right} + V_{right} \cdot t$$
$$R_{right} \cdot (V_{left} - V_{right}) = V_{right} \cdot t$$
$$R_{right} = \frac{V_{right} \cdot t}{V_{left} - V_{right}} = \frac{40.21 \times 1.4}{60.32 - 40.21}$$
$$\boxed{R_{right} = 2.798 \text{ m}}$$

### Solution (c): Torque on Each Half-Shaft

Total torque at crown wheel (case):

$$T_c = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N_c} = \frac{60 \times 60000}{2 \pi \times 1500} = 381.97 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

In an open differential, torque always splits **equally** (independent of speed):

$$T_{left} = T_{right} = \frac{T_c}{2} = \frac{381.97}{2} = 190.99 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a critical KTU insight: **An open differential always splits torque equally, even when the speeds are unequal.** A common student mistake is to assume torque splits proportionally to speed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Differential Type Comparison

| Property | Open Differential | Locked Differential | Limited-Slip Differential (LSD) | Torsen / Viscous LSD |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------|
| Speed Splitting | Free (any ratio) | No ŌĆö fixed 1:1 | Partial (friction-controlled) | Speed-sensitive |
| Torque Splitting | Always 50:50 | Always 50:50 | Biased (e.g., 65:35) | Up to 80:20 bias |
| Cornering Ability | Excellent | Poor (tire scrub) | Good | Very Good |
| Traction on Slippery Surfaces | Poor | Good | Good | Very Good |
| Driver Skill Required | None | High | None | None |
| Cost | Low | Low | Medium-High | High |
| Typical Application | Sedans, hatchbacks | Off-road vehicles, 4x4 trucks | Sports cars, SUVs | High-end AWD, performance cars |
| Construction Complexity | Simple | Simple | Moderate | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Main Components and Power Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Propeller Shaft Input] --> B[Drive Pinion Gear]
    B --> C[Crown Wheel / Ring Gear]
    C --> D[Differential Case / Cage]
    D --> E[Cross Shaft]
    E --> F[Pinion Gear 1 Planet]
    E --> G[Pinion Gear 2 Planet]
    F --> H[Left Side Gear Sun]
    G --> I[Right Side Gear Sun]
    H --> J[Left Half Shaft]
    I --> K[Right Half Shaft]
    J --> L[Left Drive Wheel]
    K --> M[Right Drive Wheel]
    D --> N[Thrust Washers and Bearings]
    H --> N
    I --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram ŌĆö Differential Operating Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Vehicle Moving Straight] -->|Equal Resistance| S2[Cross Shaft Stays Stationary]
    S2 --> S3[Entire Case Rotates as Solid Unit]
    S3 --> S4[Left Wheel Speed = Right Wheel Speed = Case Speed]
    S1 -->|Vehicle Turning| S5[Cross Shaft Begins Spinning]
    S5 --> S6[Planet Pinions Rotate on Cross Shaft]
    S6 --> S7[Outer Wheel Speeds Up]
    S6 --> S8[Inner Wheel Slows Down]
    S7 --> S9[Sum Rule: N1 plus N2 equals 2 Nc Maintained]
    S8 --> S9
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Cross-Sectional Layout Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Top View Differential Assembly] --> B[Upper Half: Bearings and Housing]
    A --> C[Center: Crown Wheel Mesh with Drive Pinion]
    A --> D[Inner Cavity: Differential Case]
    D --> E[Cross Shaft Horizontal Pinion Axis]
    D --> F[Two Side Gears Left and Right]
    E --> G[Planet Pinion 1 Above Shaft]
    E --> H[Planet Pinion 2 Below Shaft]
    F --> I[Thrust Washer Friction Surface]
    F --> J[Splined Connection to Half Shaft]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Note:** The two pinion (planet) gears are mounted on a single cross-shaft that passes through the differential case at 90┬░ to the half-shafts. They are arranged in a "butterfly" or "+" configuration so that one meshes with the left side gear and the other with the right side gear.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper - Set A]**

**Q1.** Define a differential unit. List its three primary functions in a rear-wheel-drive automobile. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A differential is an epicyclic gear device fitted between the two driving wheels that allows them to rotate at different speeds during cornering while transmitting torque from the propeller shaft. Its three primary functions are:
1. **Torque transmission** ŌĆö Carries engine torque from the propeller shaft to the drive wheels.
2. **Final reduction** ŌĆö Acts as the last speed-reduction stage (typically 3.5:1 to 4.5:1) via the crown wheel and pinion pair.
3. **Speed differentiation** ŌĆö Permits the inner and outer driving wheels to rotate at different angular velocities when the vehicle turns, preventing tire scrub and wheel hop.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** State and explain the Willis equation for a standard automotive differential. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The Willis equation for a standard automotive differential is:

$$N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$$

This equation states that the **sum of the speeds of the two driving wheels is always equal to twice the speed of the differential case (carrier)**. The differential case is driven by the crown wheel at a speed $N_c$, while $N_1$ and $N_2$ are the speeds of the left and right driving wheels. It is a direct consequence of the fact that the planet pinion gears are mounted on a free cross-shaft and their relative motion must be conserved. The equation holds in **all operating modes** ŌĆö straight-line driving, cornering, and even when one wheel is spinning freely.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **Option A**

**Q(a).** With a neat sketch, describe the **constructional details of a rear-axle differential unit** used in passenger cars. Label all major components and explain the function of each. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The rear-axle differential assembly consists of the following components, mounted inside the rear-axle housing:

1. **Differential Case / Cage (3)** ŌĆö A box-shaped steel housing that carries the cross-shaft and rotates as a single unit with the crown wheel. It is the carrier of the epicyclic gear system.

2. **Crown Wheel and Pinion (2, 13)** ŌĆö A hypoid (or spiral bevel) gear pair. The pinion is on the propeller shaft and meshes with the crown wheel bolted to the differential case. Provides the final reduction ratio (typically 3.73:1 or 4.10:1).

3. **Cross Shaft (Spider) (4)** ŌĆö A horizontal shaft passing through the differential case, perpendicular to the half-shafts. It carries the two pinion (planet) gears and rotates freely within the case.

4. **Pinion / Planet Gears (5, 6)** ŌĆö Two identical bevel gears mounted on the cross-shaft. They mesh simultaneously with both side gears. Typically have **10ŌĆō14 teeth**.

5. **Side Gears (Sun Gears) (7, 10)** ŌĆö Two bevel gears mounted on the inboard ends of the left and right half-shafts, with internal splines matching the half-shafts.

6. **Thrust Washers (8, 9, 11, 12)** ŌĆö Phosphor bronze or fiber washers placed between the side gears and the case to reduce friction and axial wear.

7. **Differential Bearings (15)** ŌĆö Tapered roller bearings supporting the differential case in the axle housing.

8. **Half-Shafts (Axle Shafts)** ŌĆö Splined shafts connecting the side gears to the drive wheels.

**Component Table for Sketch:**

| Label | Component |
|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Propeller Shaft |
| 2 | Drive Pinion |
| 3 | Differential Case |
| 4 | Cross Shaft (Spider) |
| 5, 6 | Pinion / Planet Gears |
| 7 | Left Side Gear |
| 8, 9 | Thrust Washers |
| 10 | Right Side Gear |
| 11, 12 | Thrust Washers |
| 13 | Crown Wheel |
| 14 | Bearing Cap |
| 15 | Differential Bearing |

**Q(b).** Explain the **working of a differential unit** when (i) the vehicle runs straight, and (ii) the vehicle negotiates a right-hand turn. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Straight-Line Motion:**
When the vehicle runs in a straight line, the resistance (rolling resistance and tractive resistance) offered by both driving wheels is **identical**. As a result, no relative motion exists between the two side gears, and the cross-shaft does not rotate about its own axis. The entire differential assembly ŌĆö case, cross-shaft, pinions, and side gears ŌĆö rotates as **one solid rigid unit** at the speed of the crown wheel. Therefore:

$$N_1 = N_2 = N_c$$

The vehicle moves with both wheels turning at the same rpm.

**(ii) Right-Hand Turn (Cornering):**
When the vehicle begins to turn right, the **left wheel (outer to the turn)** has to travel a **longer path** than the **right wheel (inner to the turn)** in the same time interval. The differential automatically accommodates this:

- The right wheel starts to **slow down** due to higher cornering resistance.
- This slowing is transmitted through the right side gear to the meshing pinion gear.
- The pinion gear begins to **rotate on the cross-shaft** (which acts as a fulcrum).
- The rotation of the pinion gear simultaneously **accelerates the left side gear**.
- Hence, $N_{left}$ **increases** and $N_{right}$ **decreases**, but the master equation $N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$ is preserved.

**Numerical illustration:**
If $N_c = 1000$ rpm, and the right wheel slows to 800 rpm, then:
$$N_{left} = 2(1000) - 800 = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

The difference of 200 rpm is "absorbed" by the cross-shaft rotation. The torque on each half-shaft remains **equal** (Ōēł 50% each) ŌĆö this is the key characteristic of an open differential.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö **Option B (Alternative Choice)**

**Q(a).** A car fitted with an open differential is taking a left turn. The crown wheel speed is 1400 rpm and the left wheel (inner to the turn) is rotating at 1100 rpm. The engine delivers 50 kW to the differential. The rolling radius of each wheel is 0.30 m. **[7 Marks]**
- **(i)** Determine the speed of the right (outer) wheel.
- **(ii)** Calculate the speed difference between the two wheels.
- **(iii)** Comment on what would happen if the left wheel were on a sheet of ice.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Speed of the right (outer) wheel:**
Using the master equation:
$$N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$$
$$1100 + N_{right} = 2 \times 1400$$
$$N_{right} = 2800 - 1100 = \boxed{1700 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Speed difference:**
$$\Delta N = N_{right} - N_{left} = 1700 - 1100 = \boxed{600 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(iii) Behavior on Ice:**
If the left wheel is on ice, its traction drops to nearly zero. Since the open differential splits torque **equally** (50:50) regardless of wheel speeds, the left wheel will spin up to **twice the case speed** while the right wheel stops receiving any meaningful drive torque. The vehicle will be **unable to move forward** ŌĆö it will be "stuck" with the icy wheel spinning. This is the major disadvantage of open differentials, solved in modern cars by Traction Control Systems (TCS) or by using Limited-Slip Differentials (LSD).

**Q(b).** Compare the **construction and working of an open differential with a limited-slip differential (LSD)**. Mention at least four points of comparison. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Open Differential | Limited-Slip Differential (LSD) |
|---|-----------|------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Speed Differentiation** | Allows 100% free speed differentiation; outer wheel can be 2x inner wheel speed | Speed differentiation is restricted by clutch packs or viscous coupling; ratio is limited (e.g., max 2.5:1) |
| 2 | **Torque Distribution** | Always 50:50 split; cannot bias torque to the gripping wheel | Can bias torque (e.g., 65:35 or even 80:20) toward the wheel with more traction |
| 3 | **Construction** | Simple ŌĆö case, cross-shaft, two pinions, two side gears, thrust washers | Complex ŌĆö adds clutch packs (friction discs), springs, cones, or viscous fluid chambers between side gears and case |
| 4 | **Traction on Slippery Surfaces** | Poor ŌĆö one wheel on ice results in zero motion | Good ŌĆö torque is sent to the gripping wheel, enabling escape from mud/snow |
| 5 | **Cornering Behavior** | Excellent ŌĆö no tire scrub | Slight understeer tendency due to bias ratio; some "binding" in tight turns |
| 6 | **Cost and Maintenance** | Low cost, minimal maintenance | 2-3x cost, periodic clutch/fluid replacement |
| 7 | **Heat Generation** | Negligible | Significant (friction clutches generate heat) |
| 8 | **Examples** | Maruti Alto, Hyundai Eon | BMW M-series, Ford Mustang GT |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse torque split with speed split.** In an open differential, **speed is free, torque is locked 50:50**. Many students write the opposite. This is the most common mistake.
> 2. **Do NOT use the equation $N_1 \cdot N_2 = N_c^2$**. This is a wrong formula students invent. The correct relation is the **arithmetic mean**: $(N_1 + N_2)/2 = N_c$.
> 3. **Always state the assumption** that the vehicle is in steady-state cornering (no wheel slip) when using the master equation.
> 4. **Label the diagram clearly.** Marks are awarded for each labeled component in a sketch. Unlabeled sketches fetch zero.
> 5. **For numerical problems**, always convert kW to W and rpm to rad/s correctly. A unit mismatch loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Distinguish "open diff" from "locking diff"** clearly ŌĆö many students use the terms interchangeably, which is wrong.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ŌŁÉ **Differential = Epicyclic Gear Train** with two sun gears (side gears) and two planet gears (pinions) on a free cross-shaft (carrier = differential case).
- ŌŁÉ **Three Functions:** (1) Torque transmission, (2) Final reduction, (3) Speed differentiation.
- ŌŁÉ **Master Equation:** $N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_c$ (Willis Equation for automotive differential).
- ŌŁÉ **Straight motion:** $N_1 = N_2 = N_c$, cross-shaft does not spin.
- ŌŁÉ **Turning motion:** Cross-shaft spins; one wheel speeds up, the other slows down, by the same amount.
- ŌŁÉ **Torque in open differential is ALWAYS 50:50**, regardless of wheel speed.
- ŌŁÉ **Final reduction ratio** = (Crown wheel teeth) / (Pinion teeth); typically 3.5:1 to 4.5:1.
- ŌŁÉ **Components to remember in any sketch:** Case, Crown wheel, Pinion, Cross-shaft, Pinion gears, Side gears, Thrust washers, Differential bearings, Half-shafts.
- ŌŁÉ **Disadvantage of open differential:** Cannot send torque to gripping wheel if other wheel is on a slippery surface.
- ŌŁÉ **Solutions:** Limited-Slip Differential (LSD), Locking Differential, Electronic Traction Control (TCS/ESP), Torsen differential, Viscous coupling.
- ŌŁÉ **Real-world application:** $V_{outer} = (R + t/2)\omega_{turn}$ and $V_{inner} = (R - t/2)\omega_{turn}$ ŌĆö the geometric basis for needing a differential.
- ŌŁÉ **Track width** ($t$) is the perpendicular distance between the centerlines of the two drive wheels.
- ŌŁÉ **Wheelbase** is different from track width ŌĆö do not confuse.
- ŌŁÉ **Hypoid gear** is most commonly used in automotive differentials (allows lower propeller shaft placement, more ground clearance).
- ŌŁÉ **Differential oil (hypoid gear oil)** has extreme pressure (EP) additives ŌĆö must be changed every 30,000ŌĆō60,000 km.
- ŌŁÉ **Typical differential efficiency:** 96ŌĆō98% under load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Sum of wheel speeds equals twice the case speed; torque is always equally shared."* Memorize this and you can solve 80% of KTU numerical questions on differentials.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Slip and Limited Slip Differential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Slip and Limited Slip Differential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Recap: The Open Differential Problem

A conventional **open (or free) differential** divides the input torque from the propeller shaft equally between the two drive wheels (50:50 split) while permitting them to rotate at different speeds during cornering. This is its fundamental engineering compromise ŌĆö the same mechanism that allows smooth cornering also causes it to **divert almost all torque to the wheel with the least traction** when one wheel loses grip (e.g., on ice or mud).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This is precisely the failure mode that **Non-Slip Differentials (NSD)** and **Limited Slip Differentials (LSD)** are designed to overcome, while still allowing some speed differentiation for cornering.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

**Non-Slip Differential (Locking / No-Spin Differential):**
A traction-enhancing differential in which the two output shafts are **positively locked together** when one wheel begins to lose traction, so that both wheels always rotate at the **same angular velocity** (1:1 ratio) under all conditions. The lock-up is automatic and self-reversing.

**Limited Slip Differential (LSD):**
A differential that always transfers a **finite, controlled amount of torque to the higher-traction wheel** even when the other wheel is slipping. It permits a small speed difference (slip) between the wheels during cornering, but **limits the maximum difference in torque** that can be developed between the two outputs. This finite torque transfer is characterized by the **Bias Ratio**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bias Ratio** = (Torque on high-traction wheel) ├Ę (Torque on low-traction wheel)
> - Open differential ŌåÆ 1:1
> - Typical LSD ŌåÆ 2:1 to 5:1
> - High-performance LSD ŌåÆ up to 8:1

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine two people pulling a heavy cart with two separate ropes through mud.

- **Open differential** = each person pulls independently. If the right person steps on slippery ice, *all* the cart's resistance transfers to the left rope ŌĆö but worse, the right rope goes limp and the cart stalls because the right person just spins in place.
- **Non-slip differential** = the two ropes are tied together with a knot that *cinches tight* the moment one rope goes slack. Both people must pull together, no matter the surface.
- **Limited slip differential** = the two ropes are connected through a friction clutch that **resists** any difference in pull. If the right rope tries to go slack, the clutch transfers up to a *fixed maximum* amount of force back to the left rope. The right person can still "pedal" lightly during a turn (cornering), but cannot spin freely.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Bias Ratio (B):** typically **2:1 to 5:1** for passenger cars, up to **8:1** for performance/off-road
- **Preload Torque (T_p):** **50ŌĆō200 Nm** for typical passenger LSDs
- **Slip Threshold:** the relative speed at which full lock-up occurs
- **Viscous Fluid Silicone Viscosity:** **100,000 ŌĆō 1,000,000 cSt** (centistokes)
- **Coefficient of Friction (╬╝) for clutch plates:** **0.08 ŌĆō 0.15** (paper-based) or **0.10 ŌĆō 0.20** (steel-on-steel)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Limited Slip Differentials

KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the following four categories:

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical Bias Ratio |
|------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **1. Clutch-type (Friction) LSD** | Stack of friction discs clamped by spring preload | 2.5:1 ŌĆō 4:1 |
| **2. Viscous LSD** | Shearing of high-viscosity silicone fluid | 2:1 ŌĆō 4:1 |
| **3. Torsen (Torque-Sensing) LSD** | Helical worm gear geometry resists slip | 3:1 ŌĆō 8:1 |
| **4. Electronic / Brake-Based LSD** | ECU uses wheel-speed sensors and ABS to brake slipping wheel | Variable (programmable) |

## 2.2 Clutch-Type LSD ŌĆö Operational Logic

**Construction (in brief):**
- Two **side gears** (left and right) splined to the half-shafts.
- Two **pinion gears** mounted on a **spider/cross shaft** inside the **differential case**.
- Two **clutch packs** (multi-disc friction packs) between each side gear and the case.
- A **preload spring** (Belleville or coil) pushes the side gears axially into the clutch packs.

**Working (step by step):**

1. **Straight-line driving:** Both wheels rotate at the same speed ŌåÆ side gears rotate at equal speeds ŌåÆ no relative motion between side gears and case ŌåÆ **clutch packs not engaged** beyond spring preload ŌåÆ torque still split nearly equally.
2. **Cornering:** Inner wheel slows, outer wheel speeds up ŌåÆ side gears rotate at different speeds ŌåÆ small relative motion ŌåÆ **clutch packs slip mildly** ŌåÆ still permits cornering.
3. **One wheel loses traction:** The slipping wheel's side gear tries to spin much faster than the case. The differential case begins to **rotate** (spider pinions act as levers). This **axial/radial reaction force** presses the side gear into its clutch pack, generating **friction torque** that couples the side gear to the case.
4. **Result:** The torque going to the gripping wheel increases by the friction-limited amount. **The slipping wheel cannot take all the torque.**

## 2.3 Torsen Differential ŌĆö Why It Cannot Be "Unlocked"

Torsen units use **worm gear pairs** where the angle between worm thread and gear tooth is less than the angle of repose. If the worm tries to drive the gear, friction **locks** the pair; the gear can only drive the worm by sliding. This gives an inherent mechanical bias with no wear parts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

> All formulas use **SI units** unless otherwise stated. The symbol $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid table-pipe conflicts.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Total input torque split (LSD) | $T_h + T_\ell = T_{in}$ | $T_h$ = high-side torque, $T_\ell$ = low-side torque |
| 2 | Clutch-limited difference | $T_h - T_\ell = T_{clutch}$ | $T_{clutch}$ is the biasing torque |
| 3 | Torque on each wheel | $T_h = \dfrac{T_{in}+T_{clutch}}{2}$ | High-traction wheel |
| 4 | Torque on each wheel | $T_\ell = \dfrac{T_{in}-T_{clutch}}{2}$ | Low-traction wheel |
| 5 | Bias Ratio | $B = \dfrac{T_h}{T_\ell}$ | Unitless, always $\geq 1$ |
| 6 | Clutch pack torque | $T_{clutch} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot R_m$ | $n$ = number of friction surfaces |
| 7 | Mean radius of clutch | $R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ | $R_o$ = outer, $R_i$ = inner radius |
| 8 | Preload torque | $T_p = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_s \cdot R_m$ | $F_s$ = spring preload force |
| 9 | Viscous shear stress | $\tau = \mu_v \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newtonian viscous model |
| 10 | Torsen geometry check | $\lambda < \phi$ | $\lambda$ = lead angle, $\phi$ = friction angle |
| 11 | Slip ratio (slipping wheel) | $s = \dfrac{\omega_{slip} - \omega_{veh}}{R}$ | Used in vehicle dynamics |
| 12 | Available tractive force | $F_t = \mu_{road} \cdot W$ | $\mu_{road}$ = tire-road friction |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger cars:** Torsen or clutch-type LSDs improve wet-road handling and acceleration.
- **Off-road / 4├Ś4 vehicles:** Non-slip (locking) differentials are essential for low-traction environments (mud, sand, rock crawling).
- **Racing (F1, WRC, NASCAR):** Electronically controlled LSDs optimize traction exit from corners.
- **Commercial trucks:** Viscous couplings provide smooth load transfer without wear.
- **Electric vehicles (modern):** Each wheel is driven by its own motor, so true **torque vectoring** replaces the mechanical differential entirely ŌĆö the modern evolution of the LSD concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque Distribution in a Clutch-Type LSD

### Setup
Let the ring gear deliver a total input torque $T_{in}$ to the differential case. The two output half-shafts deliver $T_\ell$ (left) and $T_h$ (right). The clutch packs generate a maximum friction (biasing) torque $T_{clutch}$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Static equilibrium of the differential case

The differential case is in rotational equilibrium (assuming negligible inertia at steady state):

$$T_{in} = T_h + T_\ell$$

**Logic:** Whatever torque enters the case from the ring gear must exit through the two half-shafts.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Biasing constraint from clutch pack

By definition of the clutch-limited slip differential, the **maximum difference** between the two output torques equals the clutch torque capacity:

$$T_h - T_\ell = T_{clutch}$$

**Logic:** The clutch packs can transmit no more than $T_{clutch}$ between the case and each side gear, so the torque differential between the two half-shafts is capped.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the linear system

Adding the two equations:

$$2T_h = T_{in} + T_{clutch}$$

$$T_h = \dfrac{T_{in} + T_{clutch}}{2}$$

Subtracting the second from the first:

$$2T_\ell = T_{in} - T_{clutch}$$

$$T_\ell = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{clutch}}{2}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Bias Ratio

$$B = \dfrac{T_h}{T_\ell} = \dfrac{T_{in} + T_{clutch}}{T_{in} - T_{clutch}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical insight:** As $T_{clutch} \to T_{in}$, the bias ratio $B \to \infty$, i.e., the differential effectively becomes a **locked (non-slip) differential** ŌĆö only the gripping wheel receives torque and the slipping wheel receives zero. This is the **mathematical bridge between an LSD and an NSD**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Clutch Pack Torque Capacity

A multi-disc clutch pack has $n$ active friction surfaces, mean radius $R_m$, axial clamping force $F$, and friction coefficient $\mu$. Each surface transmits a tangential friction force $f = \mu F$ at radius $R_m$.

**Differential element of force** on a ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$:

$$dF_{tang} = \mu \cdot p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

For uniform pressure $p$ acting on one face:

$$dT = r \cdot dF_{tang} = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r^2 \, dr$$

**Integrating from $R_i$ to $R_o$** over **$n$ surfaces**:

$$T_{clutch} = n \cdot \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^2 \, dr = n \cdot \mu \cdot p \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{3}(R_o^3 - R_i^3)$$

**Equivalent form using total axial force** $F = p \cdot \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$:

$$T_{clutch} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$

**Special case (thin clutch annulus, $R_o \approx R_i \approx R_m$):**

$$R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$$

$$T_{clutch} \approx n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot R_m$$

This simplified form is what KTU expects students to use in 14-mark numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:**
> A clutch-type limited slip differential has **6 friction surfaces** per pack. The inner and outer radii of the friction discs are **55 mm and 85 mm** respectively. The axial spring preload is **3.5 kN** and the coefficient of friction is **0.10**. If the input torque from the propeller shaft is **1800 Nm**, calculate:
>
> (a) Clutch torque capacity  
> (b) Torque transmitted to each wheel  
> (c) Bias ratio

### Solution (every step shown):

**Step (a) ŌĆö Mean radius**

$$R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2} = \dfrac{85 + 55}{2} = 70 \,\text{mm} = 0.070 \,\text{m}$$

**Step (a) ŌĆö Clutch torque capacity**

$$T_{clutch} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot R_m$$

$$T_{clutch} = 6 \times 0.10 \times 3500 \times 0.070$$

$$T_{clutch} = 6 \times 0.10 \times 3500 \times 0.070$$

$$T_{clutch} = 147 \,\text{Nm}$$

**Step (b) ŌĆö Torque on each wheel**

$$T_h = \dfrac{T_{in} + T_{clutch}}{2} = \dfrac{1800 + 147}{2} = \dfrac{1947}{2} = 973.5 \,\text{Nm}$$

$$T_\ell = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{clutch}}{2} = \dfrac{1800 - 147}{2} = \dfrac{1653}{2} = 826.5 \,\text{Nm}$$

**Step (c) ŌĆö Bias ratio**

$$B = \dfrac{T_h}{T_\ell} = \dfrac{973.5}{826.5} \approx 1.178 : 1$$

**Verification:** $T_h + T_\ell = 973.5 + 826.5 = 1800$ Nm Ō£ō  
$T_h - T_\ell = 973.5 - 826.5 = 147$ Nm = $T_{clutch}$ Ō£ō

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Open Differential | Non-Slip (Locked) | Clutch LSD | Torsen LSD | Viscous LSD |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|------------|------------|-------------|
| Speed difference allowed | Unlimited | Zero (locked) | Limited | Limited | Limited |
| Torque split (slip case) | 100:0 | 50:50 (forced) | $B$ = 2ŌĆō4 : 1 | $B$ = 3ŌĆō8 : 1 | $B$ = 2ŌĆō4 : 1 |
| Cornering behaviour | Smooth | Harsh (binding) | Smooth-moderate | Smooth | Smooth |
| Wear | Low | Moderate-High | Moderate (plates) | Very Low (gears) | Low (fluid) |
| Reaction time | Immediate | Immediate | ~50ŌĆō200 ms | Instantaneous | 100ŌĆō500 ms |
| Typical application | Economy cars | Off-road 4├Ś4 | Sedans/SUVs | Performance/AWD | Light commercial |
