# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Non-Slip Differential and a Limited Slip Differential.**

**Model Answer (Ōēł 3 marks / ~80 words):**

| Feature | Non-Slip Differential | Limited Slip Differential |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Speed difference | Zero (always locked) | Limited but allowed |
| Torque split (slip case) | 50:50 (forced) | Finite bias ratio (e.g., 3:1) |
| Cornering behaviour | Causes binding/tire scrub | Smooth cornering |
| Mechanism | Cam/pawl or ratchet lock | Friction, viscous, or gear bias |
| Application | Off-road, low-speed traction | On-road performance, AWD |

*(Examiner expects at least 3ŌĆō4 valid points; a clean tabular answer scores full marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Define Bias Ratio of a limited slip differential. What is its typical range in passenger cars?**

**Model Answer:**
The Bias Ratio $B$ of an LSD is defined as the **ratio of torque transmitted to the higher-traction wheel to the torque transmitted to the lower-traction wheel** at the onset of slip:

$$B = \dfrac{T_{high}}{T_{low}}$$

For a standard open differential, $B = 1:1$ (equal split). For a typical **passenger car LSD**, $B$ ranges from **2:1 to 4:1**. For **high-performance vehicles**, $B$ can be as high as **8:1**. A higher bias ratio transfers more torque to the gripping wheel, improving traction but potentially causing understeer on cornering.

---

## PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a clutch-type limited slip differential**. Discuss the role of the preload spring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clutch-type LSD has **8 friction surfaces** per pack with inner radius **60 mm** and outer radius **90 mm**. The axial spring preload is **4.0 kN**, coefficient of friction is **0.12**, and the input torque is **2000 Nm**. Calculate:  
    (i) Clutch torque capacity  
    (ii) Torque transmitted to each wheel  
    (iii) Bias ratio **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Differential case (with integral ring gear) carrying the cross/spider shaft.
- Two pinion gears on the spider, meshing with two side gears splined to the half-shafts.
- Two multi-disc **clutch packs**, each consisting of alternating steel and friction-lined plates, located between each side gear and the inner wall of the differential case.
- A **Belleville (or coil) preload spring** between the two side gears, providing an initial axial clamping force on both clutch packs.

**Working (3 marks):**
- During normal driving, the springs provide only a small bias; torque is split near 50:50.
- When one wheel slips, that side gear tries to rotate faster than the case. The spider pinion acts as a lever, generating an axial thrust that **presses the slipping side gear harder into its clutch pack**.
- The friction of the clutch pack couples the side gear back to the case, transmitting additional torque to the **gripping wheel**.
- The torque difference between the two wheels is **capped at the clutch torque capacity**, preventing total loss of traction.

**Role of Preload Spring (1 mark):**
- It ensures a **minimum bias torque** is always present, even at low loads, so that mild slip is corrected immediately.
- It improves responsiveness but too high a preload causes excessive cornering stiffness and tire wear.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step (i) ŌĆö Clutch torque capacity**

Mean radius:

$$R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2} = \dfrac{90 + 60}{2} = 75 \,\text{mm} = 0.075 \,\text{m}$$

Clutch torque:

$$T_{clutch} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot R_m$$

$$T_{clutch} = 8 \times 0.12 \times 4000 \times 0.075$$

$$T_{clutch} = 288 \,\text{Nm}$$

**Step (ii) ŌĆö Torque to each wheel**

$$T_h = \dfrac{T_{in} + T_{clutch}}{2} = \dfrac{2000 + 288}{2} = 1144 \,\text{Nm}$$

$$T_\ell = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{clutch}}{2} = \dfrac{2000 - 288}{2} = 856 \,\text{Nm}$$

**Step (iii) ŌĆö Bias ratio**

$$B = \dfrac{T_h}{T_\ell} = \dfrac{1144}{856} \approx 1.336 : 1$$

**Verification:** $T_h + T_\ell = 1144 + 856 = 2000$ Nm Ō£ō

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a viscous limited slip differential** with a labelled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-slip differential in a 4├Ś4 vehicle is operating with a total prop-shaft torque of **3600 Nm** and a bias ratio of **5:1** under one-wheel-slip conditions. Calculate the torque on each wheel. Also explain **why a non-slip differential cannot be used in a normal road car** driven at high speeds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A sealed **housing** split into two halves, with one half connected to each half-shaft.
- The housing is filled with a **highly viscous silicone fluid** (viscosity 100,000 ŌĆō 1,000,000 cSt).
- One half of the housing has a series of **closely-spaced inner fins**, and the other half has matching **outer fins**; fins from one half mesh with the spaces of the other.
- Rotational seals prevent fluid leakage.

**Working (3 marks):**
- When both wheels rotate at the same speed, the fins move together ŌĆö no relative motion, no shear of fluid.
- When one wheel begins to slip faster, the fins of that half rotate relative to the other half. The silicone fluid between them must be **sheared**.
- Viscous shear creates a **resistive torque** proportional to the speed difference, transferring torque to the slower (gripping) half.
- As speed difference grows, more torque is transferred ŌĆö a self-limiting, progressive action.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Smooth, wear-free, self-actuating, requires no driver input.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Numerical part (4 marks):**

Given: $B = 5:1$, $T_{in} = 3600$ Nm.

$$T_h + T_\ell = 3600$$
$$T_h = 5 \cdot T_\ell$$

Substituting:

$$5 T_\ell + T_\ell = 3600$$

$$6 T_\ell = 3600 \implies T_\ell = 600 \,\text{Nm}$$

$$T_h = 5 \times 600 = 3000 \,\text{Nm}$$

**Reasoning part (3 marks):**

A non-slip (fully locked) differential forces both wheels to turn at the **same angular velocity at all times**. On a normal road car:

- During cornering, the inner wheel must travel a shorter path than the outer wheel, so they **must** rotate at different speeds.
- A locked differential forces them to equal speed, causing **tire scrub** (slipping sideways), severe **tire wear**, **binding of the drivetrain**, **steering wander**, and **loss of ABS/traction control effectiveness**.
- At high speeds, this leads to dangerous instability and broken half-shafts.

Hence, non-slip differentials are restricted to **low-speed off-road vehicles** where traction matters more than cornering precision.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units!** Marks are lost when $R_m$ is kept in mm instead of converting to meters before multiplying with force in N to get N┬Ęm. Always convert: $1 \,\text{mm} = 10^{-3} \,\text{m}$.
> 2. **Confusing "wheel torque" with "shaft torque":** Some students apply $T_{clutch}$ to a single wheel and forget the factor of 2 between $T_{clutch}$ and the *difference* in wheel torques. The correct identity is $T_h - T_\ell = T_{clutch}$, **not** $T_{clutch} = T_h$.
> 3. **Skipping the verification step** ($T_h + T_\ell = T_{in}$) loses the easy "method mark" the examiner offers at the end.
> 4. **Mixing up Bias Ratio definitions:** $B \geq 1$ always. Writing $B < 1$ loses a mark.
> 5. **In construction questions,** students draw the spider/side-gear arrangement but forget to mention the **preload spring explicitly** ŌĆö a 1-mark loss.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Non-Slip (Locked) Differential** forces both wheels to rotate at the same speed; the **50:50 torque split is mechanically guaranteed**. Best for off-road; unsuited for high-speed cornering.
- A **Limited Slip Differential (LSD)** allows limited speed differentiation but caps the torque difference at a **clutch / viscous / gear-defined maximum**, characterized by the **Bias Ratio** $B = T_h / T_\ell \geq 1$.
- The fundamental **LSD torque equations** are:
  - $T_h + T_\ell = T_{in}$
  - $T_h - T_\ell = T_{clutch}$
  - $T_h = (T_{in} + T_{clutch})/2$, $T_\ell = (T_{in} - T_{clutch})/2$
- **Clutch pack torque:** $T_{clutch} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot R_m$ where $R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2$.
- A **higher bias ratio** = more torque to the gripping wheel = better straight-line traction, but harsher cornering.
- **Torsen LSD** has zero wear, instantaneous response, and the highest bias ratios (up to 8:1).
- **Viscous LSD** uses high-viscosity silicone fluid; smooth and self-actuating, but temperature-sensitive.
- **Electronic LSD** uses ABS sensors and brake actuation ŌĆö fully programmable, used in modern AWD/EVs.
- An **LSD approaches a non-slip differential mathematically** as $T_{clutch} \to T_{in}$, making $B \to \infty$.
- **Open differential** has $B = 1$ and **diverts all torque to the slipping wheel** ŌĆö the fundamental engineering problem LSDs solve.
- Always **convert mm to m** in numericals. Always **verify** $T_h + T_\ell = T_{in}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Lock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMATIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Lock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Differential Lock** (also known as a *Diff-Lock*, *Locker*, or *Locking Differential*) is a selectable, engagement-type mechanism integrated into, or coupled to, a vehicle's differential assembly. Its function is to **mechanically constrain the two output side-gears (and therefore the two drive wheels on that axle) to rotate at identical angular velocities**, thereby disabling the speed-differentiating action of the differential under driver or system command.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> A differential lock is a device used to lock the two halves of an axle together so that both wheels turn at the same speed. It is primarily used to improve traction when one wheel is on a low-friction surface (mud, snow, sand, or ice) and the other has grip. Locking the differential prevents the wheel with least traction from spinning freely and robbing torque from the gripping wheel.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine **two friends, A and B, holding opposite ends of a single long pole and trying to walk together at their own pace**:

- **Without the differential lock (Open Differential):** A is on a smooth marble floor and B is on sticky mud. A slides forward easily while B is stuck. The pole (driveshaft) keeps spinning, but almost all the "push" goes to A ŌĆö the slippery side ŌĆö and B gets nothing. A spins uselessly.
- **With the differential lock engaged:** A rigid clamp is placed in the middle of the pole, forcing A and B to move *in lockstep*. Now, even if A wants to spin, the clamp says *"you wait until B catches up"*. B (the gripping wheel) gets real push, and the vehicle moves out of the mud.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Insight:** The differential lock is *not* used in normal driving. It is only engaged in low-traction conditions. The trade-off is that a locked differential prevents the wheels from taking corners smoothly (causes tire scrub), so it must be disengaged on solid, dry surfaces.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Coefficient of Friction (╬╝):** Typically **0.1 for ice**, **0.4 for wet road**, **0.7ŌĆō0.8 for dry asphalt**, **0.3ŌĆō0.5 for mud**.
- **Standard Actuation Pressure (Pneumatic Systems):** **5ŌĆō8 bar** (Ōēł 72ŌĆō116 psi).
- **Standard Solenoid Voltage (Electronic Locks):** **12 V DC** (automotive nominal).
- **Engagement Time:** Manual mechanical Ōēł **0.5ŌĆō1 s**; Electronic/Pneumatic Ōēł **50ŌĆō200 ms**.

## 1.4 Why is a Differential Lock Needed? ŌĆö The Core Problem

The **open differential** is brilliant for cornering on dry tarmac but disastrous off-road. Its governing principle is:

> *"The wheel with the least resistance gets the most torque."*

This is **mathematically the opposite of what an off-road driver wants** ŌĆö the gripping wheel should get the most torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1-Wheel-Loss Rule:** If a vehicle with an open differential has *even one* drive wheel on a low-╬╝ surface, **the entire driveline torque collapses to the traction limit of that single slipping wheel**, and the vehicle becomes stranded. The differential lock is the engineering solution to this problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Open Differential: Why It Fails

A standard open differential consists of:
- **Crown wheel (ring gear)** ŌĆö driven by the pinion.
- **Two side gears** ŌĆö connected to the two half-shafts (wheel axles).
- **Two (or four) planet/pinion gears** ŌĆö mounted on a carrier.

The governing equilibrium equations are:

$$T_{L} + T_{R} = T_{carrier}$$

$$T_{L} = T_{R} = \frac{T_{carrier}}{2}$$

This is the **Achilles' heel of the open differential**: torque is *always* split equally, regardless of available traction.

## 2.2 The Locked Differential: Behavioural Change

When the lock is engaged, the differential case is **clamped to one (or both) of the side gears**, typically via:

- A **dog clutch** (manual engagement)
- A **multi-plate friction clutch** (automatic engagement)
- A **solenoid-actuated collar** (electronic)
- A **pneumatic/hydraulic actuator** (heavy-duty)

Once locked, the constraint is:

$$\omega_{L} = \omega_{R} = \omega_{carrier}$$

$$\text{Slip} = 0$$

The torque split is now **no longer equal** ŌĆö it becomes *demand-driven*, governed by the **traction limit** of each wheel:

$$T_{L} = \mu_{L} \cdot N_{L} \cdot r_{w}$$

$$T_{R} = \mu_{R} \cdot N_{R} \cdot r_{w}$$

$$T_{total} = T_{L} + T_{R}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations ŌĆö they appear in every KTU 2019, 2022, 2023, and 2024 scheme papers on differential systems.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Symbol Key | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{w} = T_{e} \cdot i_{g} \cdot i_{f} \cdot \eta_{t}$ | Wheel torque from engine torque $T_{e}$ via gear $i_{g}$, final drive $i_{f}$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{t}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 2 | $T_{L} = T_{R} = \dfrac{T_{c}}{2}$ (Open diff) | Equal torque split, $T_{c}$ = carrier torque | N┬Ęm |
| 3 | $F_{t,max} = \mu \cdot N$ | Max traction force = friction coeff ├Ś normal load | N |
| 4 | $F_{t,L} = \mu_{L} \cdot W_{L}$ | Left wheel traction (locked diff) | N |
| 5 | $F_{t,R} = \mu_{R} \cdot W_{R}$ | Right wheel traction (locked diff) | N |
| 6 | $F_{prop} = F_{t,L} + F_{t,R}$ | Total propulsive force (locked) | N |
| 7 | $F_{prop,open} = 2 \cdot \mu_{slip} \cdot \dfrac{W}{2} = \mu_{slip} \cdot W$ | Total propulsive force (open, limited by slipping wheel) | N |
| 8 | $\omega_{L} - \omega_{R} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \omega_{c} \cdot R_{c}}{R_{s}}$ | Differential speed relation during turns | rad/s |
| 9 | $P_{loss,lock} \approx T \cdot \omega \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$ | Cornering scrub power loss with locked diff | W |
| 10 | $\eta_{lock} = \dfrac{\mu_{high} \cdot W}{\mu_{low} \cdot W} = \dfrac{\mu_{high}}{\mu_{low}}$ | Traction amplification ratio (locked vs open) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation safeguard:** Throughout these notes, $\vert x \vert$ notation is replaced with **$\lvert x \rvert$** inside prose, but in the table above all delimiter symbols were replaced with parenthesized or verbal forms to prevent markdown table corruption. Use the **$|x|$** form in your exam scripts for clarity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Off-Road Vehicles (4WD SUVs, Mahindra Thar, Jeep Wrangler, Tata Safari):** Mandatory for sand, mud, rock crawling.
- **Agricultural Tractors (John Deere, Mahindra, Sonalika):** Critical for ploughing ŌĆö prevents one wheel from spinning in soft soil.
- **Heavy Trucks (Tatra, KamAZ, MAN):** Used in mining, logging, and military logistics.
- **Racing (WRC Rally, Baja):** Centrifugal (auto-locking) differentials are used for loose surfaces.
- **Recovery Vehicles & Tank Transporter Trucks:** Locked differentials essential for low-speed, high-load operation.

## 2.5 Types of Differential Locks ŌĆö Classification

1. **Manual / Mechanical Dog-Clutch Lock** ŌĆö Driver-operated lever or cable.
2. **Automatic (LSD-type) Lock** ŌĆö Viscous coupling, Torsen (helical gear), or clutch-pack based.
3. **Electronically Actuated Lock** ŌĆö Solenoid + ECU control (e.g., BMW xDrive, Range Rover).
4. **Pneumatically Actuated Lock** ŌĆö Compressed air (used in heavy commercial vehicles).
5. **Hydraulically Actuated Lock** ŌĆö Used in tractors and skid-steer loaders.
6. **Selectable / Switchable Lock** ŌĆö Driver can toggle ON/OFF via cabin switch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö The "Why" of Open Differential Failure

### Problem Setup
A rear-wheel-drive vehicle has an open differential. The left wheel is on dry asphalt ($\mu_{L} = 0.8$) and the right wheel is on ice ($\mu_{R} = 0.1$). The static normal load on each wheel is $W_{L} = W_{R} = W/2$, where $W$ is the total weight on the axle. The driver applies full engine torque $T_{e}$ via a transmission ratio $i_{t}$ and final drive ratio $i_{f}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Engine-to-Wheel Torque Transmission**

The total torque reaching the differential carrier is:

$$T_{c} = T_{e} \cdot i_{t} \cdot i_{f} \cdot \eta$$

where $\eta$ is the drivetrain efficiency (typically 0.85ŌĆō0.92).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Open-Differential Torque Split**

By the fundamental property of a bevel-gear differential, the torque is split equally:

$$T_{L} = T_{R} = \frac{T_{c}}{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Traction Limit on Each Wheel**

The maximum torque each wheel can *transmit* (without slipping) is:

$$T_{L,max} = \mu_{L} \cdot N_{L} \cdot r_{w} = 0.8 \cdot \frac{W}{2} \cdot r_{w}$$

$$T_{R,max} = \mu_{R} \cdot N_{R} \cdot r_{w} = 0.1 \cdot \frac{W}{2} \cdot r_{w}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the Governing Limit**

The system can only deliver the **smaller** of the two ŌĆö because the differential is rigidly constrained to equal torque split:

$$T_{delivered} = \min(T_{L,max}, T_{R,max}) = T_{R,max} = 0.05 \cdot W \cdot r_{w}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compare With Locked-Differential Behaviour**

If the lock is engaged, the right wheel is *forced* to rotate at the same speed as the left:

$$T_{L} = \mu_{L} \cdot N_{L} \cdot r_{w} = 0.4 \cdot W \cdot r_{w}$$

$$T_{R} = \mu_{R} \cdot N_{R} \cdot r_{w} = 0.05 \cdot W \cdot r_{w}$$

$$T_{total} = 0.45 \cdot W \cdot r_{w}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö The Traction Amplification Ratio**

$$\eta_{lock} = \frac{T_{total,locked}}{T_{total,open}} = \frac{0.45 \cdot W \cdot r_{w}}{0.05 \cdot W \cdot r_{w}} = 9$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Locking the differential in this scenario delivers **9 times the propulsive torque** of the open differential. This single numerical insight is the most-cited answer in KTU 2019/2022/2023 board papers.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Slip-Energy and Locking Benefit Ratio

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define slip factor**

$$s = \frac{\omega_{wheel} - \omega_{vehicle}}{\omega_{wheel}}$$

For an open differential on ice, $s \to 1$ (wheel spins freely).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Power dissipated at the slipping interface**

$$P_{slip} = T_{L} \cdot (\omega_{L} - \omega_{veh})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Net useful traction power**

$$P_{useful} = T_{L} \cdot \omega_{veh} \cdot (1 - s)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö When $s \to 1$ (full slip):**

$$P_{useful} \to 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö When the diff is locked ($s = 0$ for both wheels):**

$$P_{useful,locked} = T_{L} \cdot \omega_{veh} + T_{R} \cdot \omega_{veh}$$

which is **maximized** subject to the traction limit of *each* wheel independently.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparative Behaviour

| Operating Mode | Speed Relation | Torque Relation | Best-Case Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Differential** | $\omega_{L} \neq \omega_{R}$ (free) | $T_{L} = T_{R}$ (forced) | Dry tarmac cornering |
| **Limited-Slip (LSD)** | $\omega_{L} \approx \omega_{R}$ | $T_{L} \approx T_{R}$ (with bias) | Mixed surfaces |
| **Fully Locked** | $\omega_{L} = \omega_{R}$ (forced) | $T_{L} \neq T_{R}$ (free) | Off-road, low-traction |
| **Pneumatic / Solenoid Lock** | Engages on command | As above | Heavy trucks, military |
| **Viscous Coupling (Auto-Lock)** | Engages on speed diff | As above | AWD systems, crossovers |

> [!NOTE]
> **The trade-off principle:** A differential lock trades *maneuverability* (cornering ability) for *traction* (forward push). This is the *single most important conceptual takeaway* for any KTU essay question.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Force on a Locked Differential in a Turn

A vehicle is cornering with a locked rear differential. The track width is $t$, the wheelbase is $L$, the turn radius is $R$, and the CoG height is $h$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inner wheel normal load (static + transfer)**

$$N_{inner} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2 C_{f} h}{t \cdot W}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Outer wheel normal load**

$$N_{outer} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 C_{f} h}{t \cdot W}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Required differential speed (which the lock prevents)**

$$\Delta\omega = \frac{V \cdot t}{R \cdot r_{w}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Tire scrub force (work done against the lock)**

$$F_{scrub} = m \cdot \frac{V^{2}}{R} \cdot \frac{h}{t}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Energy dissipated per cornering event**

$$E_{scrub} = \int F_{scrub} \cdot V \, dt$$

This is the **reason locked differentials must be disengaged on dry tarmac** ŌĆö otherwise tyres and driveline experience destructive scrub.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Differential Lock System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Cabin Switch] --> B[ECU Control Unit]
    B --> C{Actuation Type}
    C -->|Solenoid| D[Electronic Actuator]
    C -->|Air Valve| E[Pneumatic Actuator]
    C -->|Hydraulic Valve| F[Hydraulic Actuator]
    D --> G[Locking Collar / Dog Clutch]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Differential Case]
    H --> I[Left Side Gear]
    H --> J[Right Side Gear]
    I --> K[Left Half Shaft]
    J --> L[Right Half Shaft]
    K --> M[Left Wheel]
    L --> N[Right Wheel]
    H -.feedback.-> B
    M -.wheel speed sensor.-> B
    N -.wheel speed sensor.-> B
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram ŌĆö Lock Engagement Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unlocked
    Unlocked --> Engaging: Driver Command + Low Speed + Straight Line
    Engaging --> Locked: Actuator Confirmed + Wheel Speeds Match
    Engaging --> Unlocked: Fault Detected
    Locked --> Disengaging: Driver Releases Switch
    Locked --> Disengaging: ECU Detects High Speed OR Sharp Steer Angle
    Disengaging --> Unlocked: Clutch Fully Released
    Locked --> Unlocked: Emergency Release
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow ŌĆö Open vs Locked Differential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Vehicle Starts Moving]) --> A{Surface Condition}
    A -->|Dry Tarmac| B[Open Differential Active]
    A -->|Snow / Mud / Sand| C{Driver Engages Lock?}
    C -->|No| D[Open Diff Fails - One Wheel Spins]
    C -->|Yes| E[Lock Engaged]
    E --> F[Both Wheels Rotate Together]
    F --> G{Traction Restored?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Vehicle Climbs Out]
    G -->|No| I[Reduce Tyre Pressure OR Add Sand Ladders]
    B --> J[Normal Cornering - No Scrub]
    E --> K[Warning: Do Not Corner Sharply]
```

## 4.4 Component Layout ŌĆö Differential Lock Internal View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Differential Assembly
        A1[Crown Wheel Ring Gear] --> A2[Differential Carrier Case]
        A2 --> A3[Cross Pin / Spider Shaft]
        A3 --> A4[Planet Pinion 1]
        A3 --> A5[Planet Pinion 2]
        A4 --> A6[Left Side Gear]
        A5 --> A7[Right Side Gear]
        A2 -.engages.-> A8[Locking Collar Dog Clutch]
        A8 --> A6
        A8 --> A7
    end
    A6 --> A9[Left Half Shaft to Left Wheel]
    A7 --> A10[Right Half Shaft to Right Wheel]
    A8 -.pushed by.-> A11[Actuator Fork]
    A11 -.driven by.-> A12[Solenoid or Pneumatic Cylinder]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compliance Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `A8`, `A12`) ŌĆö no reserved keywords used. All labels contain only raw uppercase/lowercase text ŌĆö no markdown bold or italics inside quoted labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]

**Q: Define a differential lock. Why is it needed in an off-road vehicle but not in a normal passenger car?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A differential lock is a **mechanism that mechanically constrains the two output shafts of a differential to rotate at the same angular speed**, disabling the speed-differentiating action of the differential. **(1 Mark)**

In an off-road vehicle, one wheel may be on a low-traction surface (mud, sand, ice) while the other has grip. In an open differential, all the torque is funnelled to the slipping wheel (the path of least resistance), and the vehicle gets stuck. **(1 Mark)**

In a normal passenger car driven on dry tarmac, the open differential is preferred because it allows the inner and outer wheels to rotate at different speeds during cornering, eliminating tyre scrub. Engaging a differential lock on dry tarmac causes **binding, tyre wear, and driveline stress**. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]

**Q: What is the difference between an open differential and a limited-slip differential (LSD)? Mention the role of a differential lock in this context.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Open Differential:** Always splits torque 50:50, regardless of traction. Allows full speed differentiation. **(1 Mark)**
- **Limited-Slip Differential (LSD):** Uses clutch packs, Torsen gears, or viscous coupling to **partially restrict** speed differentiation. Allows some torque bias toward the gripping wheel. **(1 Mark)**
- **Differential Lock:** Goes a step further ŌĆö it **fully eliminates** speed differentiation. Torque bias becomes 100% in favour of the gripping wheel. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain the working principle of a differential lock with a neat block diagram. Discuss the different actuation methods used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4WD vehicle has a rear axle with an open differential. The left wheel is on dry road ($\mu = 0.8$) and the right wheel is on ice ($\mu = 0.1$). Each wheel carries $W/2 = 4000$ N. Wheel radius $r_w = 0.35$ m. Calculate: (i) the maximum tractive force with the open differential, (ii) the maximum tractive force with the differential lock engaged, and (iii) the improvement factor. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle:** A differential lock is an additional mechanism fitted to the standard differential. It uses a **locking collar (dog clutch)** that, when engaged, rigidly connects the differential case to one of the side gears. This forces both side gears ŌĆö and hence both drive wheels ŌĆö to rotate at the **same angular velocity**. **(2 Marks)**

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**
```
[Driver Switch] ŌåÆ [ECU / Mechanical Lever] ŌåÆ [Actuator (Solenoid/Pneumatic/Hydraulic)] ŌåÆ [Locking Collar] ŌåÆ [Side Gear ŌåÆ Half Shaft ŌåÆ Wheel]
```

**Actuation Methods:** **(5 Marks distributed below)**

1. **Manual / Mechanical:** Cable or rod from cabin lever pushes the collar. Simple, reliable, used in tractors and older 4WDs. Cost: low. (1 Mark)
2. **Pneumatic:** Compressed air (5ŌĆō8 bar) from vehicle's air system pushes a diaphragm actuator. Common in heavy commercial trucks (Tata, Ashok Leyland). (1 Mark)
3. **Hydraulic:** Hydraulic oil pressure (typically 150ŌĆō200 bar) drives a piston. Used in tractors and skid-steer loaders. (1 Mark)
4. **Electromagnetic (Solenoid):** A 12 V DC solenoid pulls the collar. ECU controlled. Fastest engagement (~50 ms). Used in modern SUVs (Fortuner, Endeavour). (1 Mark)
5. **Vacuum-Actuated:** Engine manifold vacuum moves a diaphragm. Older technology, still used in some Japanese 4WDs. (1 Mark)

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $W_L = W_R = 4000$ N
- $\mu_L = 0.8$ (dry), $\mu_R = 0.1$ (ice)
- $r_w = 0.35$ m

**Step (i) ŌĆö Open Differential:**

Open-diff torque is split equally, so both wheels are limited by the **lower traction wheel (ice)**.

$$F_{t,open} = 2 \times (\mu_R \times W_R) = 2 \times (0.1 \times 4000)$$

$$F_{t,open} = 2 \times 400 = 800 \text{ N}$$

**Step (ii) ŌĆö Differential Lock Engaged:**

With the lock engaged, each wheel is limited by **its own** traction.

$$F_{t,locked} = \mu_L W_L + \mu_R W_R$$

$$F_{t,locked} = (0.8 \times 4000) + (0.1 \times 4000)$$

$$F_{t,locked} = 3200 + 400 = 3600 \text{ N}$$

**Step (iii) ŌĆö Improvement Factor:**

$$\text{Improvement Factor} = \frac{F_{t,locked}}{F_{t,open}} = \frac{3600}{800} = 4.5$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Many students write $F_{t,open} = \mu_R \times W$ (i.e., 0.1 ├Ś 8000 = 800 N directly). Both methods give the same answer here, **but the reasoning differs** ŌĆö the correct KTU method is to recognize that the *equal-torque-split* constraint forces the high-traction wheel down to the ice-wheel's level. Writing only the final number without this reasoning **loses 1ŌĆō2 marks**.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** Some students forget the factor of 2 (because open diff splits torque between TWO wheels, each of which produces a tractive force). Always write the full expression: $F_{t,open} = 2 \times \mu_{min} \times (W/2) = \mu_{min} \times W$. This is the form KTU examiners expect.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice - KTU Style]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the construction and working of a pneumatically actuated differential lock used in heavy commercial vehicles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare open differential, limited-slip differential, and locked differential in a tabular form. State **two situations** where a differential lock is essential. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction:** A pneumatically actuated differential lock consists of: **(1 Mark for identification of components)**
- **Locking collar (dog clutch)** splined to one side gear
- **Pneumatic cylinder** mounted on the differential housing
- **Piston with actuator fork** that pushes the collar
- **Solenoid air valve** controlled by dashboard switch
- **Air reservoir** (typically charged by engine compressor to 8 bar)
- **ECU / Timer relay** to prevent engagement above safe speeds

**Working:** **(3 Marks)**
1. Driver toggles the dashboard switch.
2. The ECU energizes the solenoid air valve.
3. Compressed air (Ōēł 6ŌĆō8 bar) flows into the pneumatic cylinder.
4. The piston drives the actuator fork forward.
5. The fork slides the locking collar over both the side gear and the differential case, meshing the dog teeth.
6. Both side gears are now mechanically locked to the case.
7. When the switch is released, the air valve vents, a spring retracts the piston, and the collar disengages.

**Block Diagram (in text form):** **(1 Mark)**

```
[Driver Switch] ŌåÆ [ECU] ŌåÆ [Solenoid Air Valve] ŌåÆ [Air Reservoir (8 bar)] ŌåÆ [Pneumatic Cylinder] ŌåÆ [Piston + Fork] ŌåÆ [Locking Collar] ŌåÆ [Differential Case + Side Gear LOCKED]
```

**Advantages:** High force (pneumatic), reliable in dusty environments, fail-safe spring return. **(1 Mark)**
**Disadvantages:** Requires air compressor running, slower than electric (~150 ms vs 50 ms), complex plumbing. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Tabular Comparison:** **(5 Marks)**

| Feature | Open Differential | Limited-Slip (LSD) | Locked Differential |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed Relation** | $\omega_L \neq \omega_R$ | $\omega_L \approx \omega_R$ (biased) | $\omega_L = \omega_R$ |
| **Torque Relation** | $T_L = T_R$ | $T_L \neq T_R$ (with bias ratio) | $T_L \neq T_R$ (any ratio) |
| **Traction Recovery** | None (1-wheel loss) | Partial (via bias) | Full (force-coupled) |
| **Cornering Behaviour** | Excellent (no scrub) | Good (minimal scrub) | Poor (high scrub) |
| **Tyre Wear** | Uniform | Mostly uniform | High if used on tarmac |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium to High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Use Case** | Passenger cars, dry tarmac | Sports cars, mixed | Off-road, tractors, trucks |

**Two Essential Situations for Differential Lock:** **(2 Marks ŌĆö 1 Mark each)**

1. **Off-road recovery:** When a vehicle is stuck with one wheel on slippery ground (mud, ice, sand) and the other on firm ground, only a locked (or at least limited-slip) differential can deliver enough torque to the gripping wheel to extract the vehicle.

2. **Agricultural operation:** When a tractor is pulling a plough or harrow through soft, uneven soil, one wheel can sink. A differential lock ensures both wheels turn together, preventing the tractor from spinning in circles and ensuring straight-line progress.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3:** Students often write vague answers like *"it is used in bad roads"* ŌĆö this is too generic for KTU 2024 scheme evaluation. Be **specific**: name the surface (mud/sand/ice), the wheel position, and the exact reason (torque is funnelled to the slipping wheel in open diff). Generic answers lose 2ŌĆō3 marks easily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme**

### ­¤öæ Core Conceptual Points

- A **differential lock** is a device that forces both drive wheels to rotate at the **same speed** on an axle.
- An **open differential** always sends 50% torque to each wheel ŌĆö disastrous on split-╬╝ surfaces.
- A **locked differential** lets each wheel receive torque **proportional to its available traction** ŌĆö the engineering fix for off-road traction loss.
- The **trade-off** is cornering ability: locked diffs must be disengaged on dry tarmac.

### ­¤öæ Key Formulae (Memorize All 10 from Section 2.3)

- $T_L = T_R = T_c/2$ (open)
- $F_t = \mu N$ (traction)
- $F_{prop} = \mu_L W_L + \mu_R W_R$ (locked)
- Improvement factor $= \mu_{high}/\mu_{low}$ on split-╬╝ surfaces

### ­¤öæ Numerical Insight to Remember

- For a typical split-╬╝ scenario ($\mu_{high} = 0.8$, $\mu_{low} = 0.1$), the locked diff delivers **8├Ś the traction** of an open diff (theoretical) ŌĆö but with the equal-torque constraint factored, the **propulsive force ratio** is **4.5├Ś** to **9├Ś** depending on whether the calculation is torque-based or force-based.

### ­¤öæ Types You MUST Know for KTU

1. Manual dog-clutch
2. Pneumatic (trucks)
3. Hydraulic (tractors)
4. Solenoid / Electronic (modern SUVs)
5. Viscous coupling (auto, AWD)

### ­¤öæ Common KTU 2024 Questions

- *"Define differential lock and explain its working"* (3 or 7 marks)
- *"Compare open, LSD, and locked differential"* (7 marks table)
- *"Calculate tractive force with and without diff lock"* (7 marks numerical)
- *"Explain pneumatic / electronic actuation"* (7 marks descriptive)

### ­¤öæ Examiner Pet Peeves (Lose Marks Here)

- ŌØī Forgetting to multiply by 2 in open-diff force calculation
- ŌØī Writing "diff lock is used in bad roads" (too vague)
- ŌØī Drawing a complex mechanical diagram in text when a block diagram suffices
- ŌØī Failing to mention the cornering trade-off
- ŌØī Mixing up "limited-slip" and "fully locked" ŌĆö they are NOT the same

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Housing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Housing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Differential Housing** (also termed the **Differential Carrier Housing** or **Differential Case**) is the rigid, sealed structural enclosure that houses the entire differential gear assembly ŌĆö comprising the **ring gear (crown wheel)**, **two side (sun) gears**, **two pinion (planet) gears**, the **cross pin (spider)** and the **thrust washers**. It is the central load-bearing structural member of the rear (or front, in FWD/AWD) axle assembly, and it transmits the torque received from the **drive pinion** through the ring gear to the **half-shafts** while simultaneously permitting the speed differentiation between the two driven wheels during cornering.

In KTU 2024 syllabus parlance, the differential housing is classified under the broader category of **"Final Drive & Differential Assemblies"** and must be understood as a **rotating structural component** subjected to *combined bending, torsional, and bearing-reaction loads*, unlike a non-rotating structural frame member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCMUT302 ŌĆō Module 2):**
> The differential housing is the **structural backbone** of the differential unit. It must:
> 1. Carry the ring gear mesh loads without distortion (to maintain gear tooth contact pattern).
> 2. Provide bearing seats for the carrier bearings and the pinion head bearing.
> 3. Act as an **oil reservoir** for splash lubrication of the gears.
> 4. Resist road shock loads transmitted via the half-shafts.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the differential housing as the **"hard protective skull of the differential brain"** ­¤”┤. Just as the human skull:
- **Encloses and protects** delicate machinery inside (the brain = gear set),
- **Provides mounting points** for muscles and sensory organs (the bearings and mounts),
- **Distributes impact forces** around a tough outer shell rather than letting them crush the internals,

ŌĆ”in the same way, the differential housing is a **cast or forged metallic shell** that wraps around the differential gears, holds them in precise mesh, and channels the enormous torque and road-shock forces safely into the axle housing and the vehicle chassis.

A second analogy: Think of it as the **"metal strongbox"** in a bank vault ŌĆö it is stationary in shape (does not deform) but constantly rotating as a unit (with the ring gear), holding valuables (the gears) in perfect alignment.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Key Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Material ŌĆö Most Common | **Nodular (Ductile) Cast Iron, Grade FCD450 / SG Iron 60-40-18** | ŌĆö |
| Material ŌĆö Lightweight | **Aluminium Alloy A356-T6 (gravity die-cast)** | ŌĆö |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (SG Iron) | **414 ŌĆō 552 MPa (60ŌĆō80 ksi)** | MPa |
| Yield Strength (SG Iron) | **276 ŌĆō 331 MPa** | MPa |
| Modulus of Elasticity (E) | **165 ŌĆō 170 GPa (Cast Iron)**; **70 GPa (Al)** | GPa |
| Density (Cast Iron) | **7150 kg/m┬│** | kg/m┬│ |
| Typical Oil Capacity | **1.0 ŌĆō 1.8 litres** | L |
| Operating Temperature | **80 ┬░C ŌĆō 120 ┬░C** | ┬░C |
| Ring Gear Bolts (Standard) | **M10 ├Ś 1.5, Grade 10.9, torque 95ŌĆō110 Nm** | ŌĆö |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** IS 1865 (India) and ASTM A536 (USA) govern the grade and quality of ductile iron used in differential housings. KTU examiners often expect students to know **why ductile iron is preferred over grey cast iron** ŌĆö the answer lies in its superior impact strength (Ōēź 18 J at room temperature) and fatigue life.

## 1.4 Primary Functions of the Differential Housing ŌĆö A Layered Overview

1. **Mechanical / Structural Function**
   - Acts as the rotating carrier for the ring gear and the entire differential gear train.
   - Transfers ring-gear mesh thrust into the carrier bearings and then to the axle housing.

2. **Geometric / Kinematic Function**
   - Maintains exact centre-distance between the **ring-gear axis** and the **cross-pin axis** of the pinion gears. Any distortion here causes **gear whine, uneven wear, and premature failure**.

3. **Lubrication Function**
   - Acts as a **sump/reservoir** for hypoid gear oil (API GL-5, 80W-90 / 75W-140).
   - Splash lubrication occurs as the pinion gears scoop oil and distribute it over the ring-gear teeth.

4. **Sealing Function**
   - Houses the **pinion seal**, **carrier bearing adjusters**, and provides threaded bosses for the **breather plug** to relieve internal pressure during heating.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Differential Housings

KTU 2024 module expectations require that students can distinguish between the two principal architectural types of differential housings.

### 2.1.1 Integral (Banjo / Tubular) Type
- The differential housing is **integrally cast** as part of the **axle housing tube**.
- Historically common in **solid front axles** of off-road and light commercial vehicles (e.g., Mahindra Bolero, Tata Sumo).
- **Advantages:** High rigidity, simple manufacturing, excellent ground clearance.
- **Disadvantages:** Heavier, non-serviceable differential bearings without axle removal.

### 2.1.2 Separate Carrier (Removable) Type
- The differential housing is a **stand-alone assembly** that is bolted into the axle housing as a unit.
- Dominant in modern **independent rear suspension (IRS)** passenger cars (e.g., Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta).
- **Advantages:** Easy serviceability, lighter axle housing, allows different gear ratios across models sharing the same axle.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires precise alignment shimming at assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Point:** When asked *"Which type allows adjustment of ring-gear backlash without removing the axle?"* ŌĆö the answer is the **Separate Carrier Type**, because it has externally accessible bearing cap bolts and adjuster rings (or shims).

## 2.2 Constructional Anatomy of a Typical Differential Housing

The housing is a **two-piece** construction in most modern applications:

### (a) Main Differential Case (the larger half)
- Carries the internal cavity for the side gears and pinion gears.
- Integrally carries the **ring gear mounting flange**.
- Has two **bearing journals** machined on either side for the **tapered-roller carrier bearings**.
- Includes **internal bosses** for the cross-pin (spider) which is often press-fitted and **roll-pinned** or **bolted**.

### (b) Bearing Caps (two, one per side)
- Bolted to the axle housing tube (integral type) or to the carrier housing (separate type).
- Contain the outer races of the tapered-roller carrier bearings.
- Often incorporate **adjustment shims** or **spinning adjusters** for setting **bearing preload** and **ring-gear backlash**.

### (c) Ring Gear Attachment
- The **ring gear is bolted** (not pressed) to the differential case flange using **8 to 12 high-strength bolts**.
- This allows the ring gear to be replaced without scrapping the housing ŌĆö a key serviceability feature.

### (d) Cross-Pin (Spider)
- A single **hardened steel shaft (typically EN8 / 40Cr)** passes through the case and carries the two pinion gears.
- In heavy-duty applications, a **bolt-and-nut design** replaces the press-fit cross-pin to enable removal.

## 2.3 Material Selection ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Ductile Iron

| Property | Grey Cast Iron | Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron | Aluminium A356 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | 150ŌĆō400 MPa | **414ŌĆō552 MPa** | 230ŌĆō280 MPa |
| Elongation | < 1% | **5ŌĆō18%** | 3ŌĆō6% |
| Impact Strength | 1ŌĆō5 J | **12ŌĆō22 J** | 4ŌĆō6 J |
| Fatigue Limit | 30% of UTS | **40% of UTS** | 30% of UTS |
| Density | 7.2 g/cc | 7.1 g/cc | **2.68 g/cc** |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | High |
| NVH Performance | **Excellent (damping)** | Good | Good |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not steel forging?** Forging a complex hollow shape with thin walls and bearing seats is technically possible but **3ŌĆō5├Ś more expensive** than casting. Ductile iron offers the best compromise of strength, castability, machinability, and NVH damping.

## 2.4 Load Path Through the Differential Housing

Understanding the **load path** is critical for KTU exam answers on housing design:

$$
\text{Drive Pinion Teeth} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ring Gear} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Differential Case} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Carrier Bearings} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Bearing Caps} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Axle Housing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Chassis/Body}
$$

The **most critical design region** of the housing is the **rib-web transition zone** between the ring-gear flange and the bearing journals, where the bending moment from the ring-gear mesh load reaches its maximum.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important high-yield reference for KTU ESE questions on Differential Housing.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bending Stress in Housing Wall | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | MPa | Check rib thickness |
| 2 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{b \cdot h^2}{6}$ | mm┬│ | Housing cross-section design |
| 3 | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}$ | MPa | Cross-pin shear check |
| 4 | Tangential Ring-Gear Force | $F_t = \dfrac{2 \cdot T}{d_{ring}}$ | N | Input for housing bending |
| 5 | Separating (Radial) Force | $F_r = F_t \cdot \tan(\phi_n) \cdot \cos(\beta)$ | N | Hypoid offset loading |
| 6 | Thrust (Axial) Force on Housing | $F_a = F_t \cdot \tan(\beta)$ | N | Bearing reaction |
| 7 | Bearing Reaction at Carrier | $R_c = \dfrac{F_t \cdot r_{ring}}{L_{bearing}}$ | N | Bearing life sizing |
| 8 | Deflection of Housing Wall | $\delta = \dfrac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | mm | NVH/mesh integrity |
| 9 | Critical Speed of Rotating Housing | $N_{cr} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | Avoid resonance with road input |
| 10 | LŌéüŌéĆ Bearing Life | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p \;\text{million rev}$ | ŌĆö | Carrier bearing selection |
| 11 | Heat Dissipation from Oil | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{oil} - T_{air})$ | W | Thermal management |
| 12 | Oil Sump Volume (approx.) | $V_{oil} = 0.25 \cdot \pi \cdot D_{case}^2 \cdot H_{case}$ | mm┬│ | Splash lubrication adequacy |

**Where:**

- $M$ = Bending moment (N┬Ęmm)
- $c$ = Distance from neutral axis to outer fibre (mm)
- $I$ = Second moment of area (mmŌü┤)
- $T$ = Torque transmitted (N┬Ęmm)
- $d$ = Shaft diameter (mm)
- $d_{ring}$ = Pitch diameter of ring gear (mm)
- $\phi_n$ = Normal pressure angle (typically 20┬░ŌĆō25┬░)
- $\beta$ = Spiral/helix angle of hypoid gear (typically 45┬░ŌĆō50┬░)
- $p$ = 3 for ball bearings, 10/3 for roller bearings
- $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating of bearing (N)
- $P$ = Equivalent dynamic bearing load (N)
- $h$ = Convective heat-transfer coefficient (W/m┬▓┬ĘK)
- $A$ = External surface area of housing (m┬▓)
- $D_{case}, H_{case}$ = Internal diameter and height of the housing cavity (mm)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NVH Tuning:** Engineers use **FEA (Finite Element Analysis)** on the differential housing to predict natural frequencies and ensure the **first mode Ōēź 800 Hz** so it does not couple with road excitation (typically 30ŌĆō250 Hz) and powertrain NVH (typically 200ŌĆō1500 Hz).
- **Lightweighting:** Modern EV differentials (Tesla, Lucid) use **aluminium A356-T6** housings to reduce unsprung mass by 30ŌĆō40%, directly improving ride, handling, and range.
- **Manufacturing:** Most ductile-iron housings are produced by **sand casting** (low volume) or **shell-mould casting** (high volume), then **CNC-machined** at the bearing journals and ring-gear flange to within ┬▒0.025 mm.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö Bending Stress in the Differential Housing Wall (Rib Region)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider the differential housing in the **vertical plane** containing the ring-gear axis. The ring gear exerts a tangential force $F_t$ on the drive pinion, and by reaction, the pinion pushes the ring gear (and hence the housing) with an equal and opposite tangential force. This force, applied at the **mean radius** $r_{ring}$ of the ring gear, creates a **bending moment** $M$ in the housing wall that connects the ring-gear flange to the bearing journal.

**Given:**
- Torque transmitted: $T = 1800$ N┬Ęm
- Pitch diameter of ring gear: $d_{ring} = 220$ mm  Ō¤╣  $r_{ring} = 110$ mm
- Distance from ring-gear centre to bearing centre: $L = 95$ mm
- Wall cross-section: rectangular, width $b = 40$ mm, height $h = 25$ mm

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the tangential ring-gear force.**

The ring gear transmits torque $T$ at its mean radius $r_{ring}$. Equating torque to force ├Ś radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= F_t \cdot r_{ring} \\[4pt]
F_t &= \frac{T}{r_{ring}} = \frac{1800 \times 10^3 \, \text{N┬Ęmm}}{110 \, \text{mm}} \\[4pt]
F_t &= 16\,363.64 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the bending moment at the rib cross-section.**

The force $F_t$ acts at the ring-gear radius, and the carrier bearing reaction $R_c$ acts at distance $L$ from the ring-gear centre. Taking moments about the bearing centre:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= F_t \cdot r_{ring} - R_c \cdot 0 = F_t \cdot (r_{ring}) \quad \text{(simplified cantilever model)}\\[4pt]
\text{Equivalently, using the moment arm }L\text{ from gear centre to bearing:} \quad M &= F_t \cdot L \\[4pt]
M &= 16\,363.64 \times 95 = 1\,554\,545.45 \; \text{N┬Ęmm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the second moment of area $I$ of the rectangular rib.**

For a rectangular section of width $b$ and height $h$ about the neutral axis passing through its centroid (bending in the direction of $h$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{b \cdot h^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times (25)^3}{12} \\[4pt]
I &= \frac{40 \times 15\,625}{12} = \frac{625\,000}{12} \\[4pt]
I &= 52\,083.33 \; \text{mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the distance $c$ from the neutral axis to the outermost fibre.**

$$
c = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the flexure formula to get bending stress.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{1\,554\,545.45 \times 12.5}{52\,083.33} \\[4pt]
\sigma_b &= \frac{19\,431\,818.13}{52\,083.33} \\[4pt]
\sigma_b &= 373.10 \; \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compare with allowable stress of ductile iron.**

For SG Iron FCD450 with a **factor of safety $N = 2$** and ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts} = 450$ MPa:

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{N} = \frac{450}{2} = 225 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Verdict and design correction.**

Since $\sigma_b = 373.10$ MPa $> \sigma_{allow} = 225$ MPa, the section is **under-designed**.

**Redesign:** Increase the height $h$ such that:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{6 \cdot M}{b \cdot h^2} \le \sigma_{allow}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^2 &\ge \frac{6 \cdot M}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{6 \times 1\,554\,545.45}{40 \times 225} \\[4pt]
h^2 &\ge \frac{9\,327\,272.7}{9000} = 1036.36 \\[4pt]
h &\ge 32.19 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adopt $h = 35$ mm** as a standard design value.

### 3.1.3 Result Summary

> **Conclusion:** The original 25 mm wall height produces $\sigma_b = 373.1$ MPa ŌĆö **unsafe**. The redesigned rib height of 35 mm produces $\sigma_b \approx 238$ MPa with the safety factor, which is acceptable. Engineers would also add **gussets or ribs** to further stiffen the housing and reduce deflection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö Tangential, Radial and Axial Components of Ring-Gear Mesh Force (Hypoid Gear)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A **hypoid** ring-and-pinion (used in nearly all RWD and 4WD vehicles) has:
- Tangential force at mean radius: $F_t$ (from Derivation I)
- Normal pressure angle: $\phi_n = 22.5┬░$
- Helix (spiral) angle: $\beta = 45┬░$

Determine the radial and thrust (axial) components acting on the differential housing.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose the mesh force into normal-force components.**

For a hypoid gear, the three orthogonal components acting on the ring-gear centre are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{2T}{d_{ring}} \quad \text{(tangential ŌĆö already computed)} \\[4pt]
F_r &= \frac{F_t \cdot \tan(\phi_n)}{\cos(\beta)} \quad \text{(radial / separating)} \\[4pt]
F_a &= F_t \cdot \tan(\beta) \quad \text{(axial / thrust)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute radial force $F_r$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_r &= \frac{16\,363.64 \times \tan(22.5┬░)}{\cos(45┬░)} \\[4pt]
F_r &= \frac{16\,363.64 \times 0.4142}{0.7071} \\[4pt]
F_r &= \frac{6778.41}{0.7071} = 9586.66 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute axial (thrust) force $F_a$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_a &= 16\,363.64 \times \tan(45┬░) \\[4pt]
F_a &= 16\,363.64 \times 1.0 = 16\,363.64 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Resultant mesh force on the housing.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{resultant} &= \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2 + F_a^2} \\[4pt]
F_{resultant} &= \sqrt{(16\,363.64)^2 + (9586.66)^2 + (16\,363.64)^2} \\[4pt]
F_{resultant} &= \sqrt{2.677 \times 10^8 + 9.190 \times 10^7 + 2.677 \times 10^8} \\[4pt]
F_{resultant} &= \sqrt{6.273 \times 10^8} = 25\,046 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Engineering Interpretation

> The **axial (thrust) force equals the tangential force** in magnitude for a 45┬░ helix hypoid. This enormous axial load is why the drive pinion is mounted on **two opposed tapered-roller bearings** that react both inward ŌĆö the housing must be rigid enough not to deflect under this load, otherwise **pinion deflection** causes uneven tooth contact and whine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö LŌéüŌéĆ Bearing Life of the Differential Carrier Bearing

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A tapered-roller carrier bearing has:
- Dynamic load rating: $C = 88\,000$ N
- Equivalent dynamic load: $P = 25\,000$ N
- Roller bearing exponent: $p = 10/3$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{p} = \left(\frac{88\,000}{25\,000}\right)^{10/3} \\[4pt]
L_{10} &= (3.52)^{3.333} \\[4pt]
\ln(L_{10}) &= 3.333 \times \ln(3.52) = 3.333 \times 1.2583 = 4.194 \\[4pt]
L_{10} &= e^{4.194} = 66.36 \;\text{million revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to operating hours.**

If the differential input speed averages $N = 1500$ rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Life (hours)} &= \frac{66.36 \times 10^6}{1500 \times 60} = \frac{66.36 \times 10^6}{90\,000} \\[4pt]
&= 737.3 \; \text{hours of pure rotation}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an average vehicle speed of 60 km/h, with differential rotating at engine-to-wheel ratio, the bearing life translates to **~200,000 km of vehicle service life** ŌĆö meeting KTU design expectations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Design Trade-offs of Differential Housing

| Design Aspect | Cast Iron (SG) | Aluminium (A356) | Steel Forged | Composite (PA66+GF) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight | Heavy | **Lightest metal** | Heavy | **Lightest overall** |
| Strength | **High** | Moderate | **Very High** | Moderate |
| NVH Damping | **Best** | Good | Poor | Good |
| Cost | Moderate | High | **Very High** | Moderate |
| Recyclability | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | Limited |
| Heat Dissipation | Good | **Best** | Good | Poor |
| Typical Use | Trucks, SUVs | EVs, premium cars | Heavy commercial | Prototype EVs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture of the Differential Housing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drive Pinion Shaft] -->|Torque Input| B[Pinion Teeth Engagement]
    B -->|Tangential + Radial + Thrust| C[Ring Gear / Crown Wheel]
    C -->|Bolt Connection M10 x 12| D[Differential Case Main Half]
    D -->|Houses| E[Side Gears L and R]
    D -->|Houses| F[Pinion Gears on Cross Pin]
    D -->|Houses| G[Cross Pin / Spider]
    D -->|Machined Journal| H[Left Tapered Roller Carrier Bearing]
    D -->|Machined Journal| I[Right Tapered Roller Carrier Bearing]
    H --> J[Left Bearing Cap with Shims]
    I --> K[Right Bearing Cap with Shims]
    J --> L[Axle Housing Tube]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Vehicle Chassis via Mounts]
    E --> N[Left Half Shaft CV Joint]
    E --> O[Right Half Shaft CV Joint]
    P[Hypoid Gear Oil GL5] -.Splash Lubrication.-> D
    D -->|Internal Cavity| P
    Q[Breather Plug] -.Pressure Relief.-> D
    R[Pinion Seal] -.Oil Retention.-> A
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Tree Diagram ŌĆö Load Path Through the Differential Housing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Engine Torque] --> L2[Transmission Output Shaft]
    L2 --> L3[Drive Pinion]
    L3 -->|Mesh Force Ft, Fr, Fa| L4[Ring Gear on Differential Case]
    L4 -->|Reaction Moment| L5[Differential Case Hub / Rib Web]
    L5 -->|Bending and Torsion| L6[Carrier Bearing Journals]
    L6 -->|Radial and Axial Reaction| L7[Carrier Tapered Roller Bearings]
    L7 --> L8[Bearing Caps]
    L8 --> L9[Axle Housing Tube]
    L9 --> L10[Suspension Mounts and Chassis]
    L4 -.Half of Torque.-> L11[Left Side Gear]
    L4 -.Half of Torque.-> L12[Right Side Gear]
    L11 --> L13[Left Half Shaft]
    L12 --> L14[Right Half Shaft]
    L13 --> L15[Left Wheel]
    L14 --> L16[Right Wheel]
    style L4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style L5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic ŌĆö Two-Piece Construction of the Differential Housing

```mermaid

        direction TB

            M5[Right Bearing Journal]
        end
        subgraph Right_Cover [Right Half Cover / Cap]

        end

        M4 -->|Press Fit| CRB[Carrier Bearing Tapered Roller]
```
