<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, PCMUT302]**

Define a vacuum brake. Why is a dedicated vacuum pump used in diesel engines instead of relying on intake manifold vacuum?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **vacuum brake** is a power-assisted braking system that uses the pressure differential between atmospheric pressure and a vacuum source (engine manifold or dedicated pump) to multiply the driver's pedal effort.

A dedicated vacuum pump is necessary in diesel engines because:
1. **Diesel engines do not throttle the intake air** ŌĆö they operate with the throttle wide open; hence, manifold vacuum is very low (Ōēł 15ŌĆō20 kPa, insufficient for braking).
2. **Turbocharged petrol engines** also exhibit poor manifold vacuum under boost conditions.
3. A dedicated vane-type or electric pump delivers a **stable 60ŌĆō80 kPa** vacuum regardless of engine speed or load, ensuring consistent brake feel. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, PCMUT302]**

List the main components of a vacuum booster and state the function of the **check valve**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Main components of a vacuum booster:
1. Front and rear chambers separated by a diaphragm
2. Poppet valve with vacuum and atmospheric ports
3. Reaction disc for pedal "feel"
4. Output pushrod connected to master cylinder
5. **Check valve** and vacuum hose

**Function of the check valve (1 mark):** It is a *one-way valve* that allows vacuum to flow from the manifold into the booster but prevents its backflow when the engine is switched off or manifold vacuum is lost. This **traps vacuum** in the front chamber, enabling **2ŌĆō3 power-assisted stops even with the engine off** ŌĆö a critical safety feature.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024, PCMUT302]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **vacuum-assisted hydraulic brake system**. Describe the role of each component and the three operating states (rest, boost, hold). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vacuum booster has an effective diaphragm diameter of **180 mm** and is coupled to a master cylinder of bore **20 mm**. The manifold vacuum provides a pressure differential of **60 kPa**. The pedal mechanical advantage is **4.5** and the booster efficiency is **0.88**. If the driver applies **180 N** at the pedal, calculate: (i) the diaphragm assist force, (ii) the master cylinder pressure developed, and (iii) the clamping force at a wheel cylinder of bore **32 mm** (neglecting hydraulic-line losses). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

The vacuum-assisted hydraulic brake system combines a **vacuum booster (servo unit)** with a conventional hydraulic master cylinder. The block diagram is:

```
[Pedal] ŌåÆ [Booster - amplifies force via P_atm vs P_vacuum] ŌåÆ [Master Cylinder] ŌåÆ [Hydraulic Lines] ŌåÆ [Wheel Cylinders/Calipers]
```

**Three Operating States:**

| State | Vacuum Port | Atmospheric Port | Diaphragm Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rest (Pedal Released)** | Open | Closed | Both chambers at vacuum; diaphragm at rest; no braking |
| **Boost (Pedal Pressed)** | Closed | Open | Rear chamber at atmosphere, front at vacuum; diaphragm moves forward; maximum braking force |
| **Hold (Pedal Held Steady)** | Closed | Closed | Pressure locked between chambers; braking force maintained constant |

**Roles of components:**
- **Booster:** Force amplification using $\Delta P$
- **Master cylinder:** Converts mechanical force into hydraulic pressure
- **Wheel cylinder:** Converts hydraulic pressure back into mechanical clamping force
- **Check valve:** Maintains vacuum reserve
- **Reaction disc:** Provides driver "feel"

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d_d = 180$ mm $= 0.180$ m
- $d_{mc} = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- $\Delta P = 60{,}000$ Pa
- $MA = 4.5$
- $\eta_b = 0.88$
- $F_{pedal} = 180$ N
- $d_{wc} = 32$ mm $= 0.032$ m

**Step (i) ŌĆö Diaphragm assist force:**

$$
A_d = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.180)^2}{4} = 0.02545 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{diaphragm} = \Delta P \cdot A_d = 60{,}000 \cdot 0.02545 = 1526.8 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (ii) ŌĆö Master cylinder pressure:**

$$
A_{mc} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{total} = 0.88 \cdot \left( 180 \cdot 4.5 + 1526.8 \right) = 0.88 \cdot \left( 810 + 1526.8 \right) = 0.88 \cdot 2336.8 = 2056.4 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P_{mc} = \frac{F_{total}}{A_{mc}} = \frac{2056.4}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.546 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step (iii) ŌĆö Wheel cylinder clamping force:**

$$
A_{wc} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.032)^2}{4} = 8.0425 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{wheel} = P_{mc} \cdot A_{wc} = 6.546 \times 10^{6} \cdot 8.0425 \times 10^{-4} = 5266 \text{ N}
$$

**Final answers:**
- (i) $F_{diaphragm} \approx 1527$ N
- (ii) $P_{mc} \approx 6.55$ MPa
- (iii) $F_{wheel} \approx 5266$ N

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare vacuum-assisted hydraulic brakes with **air (pneumatic) brakes** used in heavy commercial vehicles. Discuss advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the **check valve** and **reaction disc** are critical components in a vacuum booster. What happens during a brake pedal application if either component fails? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Vacuum-Assisted Hydraulic | Air (Pneumatic) Brake |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Engine manifold vacuum + driver | Compressed air reservoir |
| **Working pressure** | 6ŌĆō10 MPa (hydraulic) | 0.6ŌĆō0.8 MPa (pneumatic) |
| **Pedal effort** | Very low (Ōēł 100ŌĆō200 N) | Low (governed by foot valve) |
| **Response time** | Fast (50ŌĆō100 ms) | Slow (200ŌĆō500 ms) due to air compressibility |
| **Failure mode** | Reverts to manual after 2ŌĆō3 stops | Spring-applied, air-released ŌĆö fail-safe |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High (compressor, dryer, multi-reservoir system) |
| **Application** | Passenger cars, LCVs | Buses, trucks, trailers (HCV) |
| **Maintenance** | Simple ŌĆö brake fluid, hoses | Complex ŌĆö air dryer, drain valves |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Vacuum-assisted hydraulic brakes are preferred for **light vehicles** due to fast response and compact packaging, while pneumatic brakes dominate **heavy vehicles** because of fail-safe spring brakes and unlimited force capacity from a reservoir.

**(b) Component Significance (7 marks):**

**Check Valve (3.5 marks):**
- A one-way valve between manifold and booster front chamber.
- **Critical because** it traps vacuum when the engine is off, providing **2ŌĆō3 reserve power-assisted stops** ŌĆö essential for safety in case of engine stalling during braking.
- **Failure effect:** If the check valve leaks, vacuum is lost immediately when the engine is off; the driver must apply extremely high pedal force. The brake feels "hard" and stopping distance increases dramatically.

**Reaction Disc (3.5 marks):**
- A metal disc with rubberised faces that transmits the booster's assist force *back* to the driver's foot through the pedal lever.
- **Critical because** it provides **progressive pedal feel** ŌĆö the harder you push, the more force you feel at the pedal, allowing the driver to modulate braking precisely.
- **Failure effect:** If the disc is damaged or missing, the pedal feels "mushy" or "over-assisted." The driver cannot gauge braking intensity, leading to wheel lock-up or skidding.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Vacuum Brake Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor $\eta_b$** ŌĆö many students write $F_{total} = F_{pedal} \cdot MA + F_{diaphragm}$ without the efficiency term, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** ŌĆö mixing mm with m┬▓. Always convert diameters to **metres** before computing areas.
> 3. **Confusing absolute and gauge pressures** ŌĆö $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ is the *differential*, not absolute vacuum pressure.
> 4. **Omitting the role of the check valve** ŌĆö it is a favourite 1ŌĆō2 mark sub-question; never ignore it.
> 5. **Writing "vacuum sucks the brake"** ŌĆö technically incorrect. Vacuum brakes *use atmospheric pressure* to push; the vacuum side merely provides a low-pressure reference.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Vacuum brake** = power-assisted brake using $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$
- [ ] Standard $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa; usable manifold vacuum $\approx 50$ŌĆō$70$ kPa
- [ ] **Booster ratio** = $\left( d_{d} / d_{mc} \right)^{2}$, typically **50ŌĆō90**
- [ ] **Check valve** traps vacuum ŌåÆ enables 2ŌĆō3 power stops after engine off
- [ ] **Reaction disc** gives pedal "feel" and prevents over-assist
- [ ] **Three operating states:** Rest, Boost, Hold
- [ ] **Diesel / turbo engines** need a dedicated vacuum pump
- [ ] **Master cylinder pressure equation:** $P_{mc} = \eta_{b} \left( \dfrac{F_{pedal} \cdot MA}{A_{mc}} + \Delta P \cdot K_{booster} \right)$
- [ ] **Wheel clamping force:** $F_{wheel} = P_{mc} \cdot A_{wc} \cdot \eta_{hydraulic}$
- [ ] Vacuum brakes are for **light vehicles**; pneumatic brakes are for **heavy vehicles**
- [ ] Typical **booster efficiency** = **0.85ŌĆō0.95**
- [ ] Typical **pedal mechanical advantage** = **4ŌĆō6**
- [ ] Diagnostic check: booster should hold vacuum for **Ōēź 15 s** after engine off
- [ ] Always quote **SI units** (Pa, N, m) in derivations to score full marks in KTU ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servo Brakes - Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOSTIVE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Servo Brakes - Components # Servo Brakes ŌĆö Components ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Servo Brake (Brake Servo / Brake Booster):** A servo brake is a power-assisted braking device that multiplies the driver's foot pedal effort by using an external energy source ŌĆö typically engine manifold **vacuum**, **hydraulic pressure**, or **compressed air** ŌĆö so that a modest pedal input can generate a large clamping force at the wheel brake. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PCMUT302 ŌĆö Automotive Systems**, the *servo* is treated as an **energy-amplifying intermediary** fitted **between the brake pedal and the master cylinder**. It does not generate braking force on its own; it merely converts a low-energy mechanical input into a high-energy mechanical output at the master cylinder. **Standard reference metrics (KTU Board expected values):** - **Pedal leverage ratio (mechanical)**: ~ **5 : 1** - **Vacuum servo pressure differential**: **╬öP Ōēł 0.6 to 0.8 bar** (engine manifold vacuum) - **Boost ratio (typical passenger car)**: **3 : 1 to 5 : 1** - **Hydraulic servo operating pressure**: **40 to 80 bar** - **Air servo reservoir pressure**: **8 to 10 bar** ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy ŌĆö "Helping Hand on the Brake Pedal":** > Imagine you are trying to push open a very heavy door. You could push it yourself, but it's exhausting. Now imagine a friend stands behind the door, and every time you push a little, your friend pushes **ten times harder** in the same direction. You still *control* the door, but with a fraction of the effort. That friend is the **servo brake**. The door is the **master cylinder piston**. The energy your friend uses does **not** come from your muscles ŌĆö it comes from the engine (vacuum, hydraulic pump, or air compressor). **Why the servo sits BEFORE the master cylinder (not after):** - It multiplies **pedal force**, not pedal travel. - The driver's foot remains the *command* input; the servo is the *force multiplier*. - This is why a small woman can safely brake a 2-tonne SUV ŌĆö the **servo** does the heavy work, not the driver. ## 1.3 Classification of Servo Brakes | Type | Energy Source | Typical Application | |------|---------------|---------------------| | **Vacuum Servo** | Engine manifold vacuum | Petrol & diesel passenger cars | | **Hydraulic Servo (Hydra-boost)** | Power-steering pump pressure | Light commercial vehicles, SUVs | | **Air Servo (Air Booster)** | Compressed air from reservoir | Heavy trucks, buses, trailers | | **Electric Vacuum Pump Servo** | Electric pump (for EV / hybrid) | Electric & hybrid vehicles | > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 Module-2 syllabus emphasises **vacuum and hydraulic servos** ŌĆö these two form the bulk of the 14-mark analytical questions. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why a Servo is Needed ŌĆö The Physics When a driver presses the brake pedal, the force must: 1. Overcome return-spring stiffness of the master cylinder. 2. Generate hydraulic pressure $P_{mc}$ in the brake lines. 3. Move friction pads against the disc/drum. 4. Generate the wheel-cylinder clamping force $F_c$. Without a servo, the **entire pedal effort** $F_p$ must come from the driver's leg muscles. The KTU-standard relationship is: $$F_c = P_{mc} \cdot A_{wc} \cdot \eta_{brake}$$ where: - $A_{wc}$ = wheel cylinder area - $\eta_{brake}$ = brake efficiency (friction coefficient ├Ś mechanical efficiency) For a small car: $F_c \approx 4000 \text{ N}$ per wheel. With four wheels and pedal ratio 5, the driver must push **~ 800 N** (Ōēł 80 kg) ŌĆö exhausting in city traffic. A servo reduces this to **~ 150ŌĆō200 N**. ## 2.2 Energy Flow Logic in a Servo The servo operates on a **pressure-differential principle**. The operating sequence is: - **Idle (pedal released):** Both sides of the diaphragm are at **vacuum** (pressure-equalised). No net force ŌåÆ no boost. - **Pedal pressed:** A **valve** closes the vacuum port and opens an **atmospheric port** on one side of the diaphragm. - **Pressure differential** develops across the diaphragm area $A_d$. - The diaphragm is pushed by the **booster force** $F_b = \Delta P \cdot A_d$ ŌĆö and this force adds directly to the master cylinder input. - **Pedal released:** Valve returns to vacuum-only condition; return spring pushes diaphragm back. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 examiner can ask from this topic. Memorise the **symbols** and the **units**. | # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | KTU Usual Unit | |---|----------|----------------|----------------| | 1 | $F_b = \Delta P \cdot A_d$ | Booster force from pressure differential | $\text{N}$ | | 2 | $F_{mc} = F_p \cdot L_p + F_b$ | Total master-cylinder input force | $\text{N}$ | | 3 | $K_b = \dfrac{F_{mc}}{F_p} = 1 + \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot A_d}{F_p}$ | Boost ratio (dimensionless) | ŌĆö | | 4 | $P_{mc} = \dfrac{F_{mc}}{A_{mc}}$ | Master-cylinder line pressure | $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{bar}$ | | 5 | $\tau_{vac} = \dfrac{V_v}{Q_p}$ | Time to build vacuum (volume flow) | $\text{s}$ | | 6 | $F_{c} = \mu \cdot P_{mc} \cdot A_{wc}$ | Wheel-cylinder clamping force | $\text{N}$ | | 7 | $\eta_{servo} = \dfrac{F_b}{F_p + F_{vac-loss}}$ | Servo mechanical efficiency | ŌĆö | | 8 | $P_{boost} = P_{atm} - P_{manifold}$ | Available vacuum pressure | $\text{bar}$ | > [!WARNING] > In KTU answer scripts, **never** confuse **boost ratio** (dimensionless) with **pedal ratio** (mechanical lever ratio). They are *multiplied*, not added. ## 2.4 Why Pressure Differential, Not Just Pressure? Using a pressure differential is **energy-efficient** because: - The vacuum side requires **no continuous energy** to maintain (engine sucks it). - Only the **atmosphere side** is "expensive" ŌĆö and that comes from the free atmosphere. - The diaphragm acts as a **piston of large area** $A_d$ in a cylinder, converting tiny pressure difference into huge force. - This is the same principle as a **hydraulic ram** or a **suction cup**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Driver safety:** Reduces fatigue, improves emergency-stop reaction time by ~30 %. - **Pedal feel uniformity:** Servo is sized so pedal force is consistent across all vehicle weights. - **Failure mode (KTU favourite):** If vacuum fails, the servo becomes a **passive linkage** ŌĆö brakes still work but require ~3├Ś more pedal effort. This is called **"servo failure ŌĆö manual mode"** and is a common 7-mark question. - **EV application:** Electric vacuum pumps replace manifold vacuum in BEVs. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Booster Force from Pressure Differential **Given:** - Diaphragm effective area: $A_d$ - Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$ - Manifold vacuum pressure: $P_{vac} = 0.3 \text{ bar (absolute)}$ - Pedal force: $F_p$ - Pedal lever mechanical advantage: $L_p$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the available pressure differential.** $$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$ $$\Delta P = 1.013 - 0.3 = 0.713 \text{ bar}$$ $$\Delta P = 71300 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Express booster force as a function of diaphragm area.** $$F_b = \Delta P \cdot A_d$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö The diaphragm behaves like a hydraulic piston** (Pascal's principle applied to a low-pressure gas system). Hence force scales linearly with $A_d$: $$F_b = (P_{atm} - P_{vac}) \cdot A_d$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Master cylinder input force is the sum of mechanical pedal force plus booster force:** $$F_{mc} = F_p \cdot L_p + F_b$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Master cylinder line pressure:** $$P_{mc} = \frac{F_{mc}}{A_{mc}} = \frac{F_p \cdot L_p + \Delta P \cdot A_d}{A_{mc}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **master equation** of brake-servo analysis. A 14-mark KTU question often asks you to substitute numerical values and compute $P_{mc}$. ## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Boost Ratio **Step 1 ŌĆö Boost ratio is defined as:** $$K_b = \frac{F_{mc}}{F_p}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substituting $F_{mc}$:** $$K_b = \frac{F_p \cdot L_p + \Delta P \cdot A_d}{F_p}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Separating the terms:** $$K_b = L_p + \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_d}{F_p}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows the boost ratio is **inversely proportional to pedal force** ŌĆö a hallmark "as pedal force decreases, boost effect increases" property, mimicking muscle-boosting in real driving. **Step 4 ŌĆö Numerical example (typical KTU value):** $$K_b = 5 + \frac{71300 \cdot 0.015}{200} = 5 + 5.35 = 10.35$$ So a **200 N pedal push** delivers **~ 2070 N** to the master cylinder ŌĆö over **10├Ś amplification**. ## 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö Failure-Mode (Vacuum Lost) Analysis **Scenario:** Engine stalled, vacuum reservoir empty ŌåÆ $P_{vac} = P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar. $$\Delta P = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_b = 0$$ $$F_{mc} = F_p \cdot L_p \quad \text{(manual mode only)}$$ $$P_{mc, \text{fail}} = \frac{F_p \cdot L_p}{A_{mc}}$$ > [!WARNING] > A KTU 14-mark question may ask: *"Compare stopping performance with and without servo assist"* ŌĆö the answer must explicitly state that **pedal travel increases** and **line pressure drops** in fail mode. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A vacuum servo has diaphragm area $A_d = 150 \text{ cm}^2$ and operates between $P_{atm} = 1.0$ bar and $P_{vac} = 0.3$ bar. Pedal lever ratio $L_p = 4$. Pedal force applied $F_p = 150$ N. Master cylinder bore area $A_{mc} = 2.0 \text{ cm}^2$. Find: (a) Booster force (b) Total master-cylinder input force (c) Resulting hydraulic line pressure (d) Boost ratio **Solution:** **Step (a):** $$A_d = 150 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.015 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\Delta P = (1.0 - 0.3) \times 10^5 = 70000 \text{ Pa}$$ $$F_b = 70000 \times 0.015 = 1050 \text{ N}$$ **Step (b):** $$F_{mc} = F_p \cdot L_p + F_b = 150 \times 4 + 1050 = 600 + 1050 = 1650 \text{ N}$$ **Step (c):** $$A_{mc} = 2.0 \text{ cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$P_{mc} = \frac{1650}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.25 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 82.5 \text{ bar}$$ **Step (d):** $$K_b = \frac{1650}{150} = 11.0$$ **Interpretation:** The driver applied only 150 N (Ōēł 15 kg) but the master cylinder "sees" 1650 N (Ōēł 165 kg). This is the **servo effect**. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vacuum Servo ŌĆö Component Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR Pedal[Brake Pedal] --> PushRod[Pedal Push Rod] PushRod --> CtrlValve[Control Valve
Poppet Type] CtrlValve --> VacPort[Vacuum Port
From Manifold] CtrlPortAtm[Atmospheric Port] --> CtrlValve CtrlValve --> DiaphFront[Front Chamber] CtrlValve --> DiaphRear[Rear Chamber] DiaphFront --> Diaphragm[Diaphragm
D = 200 mm] Diaphragm --> DiaphRear Diaphragm --> BoosterRod[Booster Output Rod] BoosterRod --> McPiston[Master Cylinder Piston] McPiston --> McSpring[Return Spring] McPiston --> HydLine[Hydraulic Line to Wheels] ReturnSpring[Diaphragm Return Spring] --> Diaphragm Filter[Atmospheric Filter] --> CtrlPortAtm ``` ## 4.2 Hydraulic Servo (Hydra-Boost) ŌĆö Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB PS_Pump[Power Steering Pump] --> Acc[Accumulator] Acc --> HydServoIn[Hydraulic Supply to Servo] HydServoIn --> ServoValve[Spool Valve] Pedal[Brake Pedal] --> MechLink[Mechanical Linkage] MechLink --> ServoValve ServoValve --> BoostPiston[Boost Piston] BoostPiston --> McPiston[Master Cylinder Piston] McPiston --> HydBrakeLine[Brake Hydraulic Line] Acc -.Relief.-> Tank[Reservoir] PS_Pump -.Relief.-> Tank ``` ## 4.3 Functional State Diagram ŌĆö Servo Operation ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Braking: Pedal Pressed
Atmosphere Port Opens Braking --> BoostActive: Vacuum and Atmosphere Separated BoostActive --> Braking: Diaphragm Pushes Master Cylinder Braking --> Release: Pedal Released Release --> VacuumEqualize: Both Chambers Return to Vacuum VacuumEqualize --> Idle: Diaphragm Returned by Spring BoostActive --> FailMode: Vacuum Lost FailMode --> ManualBrake: Pedal Force Only ManualBrake --> Braking: Driver Pushes Harder ``` ## 4.4 Component Interaction Matrix | Component | Connected To | Function | Failure Consequence | |-----------|-------------|----------|---------------------| | **Brake Pedal** | Push rod | Driver input | No braking command | | **Pedal Push Rod** | Control valve | Transmits pedal motion | No valve actuation | | **Control Valve** | Vacuum port, atmospheric port, both chambers | Directs pressure differential | No boost | | **Vacuum Port** | Engine manifold / vacuum pump | Source of low pressure | Boost disabled | | **Atmospheric Port** | Air filter ŌåÆ atmosphere | Source of high pressure | No ╬öP | | **Diaphragm** | Both chambers, output rod | Converts ╬öP to force | No booster force | | **Booster Output Rod** | Master cylinder piston | Delivers amplified force | Reduced braking | | **Return Spring** | Diaphragm | Returns servo to idle | Pedal feels "sticky" | | **Reaction Disc** | Pedal rod, output rod | Provides pedal "feel" feedback | Driver loses feel | | **Master Cylinder** | Booster, brake lines | Converts force ŌåÆ hydraulic pressure | Brake failure | # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1. Define a servo brake. State its primary energy source in a conventional passenger car. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 | Module 2]** **Model Answer:** A servo brake is a power-assisted braking device that uses an external energy source to multiply the driver's pedal effort before it reaches the master cylinder. In conventional petrol/diesel passenger cars, the primary energy source is **engine manifold vacuum** (~ 0.6ŌĆō0.8 bar of vacuum differential). The servo amplifies pedal force by a factor of 3 to 5, reducing driver effort and improving safety. ### Question 2. List any four components of a vacuum servo brake. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 | Module 2]** **Model Answer:** 1. **Diaphragm** ŌĆö converts pressure differential to mechanical force. 2. **Control valve (poppet valve)** ŌĆö switches between vacuum and atmosphere. 3. **Vacuum port** ŌĆö connects to engine manifold. 4. **Atmospheric port** ŌĆö admits filtered air during braking. *(Additional acceptable answers: return spring, booster output rod, reaction disc, master cylinder push rod.)* --- ## Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on this topic carry **14 marks** and have **internal choice**. The question paper allows you to choose **either Option A or Option B** from the same module. Both options below are written as full KTU-style 14-mark questions with two sub-parts (7 + 7). --- ### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model | Module 2]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **vacuum servo brake** used in a passenger car. List all major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vacuum servo brake has the following specifications: diaphragm effective diameter $D = 220$ mm, manifold vacuum = 0.3 bar (absolute), atmospheric pressure = 1.0 bar, pedal mechanical ratio $L_p = 4$, master cylinder bore area $A_{mc} = 2.5$ cm┬▓, and pedal effort $F_p = 200$ N. Calculate: (i) Booster force $F_b$ (ii) Master cylinder input force $F_{mc}$ (iii) Hydraulic line pressure $P_{mc}$ (iv) Boost ratio $K_b$ Comment on the change in stopping performance if the vacuum source fails completely. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer ŌĆö A(a): Construction and Working **Construction (block diagram ŌĆö refer to Section 4.1):** A vacuum servo brake consists of: - **Booster shell** (two halves joined at the centre). - **Flexible diaphragm** dividing the shell into a **front chamber** and a **rear chamber**. - **Control valve** (poppet type) operated by the pedal push rod. - **Vacuum port** connected to the engine intake manifold (or vacuum pump). - **Atmospheric port** with a built-in air filter. - **Booster output rod** connected to the master cylinder piston. - **Return spring** that restores the diaphragm to its rest position. - **Reaction disc / mechanism** that feeds booster force back to the pedal for "feel." **Working:** - **Released pedal:** Control valve keeps both front and rear chambers connected to the vacuum port. Both sides of the diaphragm are at equal low pressure ŌåÆ no net force ŌåÆ booster is "neutral." - **Pressed pedal:** The poppet valve closes the vacuum passage to the rear chamber and opens the atmospheric port. The front chamber remains at vacuum while the rear chamber fills with air at atmospheric pressure. - **Pressure differential** $\Delta P$ develops across the diaphragm. The atmospheric side pushes the diaphragm toward the vacuum side, generating the **booster force** $F_b = \Delta P \cdot A_d$. - This force **adds** to the mechanical pedal force and is transmitted through the booster output rod to the **master cylinder piston**, which pressurises the brake fluid. - The **reaction disc** transmits a proportional portion of $F_b$ back to the pedal rod, giving the driver a "feel" of braking effort. - **Released pedal:** Valve returns to vacuum-only condition; return spring pushes diaphragm back; pedal pushes the valve back to idle. #### Model Answer ŌĆö A(b): Numerical Solution **Given:** - $D = 220 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow A_d = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.22)^2 = 0.0380 \text{ m}^2$ - $P_{vac} = 0.3 \text{ bar} = 30000 \text{ Pa}$ - $P_{atm} = 1.0 \text{ bar} = 100000 \text{ Pa}$ - $L_p = 4$ - $A_{mc} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ - $F_p = 200 \text{ N}$ **(i) Booster Force:** $$\Delta P = 100000 - 30000 = 70000 \text{ Pa}$$ $$F_b = \Delta P \cdot A_d = 70000 \times 0.0380 = 2660 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Master Cylinder Input Force:** $$F_{mc} = F_p \cdot L_p + F_b = 200 \times 4 + 2660 = 800 + 2660 = 3460 \text{ N}$$ **(iii) Line Pressure:** $$P_{mc} = \frac{F_{mc}}{A_{mc}} = \frac{3460}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.384 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 138.4 \text{ bar}$$ **(iv) Boost Ratio:** $$K_b = \frac{F_{mc}}{F_p} = \frac{3460}{200} = 17.3$$ **Comment on Vacuum Failure:** If the vacuum source fails (e.g., broken vacuum hose, stalled engine), then $P_{vac} = P_{atm}$, so $\Delta P = 0$ and $F_b = 0$. The master cylinder input force reduces to: $$F_{mc, \text{fail}} = F_p \cdot L_p = 200 \times 4 = 800 \text{ N}$$ The line pressure drops from 138.4 bar to 32 bar ŌĆö a **76 % reduction**. The driver must press **~4├Ś harder** and the pedal travel increases substantially. Brakes still function (failsafe design), but stopping distance increases significantly. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:** > - Forgetting to **convert cm┬▓ to m┬▓** (loses 2 marks in numerical part). > - Using $P_{vac}$ as gauge pressure instead of absolute in $\Delta P$ (loses 1 mark). > - Not stating that **brakes still work** in fail mode ŌĆö examiners want "failsafe" mentioned (loses 1 mark). > - Omitting the **reaction disc** in the construction diagram (loses 1 mark). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model | Module 2]** **(a)** Compare **vacuum servo**, **hydraulic servo (hydra-boost)**, and **air servo (air booster)** braking systems. State the energy source, typical application, working pressure, advantages, and limitations of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a functional block diagram, explain the working of a **hydraulic servo (hydra-boost) brake system** as used in light commercial vehicles. Derive the expression for the booster force developed in such a system, and explain what happens to pedal feel when the hydraulic supply pressure drops. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer ŌĆö B(a): Comparison Table | Feature | Vacuum Servo | Hydraulic Servo | Air Servo | |---------|-------------|------------------|-----------| | **Energy Source** | Engine manifold vacuum | Power-steering hydraulic pump | Compressed air reservoir | | **Operating Pressure** | 0.6ŌĆō0.8 bar ╬öP | 40ŌĆō80 bar | 8ŌĆō10 bar | | **Application** | Passenger cars (petrol/diesel) | Light CVs, SUVs, hybrids | Heavy trucks, buses, trailers | | **Boost Ratio** | 3 : 1 to 5 : 1 | 4 : 1 to 8 : 1 | 6 : 1 to 10 : 1 | | **Advantages** | Simple, cheap, no extra pump | Compact, works in EVs, higher boost | Massive force, suitable for heavy vehicles | | **Limitations** | Depends on engine running | Needs separate hydraulic circuit | Compressor cost, slow response | | **Failure Mode** | Pedal effort ├Ś3 | Pedal effort ├Ś3ŌĆō4 | Reservoir check valves limit risk | #### Model Answer ŌĆö B(b): Hydra-Boost Working, Derivation, Pedal Feel **Working (refer to Section 4.2 block diagram):** - The **power steering pump** supplies high-pressure hydraulic fluid (typically 40ŌĆō80 bar) to an **accumulator** that maintains reserve pressure. - A **spool valve** inside the hydraulic servo is mechanically linked to the brake pedal. - When the pedal is pressed, the spool valve **directs high-pressure fluid** to one side of a **boost piston**. - The boost piston, with area $A_b$, generates a booster force: $$F_b = P_{hyd} \cdot A_b$$ where $P_{hyd}$ is the regulated hydraulic supply pressure (~ 60 bar typical). - This force is added to the pedal's mechanical input and pushes the **master cylinder piston**, generating hydraulic brake pressure $P_{mc}$. - A relief valve returns excess fluid to the reservoir. **Derivation:** Step 1 ŌĆö Booster force: $$F_b = P_{hyd} \cdot A_b$$ Step 2 ŌĆö Master cylinder input force: $$F_{mc} = F_p \cdot L_p + P_{hyd} \cdot A_b$$ Step 3 ŌĆö Line pressure: $$P_{mc} = \frac{F_p \cdot L_p + P_{hyd} \cdot A_b}{A_{mc}}$$ Step 4 ŌĆö Boost ratio: $$K_b = \frac{F_{mc}}{F_p} = L_p + \frac{P_{hyd} \cdot A_b}{F_p}$$ **Pedal Feel on Hydraulic Pressure Drop:** If the hydraulic supply pressure falls (e.g., pump failure, hose leak): - $F_b = P_{hyd} \cdot A_b$ reduces proportionally. - $P_{mc}$ drops; pedal must travel **further** to generate the same braking force. - The driver feels a "**soft, spongy pedal**" with **delayed response**. - The system reverts to **manual mode** with a fixed mechanical disadvantage ratio ŌĆö braking is still possible but with greatly increased pedal effort. - The accumulator provides ~2ŌĆō3 brake applications of reserve assist before total failure. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mistakes:** > - Confusing **accumulator** with **reservoir** ŌĆö accumulator stores *pressure energy*; reservoir stores *fluid volume*. (Loses 1 mark.) > - Stating that brakes "fail" in hydra-boost failure ŌĆö they don't; they fall back to manual mode with warning. (Loses 1 mark.) > - Not drawing the **spool valve** in the block diagram. (Loses 1 mark.) > - Forgetting to mention the **reaction mechanism** in the hydraulic servo (mandatory for pedal feel). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist ŌĆö Servo Brakes Components** - **Servo brake** = force multiplier between pedal and master cylinder; uses **vacuum, hydraulic, or air** as the auxiliary energy. - **Vacuum servo** is the most common in passenger cars ŌĆö uses **engine manifold vacuum** as the low-pressure source. - **Diaphragm** converts pressure differential $\Delta P$ into booster force $F_b = \Delta P \cdot A_d$. - **Control valve (poppet type)** is the heart of the servo ŌĆö it switches between vacuum (idle) and vacuum+atmosphere (braking) modes. - **Reaction disc / mechanism** provides **pedal feel** ŌĆö without it, the driver cannot gauge braking effort. - **Boost ratio** $K_b = 1 + \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot A_d}{F_p \cdot L_p}$ ŌĆö the most-quoted KTU formula. - **Hydraulic servo (Hydra-boost)** uses **power-steering pump pressure** (40ŌĆō80 bar) and is the preferred system in **EVs and light CVs**. - **Air servo** uses **8ŌĆō10 bar compressed air** ŌĆö standard in **heavy commercial vehicles** with full air-brake systems. - **Failure mode** of all servos: brakes still work in manual mode but pedal effort multiplies by **3├ŚŌĆō4├Ś** ŌĆö a **failsafe** design. - **Electric vacuum pumps** are mandatory in **electric and hybrid vehicles** because no manifold vacuum exists. - KTU Board examiners **always** test the **pressure differential concept** and the **master cylinder input force equation** ŌĆö memorise: $$F_{mc} = F_p \cdot L_p + \Delta P \cdot A_d$$ - Units to remember: **$A_d$ in m┬▓**, **$\Delta P$ in Pa**, **$F_b$ in N**, **$P_{mc}$ in bar** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$).

topics.title: Brake Actuating Mechanisms, Brake Fluids, Regenerative Brakes in Electric Vehicles, Parking Brakes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Brake Actuating Mechanisms, Brake Fluids, Regenerative Brakes in Electric Vehicles, Parking Brakes

# Module 2 ŌĆö Brake Actuating Mechanisms, Brake Fluids, Regenerative Brakes & Parking Brakes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Brake Actuating Mechanism ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *Brake Actuating Mechanism* is the **force-transmitting subsystem** of an automotive brake system that receives the driver's input (pedal force or hand-lever force) and converts, amplifies, and transmits it to the brake shoes or brake pads to generate the friction force required to decelerate or stop the vehicle. It acts as the **"energy bridge"** between the driver's foot/hand and the actual friction-generating hardware (drum/disc).

The actuating mechanism decides how the driver's small input force (Ōēł 100ŌĆō500 N at the pedal) is converted into the very large clamping force (Ōēł 2,000ŌĆō15,000 N per wheel) needed at the brake pad-shoe interface.

**Classification of Brake Actuating Mechanisms (KTU 2024 Module-2 Focus Areas):**

| S.No | Type | Energy Source | Typical Vehicle |
|------|------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Mechanical (Lever / Cam / Cable) | Driver's muscle power | Two-wheelers, old cars, parking brakes |
| 2 | Hydraulic (MasterŌĆōWheel Cylinder) | Driver + Hydraulic fluid | Passenger cars, LCVs |
| 3 | Pneumatic (Compressed Air) | Compressor + Reservoirs | Trucks, Buses, HCVs |
| 4 | Vacuum-Assisted Hydraulic | Engine manifold vacuum | Mid-size cars |
| 5 | Hydraulic-Boost (Hydro-boost) | Power-steering pump pressure | Heavy commercial vehicles |
| 6 | Electro-Hydraulic (Brake-by-Wire) | Electric pump + ECU | Premium cars, EVs |
| 7 | Electro-Mechanical (EMB) | Electric motor per wheel | Concept & future EVs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The four "must-study" actuating mechanisms for board examinations are **Mechanical, Hydraulic, Vacuum-assisted Hydraulic, and Pneumatic**. Electro-hydraulic and EMB are now part of EV-specific topics (often asked alongside Regenerative Braking).

---

### 1.2 Brake Fluids ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> *Brake Fluid* is a specially formulated, hygroscopic (moisture-absorbing), high-boiling-point **hydraulic working medium** used in hydraulic brake systems to transmit pedal force from the master cylinder to the wheel cylinders/calipers with negligible compression and high thermal stability.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Think of brake fluid as the "blood" of the brake system. Just as blood carries pressure from your heart to your fingertips, brake fluid carries the hydraulic pressure from the master cylinder (the "heart," driven by the brake pedal) to the wheel cylinders/calipers (the "fingertips," which press the pads). If the "blood" boils or freezes, the entire system fails ŌĆö this is exactly why brake fluids have **strict boiling-point and viscosity specifications** (DOT ratings).

**Standard DOT Brake-Fluid Grades (KTU Mandatory Knowledge):**

| DOT Grade | Base | Dry Boiling Point (┬░C) | Wet Boiling Point (┬░C) | Typical Use |
|-----------|------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| DOT 3 | Glycol | **205** | **140** | Older passenger cars |
| DOT 4 | Glycol (borate ester) | **230** | **155** | Modern passenger cars |
| DOT 5.1 | Glycol (high-performance) | **260** | **180** | Sports cars, heavy braking |
| DOT 5 | Silicone | **260** | **180** | Military, off-road (NOT hygroscopic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wet Boiling Point** = boiling point after the fluid has absorbed **3.7 % water** (representing 2 years of service life).
> **Dry Boiling Point** = boiling point of fresh fluid (0 % water content).

---

### 1.3 Regenerative Braking in Electric Vehicles ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 EV Module):**
> *Regenerative Braking* (often called **Regen**) is an **energy-recovery braking technique** used in hybrid and electric vehicles (HEVs/EVs) in which the electric traction motor is **reversibly operated as a generator** during deceleration. The vehicle's **kinetic energy** is converted back into **electrical energy**, which is then stored in the high-voltage battery, thereby extending driving range.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine rolling a toy car down a ramp and then pulling it back up using a small generator attached to its wheels. The kinetic energy that would otherwise be wasted as heat in conventional brakes is instead "harvested" as electricity. In an EV, pressing the brake pedal tells the motor controller: "Stop driving the motor ŌĆö instead, **let the motor resist the wheel's motion** while generating electricity." The motor becomes a "controlled load" on the wheels.

**Key Operating Modes:**
- **Battery SoC > 80 %** ŌåÆ Regenerative braking is *limited or disabled* to prevent battery overcharge.
- **Battery SoC < 20 %** ŌåÆ Regenerative braking is *fully active* to maximize energy recovery.
- **Cold battery (< 10 ┬░C)** ŌåÆ Regenerative braking *gradually reduced* to protect lithium cells.

---

### 1.4 Parking Brake ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> The *Parking Brake* (also called *Hand Brake*, *Emergency Brake*, or *Side Brake*) is a **secondary, mechanically- or electrically-actuated braking device** used to hold the vehicle stationary when parked, and to provide emergency stopping capability if the primary hydraulic brake system fails.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** The parking brake is like the "deadbolt" on a door. Your regular brakes are the everyday lock ŌĆö used constantly, but not designed to hold a vehicle indefinitely on an incline. The parking brake uses a **mechanical ratchet/cable or an electric motor actuator** to "deadbolt" the rear wheels (or all wheels in some designs), keeping the car pinned in place even on a steep hill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Modern *Electric Parking Brakes (EPB)* use a DC motor actuator integrated with the rear caliper and are controlled via the Electronic Stability Control (ESC) ECU. They include **Hill Hold Assist** functionality ŌĆö automatically engaging when the vehicle stops on an incline.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theory ŌĆö Hydraulic Brake Actuating Mechanism

The hydraulic brake is the **most common** actuating system in passenger cars and light commercial vehicles. Its operation is governed by **Pascal's Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (1623ŌĆō1662):** "Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the walls of its container."

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Driver depresses the brake pedal.** A pedal lever pivots and pushes the master-cylinder pushrod.
2. **Master cylinder piston compresses the brake fluid.** A primary seal pressurizes fluid in the reservoir-to-line circuit.
3. **Pressure wave travels through brake lines.** Brake lines (steel or reinforced rubber) transmit the pressure at the speed of sound in the fluid (~ 1,200 m/s).
4. **Pressure reaches the wheel cylinder or caliper piston.** Per Pascal's Law, the same pressure acts on the larger piston area of the wheel cylinder.
5. **Force multiplication occurs.** Because $P = \frac{F}{A}$, a small master-cylinder piston with area $A_m$ creates pressure $P$, which on the wheel-cylinder piston with area $A_w$ produces a much larger force.
6. **Pads/shoes clamp the rotor/drum.** Friction converts kinetic energy into heat, decelerating the vehicle.

### Mathematical Force & Pressure Relationships

The hydraulic pressure developed:

$$P = \frac{F_{pedal} \cdot \eta_{lever}}{A_{master}}$$

The output clamping force at the wheel:

$$F_{clamp} = P \cdot A_{wheel} = F_{pedal} \cdot \eta_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$$

The mechanical advantage of the hydraulic system alone:

$$MA_{hydraulic} = \frac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$$

Total mechanical advantage (lever + hydraulic):

$$MA_{total} = \eta_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$$

Where:
- $F_{pedal}$ = pedal force input (N)
- $\eta_{lever}$ = pedal-lever mechanical efficiency (typically 0.85ŌĆō0.95)
- $A_{master}$ = master-cylinder piston area (m┬▓)
- $A_{wheel}$ = wheel-cylinder/caliper piston area (m┬▓)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cylinder Diameter vs. Output Force:** Doubling the wheel-cylinder diameter (with same pressure) **quadruples** the output force, because area scales with the square of diameter.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theory ŌĆö Vacuum-Assisted Hydraulic Brakes

A vacuum booster (also called a "servo") uses the **engine intake-manifold vacuum** to multiply the driver's pedal force by **3 to 6 times**.

### Operating Logic

1. With the brake pedal **released**, both sides of the booster diaphragm are at manifold vacuum (~ -75 kPa or 18 in-Hg).
2. When the driver **presses the pedal**, the control valve closes the vacuum port to the rear chamber and opens **atmospheric air** into the rear chamber.
3. The pressure difference (~ 75 kPa) acts across the large diaphragm area, pushing the master-cylinder pushrod forward ŌĆö **adding force** to the driver's input.
4. When the pedal is **released**, both chambers return to vacuum, and the system returns to rest.

### Booster Force Multiplier Equation

$$F_{boost} = \Delta P_{vacuum} \cdot A_{diaphragm}$$

$$F_{total} = F_{pedal} + F_{boost} = F_{pedal} + (P_{atm} - P_{manifold}) \cdot A_{diaphragm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrol vs Diesel Engines:** Diesel engines don't generate manifold vacuum. Diesel vehicles use a **vacuum pump** driven by the camshaft or a separate electric vacuum pump.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Theory ŌĆö Pneumatic (Air) Brake System

Used in heavy commercial vehicles (HCVs) ŌĆö buses, trucks, trailers ŌĆö because compressed air is **abundant, free, and safe** (no toxic fluid).

### Major Components

1. **Air Compressor** ŌĆö engine-driven, multi-cylinder, typically 7ŌĆō12 bar discharge.
2. **Reservoir Tanks** ŌĆö store compressed air at regulated pressure (~ 8 bar).
3. **Brake Pedal / Treadle Valve** ŌĆö controls application pressure.
4. **Relay / Quick-Release Valve** ŌĆö speeds up brake response at remote wheels.
5. **Brake Chamber (Actuator)** ŌĆö converts air pressure into mechanical force via diaphragm or piston.
6. **Slack Adjuster** ŌĆö converts chamber linear motion into rotary motion of the S-cam.

### S-Cam Brake Working

$$F_{push} = P_{air} \cdot A_{diaphragm}$$

$$T_{S-cam} = F_{push} \cdot L_{slack}$$

$$F_{shoe} = \frac{T_{S-cam}}{r_{drum} \cdot \mu_{lever}}$$

Where:
- $L_{slack}$ = slack-adjuster lever arm (typically 130ŌĆō180 mm)
- $r_{drum}$ = effective drum radius
- $\mu_{lever}$ = mechanical efficiency of S-cam mechanism

---

## 2.4 Detailed Theory ŌĆö Brake Fluids (Properties & Selection)

### Critical Properties

1. **High Boiling Point** ŌĆö must not vaporize under hard braking (temperatures at the caliper can exceed 250 ┬░C).
2. **Low Compressibility** ŌĆö maintains "firm pedal" feel.
3. **Appropriate Viscosity** ŌĆö flows at -40 ┬░C, doesn't thin at +150 ┬░C.
4. **Corrosion Inhibition** ŌĆö additives protect iron, copper, aluminum, rubber.
5. **Hygroscopicity (Glycol-based)** ŌĆö absorbs water to prevent localized corrosion, BUT lowers boiling point with age.
6. **Rubber Compatibility** ŌĆö does not attack or swell SBR/EPDM seals.

### DOT Fluid Compatibility Table

| Property | DOT 3 | DOT 4 | DOT 5.1 | DOT 5 |
|----------|-------|-------|---------|-------|
| Base | Glycol | Glycol | Glycol | Silicone |
| Color (typical) | Amber | Amber | Amber | Purple |
| Mixable with other glycol fluids? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **NO** |
| Hygroscopic? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Used in ABS? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Caution** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mixing DOT 5 (Silicone) with any glycol-based fluid will cause gel formation and complete brake failure.** Always flush the system when switching between silicone and glycol.

---

## 2.5 Detailed Theory ŌĆö Regenerative Braking in EVs

### Operating Principle

When the driver lifts the accelerator or presses the brake pedal, the **Motor Controller (Inverter)** commands the traction motor to enter **generator mode**. The back-EMF of the motor becomes the **opposing torque** that decelerates the vehicle.

### Key Equations

**Motor back-EMF (Generator Mode):**

$$E = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega_m$$

Where:
- $k_e$ = motor constant (V┬Ęs/rad)
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux (Wb)
- $\omega_m$ = motor angular speed (rad/s)

**Generator torque (negative, opposing motion):**

$$T_{gen} = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_{gen}$$

**Energy recovered during deceleration:**

$$E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \eta_{regen} \cdot \left(v_1^2 - v_2^2\right) + m \cdot g \cdot \eta_{regen} \cdot \Delta h$$

Where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $\eta_{regen}$ = overall regen efficiency (typically **0.60ŌĆō0.85**)
- $v_1, v_2$ = initial and final velocities (m/s)
- $\Delta h$ = elevation drop (m)

**Regenerative braking force at the wheel:**

$$F_{regen} = \frac{T_{gen} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain} \cdot G_{ratio}}{r_{wheel}}$$

**Maximum power regenerated:**

$$P_{regen,max} = T_{gen,max} \cdot \omega_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental challenge of regen braking:** Brake force demand varies from 0.05g (gentle stop) to > 1.0g (emergency). Regenerative braking typically provides **0.1gŌĆō0.4g** deceleration. For emergency stops (> 0.5g), **friction brakes MUST blend in**. This is called **brake blending** or **regen-friction coordination**.

### Series vs Parallel Regenerative Systems

| Aspect | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| Connection | Motor between engine & wheels | Motor coupled via clutch |
| Regen capability | **High** | Moderate |
| Brake feel smoothness | Excellent | Requires careful ECU tuning |
| Example | Toyota Prius, Tesla Model 3 | Honda Insight (1st gen) |

### BLDC Motor as Generator (Most EVs)

A BLDC motor in generator mode produces **three-phase AC**, which the inverter rectifies to DC for the high-voltage battery. The motor controller uses **PWM** to control generator torque independently of wheel speed.

---

## 2.6 Detailed Theory ŌĆö Parking Brakes

### Mechanical Parking Brake (Traditional)

- A **ratchet-pawl mechanism** on a hand lever locks the cable tension.
- Bowden cables run to **rear drum-brake levers** or **rear-disc integrated drum** ("drum-in-hat").
- The mechanical advantage of the lever: typically **5:1 to 7:1**.

### Electric Parking Brake (EPB)

A DC gear motor in the caliper pulls a screw/nut mechanism to clamp the pads. Controlled by:
- A pull-switch on the center console
- Automatic engagement when "Park" is selected
- Automatic release when accelerator is pressed (with seatbelt buckled & door closed)

### Hill Hold Assist (Auto Hold)

A sub-function of EPB/ESC that uses **wheel-speed sensors + longitudinal accelerometer** to detect a stopped-on-incline condition. The brake pressure is held for ~ 2ŌĆō3 seconds after the brake pedal is released, preventing rollback.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Master Reference Table

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Domain of Use |
|---|----------|----------------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | $P = \frac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area | Pa (N/m┬▓) | All hydraulic/pneumatic brakes |
| 2 | $F_{clamp} = F_{pedal} \cdot \eta_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$ | Output clamping force | N | Hydraulic brake force multiplication |
| 3 | $MA_{total} = \eta_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$ | Total mechanical advantage | ŌĆö (ratio) | Brake pedal force analysis |
| 4 | $F_{boost} = \Delta P_{vac} \cdot A_{diaphragm}$ | Vacuum booster assist force | N | Vacuum-assisted brakes |
| 5 | $T_{S-cam} = F_{push} \cdot L_{slack}$ | S-cam torque | N┬Ęm | Air-brake actuator analysis |
| 6 | $E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} m \eta_{regen} (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$ | Recovered kinetic energy | J | EV regen braking energy |
| 7 | $F_{regen} = \frac{T_{gen} \eta_{drivetrain} G_{ratio}}{r_{wheel}}$ | Regenerative brake force at wheel | N | EV deceleration analysis |
| 8 | $P_{regen,max} = T_{gen,max} \cdot \omega_m$ | Max regen power | W | Battery charging power limit |
| 9 | $E = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega_m$ | Back-EMF | V | Motor/generator analysis |
| 10 | $T_{gen} = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_{gen}$ | Generator torque | N┬Ęm | Regen torque control |
| 11 | $F_{decel} = \mu_{road} \cdot m \cdot g$ | Maximum friction deceleration | N | Braking capacity check |
| 12 | $a_{decel} = \frac{F_{brake,total}}{m_{vehicle}}$ | Vehicle deceleration | m/s┬▓ | Stopping distance calculation |
| 13 | $v_{initial}^2 = 2 \cdot a \cdot d$ | Kinematic stop equation | m/s | Stopping distance |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic brakes** form the **primary stop system** for > 95 % of road vehicles worldwide. Mastering Pascal's Law applications is foundational for any automotive engineer.
- **Pneumatic brakes** are the lifeline of commercial transport ŌĆö a single truck can have **6ŌĆō10 brake chambers** and multiple reservoirs; understanding this is essential for heavy-vehicle engineers.
- **Brake-fluid selection** is a critical **service-bay decision** ŌĆö wrong fluid choice can lead to brake failure and accidents.
- **Regenerative braking** can recover **15ŌĆō30 %** of the energy otherwise lost in city driving ŌĆö a major contributor to EV range extension.
- **Electric Parking Brakes** are now standard in **> 70 %** of new cars ŌĆö they integrate with ADAS features like Auto Hold, Hill Descent Control, and ESC.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydraulic Brake Force Multiplication

**Problem Statement (KTU typical):**
A hydraulic brake system has a master cylinder diameter of **20 mm** and wheel cylinder diameter of **40 mm**. The pedal lever has a mechanical ratio of **5:1** and is 90 % efficient. If the driver applies a pedal force of **200 N**, determine:
1. Hydraulic pressure developed
2. Force at each wheel cylinder
3. Total mechanical advantage

### Given Data
- $d_m = 20 \, \text{mm} = 0.020 \, \text{m}$
- $d_w = 40 \, \text{mm} = 0.040 \, \text{m}$
- Lever ratio = 5 : 1
- $\eta_{lever} = 0.90$
- $F_{pedal} = 200 \, \text{N}$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Master Cylinder Piston Area

$$A_m = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_m^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.020)^2$$

$$\boxed{A_m = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Effective Force on Master Cylinder Piston (after lever)

$$F_{master} = F_{pedal} \cdot \eta_{lever} \cdot R_{lever}$$

$$F_{master} = 200 \times 0.90 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{F_{master} = 900 \, \text{N}}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Hydraulic Pressure Developed

$$P = \frac{F_{master}}{A_m} = \frac{900}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$P = 2.8648 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 2.86 \, \text{MPa} \;\; (\approx 28.6 \, \text{bar})}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Wheel Cylinder Piston Area

$$A_w = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_w^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.040)^2$$

$$\boxed{A_w = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Clamping Force at Wheel Cylinder

$$F_{clamp} = P \cdot A_w = 2.8648 \times 10^{6} \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} = 3{,}600 \, \text{N}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Total Mechanical Advantage

$$MA_{total} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{F_{pedal}} = \frac{3{,}600}{200} = 18$$

**Verification via formula:**

$$MA_{total} = \eta_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_w}{A_m} = 0.90 \cdot \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$MA_{total} = 0.90 \cdot 4 = 3.6 \;\; \text{(hydraulic only)}$$

$$MA_{total} = 3.6 \times 5 \;\; \text{(with lever)} = 18 \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedal Travel & Volume Continuity:** Note that fluid volume conservation requires $A_m \cdot x_m = A_w \cdot x_w$. The wheel piston moves only 1/4 the distance the master piston moves ŌĆö a key design trade-off.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Problem Statement:**
An EV of mass **1,500 kg** decelerates from **80 km/h to 20 km/h** on a level road. The regenerative braking system has an efficiency of **70 %**. The remaining 30 % is dissipated by friction brakes.
Calculate:
1. Kinetic energy change
2. Electrical energy recovered
3. Energy dissipated as heat
4. Average regenerative braking power if the deceleration took **6 seconds**

### Given Data
- $m = 1{,}500 \, \text{kg}$
- $v_1 = 80 \, \text{km/h} = 22.222 \, \text{m/s}$
- $v_2 = 20 \, \text{km/h} = 5.556 \, \text{m/s}$
- $\eta_{regen} = 0.70$
- $\Delta t = 6 \, \text{s}$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Convert Velocities (with units)

$$v_1 = 80 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 22.222 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$v_2 = 20 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 5.556 \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Initial Kinetic Energy

$$KE_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (22.222)^2$$

$$KE_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot 493.83$$

$$\boxed{KE_1 = 370{,}370 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 370.4 \, \text{kJ})}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Final Kinetic Energy

$$KE_2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_2^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (5.556)^2$$

$$KE_2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot 30.864$$

$$\boxed{KE_2 = 23{,}148 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 23.1 \, \text{kJ})}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Change in Kinetic Energy (Total Energy to Dissipate)

$$\Delta KE = KE_1 - KE_2 = 370{,}370 - 23{,}148$$

$$\boxed{\Delta KE = 347{,}222 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 347.2 \, \text{kJ})}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Electrical Energy Recovered (70 %)

$$E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \Delta KE = 0.70 \times 347{,}222$$

$$\boxed{E_{regen} = 243{,}055 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 243.1 \, \text{kJ})}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Energy Dissipated as Heat by Friction Brakes

$$E_{heat} = (1 - \eta_{regen}) \cdot \Delta KE = 0.30 \times 347{,}222$$

$$\boxed{E_{heat} = 104{,}167 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 104.2 \, \text{kJ})}$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Average Regen Power over 6 seconds

$$P_{regen,avg} = \frac{E_{regen}}{\Delta t} = \frac{243{,}055}{6}$$

$$\boxed{P_{regen,avg} \approx 40{,}509 \, \text{W} \;\; (\approx 40.5 \, \text{kW})}$$

### Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In just 6 seconds of deceleration, the regen system recovered ~243 kJ** ŌĆö equivalent to the energy of about **6.7 g of gasoline** (Ōēł 7.5 mL). Over many stops in city driving, this adds up to a **15ŌĆō30 % range extension**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Mechanism Comparison

| Parameter | Mechanical | Hydraulic | Vacuum-Assisted | Pneumatic | Electro-Hydraulic (EHB) |
|-----------|------------|-----------|-----------------|-----------|------------------------|
| Energy Source | Driver muscle | Driver + fluid | Driver + vacuum | Compressed air | Electric pump |
| Force Multiplication | 5ŌĆō8├Ś | 6ŌĆō12├Ś | 18ŌĆō30├Ś | 50ŌĆō100├Ś | 20ŌĆō40├Ś |
| Response Time | Slow (~300 ms) | Fast (~100 ms) | Fast (~80 ms) | Slowest (~400ŌĆō600 ms) | Fastest (~50 ms) |
| Self-Energy? | No | No | **Yes** (vacuum = engine power) | **Yes** (compressor) | **Yes** (electric pump) |
| Pedal Feel | Spongy-lever-like | Firm | Slightly lighter | Firm-but-heavier | Adjustable (simulated) |
| Failure Mode | Cable snaps | Fluid leak ŌåÆ fail | Vacuum loss ŌåÆ pedal hard | Air leak ŌåÆ fail-safe spring | Electrical fault ŌåÆ fail-safe |
| Vehicle Class | 2-wheelers | Cars, LCV | Mid-large cars | Trucks, buses | Premium, EVs |
| Maintenance | High (cables) | LowŌĆōMedium | Medium | High (air dryer) | Very Low |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydraulic Brake Actuating System ŌĆö Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pedalInput[Brake Pedal Input<br/>F = 200 to 500 N] --> leverAmp[Lever Amplifier<br/>Ratio 5 to 7]
    leverAmp --> vacCheck{Vacuum Booster<br/>Available?}
    vacCheck -- Yes --> vacBoost[Vacuum Booster<br/>3x to 6x Multiplier]
    vacCheck -- No --> directPress[Direct Hydraulic Press]
    vacBoost --> masterCyl[Master Cylinder<br/>Diameter 19 to 23 mm]
    directPress --> masterCyl
    masterCyl --> resTank[Brake Fluid Reservoir<br/>DOT 3 / DOT 4]
    masterCyl --> brakeLines[Brake Lines and Hoses<br/>Steel + Reinforced Rubber]
    brakeLines --> absMod[ABS Modulator<br/>4 Channel]
    absMod --> frontWC[Front Wheel Cylinder<br/>Diameter 40 to 54 mm]
    absMod --> rearWC[Rear Wheel Cylinder<br/>Diameter 30 to 38 mm]
    frontWC --> frontCaliper[Front Caliper<br/>F Pads Clamp Rotor]
    rearWC --> rearCaliper[Rear Caliper or Drum<br/>F Pads Clamp Rotor]
    frontCaliper --> fricF[Friction Converts KE to Heat]
    rearCaliper --> fricR[Friction Converts KE to Heat]
```

## 4.2 Pneumatic (Air) Brake System ŌĆö Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eng[Engine] --> comp[Air Compressor<br/>7 to 12 bar]
    comp --> dryer[Air Dryer and Desiccant]
    dryer --> res1[Reservoir Tank 1<br/>30 L Primary]
    res1 --> res2[Reservoir Tank 2<br/>30 L Secondary]
    res2 --> res3[Reservoir Tank 3<br/>20 L Supply]
    res3 --> treadle[Treadle Valve<br/>Foot Operated]
    treadle --> relay[Relay and Quick Release Valve]
    relay --> frontCh[Front Brake Chamber<br/>Type 24 to 30]
    relay --> rearCh[Rear Brake Chamber<br/>Type 24 to 30]
    trailerLine[Trailer Supply Line] --> trailerCtrl[Trailer Control Valve]
    trailerCtrl --> trailerRes[Trailer Reservoir]
    trailerRes --> trailerCh[Trailer Brake Chambers]
    frontCh --> slackF[Slack Adjuster F]
    rearCh --> slackR[Slack Adjuster R]
    slackF --> scamF[S Cam Mechanism F]
    slackR --> scamR[S Cam Mechanism R]
    scamF --> drumF[Brake Drum F<br/>Friction Action]
    scamR --> drumR[Brake Drum R<br/>Friction Action]
```

## 4.3 Regenerative Braking System ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driver[Driver Releases Accelerator<br/>or Presses Brake Pedal] --> brakePedal[Brake Pedal Position Sensor]
    brakePedal --> ecu[VCM ECU<br/>Brake Controller]
    socCheck[Battery SoC Sensor<br/>Temperature Monitor] --> ecu
    ecu --> invCmd[Inverter Command<br/>Switch Motor to Gen Mode]
    invCmd --> bldc[BLDC Traction Motor<br/>Now Operating as Generator]
    bldc --> threePhase[Three Phase AC Output<br/>Variable Frequency]
    threePhase --> inverter[Power Inverter<br/>AC to DC Rectification]
    inverter --> hvBus[High Voltage DC Bus<br/>350 to 800 V]
    hvBus --> battery[HVE Battery Pack<br/>Lithium Ion]
    battery --> bms[BMS Battery Management System]
    bms --> socCheck
    hvBus --> hvLoad[12 V DC DC Converter and Auxiliaries]
    ecu --> frictionBlend[Friction Brake Blend Signal]
    frictionBlend --> escMod[ESC Hydraulic Modulator]
    escMod --> frictionPads[Friction Brake Pads<br/>Compensate Remaining Brake Demand]
    bldc -.-> regenTorque[Regenerative Torque TR<br/>Opposes Wheel Rotation]
```

## 4.4 Electric Parking Brake ŌĆö Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    switch[EPB Switch on Console<br/>Pull to Engage Push to Release]
    autoInput[Automatic Input from Gear Selector P]
    doorSense[Door Status Sensor and Seatbelt Buckle]
    escState[ESC Vehicle State and Hill Detect]

    switch --> epbECU[EPB Control Module]
    autoInput --> epbECU
    doorSense --> epbECU
    escState --> epbECU

    epbECU --> leftAct[Left Rear Caliper Actuator<br/>DC Gear Motor 12 V]
    epbECU --> rightAct[Right Rear Caliper Actuator<br/>DC Gear Motor 12 V]
    epbECU --> hillHold[Auto Hill Hold Function<br/>2 to 3 second Hold]
    epbECU --> dynMode[Dynamic Emergency Braking<br/>Hydraulic Path via ESC]

    leftAct --> leftClamp[Left Rear Pads Clamp Rotor<br/>Mechanical Screw Clamp]
    rightAct --> rightClamp[Right Rear Pads Clamp Rotor<br/>Mechanical Screw Clamp]

    leftClamp --> wheelLock[Vehicle Held Stationary]
    rightClamp --> wheelLock
    hillHold --> wheelLock
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part A questions** test **Remembering and Understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be **concise (3ŌĆō5 lines)** but **definition-accurate**.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** 
**Define "brake actuating mechanism" and list any four types commonly used in automobiles.**

**Model Answer:**
A brake actuating mechanism is the force-transmitting subsystem that receives driver input and amplifies/transmits it to the brake friction elements.

Four types:
1. Mechanical (cable/lever)
2. Hydraulic (master-wheel cylinder)
3. Pneumatic (compressed air)
4. Vacuum-assisted hydraulic

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between "dry boiling point" and "wet boiling point" of brake fluid? Why is wet boiling point important in service?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Dry Boiling Point | Wet Boiling Point |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Condition | Fresh fluid, 0 % water | After absorbing 3.7 % water (~ 2 years service) |
| Value (DOT 4) | 230 ┬░C | 155 ┬░C |

**Why wet BP matters:** Brake fluid is hygroscopic. Over time it absorbs moisture, which dramatically lowers the boiling point. If wet boiling point is exceeded during hard braking, vapor bubbles form in the brake lines ŌåÆ "vapor lock" ŌåÆ brake failure. Hence, **brake fluid must be replaced every 2 years or 40,000 km**.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part B questions** test **Applying, Analyzing, and Evaluating** levels. Expect numerical problems, derivations, comparisons, and labelled diagrams.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **vacuum-assisted hydraulic brake system**. Also derive the expression for total braking force.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydraulic brake system has a master cylinder of diameter **22 mm** and wheel cylinder diameter of **48 mm**. The pedal lever ratio is **6:1** with 88 % efficiency. If the driver applies a pedal force of **250 N**, calculate:
1. Hydraulic pressure generated
2. Clamping force at each wheel
3. Total mechanical advantage of the system

---

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Q A (a): Vacuum-Assisted Hydraulic Brake System

**Construction:**

A vacuum booster (servo) consists of:
- A **diaphragm or piston** dividing a metal housing into two chambers: **rear vacuum chamber** and **front vacuum chamber**.
- A **control valve** operated by the brake pedal pushrod.
- An **air filter** on the atmospheric port.
- A **return spring** to keep the diaphragm in the rest position.
- The **master cylinder pushrod** is mechanically linked to the diaphragm.

**Working (Step-by-step):**

1. **Brake released (rest position):** The control valve opens the vacuum port. Both front and rear chambers are connected to the engine manifold vacuum (~ -75 kPa). No pressure difference across the diaphragm ŌĆö booster exerts no force.

2. **Brake applied:** When the driver presses the pedal, the control valve closes the vacuum port to the rear chamber and opens the **atmospheric inlet**. Air rushes into the rear chamber.

3. **Force generation:** Pressure difference of ~ 75 kPa acts across the diaphragm area, producing an **assist force** that pushes the master cylinder pushrod forward. This force is added to the driver's pedal force.

4. **Brake released:** Control valve reopens the vacuum port. Both chambers return to manifold vacuum. Diaphragm returns via the return spring. Pedal returns to rest.

**Derivation of Total Braking Force:**

$$F_{total} = F_{pedal} + F_{boost}$$

$$F_{boost} = (P_{atm} - P_{manifold}) \cdot A_{diaphragm} = \Delta P_{vac} \cdot A_{d}$$

Hydraulic pressure:

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{F_{total}}{A_{master}}$$

Wheel clamping force:

$$F_{clamp} = P_{hyd} \cdot A_{wheel} = \frac{(F_{pedal} + \Delta P_{vac} \cdot A_d) \cdot A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$$

Total mechanical advantage:

$$MA_{total} = \eta_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}} + \frac{\Delta P_{vac} \cdot A_d \cdot \eta_{lever} \cdot R_{lever}}{F_{pedal} \cdot A_{master}} \cdot A_{wheel}$$

Simplified form (boost-dominant):

$$MA_{total} \approx \frac{\Delta P_{vac} \cdot A_d \cdot A_{wheel}}{A_{master} \cdot F_{pedal}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values:** A vacuum booster with diaphragm diameter **230 mm**, vacuum of **67 kPa**, and master cylinder of **23 mm** produces an assist force of ~ **2,800 N** ŌĆö equivalent to a **pedal force of ~ 350 N without the booster**.

---

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Q A (b): Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $d_m = 22 \, \text{mm} = 0.022 \, \text{m}$
- $d_w = 48 \, \text{mm} = 0.048 \, \text{m}$
- Lever ratio = 6 : 1
- $\eta_{lever} = 0.88$
- $F_{pedal} = 250 \, \text{N}$

#### **Step 1 ŌĆö Areas**

$$A_m = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.022)^2 = 3.801 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A_w = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.048)^2 = 1.810 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

#### **Step 2 ŌĆö Force at Master Cylinder (after lever)**

$$F_{master} = F_{pedal} \cdot \eta_{lever} \cdot R_{lever}$$

$$F_{master} = 250 \times 0.88 \times 6 = 1{,}320 \, \text{N}$$

#### **Step 3 ŌĆö Hydraulic Pressure**

$$P = \frac{F_{master}}{A_m} = \frac{1{,}320}{3.801 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{P = 3.473 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = 3.47 \, \text{MPa} \;(\approx 34.7 \, \text{bar})}$$

#### **Step 4 ŌĆö Clamping Force at Wheel**

$$F_{clamp} = P \cdot A_w = 3.473 \times 10^{6} \times 1.810 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} = 6{,}286 \, \text{N}}$$

#### **Step 5 ŌĆö Total Mechanical Advantage**

$$MA_{total} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{F_{pedal}} = \frac{6{,}286}{250}$$

$$\boxed{MA_{total} = 25.14}$$

**Cross-verification:**

$$MA_{total} = \eta_{lever} \cdot R_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_w}{A_m} = 0.88 \times 6 \times \frac{1.810 \times 10^{-3}}{3.801 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$MA_{total} = 0.88 \times 6 \times 4.76 = 25.14 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **regenerative braking system** used in electric vehicles. Discuss the concept of **brake blending** between regenerative and friction brakes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EV of mass **1,800 kg** is decelerating from **100 km/h to 30 km/h** on a flat road using 75 % regenerative braking efficiency. The remaining energy is dissipated by friction brakes. If the deceleration duration is **8 seconds**, compute:
1. Total kinetic energy dissipated
2. Electrical energy recovered (in kWh)
3. Average regenerative braking power
4. Heat energy dissipated by friction brakes

---

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Q B (a): Regenerative Braking

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.3):**

Main components:
1. **Brake pedal / Accelerator** with position sensors
2. **Vehicle Control Module (VCM)** ŌĆö brake controller
3. **Battery Management System (BMS)** ŌĆö SoC and temperature inputs
4. **Power Inverter** ŌĆö switches motor from drive to generate mode
5. **BLDC/PMSM Traction Motor** ŌĆö operating as generator
6. **High-Voltage Battery Pack** ŌĆö stores recovered energy
7. **ESC Hydraulic Modulator** ŌĆö controls friction brake blending

**Working:**
1. Driver lifts accelerator ŌåÆ motor controller reduces torque ŌåÆ motor acts as generator (regenerative braking force).
2. If deceleration demand > regen capacity ŌåÆ friction brakes engage.
3. ECU continuously blends the two based on brake-pedal position, SoC, and vehicle speed.

**Concept of Brake Blending:**

| Brake Demand | Regen Contribution | Friction Contribution |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Light (0.05ŌĆō0.2g) | **100 %** | 0 % |
| Medium (0.2ŌĆō0.4g) | 70ŌĆō90 % | 10ŌĆō30 % |
| Heavy (0.4ŌĆō0.6g) | 40ŌĆō60 % | 40ŌĆō60 % |
| Emergency (> 0.6g) | 20ŌĆō30 % | 70ŌĆō80 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "blending challenge":** The brake pedal must feel consistent whether the deceleration is from regen alone, friction alone, or both. The ECU modulates the hydraulic pressure to *subtract* the regen contribution from the friction demand, so the driver always feels a "natural" pedal.

---

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Q B (b): Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $m = 1{,}800 \, \text{kg}$
- $v_1 = 100 \, \text{km/h} = 27.778 \, \text{m/s}$
- $v_2 = 30 \, \text{km/h} = 8.333 \, \text{m/s}$
- $\eta_{regen} = 0.75$
- $\Delta t = 8 \, \text{s}$

#### **Step 1 ŌĆö Convert Velocities**

$$v_1 = 100 \times \frac{5}{18} = 27.778 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$v_2 = 30 \times \frac{5}{18} = 8.333 \, \text{m/s}$$

#### **Step 2 ŌĆö Initial and Final Kinetic Energies**

$$KE_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_1^2 = 0.5 \times 1800 \times (27.778)^2$$

$$KE_1 = 900 \times 771.61 = 694{,}444 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 694.4 \, \text{kJ})$$

$$KE_2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_2^2 = 0.5 \times 1800 \times (8.333)^2$$

$$KE_2 = 900 \times 69.44 = 62{,}500 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 62.5 \, \text{kJ})$$

#### **Step 3 ŌĆö Total Kinetic Energy Dissipated**

$$\Delta KE = KE_1 - KE_2 = 694{,}444 - 62{,}500$$

$$\boxed{\Delta KE = 631{,}944 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 631.9 \, \text{kJ})}$$

#### **Step 4 ŌĆö Electrical Energy Recovered (in kWh)**

$$E_{regen} = 0.75 \times 631{,}944 = 473{,}958 \, \text{J}$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 ├Ś 10ŌüČ J):

$$E_{regen} = \frac{473{,}958}{3.6 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{E_{regen} = 0.1317 \, \text{kWh}}$$

#### **Step 5 ŌĆö Average Regen Power over 8 seconds**

$$P_{regen,avg} = \frac{E_{regen}}{\Delta t} = \frac{473{,}958}{8}$$

$$\boxed{P_{regen,avg} = 59{,}245 \, \text{W} \;\; (\approx 59.2 \, \text{kW})}$$

#### **Step 6 ŌĆö Heat Energy by Friction Brakes**

$$E_{heat} = (1 - 0.75) \times 631{,}944 = 0.25 \times 631{,}944$$

$$\boxed{E_{heat} = 157{,}986 \, \text{J} \;\; (\approx 158.0 \, \text{kJ})}$$

#### **Verification:**

$$E_{regen} + E_{heat} = 473{,}958 + 157{,}986 = 631{,}944 \, \text{J} = \Delta KE \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical significance:** 0.1317 kWh recovered from one deceleration event. With ~ 100 such braking events per full charge cycle, the regen system can recover ~ 13 kWh, equivalent to **~ 50ŌĆō70 km of additional EV range** in urban driving.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (lose 2ŌĆō4 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in kinetic energy problems. Use the factor **5/18**.
>
> 2. **Mixing Pascal's Law with Lever Ratios:** Many students apply the lever ratio *before* or *after* the pressure calculation incorrectly. Always:
>    - Apply lever ratio to pedal force **first** ŌåÆ gives pushrod force.
>    - Then divide by master area ŌåÆ gives pressure.
>    - Multiply by wheel area ŌåÆ gives clamping force.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Lever Efficiency ($\eta_{lever}$):** A 90 % efficiency means 10 % is lost to friction in the pedal pivot. Examiner's key: include $\eta_{lever}$ in **every** force-multiplication step.
>
> 4. **Wrong DOT Fluid Reference:** Students often confuse DOT 5 with DOT 5.1. DOT 5 is **silicone-based** (purple), DOT 5.1 is **glycol-based**. Examiners frequently test this.
>
> 5. **Energy Unit Confusion:** kJ vs kWh. EV problems often quote kWh. Remember: **1 kWh = 3.6 ├Ś 10ŌüČ J = 3,600 kJ**.
>
> 6. **Regen Braking Efficiency:** Don't confuse motor efficiency (90ŌĆō95 %) with **overall regen efficiency** (60ŌĆō85 %), which includes battery charging/discharging, inverter losses, and drivetrain friction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Quick-Reference Revision Block**

### Brake Actuating Mechanisms
- ŌśÉ Mechanical ŌĆö used in 2-wheelers & parking brakes (cable, cam, lever)
- ŌśÉ Hydraulic ŌĆö Master + Wheel Cylinder, governed by **Pascal's Law**
- ŌśÉ Vacuum-Assisted ŌĆö uses engine manifold vacuum (or pump on diesel)
- ŌśÉ Pneumatic ŌĆö Compressed air at 7ŌĆō10 bar, used in trucks & buses
- ŌśÉ Electro-Hydraulic (EHB) / EMB ŌĆö Brake-by-wire, used in EVs
- ŌśÉ Hydraulic pressure: $P = \frac{F}{A}$
- ŌśÉ Force multiplication: $F_{clamp} = F_{pedal} \cdot \eta \cdot R_{lever} \cdot \frac{A_w}{A_m}$

### Brake Fluids
- ŌśÉ DOT 3 ŌåÆ 205 ┬░C dry, 140 ┬░C wet, Glycol
- ŌśÉ DOT 4 ŌåÆ 230 ┬░C dry, 155 ┬░C wet, Glycol-borate ester
- ŌśÉ DOT 5.1 ŌåÆ 260 ┬░C dry, 180 ┬░C wet, High-performance Glycol
- ŌśÉ DOT 5 ŌåÆ Silicone-based, **Non-hygroscopic**, **Do NOT mix** with glycol
- ŌśÉ Replace brake fluid every **2 years or 40,000 km**
- ŌśÉ Causes of brake failure: vapor lock, fluid leak, air in lines

### Regenerative Braking in EVs
- ŌśÉ Motor operates as generator during deceleration
- ŌśÉ Energy recovered: $E_{regen} = \frac{1}{2} m \eta_{regen} (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$
- ŌśÉ Brake blending = regen + friction coordination
- ŌśÉ Light braking ŌåÆ 100 % regen; Heavy braking ŌåÆ friction dominant
- ŌśÉ Limited by **battery SoC**, **temperature**, and **motor thermal limits**
- ŌśÉ Typical regen efficiency = **60ŌĆō85 %**

### Parking Brakes
- ŌśÉ Mechanical parking brake = cable + ratchet + rear drum/disc
- ŌśÉ Electric Parking Brake (EPB) = DC motor actuator + ECU control
- ŌśÉ Auto Hold / Hill Hold Assist = ESC + EPB integration
- ŌśÉ EPB interfaces with gear selector, seatbelt, and door sensors

### Numerical Problem Pattern (Master It)
- ŌśÉ Convert km/h to m/s using factor 5/18
- ŌśÉ $P = F/A$ in Pa or bar (1 bar = 10ŌüĄ Pa)
- ŌśÉ Total MA = lever efficiency ├Ś lever ratio ├Ś area ratio
- ŌśÉ $E_{regen}$ in Joules, then convert to kWh (├Ę 3.6 ├Ś 10ŌüČ)

> **End of Module 2 Notes ŌĆö PCMUT302 Automotive Systems (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

---
