## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Q: Define a hydro-elastic mount. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hydro-elastic mount** is a vibration-isolation device used in automotive powertrain mounting that combines a rubber spring with a hydraulic fluid column to provide high static stiffness and low dynamic stiffness simultaneously.

**Main components:**
1. **Rubber spring element** (provides baseline elasticity)
2. **Upper and lower fluid chambers** (contain glycol/silicone oil)
3. **Inertia track** (narrow orifice connecting the chambers)
4. **Decoupler** (one-way valve in parallel with inertia track)
5. **Metal housing, brackets, and central mounting stud**

*(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 2 for components)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the "notch frequency" in a hydro-elastic mount, and why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **notch frequency** $\omega_{notch} = \sqrt{K_{dec}/m_f}$ is the frequency at which the dynamic stiffness of the mount drops sharply due to the decoupling action of the inertia track and decoupler valve.

**Importance:**
- It is **tuned to coincide with the engine's dominant firing frequency** at idle (typically 20ŌĆō30 Hz for a 4-cylinder petrol engine).
- At this frequency, the mount provides **maximum vibration isolation** by suppressing resonance peaks.
- Without proper notch tuning, the mount would amplify rather than damp idle vibrations.

*(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for formula, 1 for definition, 1 for engineering significance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks divided into two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **ŌØ¢ QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working principle of a hydro-elastic engine mount used in modern passenger cars. Discuss its behavior at low and high excitation frequencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydro-elastic mount has a rubber stiffness $K_r = 400$ N/mm, damping $C_r = 500$ N┬Ęs/m, and a fluid column with effective stiffness $K_{fluid} = 600$ N/mm. The decoupler stiffness is $K_{dec} = 800$ N/m and fluid mass in the inertia track is $m_f = 0.08$ kg. Calculate:
- (i) The notch frequency in Hz
- (ii) The effective stiffness at low frequency ($f = 5$ Hz)
- (iii) The effective stiffness at high frequency ($f = 100$ Hz)

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Ō£ģ MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Question A**

##### **Part (a) ŌĆö Construction & Working (7 marks)**

**Construction:**

A hydro-elastic mount consists of the following components arranged concentrically:

1. **Top metal bracket** ŌĆö bolted to the engine block.
2. **Upper fluid chamber** ŌĆö a sealed cavity above the rubber filled with glycol.
3. **Main rubber spring** ŌĆö a thick-walled elastomeric body bonded between upper and lower brackets; provides the primary elasticity.
4. **Inertia track** ŌĆö a precisely machined narrow channel within the rubber body connecting the upper and lower fluid chambers.
5. **Decoupler** ŌĆö a one-way reed valve located in parallel with the inertia track.
6. **Lower fluid chamber** ŌĆö sealed cavity below the rubber.
7. **Bottom bracket with central stud** ŌĆö bolted to the chassis sub-frame.

**Working Principle:**

- **At low frequencies (< 20 Hz) ŌĆö Engine Shake:**
  - The fluid has sufficient time to flow through the inertia track between chambers.
  - Decoupler remains **closed** because the pressure differential is small.
  - The fluid effectively "locks" the upper and lower chambers together.
  - **Effective stiffness = K_rubber + K_fluid Ōēł HIGH** ŌåÆ engine is firmly held.

- **At high frequencies (> 80 Hz) ŌĆö Engine Buzz:**
  - Fluid inertia in the inertia track prevents rapid fluid motion.
  - Pressure differential across the decoupler exceeds threshold ŌåÆ **decoupler OPENS**.
  - Fluid is trapped in the upper chamber; lower chamber is decoupled.
  - **Effective stiffness Ōēł K_rubber only Ōēł LOW** ŌåÆ vibration isolation is excellent.

- **At notch frequency (~20ŌĆō25 Hz):**
  - A sharp transition occurs, providing an anti-resonance that suppresses idle firing vibrations.

*(Marks: Construction 3 + Working 4)*

---

##### **Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $K_r = 400$ N/mm $= 4 \times 10^5$ N/m
- $C_r = 500$ N┬Ęs/m
- $K_{fluid} = 600$ N/mm $= 6 \times 10^5$ N/m
- $K_{dec} = 800$ N/m
- $m_f = 0.08$ kg

---

**(i) Notch Frequency (3 marks):**

$$\omega_{notch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{dec}}{m_f}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{800}{0.08}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_{notch} &= \sqrt{10000} \\
&= 100 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{notch} = \dfrac{\omega_{notch}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{100}{2\pi} = \boxed{15.92 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

**(ii) Effective Stiffness at Low Frequency, $f = 5$ Hz (2 marks):**

At low frequency, $\omega \ll \omega_{notch}$, so the fluid behaves as a rigid column coupled to the rubber. Effective stiffness is the **sum**:

$$K_{eff,LF} = K_r + K_{fluid}$$

$$K_{eff,LF} = 4 \times 10^5 + 6 \times 10^5 = \boxed{10 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m} = 1 \text{ MN/m}}$$

---

**(iii) Effective Stiffness at High Frequency, $f = 100$ Hz (2 marks):**

$$\omega_{HF} = 2\pi \times 100 = 628.32 \text{ rad/s}$$

Check ratio: $\omega_{HF}/\omega_{notch} = 628.32/100 = 6.28 \gg 1$ ŌåÆ **decoupler is fully open, fluid decoupled**.

$$K_{eff,HF} \approx K_r = \boxed{4 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m} = 0.4 \text{ MN/m}}$$

The mount is **2.5├Ś softer at high frequency** than at low frequency ŌĆö confirming the hydro-elastic design intent.

---

#### **ŌØ¢ QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Compare a conventional rubber engine mount with a hydro-elastic mount. Explain how the hydro-elastic mount overcomes the limitations of rubber mounts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the dynamic stiffness of a simplified two-chamber hydro-elastic mount. Show how the notch frequency depends on the decoupler stiffness and fluid mass. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Ō£ģ MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Question B**

##### **Part (a) ŌĆö Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Conventional Rubber Mount | Hydro-Elastic Mount |
|---|---|---|
| **Components** | Rubber + metal brackets only | Rubber + fluid chambers + inertia track + decoupler |
| **Stiffness** | Fixed single value | Frequency-dependent (high LF, low HF) |
| **Damping** | Limited by rubber material (╬Ę Ōēł 0.05ŌĆō0.15) | Tunable via track resistance (╬Ę can reach 0.5+) |
| **Low-freq support** | Soft ŌåÆ engine sags and rocks | **Stiff** ŌåÆ engine held firmly |
| **High-freq isolation** | Requires soft mount ŌåÆ conflicts with support | **Soft** ŌåÆ excellent isolation |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate (2ŌĆō3├Ś rubber mount) |
| **Application** | Older vehicles, low-spec NVH | Modern passenger cars, diesels, hybrids |

**How hydro-elastic overcomes limitations:**

1. **Adds a hydraulic spring in parallel** with rubber, increasing low-frequency stiffness without making the rubber itself harder.
2. **Inertia track provides frequency-selectivity** ŌĆö fluid "wakes up" only above notch frequency.
3. **Decoupler prevents fluid leakage** at low frequencies (preserves high stiffness) while allowing decoupling at high frequencies (preserves isolation).
4. **Tunable damping** through fluid viscosity and track geometry ŌĆö allows engineers to suppress resonance peaks.

*(Marks: Table 4 + Explanation 3)*

---

##### **Part (b) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions:**
- Inertia track has fluid mass $m_f$ and viscous resistance $R_f$.
- Decoupler behaves as one-way valve with stiffness $K_{dec}$ (modeled as a "fluid spring" when open).
- Upper chamber has cross-sectional area $A$, lower chamber has area $A$ (symmetric).

**Force balance on engine mass** $m_e$ for harmonic excitation $x = X e^{i\omega t}$:

$$m_e \ddot{x} + C_r \dot{x} + K_r x + F_{fluid} = F_0 e^{i\omega t}$$

The **fluid force** on the upper chamber wall comes from chamber pressure $p_1$:

$$F_{fluid} = p_1 A$$

**Continuity equation** ŌĆö flow rate $q$ through inertia track equals volumetric change of upper chamber:

$$q = A \dot{x} - \dfrac{V_1}{\beta_f} \dot{p}_1$$

**Equation of motion for fluid column in track:**

$$m_f \ddot{x}_f + R_f \dot{x}_f = A(p_1 - p_2)$$

Assuming $p_2 \approx 0$ (lower chamber vented to atmosphere via decoupler at high frequency) and $x_f$ proportional to $\int q \, dt$:

After algebraic elimination of $x_f$ and $p_1$ (substituting harmonic forms), the **complex dynamic stiffness** becomes:

$$K^*(\omega) = K_r + i\omega C_r + \dfrac{i\omega K_{fluid}(K_{dec} + i\omega R_f)}{(K_{dec} - \omega^2 m_f) + i\omega R_f}$$

**Notch frequency derivation:**

At the notch, the real part of the fluid stiffness denominator vanishes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Re}[K_{dec} - \omega^2 m_f] &= 0 \\
K_{dec} - \omega^2_{notch} m_f &= 0 \\
\omega^2_{notch} &= \dfrac{K_{dec}}{m_f}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{notch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{dec}}{m_f}}}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- Larger fluid mass $m_f$ ŌåÆ **lower notch frequency** (more low-frequency decoupling).
- Stiffer decoupler $K_{dec}$ ŌåÆ **higher notch frequency**.
- Engineers tune $K_{dec}$ and $m_f$ (via track diameter and length) to place the notch at the engine's dominant firing frequency.

*(Marks: Equation setup 3 + Derivation 2 + Notch expression 2)*

---

### 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make ŌĆö Deducted Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** ŌĆö Most common error. $K_r$ is often given in N/mm but must be converted to **N/m** before plugging into formulas. Many students lose **1ŌĆō2 marks** per numerical due to this.
>
> 2. **Confusing "notch frequency" with "natural frequency"** ŌĆö The notch frequency $\sqrt{K_{dec}/m_f}$ is **NOT** the same as the engine-mount system's natural frequency $\sqrt{K_r/m_e}$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the decoupler action** ŌĆö When asked for "high-frequency stiffness," students often write $K_r + K_{fluid}$ ŌĆö this is WRONG. At high frequency, the decoupler **opens**, fluid decouples, and stiffness reduces to **$K_r$ only**.
>
> 4. **No sketch in descriptive answers** ŌĆö KTU board examiners expect a **neat labelled sketch** for 7-mark descriptive questions. Missing it costs **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Mixing up hydro-elastic mount with hydropneumatic suspension** ŌĆö They are related concepts (both use fluid + elastic elements) but **serve different purposes** (mount = vibration isolation; suspension = load-carrying + ride comfort). Examiners love to test this.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning fluid type (glycol/silicone)** ŌĆö Worth **┬Į mark** in component-listing questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ­¤ōŗ **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- Ō£ģ A **Hydro-Elastic Mount** is a **frequency-dependent**, "smart" vibration isolator combining a rubber spring with a hydraulic fluid column.

- Ō£ģ It has **three functional elements**: **Rubber spring (K_r)** + **Fluid spring (K_fluid)** + **Inertia track & decoupler (m_f, K_dec)**.

- Ō£ģ **Low frequency (< notch):** Decoupler closed ŌåÆ fluid coupled ŌåÆ **HIGH stiffness** ŌåÆ supports engine.

- Ō£ģ **High frequency (> notch):** Decoupler open ŌåÆ fluid decoupled ŌåÆ **LOW stiffness** ŌåÆ isolates vibration.

- Ō£ģ **Notch frequency formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $\omega_{notch} = \sqrt{K_{dec}/m_f}$

- Ō£ģ **Dynamic stiffness expression (board exam favorite):** $K^*(\omega) = K_r + i\omega C_r + \dfrac{i\omega K_{fluid}(K_{dec} + i\omega R_f)}{(K_{dec} - \omega^2 m_f) + i\omega R_f}$

- Ō£ģ **HagenŌĆōPoiseuille resistance** in inertia track: $R_f = \dfrac{8\mu L}{\pi r^4}$ ŌĆö narrow track ŌåÆ high damping.

- Ō£ģ **Typical notch frequency** is tuned to engine idle firing frequency: $f_{idle} = n_{cyl} \cdot N / (2 \cdot 60)$.

- Ō£ģ **Fluid used:** Glycol or silicone oil ŌĆö chosen for high boiling point and stable viscosity.

- Ō£ģ **Modern vehicles** use **3ŌĆō4 hydro-elastic mounts** per powertrain; one is typically a **torque reaction mount**.

- Ō£ģ **Hydro-elastic mounts** are distinct from **hydropneumatic suspension** ŌĆö don't confuse in exam.

- Ō£ģ **Always convert N/mm ŌåÆ N/m** before plugging into formulas (multiply by 1000).

- Ō£ģ **Industry names:** Ford calls them "Hydroelastic mounts"; BMW uses "Hydromounts"; both refer to the same principle.

- Ō£ģ **Significance:** Hydro-elastic mounts are a cornerstone technology enabling modern NVH targets in ICE and hybrid vehicles, with growing relevance in EVs where engine noise masking is absent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydro-Gas Suspension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydro-Gas Suspension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Hydro-Gas Suspension (Hydropneumatic Suspension)** is a type of vehicle suspension system that replaces conventional mechanical coil springs with a sealed pressurized gas chamber and uses hydraulic fluid (oil) as the working medium for both force transmission and damping. The system comprises a sphere or cylinder divided by a flexible membrane/rolling diaphragm into two distinct chambers: the **lower chamber** containing incompressible hydraulic fluid connected to the wheel cylinder, and the **upper chamber** containing a compressible, inert, high-pressure gas (typically **nitrogen (NŌéé) at 25ŌĆō70 bar**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** The pressurized gas acts as the **springing medium** (energy storage element), while the hydraulic oil serves as the **force transmission and damping medium**. This is the foundational principle of the Citro├½n-type hydropneumatic suspension, which revolutionized automotive ride comfort.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **syringe sealed at the nozzle with a trapped air pocket** (instead of water). When you push the plunger inward:
- The trapped air gets compressed ŌåÆ pushes back with increasing force (like a spring).
- The fluid transmits this force to the load.

A hydro-gas suspension works on **exactly the same principle**, but engineered for automotive-grade durability:
- A **pressurized nitrogen gas bubble** replaces the mechanical coil spring.
- **Mineral oil** (hydraulic fluid) replaces the air-water mixture and acts as both the force carrier and damper.
- A **flexible rubber membrane** keeps the gas and oil permanently separated (gas would otherwise dissolve into the oil under pressure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nitrogen?** Nitrogen is chemically inert, non-flammable, has low solubility in hydraulic oil, and exhibits predictable thermodynamic behavior. Standard fill pressure is **25ŌĆō70 bar (Ōēł 2.5ŌĆō7 MPa)** depending on vehicle class.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gas used:** Nitrogen (NŌéé) ŌĆö inert, dry, oil-free.
- **Operating pressure range:** **25ŌĆō70 bar** (typical sedan values).
- **Polytropic index (n) for nitrogen (adiabatic process):** **n = 1.4**.
- **Effective spring rate (progressive):** Increases with compression due to non-linear gas compression.
- **Self-leveling capability:** Maintains constant ride height regardless of load (up to rated capacity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The hydro-gas suspension unit consists of two functionally distinct halves coupled by a **rolling rubber diaphragm**:

1. **Lower Hydraulic Chamber:** Connected to the wheel cylinder via a hydraulic line. As the wheel encounters a bump, the piston pushes oil upward into the suspension sphere.
2. **Upper Gas Chamber:** The incoming oil pushes against the flexible diaphragm, compressing the nitrogen gas. The compressed gas stores potential energy and pushes back on the diaphragm ŌåÆ pushing oil back ŌåÆ pushing the wheel back to its rest position.
3. **Hydraulic Damping:** A **calibrated orifice / throttle valve** in the hydraulic circuit restricts the flow of oil between the wheel cylinder and the sphere, dissipating kinetic energy as heat (laminar + turbulent flow losses).
4. **Self-Leveling:** A height-control valve admits additional gas pressure or vents oil to maintain the design ride height irrespective of vehicle load (passenger vs. cargo variations).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Progressive Spring Rate:** Unlike steel springs (linear), the gas spring's stiffness increases as it compresses (PV^n = constant). This gives a soft ride over small bumps and a firm response under heavy loads or large bumps.
- **Compact Packaging:** A sphere of radius **8ŌĆō10 cm** can replace a large coil spring, freeing engine bay and trunk space.
- **Integrated Damping:** The hydraulic fluid inherently provides damping, eliminating the need for a separate telescopic damper (in classic Citro├½n designs).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law (isothermal) | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ | $P$ [Pa], $V$ [m┬│], $n$ [mol], $R = 8.314$ J/mol┬ĘK, $T$ [K] | Baseline gas behavior |
| 2 | Polytropic Process (gas spring) | $P \cdot V^{n} = P_{0} \cdot V_{0}^{n}$ | $n = 1.4$ (adiabatic for NŌéé) | Real compression process |
| 3 | Effective Spring Rate | $K_{e} = \dfrac{n \cdot P_{0} \cdot A^{2}}{V_{0}}$ | $A$ [m┬▓], $V_{0}$ [m┬│], $P_{0}$ [Pa] | Stiffness at design point |
| 4 | Generalized Spring Rate at any compression | $K = n \cdot P_{0} \cdot \dfrac{A^{2}}{V_{0}} \left(\dfrac{V_{0}}{V}\right)^{n+1}$ | ŌĆö | Progressive (non-linear) stiffness |
| 5 | Static Force on Wheel | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ [N], $P$ [Pa], $A$ [m┬▓] | Hydraulic force transmission |
| 6 | Damping Force (orifice flow) | $F_{d} = C_{d} \cdot A_{o} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^{2}}{2}$ | $C_{d}$ [ŌĆö], $A_{o}$ [m┬▓], $v$ [m/s] | Energy dissipation |
| 7 | Critical Damping Coefficient | $C_{c} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{K_{e} \cdot m}$ | $m$ [kg] (sprung mass) | Threshold for non-oscillatory return |
| 8 | Ride Frequency | $f_{n} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{e}}{m}}$ | $f_{n}$ [Hz] | Comfort metric (target 1ŌĆō1.5 Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** When the gas is compressed **slowly** (isothermal), use $n = 1$. For **rapid, dynamic** suspension inputs (bump impacts, $\sim 0.1$ŌĆō$0.5$ s), use **$n = 1.4$ (adiabatic)**. Always check the question wording for "sudden" or "slow".

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- Used in **Citro├½n** vehicles (2CV, DS, CX, BX, XM, C5, C6) for over 6 decades.
- Applications in **active suspension research**, **military vehicles** (high wheel travel), and **railway rolling stock**.
- Forms the conceptual foundation of **active & semi-active hydropneumatic systems** (e.g., Hydractive suspension).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Spring Rate of a Hydro-Gas Unit

We start with the polytropic compression equation for the trapped nitrogen gas, since the gas is the springing element.

**Step 1: Polytropic Process Equation**
For an ideal gas undergoing a reversible polytropic process:

$$P \cdot V^{n} = P_{0} \cdot V_{0}^{n} = C \quad \text{(constant)}$$

This means at any compressed state, $P = P_{0} (V_{0}/V)^{n}$.

**Step 2: Relating Piston Displacement to Gas Volume**
The piston of area $A$ moves by a small displacement $x$ (positive upward during compression). The gas volume decreases proportionally to piston displacement:

$$V = V_{0} - A \cdot x$$

At the static equilibrium (unloaded) condition: $V = V_{0}$ and $P = P_{0}$.

**Step 3: Relating Force to Gas Pressure**
The hydraulic fluid transmits the gas pressure to the piston without loss (Pascal's Law). Therefore, the force exerted by the gas spring on the piston is:

$$F = P \cdot A = A \cdot P_{0} \left(\dfrac{V_{0}}{V}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 4: Define Effective Spring Rate**
The effective spring rate $K_{e}$ is defined as the slope of the force-displacement curve evaluated at the design point (where $V = V_{0}$):

$$K_{e} = \left.\dfrac{dF}{dx}\right|_{x=0}$$

**Step 5: Differentiate the Force Expression**
We need to differentiate $F = A \cdot P_{0} \cdot V_{0}^{n} \cdot V^{-n}$ with respect to $x$. Using the chain rule:

$$\dfrac{dF}{dx} = A \cdot P_{0} \cdot V_{0}^{n} \cdot (-n) \cdot V^{-n-1} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dx}$$

**Step 6: Substitute Geometric Relation**
From Step 2, $dV/dx = -A$ (volume decreases as piston moves up). Substituting:

$$\dfrac{dF}{dx} = A \cdot P_{0} \cdot V_{0}^{n} \cdot (-n) \cdot V^{-n-1} \cdot (-A)$$

$$\dfrac{dF}{dx} = n \cdot A^{2} \cdot P_{0} \cdot V_{0}^{n} \cdot V^{-n-1}$$

**Step 7: Evaluate at the Design Point ($V = V_0$)**
At the static equilibrium, $V = V_0$, so $V_0^{n} \cdot V_0^{-n-1} = V_0^{-1} = 1/V_0$:

$$K_{e} = n \cdot P_{0} \cdot \dfrac{A^{2}}{V_{0}}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU board formula** for the linearized (small-signal) spring rate of a hydropneumatic unit.

### 3.2 Derivation of Progressive (Non-Linear) Spring Rate at Arbitrary Compression

The effective stiffness increases as the gas is compressed. If the piston has moved up by $x$ from rest, the new gas volume is $V = V_0 - Ax$, and the corresponding stiffness is:

$$K(x) = n \cdot P_{0} \cdot \dfrac{A^{2}}{V_{0}} \left(\dfrac{V_{0}}{V_{0} - A \cdot x}\right)^{n+1}$$

This is the **progressive spring rate** ŌĆö a key advantage of hydro-gas suspension over linear steel coil springs.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A hydro-gas suspension sphere has nitrogen gas at initial pressure $P_0 = 3 \times 10^{6}$ Pa and initial gas volume $V_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬│. The piston area is $A = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬▓. A vehicle load compresses the gas by $x = 0.05$ m. Assuming **adiabatic compression** ($n = 1.4$), calculate:
- (a) The new gas pressure $P$.
- (b) The effective spring rate at this compressed state.
- (c) The static force on the wheel.
- (d) The natural ride frequency for a sprung mass of $m = 400$ kg.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): New Gas Pressure**
The new gas volume is $V = V_0 - A \cdot x = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} - (4 \times 10^{-3})(0.05)$.

$$V = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} - 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.3 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{3}$$

Applying $P \cdot V^{1.4} = P_0 \cdot V_0^{1.4}$:

$$P = P_0 \left(\dfrac{V_0}{V}\right)^{1.4} = 3 \times 10^{6} \left(\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.3 \times 10^{-3}}\right)^{1.4}$$

$$\dfrac{V_0}{V} = \dfrac{1.5}{1.3} = 1.1538$$

$$1.1538^{1.4} \approx 1.226$$

$$P \approx 3 \times 10^{6} \times 1.226 = 3.679 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa} \approx 3.68 \ \text{MPa}$$

**Part (b): Effective Spring Rate at Compressed State**

$$K = n \cdot P_0 \cdot \dfrac{A^2}{V_0} \left(\dfrac{V_0}{V}\right)^{n+1} = 1.4 \times 3 \times 10^{6} \times \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-3})^2}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times (1.1538)^{2.4}$$

$$\dfrac{A^2}{V_0} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0667 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}$$

$$1.1538^{2.4} \approx 1.415$$

$$K = 1.4 \times 3 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0667 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.415$$

$$K \approx 6.34 \times 10^{4} \ \text{N/m} = 63.4 \ \text{kN/m}$$

**Part (c): Static Force on the Wheel**
Using the compressed pressure from Part (a):

$$F = P \cdot A = 3.679 \times 10^{6} \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.47 \times 10^{4} \ \text{N} = 14.7 \ \text{kN}$$

**Part (d): Natural Ride Frequency**

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{6.34 \times 10^{4}}{400}}$$

$$\sqrt{158.5} \approx 12.59 \ \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{12.59}{2\pi} \approx 2.0 \ \text{Hz}$$

This is slightly above the comfort target of 1ŌĆō1.5 Hz, suggesting the gas volume should be increased to soften the ride.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison Table

| Parameter | Steel Coil Spring | Hydro-Gas Suspension |
|---|---|---|
| Spring rate behavior | **Linear** | **Progressive (non-linear)** |
| Damping | Requires separate damper | **Integrated** in hydraulic circuit |
| Self-leveling | Not possible | **Inherent** with height valve |
| Packaging | Long, bulky | **Compact sphere** |
| Failure mode | Gradual sag | **Sudden loss** if seal fails |
| Maintainability | Replace spring | Refill gas + bleed oil |
| NVH performance | Good | **Excellent** (no metal-to-metal contact) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Wheel and Tire Assembly] -->|Vertical Bump Input| nodeB[Wheel Cylinder Piston]
    nodeB -->|Displaces Hydraulic Oil| nodeC[Hydraulic Fluid Line]
    nodeC -->|Oil Flow With Damping| nodeD[Calibrated Orifice Throttle Valve]
    nodeD -->|Oil Enters Sphere| nodeE[Lower Hydraulic Chamber of Sphere]
    nodeE -->|Pushes Diaphragm Upward| nodeF[FLEXIBLE RUBBER DIAPHRAGM]
    nodeF -->|Compresses Nitrogen| nodeG[Upper Gas Chamber Nitrogen N2]
    nodeG -->|Stores Potential Energy| nodeH[Gas Spring Reaction Force]
    nodeH -->|Pushes Diaphragm Down| nodeF
    nodeF -->|Displaces Oil Out| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Throttles Return Flow| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Returns Oil| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Pushes Wheel Back| nodeA
    nodeI[Height Control Valve] -->|Adds or Vents Gas| nodeG
    nodeJ[Hydraulic Accumulator Reservoir] -->|Supplies Make up Oil| nodeD
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow ŌĆö Suspension Response Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Bump Detected by Wheel] --> stepB[Wheel Piston Moves Upward]
    stepB --> stepC[Oil Forced Into Sphere Through Orifice]
    stepC --> stepD[Diaphragm Bulges Into Gas Chamber]
    stepD --> stepE[Nitrogen Gas Compressed PVn Constant]
    stepE --> stepF[Gas Pressure Rises Sharply]
    stepF --> stepG[Force Transmitted Back Through Oil]
    stepG --> stepH[Piston Returns to Equilibrium]
    stepH --> stepI[Kinetic Energy Dissipated as Heat at Orifice]
```

### 4.3 Component Interaction Matrix

| Component | Function | Coupled To | Energy Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nitrogen Gas (NŌéé) | Spring medium | Diaphragm | Potential (pressure) |
| Hydraulic Oil (Mineral) | Force transmission & damping | Piston, Orifice, Lines | Kinetic ŌåÆ Heat |
| Rolling Diaphragm | Gas-oil separator | Both chambers | Mechanical isolation |
| Orifice Valve | Damping element | Hydraulic line | Flow restriction |
| Height Control Valve | Self-leveling actuator | Gas chamber | Pressure regulation |
| Accumulator | Oil reserve & pulsation dampening | Hydraulic line | Volume buffer |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** In board exams, always draw the **diaphragm as a curved arc** inside the sphere, label both chambers clearly, and indicate the gas fill valve at the top. The orifice must be shown in the oil line between the wheel cylinder and sphere.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Define hydro-gas suspension. List any two advantages over conventional suspension.*

**Model Answer:**
Hydro-gas (hydropneumatic) suspension is a vehicle suspension system in which a pressurized inert gas (typically nitrogen at 25ŌĆō70 bar) acts as the springing medium and hydraulic oil acts as the force-transmitting and damping medium, with the two separated by a flexible rolling diaphragm.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Progressive (non-linear) spring rate** ŌĆö provides a soft ride over small inputs and a firm response under heavy loads.
2. **Inherent self-leveling** ŌĆö maintains constant ride height irrespective of vehicle load via a height-control valve.
3. **Compact packaging** ŌĆö a small sphere replaces a bulky coil spring, freeing chassis space.
4. **Integrated damping** ŌĆö no separate telescopic damper is required in classic designs.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Why is nitrogen gas preferred over air in hydro-gas suspension systems?*

**Model Answer:**
Nitrogen is preferred because:
1. It is **chemically inert** ŌĆö does not react with hydraulic oil or rubber seals.
2. It has **low solubility in oil** ŌĆö prevents gas from dissolving into the hydraulic fluid under high pressure (which would cause "aeration" and loss of spring stiffness).
3. It is **non-flammable and dry** ŌĆö improves safety and prevents moisture-induced corrosion of internal components.
4. It exhibits **predictable polytropic behavior** ŌĆö allows accurate engineering calculations using $PV^n = \text{constant}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per point, any 3)*

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a hydro-gas suspension system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydro-gas suspension unit has nitrogen at $P_0 = 4 \times 10^6$ Pa and $V_0 = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬│. The piston area is $A = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬▓. If the gas is compressed adiabatically by $x = 0.04$ m, determine: (i) the new gas pressure, (ii) the effective spring rate at this state, and (iii) the static force on the wheel. Use $n = 1.4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**
The hydro-gas suspension unit consists of:
- A **spherical chamber** divided into two parts by a **flexible rolling diaphragm** (typically butyl or nitrile rubber).
- **Upper chamber** contains pressurized **nitrogen gas** (25ŌĆō70 bar).
- **Lower chamber** contains **hydraulic oil** (mineral-based) connected via a hydraulic line to a wheel cylinder.
- A **calibrated orifice / throttle valve** in the line provides damping.
- A **height control valve** regulates gas pressure for self-leveling.

**Working:** When the wheel encounters a bump, the wheel cylinder piston pushes oil upward through the orifice into the sphere's lower chamber. The incoming oil pushes the diaphragm upward, compressing the nitrogen gas. The compressed gas stores energy and pushes back on the diaphragm, forcing oil out through the orifice (which dissipates energy as heat due to viscous losses). The oil returns to the wheel cylinder, pushing the wheel back to its rest position. The height control valve adds or vents gas to maintain ride height under varying loads.

*(Diagram: 3 marks, Working: 4 marks ŌĆö distribute per KTU marking scheme)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) New gas pressure:**
$V = V_0 - Ax = 2 \times 10^{-3} - (5 \times 10^{-3})(0.04) = 2 \times 10^{-3} - 2 \times 10^{-4} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬│

Using $P \cdot V^{1.4} = P_0 \cdot V_0^{1.4}$:

$$P = P_0 \left(\dfrac{V_0}{V}\right)^{1.4} = 4 \times 10^6 \left(\dfrac{2.0}{1.8}\right)^{1.4} = 4 \times 10^6 \times (1.1111)^{1.4}$$

$(1.1111)^{1.4} \approx 1.161$

$$P \approx 4.644 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa} = 4.64 \ \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Effective spring rate at compressed state:**

$$K = n \cdot P_0 \cdot \dfrac{A^2}{V_0} \left(\dfrac{V_0}{V}\right)^{n+1} = 1.4 \times 4 \times 10^6 \times \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times (1.1111)^{2.4}$$

$$\dfrac{A^2}{V_0} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}$$

$(1.1111)^{2.4} \approx 1.305$

$$K = 1.4 \times 4 \times 10^6 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.305 \approx 9.14 \times 10^4 \ \text{N/m} = 91.4 \ \text{kN/m}$$

**(iii) Static force on wheel:**
$F = P \cdot A = 4.644 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.32 \times 10^4 \ \text{N} = 23.2 \ \text{kN}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the role of the rolling diaphragm and the height control valve in a hydro-gas suspension system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the effective spring rate of a hydro-gas suspension unit starting from the polytropic process equation. State the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Diaphragm and Height Control Valve (7 marks):**

**Rolling Diaphragm (3.5 marks):**
- It is a thin, flexible, reinforced rubber membrane (typically fabric-reinforced nitrile or butyl rubber) that permanently separates the nitrogen gas from the hydraulic oil.
- **Functions:**
  1. Prevents gas from dissolving into the oil under high pressure.
  2. Prevents oil contamination of the gas (which would alter spring characteristics).
  3. Transmits pressure from the gas chamber to the oil chamber without leakage.
  4. Its rolling (inverted) shape accommodates large piston displacements without stretching or tearing.

**Height Control Valve (3.5 marks):**
- It is an electrohydraulic or purely mechanical valve that regulates the volume of oil in the suspension circuit.
- **Functions:**
  1. **Maintains constant ride height** regardless of vehicle load (passenger vs. cargo).
  2. When the vehicle is loaded, the body lowers ŌåÆ a lever opens the valve ŌåÆ high-pressure oil from an accumulator is admitted to the suspension sphere ŌåÆ the gas is further compressed ŌåÆ body rises back to design height.
  3. When the load is removed, the body rises ŌåÆ the valve vents excess oil back to the reservoir.
  4. Eliminates the need for the driver to manually adjust suspension geometry.

**(b) Derivation of Effective Spring Rate (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Nitrogen behaves as an ideal gas.
2. Compression is polytropic: $PV^n = \text{constant}$, with $n = 1.4$ (adiabatic).
3. Hydraulic fluid is incompressible.
4. No leakage past seals or through the orifice.
5. Piston motion is quasi-static for the purpose of force calculation.

**Derivation:** *(Same as Section 3.1 of these notes ŌĆö refer to steps 1 through 7)*

Starting from $P V^n = P_0 V_0^n$ and $V = V_0 - Ax$, with $F = PA$:

$$F = A P_0 \left(\dfrac{V_0}{V_0 - Ax}\right)^{n}$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{dF}{dx} = n A^2 P_0 \dfrac{V_0^n}{(V_0 - Ax)^{n+1}}$$

At equilibrium ($x = 0$):

$$\boxed{K_e = \dfrac{n P_0 A^2}{V_0}}$$

*(Each step carries 1 mark: assumptions 2 marks, derivation steps 5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using $n = 1$ (isothermal) instead of $n = 1.4$ (adiabatic)** for dynamic bump inputs. The board deducts **1ŌĆō2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(V_0/V)^{n+1}$ factor** when calculating stiffness at a compressed state (not at the design point). The progressive stiffness is the most tested concept.
> 3. **Unit errors**: Pressure in Pa (not bar), Area in m┬▓ (not cm┬▓). 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa. Mixing units costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing gas spring rate with system ride frequency** ŌĆö students often write $f_n$ as the spring rate itself. Remember: $K$ has units of N/m, $f_n$ has units of Hz.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in Part B (a) ŌĆö KTU mandates a **neat labelled sketch** for full marks on construction-type questions.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ Hydro-gas suspension uses **pressurized nitrogen gas (25ŌĆō70 bar)** as the springing medium and **hydraulic oil** as the force-transmitting and damping medium.
- Ō£ģ A **rolling rubber diaphragm** permanently separates the gas and oil chambers.
- Ō£ģ The gas compression follows the **polytropic process**: $P V^n = P_0 V_0^n$.
- Ō£ģ For **dynamic (rapid) inputs** ŌåÆ use **$n = 1.4$ (adiabatic)**. For **slow/static** ŌåÆ use $n = 1$ (isothermal).
- Ō£ģ **Effective linearized spring rate:** $K_e = \dfrac{n P_0 A^2}{V_0}$.
- Ō£ģ **Progressive spring rate** at compression $x$: $K(x) = K_e \left(\dfrac{V_0}{V_0 - Ax}\right)^{n+1}$.
- Ō£ģ **Static force:** $F = P \cdot A$ (Pascal's Law).
- Ō£ģ **Natural ride frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K_e}{m}}$ ŌĆö target **1ŌĆō1.5 Hz** for comfort.
- Ō£ģ **Damping** is provided by a **calibrated orifice** in the hydraulic line (viscous + turbulent flow losses).
- Ō£ģ **Self-leveling** is achieved via a **height control valve** that admits or vents oil/gas based on body position.
- Ō£ģ Key advantages: **progressive spring rate, self-leveling, compact packaging, integrated damping, superior NVH**.
- Ō£ģ Key disadvantage: **sudden failure** if the diaphragm ruptures (no progressive degradation like a sagging spring).
- Ō£ģ **Nitrogen properties** to remember: inert, low solubility in oil, non-flammable, predictable $PV^n$ behavior.
- Ō£ģ Famous implementation: **Citro├½n** (2CV, DS, CX, BX, XM, C5, C6) ŌĆö over 6 decades of production use.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Suspension System, Hydraulic Dampers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Suspension System, Hydraulic Dampers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Active Suspension System (ASS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** An **Active Suspension System** is a computer-controlled, closed-loop vehicle suspension architecture that uses an external energy source (typically hydraulic, electro-mechanical, or pneumatic actuators) to continuously generate forces on the sprung mass in real-time, in response to road disturbances, in order to independently regulate vehicle ride height, body attitude, and vibration isolation.

In a **passive suspension**, the damper and spring are the *only* energy-dissipating and energy-storing elements. In an **active suspension**, the actuator is layered *on top of* the passive spring-damper, and an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** commands the actuator to apply a force $F_{act}(t)$ that actively counteracts road-induced vibrations.

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of it this way:** A passive suspension is like a passenger sitting on a wooden chair on a bumpy bus ŌĆö they just endure the bumps. An **active suspension is like a passenger sitting on a smart, motorized seat that automatically tilts and lifts to cancel out every bump** before it reaches the passenger's body. The actuator does the "anti-bump" work in real time.

**Key physical quantities used in ASS analysis:**

- Sprung mass: $m_s$ (kg) ŌĆö the car body
- Unsprung mass: $m_u$ (kg) ŌĆö wheel, tire, brake assembly
- Suspension stiffness: $K_s$ (N/m) ŌĆö typically **20,000ŌĆō40,000 N/m**
- Tire stiffness: $K_t$ (N/m) ŌĆö typically **150,000ŌĆō250,000 N/m**
- Damping coefficient: $C_s$ (N┬Ęs/m) ŌĆö typically **1,000ŌĆō4,000 N┬Ęs/m**
- Active actuator force: $F_{act}$ (N)
- Damping ratio: $\zeta$ (dimensionless) ŌĆö typically **0.2ŌĆō0.4** for passive

---

## 1.2 Hydraulic Dampers (Shock Absorbers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** A **hydraulic damper** (shock absorber) is a passive hydraulic energy-dissipating device that converts the kinetic energy of relative motion between the sprung and unsprung masses into thermal energy via the controlled flow of a viscous fluid (oil) through calibrated orifices and valves, thereby attenuating oscillatory motion in a vehicle suspension system.

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** A hydraulic damper is like a **"syringe with a small hole"**. If you push the plunger fast, the fluid is forced through the small hole, and you feel resistance. This resistance (damping force) is what kills the bouncing. Push it slowly, and the fluid has time to leak through easily ŌĆö little resistance. The damper's job is to *resist fast motion but not slow motion*, which is exactly what oscillation-damping requires.

**Standard Classification of Hydraulic Dampers used in KTU syllabus:**

1. **Telescopic (Double-Acting) Damper** ŌĆö most common
2. **Twin-Tube Damper** ŌĆö standard passenger cars
3. **Mono-Tube Damper** ŌĆö high-performance vehicles
4. **Gas-Charged Damper** ŌĆö prevents cavitation

**Standard damping force equation (linear viscous model):**

$$F_d = C_s \cdot \dot{x}_{rel}$$

where $\dot{x}_{rel}$ is the relative velocity between sprung and unsprung masses.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Suspension Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Taxonomy (Must memorize):**

| Level | Type | Energy Source | Power Consumption | KTU Tag |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | **Passive** | None | 0 W | Reference baseline |
| 1 | **Semi-Active** | Adjusts $C_s$ only | ~10ŌĆō50 W (low) | "Smart damper" |
| 2 | **Slow-Active** | Hydraulic, 1ŌĆō5 Hz | ~500ŌĆō2000 W | Heavy vehicles |
| 3 | **Fast-Active** | Hydraulic, up to 30 Hz | ~3000ŌĆō6000 W | Premium cars |
| 4 | **Fully Active** | Hydractive / Electromech. | up to 10 kW | Lotus, Mercedes, Citro├½n |

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of Active Suspension

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):

1. **Sensing Stage:** Accelerometers on sprung mass ($m_s$) and at wheel hub measure body acceleration $\ddot{z}_s$ and wheel acceleration $\ddot{z}_u$. A position sensor measures suspension travel $z_s - z_u$.
2. **Computation Stage:** The ECU computes the optimal actuator force $F_{act}$ in **real time (1ŌĆō5 ms loop time)** using a control law (e.g., skyhook, groundhook, LQR).
3. **Actuation Stage:** The hydraulic servo-valve modulates high-pressure oil flow to a double-acting cylinder, generating $F_{act}$ on $m_s$.
4. **Feedback Stage:** Force sensor in the actuator closes the loop, correcting any deviation from the commanded value.

### Why Active? ŌĆö The Engineering Rationale

> A passive spring-damper must compromise between **ride comfort** (low stiffness, low damping ŌĆö soft, floaty) and **road holding** (high stiffness, high damping ŌĆö harsh but stable). An active system **decouples** this compromise by *replacing* the damping force with an actuator force that is *commanded*, not *reactive*.

---

## 2.3 Control Strategies for Active Suspension (KTU High-Yield)

### (a) Skyhook Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** Imagine $m_s$ is connected by a virtual damper to a *fictional fixed point in the sky*. The damper dissipates energy regardless of wheel motion.

$$F_{sky} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_s$$

The actuator applies this force, isolating body motion from road input.

### (b) Groundhook Control

$$F_{ground} = -C_{ground} \cdot \dot{z}_u$$

Used to improve tire contact; less common alone.

### (c) Hybrid Sky-Ground Control

$$F_{act} = -C_1 \dot{z}_s - C_2 (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

Tunable trade-off between comfort and handling.

---

## 2.4 Hydraulic Damper ŌĆö Operational Analysis

### Twin-Tube Damper ŌĆö Detailed Operation:

A **twin-tube telescopic damper** has:
- An **inner (working) tube** divided by a piston with metering valves
- An **outer (reserve) tube** containing a small gas pre-charge or air
- **Compression stroke:** piston moves down, oil flows through compression valve (orifice ~ ├ś 8ŌĆō10 mm) into reserve tube
- **Rebound stroke:** piston moves up, oil flows through rebound valve (smaller orifice ├ś 5ŌĆō6 mm) ŌĆö *rebound damping is 2ŌĆō3├Ś compression damping*

### Mono-Tube Damper ŌĆö Advantages:

- Gas (NŌéé) at **~20ŌĆō30 bar** separated by a floating piston ŌĆö eliminates cavitation
- Larger piston area ŌåÆ higher force capacity
- Better thermal dissipation (50% surface area exposed)
- **Disadvantage:** cannot be inverted; installation length is critical

### Damping Force Characteristics:

A real damper is **non-linear**. Empirical KTU model:

$$F_d = C_{low} \cdot v + C_{high} \cdot v \cdot \tanh(k v)$$

where $v = \dot{x}_{rel}$ and the $\tanh(\cdot)$ term represents progressive valve opening.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS ŌĆö MEMORIZE FOR ESE**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_d = C_s \cdot v$ | Linear viscous damping force | N |
| 2 | $F_{spring} = K_s \cdot \Delta x$ | Spring restoring force | N |
| 3 | $F_{act} = C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_s$ | Skyhook active force | N |
| 4 | $\zeta = \dfrac{C_s}{2 \sqrt{K_s m_s}}$ | Damping ratio (passive) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{m_s}}$ | Natural frequency (sprung) | rad/s |
| 6 | $\omega_{n,u} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t + K_s}{m_u}}$ | Unsprung natural frequency | rad/s |
| 7 | $P_{hyd} = Q \cdot \Delta p$ | Hydraulic actuator power | W |
| 8 | $F_{act} = P_s \cdot A_p - P_r \cdot A_r$ | Hydraulic cylinder force | N |
| 9 | $Q = C_d A_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow (Bernoulli) | m┬│/s |
| 10 | $\eta_{v} = 1 - \dfrac{C_s \cdot L}{V \cdot \beta_e}$ | Bulk modulus ŌĆö cavitation check | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Notation:** $\dot{z}_s$ is the *absolute* velocity of the sprung mass (used in skyhook), while $(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$ is the *relative* velocity across the damper (used in passive damping). **Do NOT confuse these in exam answers.**

### Real-World Engineering Utility:

- **Citro├½n Hydractive** (France, 1990s): First production hydraulic ASS using central hydropneumatic sphere
- **Mercedes Active Body Control (ABC)** ŌĆö uses high-pressure (~200 bar) hydraulic struts
- **Lotus Active Suspension** ŌĆö magneto-rheological dampers (semi-active)
- **Boeing 787 landing gear** ŌĆö uses electro-hydrostatic ASS
- **Tata, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland** trucks use **Slow-Active** hydraulic systems for cab suspension

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Quarter-Car Model ŌĆö Two-Degree-of-Freedom (2-DOF) Dynamic Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Always use the **quarter-car 2-DOF model** for ASS analysis. The full vehicle is a 7-DOF model and is *not* required in the 2024 syllabus.

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Diagram Setup

Consider one wheel + one quarter car body:
- $m_s$ : sprung mass (kg)
- $m_u$ : unsprung mass (kg)
- $K_s$ : suspension spring stiffness (N/m)
- $C_s$ : suspension damping coefficient (N┬Ęs/m)
- $K_t$ : tire stiffness (N/m)
- $z_s$ : vertical displacement of $m_s$ (m)
- $z_u$ : vertical displacement of $m_u$ (m)
- $z_r$ : road input displacement (m)
- $F_{act}$ : active force from actuator (N), positive = upward on $m_s$

### 3.1.2 Equation of Motion for Sprung Mass ($m_s$)

Newton's second law on $m_s$ (upward positive):

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -F_{spring} - F_{damper} + F_{act}$$

Substituting force expressions:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -K_s (z_s - z_u) - C_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + F_{act}$$

Rearranged into standard form:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s + C_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + K_s (z_s - z_u) = F_{act}$$

### 3.1.3 Equation of Motion for Unsprung Mass ($m_u$)

Newton's second law on $m_u$:

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u = +F_{spring} + F_{damper} - F_{tire} - F_{act}$$

Substituting:

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u = K_s (z_s - z_u) + C_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - K_t (z_u - z_r) - F_{act}$$

Rearranged:

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u - C_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - K_s (z_s - z_u) + K_t (z_u - z_r) = -F_{act}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Insight:** The actuator force $F_{act}$ appears with **opposite signs** in the two equations because it acts on both masses through a rigid strut. Energy is *injected* into the system from an external source ŌĆö this is the key physical difference from passive.

### 3.1.4 State-Space Matrix Form

Define state vector $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} z_s - z_u \\ \dot{z}_s \\ z_u - z_r \\ \dot{z}_u \end{bmatrix}$, input $u = F_{act}$, disturbance $w = \dot{z}_r$.

The state-space representation in matrix form:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A \mathbf{x} + B u + E w$$

with the system matrix (KTU 2024 standard form):

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\ -\dfrac{K_s}{m_s} & -\dfrac{C_s}{m_s} & 0 & \dfrac{C_s}{m_s} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ \dfrac{K_s}{m_u} & \dfrac{C_s}{m_u} & -\dfrac{K_t}{m_u} & -\dfrac{C_s}{m_u} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{m_s} \\ 0 \\ -\dfrac{1}{m_u} \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.2 Skyhook Active Force ŌĆö Closed-Form Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step derivation** of the skyhook control law from first principles.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the objective:** Minimize absolute body velocity $\dot{z}_s$ (improve ride comfort).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define a fictional "sky-damper":** A damper connecting $m_s$ to an inertial (sky) frame, with damping coefficient $C_{sky}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö The force this sky-damper would apply:**

$$F_{sky, ideal} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_s$$

The negative sign ensures the force opposes body motion (energy dissipation).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sum of forces on $m_s$:** In addition to passive spring-damper, the active actuator must replicate $F_{sky, ideal}$. However, the actuator sits *between* $m_s$ and $m_u$, not between $m_s$ and the sky. Therefore, we must **cancel** the existing passive damping force and **add** the skyhook force.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Net commanded actuator force:**

$$F_{act} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_s + C_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substituting back** into the sprung-mass equation:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -K_s(z_s - z_u) - C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + \left[-C_{sky}\dot{z}_s + C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)\right]$$

The $C_s$ terms cancel exactly:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -K_s(z_s - z_u) - C_{sky} \dot{z}_s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beautiful Result:** The sprung mass now behaves as if connected to the *sky* by a damper $C_{sky}$, completely decoupled from the unsprung mass motion. This is the elegance of skyhook control.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final skyhook-governed equation:**

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s + C_{sky} \dot{z}_s + K_s (z_s - z_u) = 0$$

---

## 3.3 Hydraulic Damper ŌĆö Damping Coefficient Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of damping coefficient $C_s$ from first principles using orifice flow.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assumptions:**
- Incompressible oil of density $\rho$ Ōēł **860 kg/m┬│** (typical hydraulic oil)
- Steady-state flow through an orifice of area $A_v$ and discharge coefficient $C_d$ Ōēł **0.6ŌĆō0.65**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Volume flow rate through the orifice** (Bernoulli's equation):

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

where $\Delta p$ is the pressure drop across the valve.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Relate piston velocity to flow:** If piston area is $A_p$, then:

$$Q = A_p \cdot v_{piston} = A_p \cdot \dot{x}_{rel}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Equate and solve for $\Delta p$:**

$$A_p^2 \cdot \dot{x}_{rel}^2 = C_d^2 \cdot A_v^2 \cdot \frac{2 \, \Delta p}{\rho}$$

$$\Delta p = \frac{\rho \, A_p^2 \, \dot{x}_{rel}^2}{2 \, C_d^2 \, A_v^2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Damping force on piston:**

$$F_d = \Delta p \cdot A_p = \frac{\rho \, A_p^3 \, \dot{x}_{rel}^2}{2 \, C_d^2 \, A_v^2}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Linearized damping coefficient** (around a working point $v_0$):

$$C_s = \left. \frac{dF_d}{d\dot{x}_{rel}} \right|_{\dot{x}_{rel}=v_0} = \frac{\rho \, A_p^3 \, v_0}{C_d^2 \, A_v^2}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö For low velocities**, the linear approximation dominates:

$$F_d \approx C_s \cdot \dot{x}_{rel}$$

where:

$$C_s = \frac{\rho \, A_p^3}{C_d^2 \, A_v^2} \cdot v_0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** $C_s$ is **inversely proportional to $A_v^2$**. Halving the orifice area **quadruples** the damping coefficient. This is why manufacturers use shim stacks with progressively opening valves ŌĆö they get soft damping at low $v$ and firm damping at high $v$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Passive vs Active

| Parameter | Passive Suspension | Slow-Active | Fast-Active | Skyhook Active |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F_{act}$ (N) | 0 | 500ŌĆō1500 | up to 5000 | $C_{sky}\dot{z}_s$ |
| Bandwidth | ŌĆö | 1ŌĆō5 Hz | 5ŌĆō30 Hz | up to 25 Hz |
| Energy | None | 0.5ŌĆō2 kW | 3ŌĆō6 kW | ~3 kW |
| Ride comfort | LowŌĆōMedium | Medium | High | Very High |
| Handling | LowŌĆōMedium | High | Very High | High |
| Cost (relative) | 1├Ś | 5ŌĆō8├Ś | 15ŌĆō25├Ś | 10ŌĆō20├Ś |
| Reliability | Excellent | Good | Moderate | Moderate |
| KTU expected in Q | Yes (base) | Yes (common) | Yes (advanced) | Yes (control) |
