# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an Active Suspension System. How does it differ from a passive suspension system in terms of energy management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Active Suspension System (ASS)** is a computer-controlled suspension that uses an external energy source (hydraulic, pneumatic, or electromechanical actuator) to generate real-time forces on the sprung mass, commanded by an Electronic Control Unit (ECU), to actively control body motion in response to road inputs. **(1 mark)**

**Differences in energy management:**

| Aspect | Passive | Active |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy source | None ŌĆö only dissipates stored energy | External source (pump, motor) |
| Energy direction | One-way (dissipative only) | Two-way (inject + dissipate) |
| Adaptivity | Fixed $K_s$, $C_s$ | Variable force, real-time |
| Control | Open loop | Closed loop with sensors |

**(2 marks)** ŌĆö The active system *adds* energy when needed (e.g., lifting a dipping wheel) and *removes* energy via commanded forces, whereas a passive system can only dissipate via the damper.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the four main types of suspension systems classified by energy source.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four main classifications, in increasing order of sophistication, are:

1. **Passive Suspension** ŌĆö Uses only springs and dampers. No external power. Fixed $K_s$ and $C_s$. Most common, cheapest, most reliable. **(0.75 mark)**
2. **Semi-Active Suspension** ŌĆö Uses variable dampers (e.g., magneto-rheological) where $C_s$ is electronically controlled, but **no energy is added** to the system. Power consumption ~10ŌĆō50 W. **(0.75 mark)**
3. **Slow-Active Suspension** ŌĆö Uses hydraulic actuators with **low bandwidth (1ŌĆō5 Hz)**. Suited for heavy vehicles (trucks, buses) controlling body roll and pitch. **(0.75 mark)**
4. **Fully Active (Fast-Active) Suspension** ŌĆö Uses high-bandwidth (up to 30 Hz) hydraulic or electro-mechanical actuators. Replaces both spring and damper functions. Used in premium vehicles (Mercedes ABC, Citro├½n Hydractive). **(0.75 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** Module 3 Part B allows internal choice. **Question A** and **Question B** below are two completely independent alternatives. Each is worth 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat **quarter-car 2-DOF model**, derive the equations of motion for an **active suspension system** with sprung mass $m_s$, unsprung mass $m_u$, spring stiffness $K_s$, damping coefficient $C_s$, tire stiffness $K_t$, and active force $F_{act}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Skyhook control strategy** for active suspensions. Derive the governing equation of motion for the sprung mass under skyhook control and show how the passive damping term cancels out.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Diagram (1 mark):** Draw a clear quarter-car model showing $m_s$ on top connected to $m_u$ via spring $K_s$, damper $C_s$, and actuator $F_{act}$. $m_u$ is connected to road $z_r$ via tire stiffness $K_t$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö FBD of sprung mass $m_s$ (1 mark):**

The forces acting on $m_s$ (upward positive) are:
- Spring force: $-K_s(z_s - z_u)$
- Damper force: $-C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$
- Actuator force: $+F_{act}$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Newton's second law (1 mark):**

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -K_s(z_s - z_u) - C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + F_{act}$$

Rearranged:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s + C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + K_s(z_s - z_u) = F_{act}$$

**(Step 3 = 1 mark)** ŌĆö Equation of motion for sprung mass.

**Step 4 ŌĆö FBD of unsprung mass $m_u$ (1 mark):**

The forces on $m_u$ (upward positive):
- Spring force from $K_s$: $+K_s(z_s - z_u)$
- Damper force: $+C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$
- Tire spring force: $-K_t(z_u - z_r)$
- Reaction from actuator: $-F_{act}$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Newton's law on $m_u$ (1 mark):**

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u = K_s(z_s - z_u) + C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - K_t(z_u - z_r) - F_{act}$$

Rearranged:

$$m_u \ddot{z}_u - C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) - K_s(z_s - z_u) + K_t(z_u - z_r) = -F_{act}$$

**(Step 5 = 1 mark)** ŌĆö Equation of motion for unsprung mass.

**Step 6 ŌĆö State-space form (1 mark):** Write the coupled equations in matrix form with state vector $\mathbf{x} = [z_s - z_u,\; \dot{z}_s,\; z_u - z_r,\; \dot{z}_u]^T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total marks: 1+1+1+1+1+1 = 7 marks**

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Concept of skyhook (1 mark):**

The **skyhook control strategy** is a benchmark active suspension control law in which the sprung mass is conceptually connected to a *fictional inertial (sky) reference* by a virtual damper of coefficient $C_{sky}$. The objective is to dissipate body kinetic energy in an absolute frame, achieving perfect ride isolation.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define ideal sky force (1 mark):**

The force that the sky-damper would apply on $m_s$:

$$F_{sky, ideal} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_s$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute required actuator force (2 marks):**

Since the actuator sits between $m_s$ and $m_u$ (not $m_s$ and sky), the commanded actuator force must:

1. Cancel the existing passive damper force
2. Inject the sky-damper force

$$F_{act} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{z}_s + C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into sprung-mass equation (1 mark):**

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -K_s(z_s - z_u) - C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u) + \left[-C_{sky}\dot{z}_s + C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)\right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify and show cancellation (1 mark):**

The $C_s$ terms cancel:

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s = -K_s(z_s - z_u) - C_{sky} \dot{z}_s$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final governing equation (1 mark):**

$$m_s \ddot{z}_s + C_{sky} \dot{z}_s + K_s(z_s - z_u) = 0$$

This shows $m_s$ behaves as if damped to the *sky* ŌĆö fully decoupled from the unsprung mass's instantaneous motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total marks: 1+1+2+1+1+1 = 7 marks**

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **construction and working of a twin-tube telescopic hydraulic damper** with a neat sketch. Compare it with a **mono-tube damper**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hydraulic damper has piston area $A_p = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$, valve area $A_v = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2$, discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.62$, and oil density $\rho = 860 \; kg/m^3$. The piston is moving at $v_0 = 0.25 \; m/s$. Calculate: **(i)** the volumetric flow rate $Q$, **(ii)** the pressure drop $\Delta p$ across the valve, **(iii)** the damping force $F_d$, and **(iv)** the linearized damping coefficient $C_s$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Components of a twin-tube damper (2 marks):**

1. **Piston rod** (top mount eye)
2. **Piston** with calibrated **rebound valve** (top side, smaller orifice) and **compression valve** (bottom side, larger orifice)
3. **Inner (working) tube** ŌĆö contains the oil
4. **Outer (reserve) tube** ŌĆö partially filled with oil, partially with low-pressure gas
5. **Bottom valve** at base of inner tube
6. **Bottom mount eye**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Working ŌĆö Compression stroke (2 marks):**

When the wheel moves up relative to the body (compression), the piston moves down. Oil below the piston is forced through the **compression valve** (larger orifice ├ś 8 mm) into the **reserve tube**. The displaced oil volume = $A_p \cdot \Delta x$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Working ŌĆö Rebound stroke (1 mark):**

When the wheel moves down (rebound), the piston moves up. Oil above the piston is forced through the **rebound valve** (smaller orifice ├ś 5 mm) and the **bottom valve**, creating higher resistance. Hence rebound damping is typically 2ŌĆō3├Ś compression damping.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Comparison table with mono-tube (2 marks):**

| Feature | Twin-Tube | Mono-Tube |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Chambers | Two (inner + reserve) | One + gas chamber |
| Gas precharge | Low (or none) | High (20ŌĆō30 bar) |
| Cavitation resistance | Moderate | Excellent |
| Inverted operation | Possible | Not possible |
| Thermal capacity | Moderate | High (50% more surface) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total marks: 2+2+1+2 = 7 marks**

---

**Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $A_p = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$
- $A_v = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2$
- $C_d = 0.62$
- $\rho = 860 \; kg/m^3$
- $v_0 = 0.25 \; m/s$

**Part (i) ŌĆö Volumetric flow rate (1.5 marks):**

$$Q = A_p \cdot v_0 = (3.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.25)$$

$$\boxed{Q = 8.75 \times 10^{-4} \; m^3/s}$$

**Part (ii) ŌĆö Pressure drop (2 marks):**

From Bernoulli orifice equation:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

Solving for $\Delta p$:

$$\Delta p = \frac{\rho}{2} \left(\frac{Q}{C_d \cdot A_v}\right)^2$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta p = \frac{860}{2} \left(\frac{8.75 \times 10^{-4}}{0.62 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^2$$

$$\Delta p = 430 \times \left(\frac{8.75 \times 10^{-4}}{7.44 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^2$$

$$\Delta p = 430 \times (11.76)^2 = 430 \times 138.3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta p \approx 5.95 \times 10^{4} \; Pa \approx 59.5 \; kPa}$$

**Part (iii) ŌĆö Damping force (1.5 marks):**

$$F_d = \Delta p \cdot A_p = (5.95 \times 10^4)(3.5 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F_d \approx 208.3 \; N}$$

**Part (iv) ŌĆö Linearized damping coefficient (2 marks):**

$$C_s = \frac{\rho \cdot A_p^3 \cdot v_0}{C_d^2 \cdot A_v^2}$$

$$C_s = \frac{860 \times (3.5 \times 10^{-3})^3 \times 0.25}{(0.62)^2 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-4})^2}$$

$$C_s = \frac{860 \times 4.29 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.25}{0.3844 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$C_s = \frac{9.22 \times 10^{-6}}{5.535 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{C_s \approx 1666 \; N \cdot s / m}$$

**Verification using $F_d = C_s \cdot v_0$:** $C_s = F_d / v_0 = 208.3 / 0.25 = 833.2 \; N \cdot s/m$ (a discrepancy due to linearization ŌĆö KTU accepts both methods).

> [!NOTE]
> **Total marks: 1.5+2+1.5+2 = 7 marks**

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2ŌĆō4 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in actuator force:** The active force $F_{act}$ appears with **opposite signs** in the two EOMs. Students often write $+F_{act}$ on both ŌĆö *loses 1 mark*.
> 2. **Confusing $\dot{z}_s$ with $(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$:** Skyhook uses *absolute* body velocity. Passive uses *relative* velocity. Mixing them gives wrong skyhook cancellation ŌĆö *loses 2 marks*.
> 3. **Forgetting $K_t$ in unsprung mass equation:** The tire spring connects $m_u$ to $z_r$, not to ground. Forgetting $K_t(z_u - z_r)$ ŌĆö *loses 1 mark*.
> 4. **Linearizing without justification:** In the damping coefficient derivation, students jump to $C_s$ without showing the differentiation step $C_s = dF_d/d\dot{x}_{rel}$ ŌĆö *loses 1 mark*.
> 5. **Units of damping coefficient:** $C_s$ is in **N┬Ęs/m**, not N/m or N. Always write the full unit string.
> 6. **Skyhook final equation must NOT contain $C_s$ term:** A common error leaves the passive damper term in the final skyhook result. If present, the answer is marked *wrong* ŌĆö *loses 1 mark*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY ŌĆö RE-READ BEFORE EXAM**

### Active Suspension ŌĆö Key Points

- ŌśÉ Active suspension **adds energy** to the system via an actuator; passive cannot.
- ŌśÉ Four classes: **Passive, Semi-Active, Slow-Active, Fast-Active**.
- ŌśÉ Fast-Active bandwidth is up to **30 Hz**; slow-active is 1ŌĆō5 Hz.
- ŌśÉ Actuator force $F_{act}$ appears with **opposite signs** on $m_s$ and $m_u$.
- ŌśÉ Skyhook control uses **absolute body velocity** $\dot{z}_s$ (not relative).
- ŌśÉ Skyhook command: $F_{act} = -C_{sky}\dot{z}_s + C_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$.
- ŌśÉ Skyhook result: $m_s \ddot{z}_s + C_{sky}\dot{z}_s + K_s(z_s - z_u) = 0$ ŌĆö passive $C_s$ cancels.
- ŌśÉ Quarter-car 2-DOF is the KTU standard analysis model.

### Hydraulic Damper ŌĆö Key Points

- ŌśÉ Damping force (linear): $F_d = C_s \dot{x}_{rel}$.
- ŌśÉ Twin-tube: two chambers, low gas precharge, **rebound > compression damping** (2ŌĆō3├Ś).
- ŌśÉ Mono-tube: single chamber + high-pressure gas (**20ŌĆō30 bar**), no cavitation.
- ŌśÉ Orifice flow: $Q = C_d A_v \sqrt{2\Delta p / \rho}$.
- ŌśÉ $C_s \propto 1/A_v^2$ ŌĆö halving valve area **quadruples** damping.
- ŌśÉ Damping force (non-linear): $F_d = C_{low} v + C_{high} v \tanh(kv)$.
- ŌśÉ Natural frequency (sprung): $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_s / m_s}$, typically **1ŌĆō1.5 Hz**.
- ŌśÉ Unsprung natural frequency: $\omega_{n,u} = \sqrt{(K_t + K_s)/m_u}$, typically **10ŌĆō15 Hz**.

### Must-Memorize Numerical Benchmarks

- ŌśÉ Typical $K_s$: **20ŌĆō40 kN/m** | Typical $K_t$: **150ŌĆō250 kN/m**
- ŌśÉ Typical $C_s$: **1ŌĆō4 kN┬Ęs/m** | Damping ratio $\zeta$: **0.2ŌĆō0.4**
- ŌśÉ Oil density: **860 kg/m┬│** | Discharge coefficient: **0.6ŌĆō0.65**
- ŌśÉ Sprung mass natural frequency: **~1.2 Hz** (ride comfort optimum)
- ŌśÉ Hydraulic supply pressure (active): **150ŌĆō200 bar**
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Filled Dampers, Tyres and Wheels - Types of Wheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Filled Dampers, Tyres and Wheels - Types of Wheels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Context Within Module 3 ŌĆö Suspension & Wheel Assembly

Module 3 of *Automotive Systems* focuses on two interconnected subsystems that directly govern ride comfort, handling, and road-holding:
- **Gas Filled Dampers** (a subset of hydraulic dampers/struts) ŌĆö energy-dissipating devices that control sprung-mass oscillation.
- **Tyres and Wheels** ŌĆö the final mechanical link between the vehicle chassis and the road surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PCMUT302, Module 3):** *Gas filled dampers, Tyres and wheels ŌĆō Types of wheels, construction, materials, specifications, and wheel mounting.* This note focuses on **Types of Wheels** as the principal sub-topic.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definition ŌĆö Automobile Wheel

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A *wheel* in an automobile is a rotating, load-bearing structural disc that mounts the pneumatic tyre to the axle hub (or brake drum/disc) via studs/bolts. It transmits all vertical, longitudinal, and lateral road forces between the tyre and the suspension/steering system, while also providing a sealed cavity for tubeless-tyre air retention and serving as a heat sink for the brake assembly.

In simple terms ŌĆö a wheel is the **"metal skeleton"** that holds the tyre and bolts to the hub.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Wheel as a "Spinning Foot"

Imagine your foot inside a shoe. The shoe (tyre) grips the ground; the foot (wheel rim + hub) carries your body weight and lets you pivot. The wheel performs the same job for a car:
- It **carries the vertical load** (vehicle weight Ōēł curb weight / 4 per wheel for a sedan).
- It **absorbs steering rotation** (so the tyre can change direction).
- It **transmits braking torque** (from the brake rotor to the tyre-road contact patch).

Without the wheel, the tyre would have no rigid backbone to mount on, and no way to rotate on the axle.

---

### 1.4 Functional Requirements of an Automotive Wheel

A KTU examiner will frequently test these requirements as a short-answer (3-mark) item. Memorize them:

| S. No. | Functional Requirement | Engineering Justification |
|:------:|:------------------------|:---------------------------|
| 1 | High static & fatigue strength | Endures cyclic road loading Ōēł **10ŌüĘ cycles/yr** |
| 2 | Low unsprung mass | Improves ride & handling; reduces NVH |
| 3 | Adequate heat dissipation | Brake energy can reach **600 ┬░C** at rotor |
| 4 | Corrosion resistance | Exposed to water, salt, mud |
| 5 | Dimensional accuracy | Run-out **Ōēż 0.5 mm** lateral, **Ōēż 0.8 mm** radial (typical OEM) |
| 6 | Tyre bead-seating integrity | Air-tightness for tubeless tyres (Ōēź **1.5 bar**) |
| 7 | Aesthetic appeal | Influences vehicle marketability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric (Bold):** OEM passenger-car wheel **unsprung mass** typically lies in the range **7ŌĆō15 kg** per wheel, while forged aluminium wheels bring this down to **5ŌĆō9 kg**.

---

### 1.5 Broad Classification of Automotive Wheels

Wheels are categorized at two levels:

1. **By Construction / Geometry**
   - Disc (Solid) Wheel
   - Spoke Wheel
     - Wire Spoke
     - Cast Spoke
2. **By Manufacturing Process / Material**
   - Pressed Steel
   - Cast Aluminium Alloy
   - Forged Aluminium / Magnesium
   - Carbon-Fiber Composite (premium segment)
   - Cast Iron (vintage/heavy commercial)

The remaining sections of these notes will expand each category with engineering rigour and KTU exam-ready depth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Disc (Solid) Wheels

The **disc wheel** is the modern default on cars, SUVs, light trucks, and most commercial vehicles. It consists of a single pressed-metal disc that forms both the rim and the wheel face in one piece.

#### 2.1.1 Constructional Anatomy

A pressed-steel disc wheel has three integrated regions:

1. **Rim** ŌĆö the outer cylindrical flange that houses the tyre bead. Includes *drop centre* (for tubeless-tyre mounting) and *safety humps* (prevent bead slip during cornering).
2. **Disc (Web)** ŌĆö the flat central plate carrying the brake-side geometry and ventilation cut-outs.
3. **Hub / Spigot Mounting** ŌĆö the central bore that locates concentrically on the axle hub, surrounded by **4, 5, or 6 lug-stud holes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Disc"?** The name derives from the *continuous, solid disc-shaped web* (unlike a spoked wheel that has openings between spokes). This single-piece geometry offers superior airtightness for tubeless tyres.

#### 2.1.2 Why Steel? ŌĆö Material Logic

Pressed-steel disc wheels dominate the **mass-market passenger-car segment** because:

- **Formability:** Deep-drawing of low-carbon steel sheets (e.g., *EN 10130 DC04*) allows the complex rim-flange profile in one stamping operation.
- **Cost:** Sheet-steel is **5ŌĆō10├Ś cheaper per kg** than aluminium alloy.
- **Repairability:** Minor rim-flange damage can be hammered back, unlike brittle cast alloys.
- **Fatigue behaviour:** Steel's endurance limit Ōēł **250ŌĆō350 MPa** suits cyclic loading.

#### 2.1.3 Sub-Types of Disc Wheels

| Sub-Type | Construction | Typical Application |
|:---------|:-------------|:--------------------|
| **Pressed-steel disc** | Single stamped steel sheet welded to rim | Hatchbacks, sedans, LCV |
| **Aluminium-alloy disc** | Low-pressure cast or flow-formed | Premium sedans, SUVs |
| **Composite disc (hybrid)** | Cast aluminium face welded to steel rim | Mid-segment cars (cost/weight balance) |
| **Two-piece disc** | Bolted face + rim for serviceability | Off-road / heavy-duty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-piece welded steel wheels** are common in Indian-market vehicles (e.g., Maruti, Hyundai) where the **rim** is rolled from a steel strip and then **continuously flash-welded** to the stamped disc.

---

### 2.2 Spoke Wheels

A **spoke wheel** has an open geometry ŌĆö radial members (spokes) connect the central hub to an outer rim. The rim is essentially a separate ring on which the tyre is mounted.

#### 2.2.1 Wire-Spoke Wheel

- **Construction:** A sheet-metal or cast hub carries **36ŌĆō40 stainless-steel wire spokes** (typically **├ś 2.5ŌĆō4 mm**). The spokes terminate in threaded nipples on the rim.
- **Working Principle ŌĆö Torsion vs. Tension:** Wire spokes carry load **purely in tension**; the rim is pre-tensioned so the spoke never goes slack under cornering/braking torque.
- **Applications:** Vintage cars, motorcycles, Royal Enfield, and high-end bicycles.
- **Advantages:**
  - Lowest unsprung mass for given strength
  - Excellent heat dissipation (open structure)
  - Spring-like compliance improves ride
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Periodic spoke-tension adjustment
  - Not suited for tubeless tyres (wire ends puncture inner liner)
  - Slow, labour-intensive manufacture

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A wire-spoke wheel is essentially a *pre-stressed tension structure* ŌĆö analogous to a bicycle wheel or a cable-stayed bridge. The wheel as a whole behaves like a thin-walled pressure vessel.

#### 2.2.2 Cast-Spoke (Magnesium / Aluminium) Wheel

- **Construction:** The hub, spokes, and rim are cast as a **single-piece** casting in magnesium or aluminium alloy.
- **Process:** Gravity die-casting, low-pressure die-casting, or (premium) high-pressure die-casting.
- **Applications:** Vintage sports cars (e.g., classic MG, Jaguar E-Type knock-offs), mid-20th-century racing.
- **Status today:** Largely superseded by cast-aluminium disc wheels; seen only on restored classics.

---

### 2.3 Manufacturing Process Taxonomy ŌĆö A KTU High-Yield View

| Process | Material | Wheel Type Produced | Tensile Strength | Cost |
|:--------|:---------|:--------------------|:-----------------|:-----|
| **Stamping / Deep Drawing** | Steel sheet (1.5ŌĆō3 mm) | Pressed-steel disc | Ōēł **400 MPa** | Low |
| **Low-Pressure Die Casting (LPDC)** | Al-Si alloy (e.g., A356) | Cast alloy disc | Ōēł **220ŌĆō260 MPa** | Medium |
| **High-Pressure Die Casting (HPDC)** | Al-Si alloy | Cast alloy disc | Ōēł **240ŌĆō300 MPa** | Medium |
| **Forging (Closed-Die)** | 6061-T6 / 2014A Al | Forged disc | Ōēł **360ŌĆō420 MPa** | High |
| **Flow Forming** | Spun Al blank + roll | Hybrid forged-look | Ōēł **300ŌĆō340 MPa** | Medium-High |
| **Resin Transfer Moulding** | Carbon fibre + epoxy | Composite disc | Ōēł **500ŌĆō800 MPa** (tensile fibre) | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forging = Strength + Lightness.** A forged aluminium wheel is **20ŌĆō30% lighter** than an equivalent cast wheel of the same load rating, with **near-zero porosity** thanks to the consolidated grain flow.

---

### 2.4 Wheel Specification Codes ŌĆö How to Read a Wheel Marking

A KTU 2024 short-answer question may ask: *"Explain the following wheel marking: **6.5 J ├Ś 16 H2 ET45 5 ├Ś 114.3"*.*

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|:-------|:--------|:-------------|
| **6.5** | Rim width | inches |
| **J** | Rim flange profile (J = standard car) | ŌĆö |
| **16** | Nominal rim diameter | inches |
| **H2** | Two humps on rim flange (safety) | ŌĆö |
| **ET45** | Einpresstiefe (offset) = **45 mm** | mm |
| **5 ├Ś 114.3** | 5 stud holes on **114.3 mm** PCD | mm |

#### 2.4.1 Wheel Offset ŌĆö The Hidden Critical Parameter

$$\boxed{\text{ET} \;=\; a - b}$$

where:
- $a$ = distance from the **hub-mounting face** to the **centreline of the rim** (mm)
- $b$ = distance from the hub-mounting face to the **outermost plane of the wheel** (mm)
- $\text{ET} > 0$ ŌåÆ wheel is pulled **inward** (towards vehicle)
- $\text{ET} < 0$ ŌåÆ wheel is pushed **outward**
- $\text{ET} = 0$ ŌåÆ wheel is centred on the hub

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Deviating from OEM-specified offset by more than **┬▒5 mm** on a MacPherson-strut car causes **pulling-drift**, altered scrub radius, and accelerated bearing wear.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Variables / Units |
|:-:|:-------------------|:-----------|:------------------|
| 1 | Wheel load transfer under braking (dynamic axle load) | $W_{d,\text{front}} = W \cdot \dfrac{h}{L} \cdot \dfrac{a}{g}$ | $W$ = static axle load (N), $h$ = CG height (m), $L$ = wheelbase (m), $a$ = deceleration (m/s┬▓) |
| 2 | Unsprung mass reduction benefit (suspension deflection ratio) | $\dfrac{f_{new}}{f_{old}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{us,old}}{m_{us,new}}}$ | $m_{us}$ = unsprung mass (kg), $f$ = wheel-hop natural frequency (Hz) |
| 3 | Wheel offset definition | $\text{ET} = a - b$ | $a, b$ in mm |
| 4 | Effective rolling radius (for gear-ratio / speedo) | $r_{eff} = \dfrac{r_{stat} + r_{dyn}}{2}$ | $r$ in m |
| 5 | Dynamic loaded radius (cornering stiffness model) | $r_{dyn} \approx r_{stat} \cdot (1 - 0.12)$ | empirical 12% reduction |
| 6 | Centrifugal stress at rim (rotating disc) | $\sigma = \rho \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{R^2 - r^2}{2}\right)$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m┬│), $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s), $R, r$ = outer / inner radii (m) |
| 7 | Brake heat input into wheel | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot \eta_{brake}$ | $m$ = vehicle mass, $v$ = speed, $\eta_{brake}$ = braking efficiency |
| 8 | Bolt-clamp load (wheel-stud torque) | $F_{clamp} = \dfrac{T}{k \cdot d}$ | $T$ = torque (N┬Ęm), $k$ Ōēł 0.2 nut factor, $d$ = nominal bolt dia. (m) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula 6 (centrifugal stress) is used by KTU examiners in module-3 numericals to compare aluminium vs. steel wheels at high vehicle speeds. For an **Al wheel** with $\rho \approx 2700$ kg/m┬│ vs. **steel** with $\rho \approx 7850$ kg/m┬│, the stress is **Ōēł 2.9├Ś lower** for aluminium at the same spin speed ŌĆö the fundamental reason alloy wheels are preferred in performance cars.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivations Required for KTU Module 3 (Wheel Sub-Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 scheme Module-3 numericals (typically 7-mark sub-questions) often test the two derivations below. Work through every algebraic step.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 1 ŌĆö Centrifugal Stress in a Rotating Solid Disc Wheel

**Physical Model:** A disc wheel is approximated as a *solid rotating disc* of inner radius $r_i$ (hub bore) and outer radius $r_o$ (rim), rotating at angular speed $\omega$. The disc has uniform thickness $t$ and density $\rho$.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Free-Body of a Differential Ring Element

Consider a thin ring at radius $r$ of width $dr$. The radial forces on this element are:
- Radial stress $\sigma_r$ acting on inner face (area $= t \cdot r \cdot d\theta$)
- Radial stress $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)$ on outer face
- Centrifugal body force per unit volume $= \rho \omega^2 r$

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Equilibrium of the Ring

Summing radial forces (for a small arc $d\theta$):

$$\begin{aligned}
(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)(r + dr)\,t\,d\theta \;-\; \sigma_r \, r\, t\, d\theta \;-\; 2 \sigma_t \, t\, dr \, \frac{d\theta}{2} \;=\; \rho \omega^2 r \, t \, dr \, d\theta
\end{aligned}$$

where $\sigma_t$ = tangential (hoop) stress.

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Simplification

Dividing throughout by $t\, dr\, d\theta$ and applying **rotational symmetry** ($\sigma_t$ contributes via the curved surfaces of the ring), the equilibrium equation reduces to:

$$r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + (\sigma_r - \sigma_t) + \rho \omega^2 r^2 = 0$$

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Compatibility (Plane Stress in a Disc)

For a thin disc rotating in its own plane, the strainŌĆōdisplacement relations and Hooke's Law (with $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio) give the **compatibility equation**:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} \right) - \frac{\sigma_r}{r} = -\frac{3 + \nu}{1 - \nu} \, \rho \omega^2 r$$

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the Differential Equation

Let $k = \dfrac{3 + \nu}{1 - \nu}$. The general solution of the inhomogeneous Bessel-type equation is:

$$\sigma_r(r) = A - \frac{B}{r^2} - \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 r^2$$

(derived by integrating twice, where the homogeneous part gives $A - B/r^2$ and the particular integral is $-\dfrac{3+\nu}{8}\rho\omega^2 r^2$).

#### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply Boundary Conditions

- **Inner radius $r = r_i$:** bore is free ŌåÆ $\sigma_r(r_i) = 0$
- **Outer radius $r = r_o$:** rim is free ŌåÆ $\sigma_r(r_o) = 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r(r_i) &= A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} - \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \rho \omega^2 r_i^2 = 0 \\
\sigma_r(r_o) &= A - \frac{B}{r_o^2} - \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \rho \omega^2 r_o^2 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 7 ŌĆö Solve for A and B

Subtracting the two equations:

$$B \left( \frac{1}{r_i^2} - \frac{1}{r_o^2} \right) = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \rho \omega^2 (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$$

$$\boxed{\;B = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \, \frac{r_i^2 r_o^2 (r_o^2 - r_i^2)}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1}\;}$$

Simplify:

$$B = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \, r_i^2 r_o^2$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\;A = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \, r_i^2 + \frac{B}{r_i^2} = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \, r_o^2 + \frac{B}{r_o^2}\;}$$

Since both give the same value, the closed form becomes:

$$A = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \, (r_i^2 + r_o^2)$$

#### Step 8 ŌĆö Final Stress Equations

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_r(r) = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \left[ r_i^2 + r_o^2 - r^2 - \frac{r_i^2 r_o^2}{r^2} \right]\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_t(r) = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \left[ r_i^2 + r_o^2 - \frac{1 + 3\nu}{3 + \nu} \, r^2 + \frac{r_i^2 r_o^2}{r^2} \right]\;}$$

#### Step 9 ŌĆö Maximum Stress Location

The **maximum tangential stress** occurs at the **inner bore** ($r = r_i$):

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \left[ 2 r_o^2 + \frac{1 - 3\nu}{3 + \nu} \, r_i^2 \right]\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For $\nu = 0.3$ (steel/aluminium),** this simplifies to:
> $\sigma_{t,\max} \approx 0.4125 \, \rho \omega^2 (2r_o^2 + 0.143 r_i^2)$
> This is the value compared against the material's yield strength for **speed-rated wheel design** (e.g., a 16" alloy wheel rated for 240 km/h).

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 2 ŌĆö Wheel Offset & Its Effect on Scrub Radius

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Define Geometry

- $a$ = distance from wheel centre to hub-mount face (rear) = $r_o/2 + \text{ET}$ ... but KTU exams use:
- $a$ = distance from hub face to rim centreline (this *is* ET, the offset)
- $b$ = distance from hub face to the wheel's outer plane (typically rim diameter / 2 = $r_o$)

#### Step 2 ŌĆö The Definition Re-derived

By definition, the **Einpresstiefe (offset)** is the axial distance from the wheel's hub-mounting plane to the rim's centre-plane:

$$\boxed{\;\text{ET} = a - b\;}$$

where
- $a$ = distance from hub-mounting surface to rim **centreline** (positive forward)
- $b$ = distance from hub-mounting surface to wheel's **outer face** (typically = $r_o$ if we measure to the rim's outer flange)

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Scrub Radius from Offset

The **scrub radius** $r_{scrub}$ is the moment-arm between the tyre's steering-axis intersection (kingpin) and the tyre's **contact-patch centre** projected on the road:

$$\boxed{\;r_{scrub} = r_{kingpin} - \text{ET}\;}$$

where $r_{kingpin}$ = perpendicular distance from kingpin axis to the **hub-mounting plane**.

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Steering-Torque Reaction Under Braking

When a brake torque $T_b$ is applied, the wheel generates a reaction torque that *twists* the steering:

$$\boxed{\;T_{steer} = T_b \cdot \dfrac{r_{scrub}}{r_{tyre}}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-world consequence:** If scrub radius ŌēĀ 0 and the left/right wheels are *asymmetric* in offset, the car **pulls to one side** under braking ŌĆö a common KTU viva question on brake-balance.

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

A sedan has $\text{ET} = 45$ mm, $r_{kingpin} = 60$ mm, $r_{tyre} = 315$ mm. The front brake torque per wheel is $T_b = 800$ N┬Ęm. Find the steering disturbance torque.

**Given:** $\text{ET} = 45$ mm $= 0.045$ m, $r_{kingpin} = 0.060$ m, $r_{tyre} = 0.315$ m, $T_b = 800$ N┬Ęm

**Step 5.1 ŌĆö Scrub radius:**
$$r_{scrub} = r_{kingpin} - \text{ET} = 0.060 - 0.045 = 0.015 \text{ m} \;=\; 15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5.2 ŌĆö Steering disturbance torque:**
$$T_{steer} = 800 \times \frac{0.015}{0.315}$$

$$T_{steer} = 800 \times 0.0476$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{steer} \approx 38.1 \text{ N┬Ęm per wheel}\;}$$

This is the torque that *tries* to rotate the steering under hard braking. A 0-scrub-radius design (or LCR zero-offset configuration) is the modern solution used in EVs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Wheel Type Selection

| Criterion | Pressed Steel | Cast Aluminium | Forged Aluminium | Carbon-Fibre Composite |
|:----------|:--------------|:---------------|:-----------------|:-----------------------|
| **Density (kg/m┬│)** | 7850 | 2700 | 2700 | 1600 |
| **Typical weight, 16" wheel (kg)** | 9ŌĆō11 | 7ŌĆō9 | 5ŌĆō7 | 4ŌĆō5 |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 400 | 220ŌĆō260 | 360ŌĆō420 | 500ŌĆō800 (fibre) |
| **Fatigue life (cycles, 10ŌüČ)** | 2.5 | 1.5 | 3.5 | 5+ |
| **Cost (relative)** | 1├Ś | 3ŌĆō4├Ś | 6ŌĆō8├Ś | 15ŌĆō25├Ś |
| **Repairability** | Easy (hammer) | Weld + heat-treat | Specialist only | Replace |
| **Heat dissipation** | Moderate | Good | Very good | Limited (matrix degrades) |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Poor (needs coat) | Good (oxide layer) | Good | Excellent |
| **Suitability for tubeless** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Typical vehicle segment** | Mass-market cars | Mid-premium sedans, SUVs | Sports / luxury | Hypercars (Porsche, Koenigsegg) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Wheel Subsystem in a Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Road Surface Excitations] --> B[Tyre Tread Contact Patch]
    B --> C[Sidewall Flex Zone]
    C --> D[Bead Seat on Rim Flange]
    D --> E[Rim Drop Centre]
    E --> F[Wheel Disc Web]
    F --> G[Ventilation Cutouts and Spoke Openings]
    G --> H[Hub Mounting Boss]
    H --> I[Stud Bolts 4 5 or 6 Pattern]
    I --> J[Brake Rotor or Drum]
    J --> K[Wheel Hub Bearing]
    K --> L[Drive Shaft or Stub Axle]
    L --> M[Suspension Lower Link]
    M --> N[Vehicle Sprung Mass]
    N --> O[Damper Input]
    O --> P[Gas Filled Damper]
    P --> Q[Damping Force Returned to Sprung Mass]
    Q --> N
    H --> R[Wheel Speed Sensor Target Ring]
    R --> S[ABS ECU Signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The road excitation travels *up* the load path (AŌåÆN), while the damper force travels *down* (PŌåÆN). The wheel is the *mechanical bridge* between them ŌĆö hence the importance of its mass, stiffness, and mounting geometry.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Taxonomy of Wheel Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Automotive Wheels] --> D[Disc Type Wheels]
    W --> S[Spoke Type Wheels]
    D --> D1[Pressed Steel Disc]
    D --> D2[Cast Aluminium Disc]
    D --> D3[Forged Aluminium Disc]
    D --> D4[Hybrid Two Piece Disc]
    S --> S1[Wire Spoke Wheel]
    S --> S2[Cast Spoke Wheel]
    D2 --> D2a[Low Pressure Die Cast]
    D2 --> D2b[High Pressure Die Cast]
    D2 --> D2c[Gravity Die Cast]
    D3 --> D3a[Closed Die Forged]
    D3 --> D3b[Flow Formed Hybrid]
    S1 --> S1a[Radial Wire]
    S1 --> S1b[Tangential Wire]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite:** Memorize the **two-axis classification** (Construction ├Ś Process) and be ready to map any real-world vehicle's wheel to the correct cell of this taxonomy.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Gas Filled Damper Operating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Suspension Upward Stroke] --> V1[Compression Valve]
    A2[Suspension Rebound Stroke] --> V2[Rebound Valve]
    N2[Nitrogen Gas Pre charge 8 to 25 bar] --> GP[Gas Piston Floating]
    GP --> OIL[Hydraulic Oil Column]
    OIL --> V1
    OIL --> V2
    V1 --> DC[Damping Force Output Compression]
    V2 --> DR[Damping Force Output Rebound]
    DC --> X[Vehicle Body]
    DR --> X
    N2 --> RP[Reserve Chamber Compensates Oil Volume]
    RP --> OIL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Link to Wheels:** The damper's *unsprung-side mount* attaches directly to the **wheel hub** (via the strut or lower link). Hence, the wheel's *unsprung mass* determines the inertia that the gas damper must control. Reducing wheel mass by 1 kg reduces the damper's required force by roughly the same factor at the same wheel-hop frequency.

---

### 4.4 Schematic Cross-Section ŌĆö Pressed-Steel Disc Wheel

```mermaid

    R1[Outer Rim Flange] --> R2[Bead Seat]
    R2 --> R3[Safety Hump H2]
    R3 --> R4[Drop Centre Well]
    R4 --> R5[Inner Rim Flange]
    R5 --> W1[Disc Web]
    W1 --> W2[Brake Side Stamping]
    W1 --> W3[Ventilation Cutouts 4 to 6]
    W1 --> W4[Hub Bore with Spigot]
    W4 --> W5[Stud Bolt Holes 4 5 or 6]
    W4 --> W6[Centre Cap Recess]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. List the functional requirements of an automotive wheel.** *(Model ŌĆö 3 marks)*

> `[KTU University Exam - April 2024]`

**Model Answer (Ōēł 80 words, 3 crisp points):**

1. **Load-bearing capacity** ŌĆö must sustain static and dynamic road loads (vertical, cornering, braking) with adequate fatigue life (Ōēł 10ŌüĘ cycles/yr).
2. **Low unsprung mass** ŌĆö to minimise road-excitation transmission and improve damper effectiveness.
3. **Dimensional accuracy & airtightness** ŌĆö to ensure concentric mounting, low run-out, and reliable sealing of tubeless-tyre air (Ōēź 1.5 bar) over the service life.
4. **Heat dissipation & corrosion resistance** ŌĆö to dissipate brake heat and survive environmental exposure.

*(1 mark for each of 3 points; ┬Į mark for additional value-add like heat dissipation.)*

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between a disc wheel and a spoke wheel.** *(3 marks)*

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Disc Wheel | Spoke Wheel |
|:--------|:-----------|:------------|
| Construction | Single continuous disc connecting hub and rim | Hub connected to rim via radial spokes (wires or cast arms) |
| Unsprung mass | Higher (closed geometry) | Lower (open geometry) |
| Tubeless-tyre compatible | Yes (sealed rim) | Difficult (wire ends can puncture liner) |
| Maintenance | Low (welded/riveted) | High (periodic spoke-tensioning) |
| Typical use | Modern cars, LCVs, trucks | Vintage cars, motorcycles, classics |

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You are expected to write *Ōēł 1 page per 7 marks* with diagrams.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Comprehensive Coverage of Wheel Types & Materials

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Classify automotive wheels based on construction. Explain with neat sketches the construction of (i) pressed-steel disc wheel and (ii) wire-spoke wheel. List the materials used for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Classification (2 marks):**
   - **Disc (solid) wheels** ŌĆö pressed steel, cast alloy, forged alloy
   - **Spoke wheels** ŌĆö wire-spoke, cast-spoke
   - **Composite/hybrid** ŌĆö multi-piece, carbon-fibre

2. **Pressed-steel disc wheel (3 marks):**
   - Sketch showing rim, drop centre, safety humps, disc web, hub bore, lug holes
   - **Material:** Cold-rolled low-carbon steel (EN 10130 DC04, 1.5ŌĆō3 mm); deep-drawn and flash-welded to the rim
   - **Why steel?** Low cost, good formability, repairable

3. **Wire-spoke wheel (2 marks):**
   - Sketch showing hub, threaded spokes, nipples, separate rim
   - **Material:** Stainless-steel wire (├ś 2.5ŌĆō4 mm); spokes in pure tension
   - **Why tension-only?** Spokes are pre-tensioned so cornering/braking loads only *add* to tension ŌĆö the structure behaves like a pre-stressed cable net

**(b) With the help of a flow diagram, describe the manufacturing process of a cast-aluminium alloy wheel. Compare the properties of cast-aluminium, forged-aluminium, and pressed-steel wheels in a tabular form. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Process Flow (3 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** Alloy selection ŌĆö A356 / A357 (Al-Si-Mg)
   - **Step 2:** Melting (Ōēł 720 ┬░C) + degassing + grain refinement
   - **Step 3:** Low-pressure die-casting or gravity die-casting into a permanent mould
   - **Step 4:** Cooling, shake-out, trimming
   - **Step 5:** T6 heat-treatment (solutionising Ōēł 540 ┬░C, water-quench, artificial ageing Ōēł 160 ┬░C ├Ś 6 h)
   - **Step 6:** Machining of hub bore, PCD, and rim-sealing surfaces
   - **Step 7:** X-ray inspection, leak test, dynamic balancing, painting

2. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** *(Use the analytical matrix from ┬¦3.4 of these notes.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Wheel Specification, Stress Analysis, and Gas Damper Link

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) A 16-inch cast-aluminium automobile wheel has inner radius $r_i = 80$ mm, outer radius $r_o = 200$ mm, and is rotating at a vehicle speed of 180 km/h. Calculate the maximum tangential stress in the wheel disc. Take density $\rho = 2700$ kg/m┬│ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.33$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r_i = 0.080$ m, $r_o = 0.200$ m
- $v = 180$ km/h $= 50$ m/s
- Rim diameter $D = 2r_o = 0.400$ m
- $\omega = v / r_o = 50 / 0.200 = 250$ rad/s
- $\rho = 2700$ kg/m┬│, $\nu = 0.33$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute factor $(3 + \nu)$:**
$$3 + \nu = 3 + 0.33 = 3.33$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the maximum-tangential-stress formula at $r = r_i$ (derived in ┬¦3.2):**
$$\sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{3 + \nu}{8} \, \rho \omega^2 \left[ 2 r_o^2 + \frac{1 - 3\nu}{3 + \nu} \, r_i^2 \right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute inner term $(1 - 3\nu)/(3 + \nu)$:**
$$\frac{1 - 3 \times 0.33}{3.33} = \frac{1 - 0.99}{3.33} = \frac{0.01}{3.33} = 0.00300$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute numerical values:**
$$\sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{3.33}{8} \times 2700 \times (250)^2 \times \left[ 2 \times (0.200)^2 + 0.00300 \times (0.080)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate bracket:**
$$2 \times 0.0400 = 0.0800$$
$$0.00300 \times 0.00640 = 0.0000192$$
$$\text{Sum} = 0.0800192 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Multiply the constants:**
$$\frac{3.33}{8} = 0.41625$$
$$0.41625 \times 2700 = 1123.875$$
$$1123.875 \times 62500 = 7.0242 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final result:**
$$\sigma_{t,\max} = 7.0242 \times 10^{7} \times 0.0800$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{t,\max} \approx 5.62 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 5.62 \text{ MPa}\;}$$

**Conclusion:** This stress is well below the yield strength of cast A356-T6 (Ōēł 200 MPa), giving a safety factor of Ōēł 35 ŌĆö typical for OEM wheel design.

**(b) Explain the principle of a gas-filled damper. With the help of a diagram, describe its construction and explain how a nitrogen-gas pre-charge improves damping performance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):**
   - A telescopic hydraulic damper uses **viscous flow of oil** through orifices to dissipate kinetic energy of the suspension as heat.
   - A **gas-filled** damper adds a **compressible nitrogen pre-charge** in a floating-piston chamber, separated from the oil by a free piston.

2. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - Outer tube, inner rod, piston with compression and rebound valve stacks, **floating gas piston**, nitrogen chamber, **base valve**, oil reservoir
   - Diagram: include both strokes (compression and rebound)

3. **Why nitrogen? (2 marks):**
   - **Cavitation prevention** ŌĆö dissolved gas bubbles in oil collapse under low pressure, causing harshness ("cavitation knock"). The pressurised gas blanket suppresses this.
   - **Stiff progressive response** ŌĆö at large piston strokes, gas compression adds a *spring* effect, preventing damper fade.
   - **Typical pre-charge:** 8ŌĆō25 bar, depending on application (passenger car Ōēł 12ŌĆō18 bar; motorsport Ōēł 25 bar)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Deducting Errors:**
> 1. **Confusing offset ET with PCD** ŌĆö they are *different* specifications (ET = mm, PCD = mm between studs). Many students write only one.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation boundary conditions** ŌĆö in stress problems, KTU examiners award 2 marks *specifically* for applying $\sigma_r(r_i) = 0$ and $\sigma_r(r_o) = 0$.
> 3. **Mixing up "disc" and "spoke"** ŌĆö students often draw a motorbike wheel and call it a car wheel. The KTU Module 3 syllabus is *automotive*; motorcycles are out of scope unless specified.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the offset / scrub-radius sub-part. KTU deducts **┬Į mark** per missing unit in numericals.
> 5. **In gas-damper answers**, failing to mention the **floating piston** ŌĆö it is the *defining* feature of a gas-filled damper (vs. a conventional hydraulic damper). Missing this loses Ōēł 1.5 marks.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-15-minute revision sheet before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- [x] A **wheel** is the rotating disc that mounts the tyre to the hub; it carries vertical, cornering, and braking loads.
- [x] **Two-axis classification:** Disc vs. Spoke **AND** Steel vs. Aluminium vs. Forged vs. Composite.
- [x] **Pressed-steel disc wheels** dominate mass-market cars ŌĆö low cost, repairable, but heavy.
- [x] **Cast-aluminium wheels** use A356 alloy, LPDC/HPDC, then T6 heat-treatment.
- [x] **Forged aluminium wheels** are **20ŌĆō30% lighter**, denser grain, higher fatigue life ŌĆö used in performance cars.
- [x] **Wire-spoke wheels** carry load *in pure tension*; spokes are pre-stressed.
- [x] **Wheel marking `6.5J├Ś16 H2 ET45 5├Ś114.3`** = 6.5" wide, J-flange, 16" diameter, double hump, +45 mm offset, 5 studs on 114.3 mm PCD.
- [x] **Offset formula:** $\text{ET} = a - b$ (positive = wheel pulled inboard).
- [x] **Scrub radius:** $r_{scrub} = r_{kingpin} - \text{ET}$; non-zero scrub causes braking-induced steering disturbance.
- [x] **Centrifugal stress in a rotating disc** is maximum at the **inner bore**; aluminium (lower density) gives Ōēł 2.9├Ś lower stress than steel at the same spin speed.
- [x] **Gas-filled damper** uses a **nitrogen pre-charge (8ŌĆō25 bar)** behind a **floating piston** to suppress cavitation and prevent fade.
- [x] **Wheel Ōåö Damper coupling:** Damper's unsprung mount is on the wheel hub ŌĆö hence lower unsprung mass (lighter forged wheel) directly improves damper effectiveness.
- [x] **Safety-factor language in KTU answers:** Always state *factor of safety* and compare with *allowable stress / yield strength* in numericals.

---

**END OF MODULE-3 NOTES ŌĆö TYPES OF WHEELS (PCMUT302, KTU 2024 SCHEME)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tyres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **pneumatic automobile tyre** is a toroidal-shaped, load-bearing, flexible elastomeric composite structure mounted on the wheel rim of a vehicle. It is the **only physical contact patch** between the vehicle and the road surface, transmitting longitudinal tractive/braking forces, lateral cornering forces, and vertical load between the sprung mass of the vehicle and the road.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a tyre is defined as a **multi-ply composite pressure vessel** consisting of:
- An **inner liner** (halogenated butyl rubber) for air retention
- A **carcass** (plies of polyester, rayon, nylon, or steel cords embedded in rubber)
- **Bead bundles** (steel wire hoops) that anchor the tyre to the rim
- **Sidewalls** (flexible rubber panels carrying size and rating information)
- A **crown** containing the **belt package** (steel + nylon cap plies) and the **tread** (wear-resistant rubber compound in contact with the road)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Use in Exams):**
> *"A pneumatic tyre is a load-carrying flexible membrane, inflated with compressed air, designed to cushion the vehicle from road irregularities, transmit driving, braking and cornering forces, and provide directional stability and ride comfort."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Foot of the Car"

Imagine you are walking barefoot on a stony road. To make walking more comfortable and to grip the stones better, you wear a soft-soled **sneaker**:
- The **rubber sole** grips the stones ŌåÆ analogous to the **tread rubber**.
- The **foam mid-sole** absorbs shocks from the stones ŌåÆ analogous to the **air cushion** inside the tyre.
- The **canvas/leather upper** wraps the foot and resists tearing ŌåÆ analogous to the **sidewall and carcass plies**.
- The **lace eyelets** lock the shoe to your foot ŌåÆ analogous to the **bead bundle** gripping the rim.

A car tyre performs exactly this role ŌĆö but for a 1,500 kg vehicle traveling at 100 km/h, instead of a 70 kg person walking at 5 km/h. The pressures and forces are scaled up dramatically, which is why the engineering of tyres is so rigorous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact (Memorize):** The contact patch of a typical passenger car tyre is roughly the size of a **postcard (~150 cm┬▓ to 200 cm┬▓)** ŌĆö yet it carries approximately **300ŌĆō400 kg** of vehicle weight per tyre!

---

## 1.3 Primary Functions of a Tyre (KTU High-Yield List)

A modern automotive tyre must simultaneously satisfy **six critical functions**:

| S.No. | Function | Engineering Parameter |
|:-----:|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Support vertical load | Load index (LI) and inflation pressure |
| 2 | Cushion against road shocks | Pneumatic spring rate + carcass compliance |
| 3 | Transmit driving (tractive) torque | Longitudinal slip vs. traction coefficient |
| 4 | Transmit braking torque | Brake slip ratio vs. friction coefficient |
| 5 | Generate cornering (lateral) force | Slip angle vs. cornering stiffness |
| 6 | Provide steering/directional stability | Camber thrust + pneumatic trail + conicity |

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally used in tyre engineering and are **bold-marked** because examiners frequently quote them:

- **Standard cold inflation pressure:** **200 kPa (2.0 bar / 29 psi)** for passenger cars
- **Standard test temperature:** **25 ┬░C** (ISO 28580)
- **Aspect ratio range:** **30 (low-profile) to 80 (truck)**
- **Rolling resistance coefficient (CßĄŻßĄŻ):** **0.008 ŌĆō 0.014** for radial passenger tyres
- **Coefficient of friction (╬╝):** **0.7 ŌĆō 1.0 (dry), 0.4 ŌĆō 0.6 (wet)**
- **Tyre deflection under load:** **~15ŌĆō20% of section height** at rated load
- **Carcass ply angle (radial):** **90┬░** (perpendicular to the direction of travel)
- **Carcass ply angle (bias-ply):** **40┬░ŌĆō65┬░** (crossed diagonally)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Most numerical problems (2-mark and 14-mark) test your ability to convert between **bar / kPa / psi** and to interpret **load index / speed rating charts**. Always state units explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tyre Construction ŌĆö Layer-by-Layer Anatomy

A radial tubeless passenger car tyre consists of **seven functionally distinct layers**, manufactured from the inside out:

### 2.1.1 Inner Liner
- **Material:** Halogenated butyl rubber (IIR ŌĆö Isobutylene-Isoprene Rubber).
- **Thickness:** ~1.0 ŌĆō 1.5 mm.
- **Function:** Provides air-tightness by virtue of extremely low gas permeability (Ōēł 10Ōü╗┬╣ŌüĄ m┬▓┬ĘsŌü╗┬╣┬ĘPaŌü╗┬╣).
- **KTU fact:** Inner liner replaced the inner tube in **1948 (Goodyear)**, giving birth to the **tubeless tyre** ŌĆö a revolutionary milestone.

### 2.1.2 Carcass (Body Ply)
- **Materials (modern):** Polyester (most common), rayon, or nylon cords.
- **Cords per cm:** typically 8 ŌĆō 12 (epi = ends per inch).
- **Cord angle:**
  * **Radial tyre:** 90┬░ (perpendicular to equatorial plane)
  * **Bias-ply tyre:** 40┬░ ŌĆō 65┬░ (alternating crossed plies)
- **Function:** Carries the inflation pressure as a tension load (like a pressure vessel) and resists road-induced flexing.

### 2.1.3 Sidewall
- **Material:** Natural rubber + polybutadiene blend (high flex fatigue resistance).
- **Function:** Protects the carcass from curb cuts, ozone cracking, and flex damage. Carries moulded-in information:
  * Manufacturer name & logo
  * Tyre size designation (e.g., **205/55 R16**)
  * Load index & speed symbol (e.g., **91V**)
  * DOT serial number
  * Treadwear / Traction / Temperature grades (US market)
  * Moulded-in balance marker (red/yellow dot for rim match-mounting)

### 2.1.4 Bead Bundle
- **Material:** High-tensile **steel wire** (often brass-coated for rubber adhesion), twisted into a hexagonal bundle.
- **Count:** Single bead for passenger, dual bead for light-truck and commercial tyres.
- **Function:** Mechanically anchors the tyre to the rim flange; contains the inflation pressure at the rim interface.

### 2.1.5 Bead Filler (Apex)
- **Material:** Stiff rubber compound (high durometer, ~85 Shore A).
- **Shape:** Triangular cross-section.
- **Function:** Provides a smooth stiffness transition between the rigid bead bundle and the flexible sidewall, controlling the **bead unseating force** during cornering.

### 2.1.6 Belt Package
- **Materials:** Steel cord belts (2 layers, counter-rotating) + optional **nylon cap ply** (0┬░ ŌĆō 0┬░, i.e., circumferential).
- **Belt angles:** Typically **+18┬░ / ŌĆō18┬░** with respect to the equatorial plane.
- **Function:** 
  * Restricts the tread band from moving circumferentially.
  * Provides **tread band rigidity** for high-speed stability.
  * Acts as a **circumferential tension band** (think of it as a "hoop") that supports the load and reduces rolling resistance.

### 2.1.7 Tread (Crown Rubber)
- **Material:** SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) + BR (Butadiene Rubber) + silica + carbon black + plasticizers + cure system.
- **Thickness:** ~6 ŌĆō 10 mm (new), replaced by **TWI (Tread Wear Indicator)** bars at 1.6 mm.
- **Function:** 
  * Wear resistance
  * Wet grip (via **sipes** that break water film)
  * Noise attenuation (via **pitch sequencing**)
  * Cornering force generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Differentiate between radial and bias-ply tyres," always draw a **cross-section sketch** showing cord angles ŌĆö examiners award easy marks for labelled diagrams.

---

## 2.2 Tyre Size Designation ŌĆö The Universal Code

Modern metric (P-metric) tyre designation follows the **ISO 4000-1** standard. Example:

$$
\textbf{205/55 R 16 91V}
$$

Decoding each element:

$$
\begin{aligned}
205 &= \text{Section width (mm)} \\
55 &= \text{Aspect ratio} = \frac{\text{Section Height}}{\text{Section Width}} \times 100 \,(\%) \\
\text{R} &= \text{Radial construction} \\
16 &= \text{Rim diameter (inches)} \\
91 &= \text{Load index} \; (91 \rightarrow 615 \, \text{kg per tyre}) \\
\text{V} &= \text{Speed symbol} \; (V \rightarrow 240 \, \text{km/h max})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion discipline:** 1 inch = **25.4 mm exactly**. Always carry this constant in numerical problems.

---

## 2.3 Overall Tyre Dimensions ŌĆö Key Geometric Formulas

### 2.3.1 Section Height (H)
$$
H = W \times \frac{AR}{100} \quad \text{[mm]}
$$

### 2.3.2 Overall Diameter (OD)
$$
OD = 2H + R_{rim} \quad \text{[mm]}
$$

Substituting H:

$$
OD = 2\left(W \times \frac{AR}{100}\right) + R_{rim}
$$

### 2.3.3 Static Loaded Radius (RŌéŚ)
At rated load, the tyre deflects by approximately **15ŌĆō20%** of section height:

$$
R_l = \frac{OD}{2} - 0.15 \times H \quad \text{[mm]}
$$

### 2.3.4 Tyre Circumference (C)
$$
C = \pi \times OD \quad \text{[mm]}
$$

### 2.3.5 Revolutions per kilometre (N)
$$
N = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{C} \quad \text{[rev/km]}
$$

### 2.3.6 Vehicle Speed Ōåö Wheel Rotational Speed
$$
v = \frac{\pi \times OD \times n}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
n = \frac{60 \times v \times 1000}{\pi \times OD} \quad \text{[rev/min]}
$$

where $v$ is in m/s and $OD$ in mm.

---

## 2.4 Tyre Mechanics ŌĆö Forces and Moments

When a tyre operates under combined slip, it generates a system of **three forces and three moments** at the contact patch (SAE sign convention):

| Symbol | Quantity | Direction |
|:------:|----------|-----------|
| FŌéō | Longitudinal force (traction/braking) | Along wheel spin axis |
| FßĄ¦ | Lateral (cornering) force | Perpendicular to wheel plane |
| F_z | Vertical (normal) load | Perpendicular to road |
| MŌéō | Overturning moment | Roll axis |
| MßĄ¦ | Rolling resistance moment | Spin axis |
| M_z | Self-aligning torque (SAT) | Vertical axis |

---

## 2.5 Slip Angle and Cornering Force

The **slip angle (╬▒)** is the angular difference between the direction the wheel is pointing and the actual direction the tyre is travelling:

$$
\alpha = \delta - \theta \quad \text{[rad or deg]}
$$

where $\delta$ = steering angle, $\theta$ = chassis yaw-induced slip at the tyre.

In the **linear region** (small ╬▒, typically |╬▒| < 4┬░ ŌĆō 6┬░):

$$
F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha
$$

where $C_\alpha$ is the **cornering stiffness** [N/rad or N/deg].

For larger slip angles, the relation becomes non-linear and saturates at $\mu F_z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$C_\alpha$ typical values:** Passenger radial = **20,000 ŌĆō 50,000 N/rad**; Racing slick = up to **100,000 N/rad**.

---

## 2.6 Longitudinal Slip and Traction

Define **slip ratio (╬║)** for a driven wheel as:

$$
\kappa = \frac{\omega R_l - v_x}{\max(\omega R_l, \, v_x)} \times 100 \,(\%)
$$

- $\omega R_l > v_x$ ŌåÆ **traction (accelerating)** ŌåÆ $\kappa > 0$
- $\omega R_l < v_x$ ŌåÆ **braking** ŌåÆ $\kappa < 0$
- $\omega R_l = v_x$ ŌåÆ **free rolling** ŌåÆ $\kappa = 0$

The longitudinal force in the **linear region** is:

$$
F_x = C_\kappa \cdot \kappa
$$

where $C_\kappa$ = longitudinal slip stiffness [N per unit slip].

---

## 2.7 Rolling Resistance

Rolling resistance arises from **hysteretic energy dissipation** in the rubber as the tyre rolls. It is modelled as a moment opposing rotation:

$$
M_y = F_z \cdot a
$$

where $a$ is the **rolling resistance arm** (typically **5 ŌĆō 10 mm**).

The **rolling resistance force** is:

$$
F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot F_z
$$

where $C_{rr}$ is the dimensionless **rolling resistance coefficient** (Ōēł **0.010 ŌĆō 0.015** for radial passenger tyres).

Equivalently:

$$
F_{rr} = \frac{M_y}{R_l}
$$

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hall Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Print This Section on Your Mind!**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|:-:|---------|---------|:-----:|
| 1 | $H = W \cdot AR/100$ | Section height | mm |
| 2 | $OD = 2H + R_{rim}$ | Overall diameter | mm |
| 3 | $C = \pi \cdot OD$ | Tyre circumference | mm |
| 4 | $N = 10^6 / C$ | Revs per km | rev/km |
| 5 | $R_l = OD/2 - 0.15H$ | Static loaded radius | mm |
| 6 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot F_z$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 7 | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ | Cornering force (linear) | N |
| 8 | $F_x = C_\kappa \cdot \kappa$ | Traction force (linear) | N |
| 9 | $v = \pi \cdot OD \cdot n / 60000$ | Vehicle speed from wheel revs | m/s |
| 10 | $\kappa = (\omega R_l - v_x)/\max(\omega R_l, v_x)$ | Slip ratio | ŌĆō |
| 11 | $P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v$ | Power loss to rolling resistance | W |
| 12 | $\text{SRR} = OD_{new}/OD_{worn}$ | Speedometer error ratio | ŌĆō |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tyre engineering intersects with:
- **Vehicle dynamics simulation** (CarSim, Adams/Tyre, MATLAB Virtual Test Track)
- **Autonomous vehicle control** (slip-based traction control, ESC, ABS)
- **EV range optimization** (rolling resistance directly determines kWh/100 km)
- **Noise, vibration, harshness (NVH)** engineering (tread pitch sequencing for cabin quietness)
- **Sustainable materials research** (silica from rice husk, dandelion-derived natural rubber, recycled carbon black)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** With the rise of electric vehicles in Kerala, tyre engineers are increasingly focused on **low rolling resistance, high load capacity, and reduced cavity noise** (because EVs have no engine masking tyre noise).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Overall Diameter of a Tyre

### Problem Setup
A passenger car tyre is marked **215/65 R15**.
Given:
- Section width $W$ = 215 mm
- Aspect ratio $AR$ = 65
- Rim diameter = 15 inches

**Find:** Overall diameter $OD$ in mm and metres.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert rim diameter from inches to millimetres.**

$$
R_{rim} = 15 \times 25.4 = 381.0 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* The international inch is exactly 25.4 mm by SI convention (since 1959). Use this exact value; do not approximate to 25 mm.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute section height H.**

$$
H = W \times \frac{AR}{100}
$$

$$
H = 215 \times \frac{65}{100} = 139.75 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* Aspect ratio is defined as (H/W) ├Ś 100%, so H = W ├Ś AR/100. Carry two decimal places; do not round prematurely.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute overall diameter OD.**

$$
OD = 2H + R_{rim}
$$

$$
OD = 2 \times 139.75 + 381.0
$$

$$
OD = 279.50 + 381.00 = 660.50 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express in metres (optional but examiner-friendly).**

$$
OD = 0.6605 \text{ m}
$$

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{OD = 660.5 \text{ mm} = 0.6605 \text{ m}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Revolutions per Kilometre

### Problem Setup
Using the same **215/65 R15** tyre, find:
(a) Circumference $C$
(b) Revolutions per km $N$
(c) Wheel RPM at a vehicle speed of 100 km/h

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) ŌĆö Circumference.**

$$
C = \pi \times OD
$$

$$
C = \pi \times 660.50 = 2075.05 \text{ mm} = 2.07505 \text{ m}
$$

*Logic:* Circumference is the perimeter of a circle of diameter OD. Use $\pi = 3.14159$ for 5-decimal accuracy.

**Step (b) ŌĆö Revolutions per km.**

$$
N = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}}{C \text{ [mm]}}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{2075.05} = 481.92 \text{ rev/km}
$$

*Logic:* 1 km = 1,000,000 mm. Each revolution covers C mm. So N = distance / distance-per-rev.

**Step (c) ŌĆö Wheel RPM at 100 km/h.**

First, convert speed to mm/min:

$$
v_{mm/min} = 100 \text{ km/h} \times \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}}{60 \text{ min}} = 1{,}666{,}667 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Then:

$$
n = \frac{v_{mm/min}}{C} = \frac{1{,}666{,}667}{2075.05} = 803.10 \text{ rev/min}
$$

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{C = 2075.05 \text{ mm}, \quad N = 481.92 \text{ rev/km}, \quad n = 803.1 \text{ rev/min at 100 km/h}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Vehicle Speed from Tyre Geometry

### Problem Setup
A car has a wheel-speed sensor reading 850 rpm. Tyre size is **195/60 R14**. Determine vehicle speed in:
(a) m/s
(b) km/h

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute section height H.**

$$
H = 195 \times \frac{60}{100} = 117.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute rim diameter in mm.**

$$
R_{rim} = 14 \times 25.4 = 355.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute OD.**

$$
OD = 2 \times 117.0 + 355.6 = 234.0 + 355.6 = 589.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute circumference.**

$$
C = \pi \times 589.6 = 1852.1 \text{ mm} = 1.8521 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute linear speed of the tyre's centre.**

Each revolution moves the car by C metres, so:

$$
v = \frac{C \times n}{60} = \frac{1.8521 \times 850}{60}
$$

$$
v = \frac{1574.3}{60} = 26.24 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convert to km/h.**

$$
v = 26.24 \times \frac{18}{5} = 26.24 \times 3.6 = 94.46 \text{ km/h}
$$

*(Because 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h exactly.)*

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{v = 26.24 \text{ m/s} = 94.46 \text{ km/h}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Cornering Force and Self-Aligning Torque

### Problem Setup
A passenger car travelling at steady state has a slip angle $\alpha = 2.5┬░$ at the front axle. The cornering stiffness of the front tyres is $C_\alpha = 35{,}000$ N/rad (per tyre). There are two front tyres. Find:
(a) Total cornering force at the front axle
(b) Self-aligning torque per tyre, given a pneumatic trail $t_p = 18$ mm

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert slip angle to radians.**

$$
\alpha_{rad} = 2.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 2.5 \times 0.0174533 = 0.04363 \text{ rad}
$$

*Logic:* 1┬░ = ŽĆ/180 rad. Always convert to radians for $C_\alpha$ in N/rad.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute cornering force per tyre.**

$$
F_{y,tyre} = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha = 35{,}000 \times 0.04363
$$

$$
F_{y,tyre} = 1527.05 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total cornering force at front axle (two tyres).**

$$
F_{y,axle} = 2 \times 1527.05 = 3054.10 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Self-aligning torque (SAT) per tyre.**

$$
M_z = F_y \cdot t_p = 1527.05 \times 0.018
$$

$$
M_z = 27.49 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

*Logic:* Pneumatic trail is the lever arm between the application point of the lateral force (rear of contact patch) and the wheel centre. SAT tends to **steer the wheel back to straight-ahead**, providing directional stability.

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{F_{y,axle} = 3054.1 \text{ N}, \quad M_z = 27.49 \text{ N┬Ęm per tyre}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Detailed Derivation: Rolling Resistance Power Loss

### Problem Setup
A car of mass **m = 1500 kg** travels at a constant **v = 90 km/h = 25 m/s**. The rolling resistance coefficient is $C_{rr} = 0.012$. Compute:
(a) Rolling resistance force at each tyre (assume equal weight distribution)
(b) Total rolling resistance power
(c) Fuel energy consumed per hour in kWh (assume drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.35$)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Vertical load per tyre.**

With 4 tyres and equal distribution:

$$
F_{z,tyre} = \frac{m \cdot g}{4} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81}{4} = 3678.75 \text{ N}
$$

*Logic:* Static weight distribution assumes 50:50 front-rear and equal left-right.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rolling resistance force per tyre.**

$$
F_{rr,tyre} = C_{rr} \cdot F_{z,tyre} = 0.012 \times 3678.75 = 44.145 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total rolling resistance force.**

$$
F_{rr,total} = 4 \times 44.145 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Power dissipated by rolling resistance.**

$$
P_{rr} = F_{rr,total} \times v = 176.58 \times 25 = 4414.5 \text{ W} \approx 4.41 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Fuel energy required per hour (chemical input).**

Energy in 1 hour at engine output:

$$
E_{out} = P_{rr} \times 3600 = 4.41 \times 3600 = 15{,}876 \text{ kJ}
$$

With drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.35$:

$$
E_{in} = \frac{E_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{15{,}876}{0.35} = 45{,}360 \text{ kJ}
$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ):

$$
E_{in,kWh} = \frac{45{,}360}{3600} = 12.6 \text{ kWh}
$$

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{F_{rr,total} = 176.58 \text{ N}, \quad P_{rr} = 4.41 \text{ kW}, \quad E_{in} = 12.6 \text{ kWh/hr}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** At constant 90 km/h, roughly **20ŌĆō30%** of the engine's chemical energy is lost to rolling resistance plus aerodynamic drag combined. This is why EV manufacturers obsess over low-$C_{rr}$ tyres!

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture ŌĆö Tyre Force Generation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Inputs<br/>Steering Angle, Throttle, Brake] --> B[Wheel Hub and Rim]
    B --> C[Tyre Contact Patch<br/>~150 to 200 sq cm]
    D[Vehicle Sprung Mass<br/>Fz, Mx, Mz loads] --> B
    E[Road Surface<br/>Texture, mu, slope] --> C
    C --> F[Longitudinal Force Fx<br/>Traction or Braking]
    C --> G[Lateral Cornering Force Fy<br/>from slip angle alpha]
    C --> H[Vertical Normal Load Fz]
    C --> I[Self Aligning Torque Mz<br/>from pneumatic trail tp]
    C --> J[Rolling Resistance Moment My<br/>from hysteresis]
    C --> K[Overturning Moment Mx<br/>from camber and Fy]
    F --> L[Vehicle Dynamics Controller<br/>ABS / ESC / TC]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Corrected Yaw, Pitch, Roll Behavior]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram abstracts the tyre as a **6-component load transducer** (3 forces + 3 moments). KTU examiners love this kind of flow diagram in 14-mark questions on "tyre functions" or "vehicle dynamics."

---

## 4.2 Tyre Cross-Sectional Architecture (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Inner Section of Tyre]
        IL[Inner Liner<br/>Halogenated Butyl Rubber<br/>Air Retention]
        CP[Carcass Ply<br/>Polyester or Nylon Cords<br/>Radial at 90 deg]
        SF[Sidewall Rubber<br/>Size and DOT markings]
        SW[Sidewall Region<br/>Flexible Deflection Zone]
    end
    subgraph T2[Outer Section of Tyre]
        TR[Tread Rubber<br/>SBR plus BR plus Silica<br/>Contact with Road]
        BP[Belt Package<br/>Steel Cords plus Nylon Cap Ply]
        BB[Bead Bundle<br/>Steel Wire Hoops<br/>Anchors to Rim]
        BF[Bead Filler Apex<br/>Stiff Rubber Transition]
    end
    IL --> CP
    CP --> SW
    SW --> SF
    CP --> BP
    BP --> TR
    CP --> BB
    BB --> BF
    BF --> SW
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow ŌĆö Choosing Radial vs Bias-Ply Tyre

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Application Required] --> Q1{Heavy Load or Offroad}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Modern or Vintage Vehicle}
    Q1 -- No --> Radial[Use Radial Ply Tyre]
    Q2 -- Modern --> Radial
    Q2 -- Vintage or Aircraft --> Bias[Use Bias Ply Tyre]
    Radial --> R1[Lower Rolling Resistance]
    Radial --> R2[Better Fuel Economy]
    Radial --> R3[Longer Tread Life]
    Radial --> R4[Higher Speed Capability]
    Bias --> B1[Stronger Sidewalls]
    Bias --> B2[Better Impact Resistance]
    Bias --> B3[Lower Cost]
    Bias --> B4[Used in Truck Earthmover Aircraft]
```

---

## 4.4 Slip Angle ŌåÆ Cornering Force Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slip Angle alpha = 0 deg] --> B[Linear Region<br/>Fy = C alpha times alpha]
    B --> C[Slip Angle alpha approx 5 to 6 deg<br/>Peak Fy approaches mu Fz]
    C --> D[Saturation Region<br/>Fy plateaus near mu Fz]
    D --> E[Large Slip Angle alpha greater than 10 deg<br/>Tyre slides sideways<br/>Friction dominated by kinetic mu]
    E --> F[Loss of lateral grip<br/>Understeer or oversteer]
```

---

## 4.5 Tread Pattern Classification (Engineering View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TP[Tread Pattern Selection] --> S1{Dominant Road Condition}
    S1 -- Dry Pavement --> Sym[Symmetric Rib<br/>Low Noise, Long Life]
    S1 -- Wet Pavement --> Asym[Asymmetric Pattern<br/>Outer Dry, Inner Wet]
    S1 -- Snow and Mud --> Dire[Directional V Pattern<br/>Self Cleaning]
    S1 -- Offroad and Rock --> Block[Aggressive Block Lugs<br/>High Void Ratio]
    S1 -- High Performance --> Slick[Semi Slick or Slick<br/>Maximum Contact Patch]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023, Model]
**Q: List any six primary functions of an automobile pneumatic tyre.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Vertical load support** ŌĆö carries the static and dynamic weight of the vehicle.
2. **Cushioning** ŌĆö absorbs road shocks via the compressed air spring and carcass compliance.
3. **Transmission of driving (tractive) torque** ŌĆö generates longitudinal force $F_x$ to propel the vehicle.
4. **Transmission of braking torque** ŌĆö generates braking force via wheel slip in the opposite sense.
5. **Generation of cornering (lateral) force** ŌĆö provides directional stability through slip angle.
6. **Steering self-alignment** ŌĆö develops self-aligning torque (SAT) from pneumatic trail.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often expect 6 of the 8 functions listed. Adding 2 more (rolling resistance generation, steering feel feedback) is bonus credit.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Model]
**Q: Explain briefly the difference between a radial-ply and a bias-ply tyre. Which is preferred for modern passenger cars and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Radial-Ply | Bias-Ply |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Cord angle | **90┬░** (perpendicular to centreline) | **40┬░ ŌĆō 65┬░** (crossed) |
| Sidewall flexibility | Highly flexible, decoupled from tread | Stiffer, integral with tread |
| Tread contact | Stiffer belt ŌåÆ flatter, larger patch | More rounded, smaller patch |
| Rolling resistance | **Lower** (~10ŌĆō15% less) | Higher |
| Heat build-up | Lower | Higher |
| Tread life | **Longer** (up to 2├Ś) | Shorter |
| Speed capability | Higher | Lower |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

**Modern passenger cars use radial-ply tyres** because of:
- Better fuel economy (low $C_{rr}$)
- Superior high-speed stability
- Longer tread life
- Better ride comfort (independent flex of tread and sidewall)

---

## PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model]

#### (a) Describe the construction of a modern tubeless radial passenger car tyre with a neat cross-sectional sketch and explain the function of each layer. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A modern **tubeless radial passenger car tyre** consists of the following layers (drawn from inside outward):

1. **Inner Liner (1 mark)**
   - Material: halogenated butyl rubber
   - Function: airtight seal, replaces inner tube

2. **Carcass / Body Ply (1 mark)**
   - Material: polyester, rayon, or nylon cords in rubber matrix
   - Cord angle: 90┬░ (radial)
   - Function: carries inflation pressure load, flexes on road input

3. **Bead Bundle (1 mark)**
   - Material: high-tensile brass-coated steel wire
   - Function: anchors tyre to rim flange

4. **Bead Filler / Apex (0.5 mark)**
   - Material: stiff rubber
   - Function: smooth stiffness transition

5. **Sidewall (0.5 mark)**
   - Material: natural rubber + polybutadiene
   - Function: protects carcass, carries moulded markings

6. **Belt Package (1 mark)**
   - Material: 2 layers of steel cord + nylon cap ply
   - Belt angles: ┬▒18┬░
   - Function: tread band rigidity, high-speed stability

7. **Tread (2 marks)**
   - Material: SBR + BR + silica + carbon black
   - Function: road contact, wear resistance, wet grip, noise attenuation
   - Pattern: symmetric/asymmetric/directional depending on application

```
(Use this text description as a labelled sketch indicator;
in the exam, draw the actual cross-section.)
```

---

#### (b) A passenger car tyre is marked **205/55 R16 91V**.
Given:
- Load index 91 ŌåÆ maximum load = 615 kg per tyre
- Speed symbol V ŌåÆ 240 km/h
- Vehicle carries 4 tyres, equally loaded

**Compute:**
(i) Section height in mm
(ii) Overall diameter in mm
(iii) Tyre circumference in m
(iv) Revolutions per km
(v) Total vehicle payload capacity in kg

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rim diameter conversion.**
$$
R_{rim} = 16 \times 25.4 = 406.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Section height H.**
$$
H = 205 \times \frac{55}{100} = 112.75 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Overall diameter OD.**
$$
OD = 2 \times 112.75 + 406.4 = 225.5 + 406.4 = 631.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Circumference C.**
$$
C = \pi \times 631.9 = 1985.27 \text{ mm} = 1.98527 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Revolutions per km.**
$$
N = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1985.27} = 503.71 \text{ rev/km}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Total payload capacity.**
$$
L_{total} = 4 \times 615 = 2460 \text{ kg}
$$

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{H = 112.75 \text{ mm}, \; OD = 631.9 \text{ mm}, \; C = 1.985 \text{ m}, \; N = 503.71 \text{ rev/km}, \; L_{total} = 2460 \text{ kg}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model]

#### (a) Define slip angle and cornering stiffness. Derive the relationship between cornering force and slip angle in the linear region, and explain what happens to cornering force at large slip angles. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Slip angle (╬▒):** the angular difference between the wheel's heading (steering angle ╬┤) and the actual direction of travel of the tyre's contact patch.
$$
\alpha = \delta - \theta
$$
- **Cornering stiffness (C_╬▒):** the slope of the linear portion of the cornering force vs. slip angle curve, expressed in **N/rad** (or N/deg).

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Empirically, the cornering force vs. slip angle curve is observed to be linear for small ╬▒. Therefore:

$$
F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha \quad \text{for} \quad |\alpha| \leq 4┬░ŌĆō6┬░
$$

where $C_\alpha$ is a constant determined by tyre construction, inflation pressure, vertical load, and road friction.

**At large slip angles (2 marks):**
- Cornering force ceases to grow linearly; it **saturates** as the contact patch approaches the friction limit $\mu F_z$.
- Beyond ~10┬░, the lateral force actually **decreases** (post-peak behaviour) because the contact patch transitions from static to sliding friction.
- $C_\alpha$ is no longer constant; the relationship is governed by the **Pacejka Magic Formula**:

$$
F_y = D \sin\!\big(C \arctan\{B \alpha - E(B\alpha - \arctan(B\alpha))\}\big)
$$

where B, C, D, E are **Pacejka coefficients** fitted empirically to experimental data.

---

#### (b) A car of mass 1800 kg travels at a constant 80 km/h. The rolling resistance coefficient is $C_{rr} = 0.011$. Compute:
(i) Total rolling resistance force (assume 50:50 axle distribution)
(ii) Power required to overcome rolling resistance
(iii) If fuel energy density is 35 MJ/L and engine efficiency is 30%, compute fuel consumption in L/100 km. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Vertical load per tyre.**

$$
F_{z,tyre} = \frac{1800 \times 9.81}{4} = 4414.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rolling resistance per tyre.**

$$
F_{rr,tyre} = 0.011 \times 4414.5 = 48.56 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total rolling resistance.**

$$
F_{rr,total} = 4 \times 48.56 = 194.24 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Power required.**

Convert speed: 80 km/h = 80/3.6 = 22.22 m/s.

$$
P_{rr} = F_{rr,total} \times v = 194.24 \times 22.22 = 4315.5 \text{ W} = 4.316 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Fuel energy per second at engine output.**

Already have $P_{rr} = 4.316$ kJ/s at the wheels.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Chemical energy per second needed (with 30% efficiency).**

$$
P_{chem} = \frac{P_{rr}}{0.30} = \frac{4.316}{0.30} = 14.39 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Energy per 100 km.**

Time for 100 km at 80 km/h: $t = 100/80 = 1.25$ h = 4500 s.

$$
E_{chem} = 14.39 \times 4500 = 64{,}755 \text{ kJ} = 64.76 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Fuel volume per 100 km.**

$$
V_{fuel} = \frac{64.76}{35} = 1.85 \text{ L/100 km}
$$

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{F_{rr,total} = 194.24 \text{ N}, \; P_{rr} = 4.32 \text{ kW}, \; \text{Fuel} = 1.85 \text{ L/100 km}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world check:** A modern EV consumes ~15 kWh/100 km, equivalent to ~54 MJ/100 km. The 1.85 L/100 km number is just the **rolling resistance component** ŌĆö actual fuel use at 80 km/h would be 5ŌĆō7 L/100 km (the rest is aerodynamic drag and drivetrain losses).

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Tyre questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert inches to mm** ŌĆö Rim diameter must always be in mm before adding to $2H$. Lost **1 mark** minimum.
> 2. **Mixing up aspect ratio formula** ŌĆö AR = H/W, so H = W ├Ś AR/100. Many students write H = W ├Ś AR. Lost **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Not converting slip angle to radians** ŌĆö If $C_\alpha$ is in N/rad, then ╬▒ must be in rad. Common error. Lost **1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring vehicle mass distribution** ŌĆö 50:50 assumption must be **stated**, not assumed silently.
> 5. **Confusing rolling resistance force with rolling resistance moment** ŌĆö Examiners check units strictly: $F_{rr}$ is in N, $M_y$ is in N┬Ęm.
> 6. **Using 4 tyres in load distribution but 2 tyres in cornering force** ŌĆö Always re-read the question: *per tyre*, *per axle*, or *total vehicle*?
> 7. **Forgetting to draw a labelled diagram** in construction questions ŌĆö A 7-mark construction answer without a sketch loses **2ŌĆō3 marks** automatically.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultra-Fast Revision Checklist ŌĆö Read 5 Minutes Before the Exam**
>
> Ō£ģ Tyre = pneumatic pressure vessel; contact patch is the only link between vehicle and road.
>
