> Ō£ģ **Tyre size 205/55 R16** decodes as: width 205 mm, AR 55%, Radial, 16-inch rim.
>
> Ō£ģ **7-layer construction:** inner liner ŌåÆ carcass ŌåÆ bead bundle ŌåÆ bead filler ŌåÆ sidewall ŌåÆ belt package ŌåÆ tread.
>
> Ō£ģ **Cord angle:** Radial = 90┬░; Bias = 40┬░ŌĆō65┬░.
>
> Ō£ģ **OD = 2H + R_rim** where R_rim is converted from inches to mm using **25.4**.
>
> Ō£ģ **Slip ratio** uses max(ŽēR_l, v_x) in denominator; sign convention: traction (+) and braking (ŌłÆ).
>
> Ō£ģ **Slip angle ╬▒ = ╬┤ ŌłÆ ╬Ė**; cornering force $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ in the linear region (|╬▒| < 4ŌĆō6┬░).
>
> Ō£ģ **Pacejka Magic Formula** governs non-linear tyre behaviour ŌĆö know the 4 coefficients B, C, D, E.
>
> Ō£ģ **Rolling resistance** $F_{rr} = C_{rr} F_z$; typical $C_{rr}$ = **0.010ŌĆō0.015** for radials.
>
> Ō£ģ **Self-aligning torque** $M_z = F_y \cdot t_p$ (pneumatic trail).
>
> Ō£ģ **Tubeless** uses inner liner; **tubed** uses separate inner tube (obsolete for cars).
>
> Ō£ģ **TWI (Tread Wear Indicator)** appears at **1.6 mm** of remaining tread ŌĆö legal minimum.
>
> Ō£ģ **6 functions:** support, cushion, traction, braking, cornering, stability.
>
> Ō£ģ **3 forces + 3 moments** at contact patch in SAE convention.
>
> Ō£ģ **Asymmetric patterns** for high-performance: outer shoulder = dry grip; inner = wet drainage.
>
> Ō£ģ **Directional (V-pattern) tyres** must not be cross-rotated (side-specific mounting).
>
> Ō£ģ **Load index 91 = 615 kg**; **Speed symbol V = 240 km/h**. Memorize the chart ranges: LI 80ŌĆō100 covers most passenger cars.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Tubed and Tubeless
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types - Tubed and Tubeless # SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Pneumatic Tyre? A **pneumatic tyre** is a toroidal-shaped, load-bearing, air-pressurised flexible cushion mounted on the wheel rim of an automobile. It performs four critical functions simultaneously: 1. Supports the vehicle load (static + dynamic) 2. Cushions road shocks (ride comfort) 3. Transmits driving, braking, and cornering forces (traction) 4. Provides directional stability and steering control > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** *"Tyres are the only point of contact between the vehicle and the road. Their classification into **tubed** and **tubeless** types is based on the method of air retention inside the tyre assembly."* ## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Types ŌĆö At a Glance The classification of automotive pneumatic tyres is based on **how compressed air is contained within the tyre-wheel assembly**. ### A. Tubed Tyre (Conventional Tyre) A tyre assembly in which the compressed air is retained by a **separate, flexible inner tube** made of butyl rubber, placed inside the tyre casing. ### B. Tubeless Tyre A tyre assembly in which the compressed air is retained **directly between the tyre casing and the wheel rim**, eliminating the need for a separate inner tube. An **air-retentive inner liner** bonded to the inside of the casing performs the sealing function. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Balloon Within a Box > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > - **Tubed Tyre = Balloon inside a Box:** Imagine filling a small balloon (the **inner tube**) with air and placing it inside a rigid cardboard box (the **tyre casing**). If you pierce the balloon, ALL the air escapes instantly because nothing holds the box against the rim. > - **Tubeless Tyre = Box with Sealed Lid:** Imagine an airtight plastic box with a rubber gasket lid (the **bead-to-rim seal**). If you poke a tiny hole, the air leaks out *slowly* because the gasket maintains pressure on the rim. This is exactly why tubeless tyres deflate gradually upon puncture ŌĆö a critical safety feature. ## 1.4 Historical Evolution Snapshot | Era | Dominant Type | Reason | |---|---|---| | Pre-1955 | Tubed (only) | Simple construction; no rim sealing technology | | 1955ŌĆō1970 | Transition Period | Development of butyl rubber inner liners and safety humps | | 1970ŌĆō1990 | Tubeless gaining share | Fuel economy mandates; safety legislation | | 1990ŌĆōPresent | Tubeless (dominant) | Mandated for new passenger cars worldwide | | Present (Two-Wheelers) | Tubeless now dominant | Even motorcycles/scooters adopted tubeless since 2005+ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** In India, all new passenger cars sold after 1983 are legally required to use **tubeless tyres**. Two-wheeler manufacturers adopted tubeless in bulk after 2010. Examiners often frame questions around this transition. # SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Construction of a Tubed Tyre A tubed tyre assembly consists of **three major sub-components** stacked concentrically: 1. **Wheel Rim** (typically a drop-centre or flat-base rim) ŌĆö provides mechanical support. 2. **Inner Tube** ŌĆö a doughnut-shaped, seamless, air-impermeable tube made of **butyl rubber** (or natural rubber blended with halobutyl). Wall thickness typically **1.5 mm to 2.5 mm**. Contains the **valve stem** (Schrader valve) for inflation/deflation. 3. **Tyre Casing** ŌĆö the outer rubber-and-fabric structure. **Does NOT need to be air-tight** because the tube holds the air. ### Cross-Sectional Anatomy of a Tubed Tyre | Component | Material | Function | |---|---|---| | Tread | Natural/Synthetic rubber + carbon black + SBR | Road contact; wear resistance; grip | | Casing Plies | Nylon/Polyester/Rayon cords in rubber matrix | Load-bearing; structural strength | | Sidewall | Flex-resistant rubber | Protects plies; carries markings | | Beads | High-tensile steel wire bundles | Anchors tyre firmly to rim | | Inner Tube | Butyl rubber | **Holds the compressed air** | | Rim Tape | Rubberised fabric | Protects tube from rim spoke ends | ## 2.2 Detailed Construction of a Tubeless Tyre A tubeless tyre assembly has **two sub-components only**, but with critically enhanced design features: 1. **Wheel Rim** ŌĆö must have a **safety hump** (also called a bead-retention ridge or safety rib) on the rim flange. This hump prevents the bead from being pushed into the rim well under sudden deflation. 2. **Tyre Casing with Inner Liner** ŌĆö the casing carries a bonded **halobutyl rubber inner liner** of thickness approximately **1 mm to 2 mm** that is **virtually air-impermeable** (10├Ś less permeable than natural rubber). ### Key Design Differences from Tubed Tyre | Design Feature | Tubed Tyre | Tubeless Tyre | |---|---|---| | Inner tube | Present (separate) | **Absent** | | Inner liner | Not mandatory | **Mandatory** (halobutyl) | | Bead-to-rim seal | Loose (rim holds tube via flange) | **Hermetic** (bead presses airtight) | | Rim type | Drop-centre or flat-base | Drop-centre with **safety hump** | | Valve | On inner tube | **Mounted directly on rim** (rubber-grommeted metal valve) | ## 2.3 Operational Mechanics ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Each Behaviour ### Why does a tubeless tyre deflate SLOWLY on puncture? When a nail pierces a tubeless tyre, the nail's body **plugs** the puncture channel. The compressed air inside escapes only **around the nail's circumference**, which has a very small cross-sectional area. The escape rate is governed by viscous flow through a thin annular gap. The tubeless design, therefore, retains **partial pressure** long enough for the driver to notice and react (typically 1ŌĆō10 minutes of mobility). ### Why does a tubed tyre deflate INSTANTLY on puncture? The inner tube is a sealed balloon. Any puncture creates a hole through which **the entire tube volume vents** to atmosphere, governed only by choked-flow dynamics. There is no rim to "back up" the seal. Air loss is **catastrophic and immediate (within seconds)**. ### Why is rolling resistance lower in tubeless? - Absence of the inner tube eliminates **tube-to-casing friction** (called *inter-ply hysteresis*). - Lower unsprung mass ŌåÆ less rotational inertia. - Better heat dissipation (no trapped air between tube and casing). - Net effect: **3ŌĆō7% fuel efficiency improvement**, often quoted in KTU answer keys. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Aspect Ratio (AR) | $AR = \dfrac{H}{W} \times 100$ | % | Tyre sidewall designation (e.g., 65 series) | | 2 | Overall Diameter (D) | $D = 2H + 2R$ | mm | Speedometer calibration, gear ratio | | 3 | Revolutions per km (N) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | rev/km | Odometer accuracy | | 4 | Tyre Load Capacity (approx.) | $L = k \cdot p \cdot A_c$ | N | Where $p$ = inflation pressure, $A_c$ = contact patch area | | 5 | Contact Patch Area (approx.) | $A_c \approx \dfrac{L}{p}$ | m┬▓ | Approximation for design calcs | | 6 | Permeability of Butyl Rubber | $Q \propto \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{L}$ | cm┬│┬Ęatm/day | Inner tube air-loss estimation | | 7 | Rolling Resistance Coeff. (Tubed) | $C_{rr,\text{tubed}} \approx 0.012$ŌĆō$0.015$ | dimensionless | Empirical | | 8 | Rolling Resistance Coeff. (Tubeless) | $C_{rr,\text{tubeless}} \approx 0.008$ŌĆō$0.012$ | dimensionless | Empirical | | 9 | Ply Rating Conversion | $PR = 2 \times (n-1)$ for n plies | ŌĆö | Load index equivalence | | 10 | Pneumatic Trail (alignment) | $t_p \approx 30$ŌĆō$50$ mm | mm | Steering returnability | > [!NOTE] > **Notation used in formulas:** > $H$ = Section Height, $W$ = Section Width, $R$ = Rim Diameter, $p$ = Inflation pressure, $L$ = Vertical load, $Q$ = Permeation rate, $\Delta P$ = Pressure differential, $t$ = Time, $L$ = Wall thickness. ## 2.5 Comparative Engineering Matrix ŌĆö Tubed vs Tubeless | Performance Parameter | Tubed Tyre | Tubeless Tyre | Winner | |---|---|---|---| | Initial air-loss rate | Higher (tube permeation) | **Very low** (halobutyl liner) | Tubeless | | Puncture behaviour | **Sudden deflation** | Slow deflation | Tubeless (safety) | | Repairability of puncture | Easy (patch the tube) | Plug-patch from outside | Tie (different methods) | | Rolling resistance | Higher | **3ŌĆō7% lower** | Tubeless | | Heat dissipation | Poor (trapped air) | **Better** (direct rim contact) | Tubeless | | Unsprung mass | Higher | **Lower** | Tubeless | | Initial cost | **Lower** | Slightly higher | Tubed | | Fuel efficiency | Baseline | **Improved** | Tubeless | | Suitability for run-flat | Not feasible | **Yes (with stiff sidewalls)** | Tubeless | | Use in heavy commercial vehicles | Still common (truck/bus) | Limited; mixed | Context-dependent | | Risk of aquaplaning | Same | Same | Neutral | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > The shift from tubed to tubeless is **not just a design preference** ŌĆö it is a **safety-driven regulatory mandate**. The slow-deflation behaviour of tubeless tyres is the foundation of modern **TPMS (Tyre Pressure Monitoring Systems)** and **run-flat technology**, both of which assume the tyre can hold residual pressure for several minutes after a puncture event. KTU examiners frequently link this topic to TPMS, ABS sensor integration, and fuel-economy regulations. # SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Tyre Section Height from Nominal Designation A standard passenger-car tyre is designated as: **205/65 R 15 91V** | Field | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 205 | Section Width (W) in mm | $W = 205$ mm | | 65 | Aspect Ratio (AR) in % | $AR = 65$% | | R | Radial construction | ŌĆö | | 15 | Rim Diameter (R) in inches | $R = 15'' = 381$ mm | | 91 | Load Index | $L = 615$ kg | | V | Speed Symbol | $V_{\max} = 240$ km/h | ### Step-by-Step Calculation of Section Height (H) $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given:}\quad W &= 205 \text{ mm}, \quad AR = 65\% \\[4pt] \text{Aspect Ratio definition:}\quad AR &= \dfrac{H}{W} \times 100 \\[4pt] \text{Step 1: Rearrange for } H \quad &\Rightarrow H = \dfrac{AR \times W}{100} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Substitute numerical values}\quad H &= \dfrac{65 \times 205}{100} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Compute numerator}\quad H &= \dfrac{13325}{100} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4: Final value}\quad \boxed{H &= 133.25 \text{ mm}} \end{aligned}$$ ### Interpretation The sidewall height is **133.25 mm**, meaning the sidewall is roughly **65% as tall as the tyre is wide**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Overall Tyre Diameter (D) The overall (outer) diameter of the wheel-tyre assembly determines the **rolling circumference**, which feeds into the speedometer and odometer calculations. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Total diameter } D &= \text{Rim diameter} + 2 \times \text{Sidewall height} \\[4pt] D &= (R_{\text{rim}} \times 25.4) + 2H \quad \text{(converting inches to mm)} \\[4pt] D &= (15 \times 25.4) + 2 \times 133.25 \\[4pt] D &= 381 + 266.5 \\[4pt] \boxed{D &= 647.5 \text{ mm}} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Revolutions per Kilometre (Speedometer Calibration) This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions** as a sub-part. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Circumference of tyre } C &= \pi \times D \\[4pt] C &= \pi \times 647.5 \quad \text{mm} \\[4pt] C &= 3.1416 \times 647.5 \\[4pt] C &\approx 2034.18 \text{ mm per revolution} \\[4pt] \text{Revolutions per km:}\quad N &= \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}}{C \text{ mm/rev}} \\[4pt] N &= \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{2034.18} \\[4pt] \boxed{N &\approx 491.6 \text{ revolutions per km}} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Comparison of Air-Loss Rate: Tubed vs Tubeless Air permeation through a rubber membrane follows **Fick's Law of Diffusion**, simplified for engineering as: $$\begin{aligned} Q &= \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{L} \quad \text{(volume of air per unit time)} \end{aligned}$$ Where $k$ is the permeability constant (cm┬│┬Ęcm / cm┬▓┬Ęatm┬Ęday). ### Comparison of Permeability Constants (Industry Data) | Material | Permeability Constant $k$ (relative) | Used In | |---|---|---| | Natural Rubber (NR) | 100 (baseline) | Old tubes | | Butyl Rubber (IIR) | **10** | Modern tubes | | Halobutyl (CIIR / BIIR) | **8** | Tubeless inner liner | ### Step-by-Step Numerical Comparison $$\begin{aligned} \text{Assumed geometry:}\quad A &= 3000 \text{ cm}^2 \text{ (inner surface area)} \\[4pt] \Delta P &= 2.0 \text{ bar} \approx 2.0 \text{ atm} \\[4pt] L &= 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ cm (tube wall thickness)} \\[4pt] Q_{\text{butyl}} &= \dfrac{10 \times 3000 \times 2.0}{0.15} \\[4pt] Q_{\text{butyl}} &= \dfrac{60{,}000}{0.15} \\[4pt] \boxed{Q_{\text{butyl}} &= 400{,}000 \text{ relative units/day}} \\[4pt] Q_{\text{halobutyl liner}} &= \dfrac{8 \times 3000 \times 2.0}{0.15} \\[4pt] Q_{\text{halobutyl liner}} &= \dfrac{48{,}000}{0.15} \\[4pt] \boxed{Q_{\text{halobutyl liner}} &= 320{,}000 \text{ relative units/day}} \end{aligned}$$ ### Interpretation Halobutyl liner of a tubeless tyre loses air at **80% the rate** of a butyl tube. Combined with the absence of an inner air-space, the **net air-loss of a tubeless assembly is 30ŌĆō40% lower** than a comparable tubed assembly under identical conditions. This is why **TPMS warning thresholds for low pressure** are typically set at **15ŌĆō25% below recommended inflation pressure**. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Decision Tree for Tyre Selection ``` START Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆ Application = Passenger Car (PC) / Two-Wheeler (2W) Ōöé ŌööŌöĆ RECOMMENDATION: Tubeless (mandatory by regulation) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆ Application = Heavy Commercial Vehicle (HCV) ŌĆö Trucks/Buses Ōöé ŌööŌöĆ Tubeless gaining share; tubed still used in some markets Ōöé due to retread economics Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆ Application = Off-Road / Agricultural / Industrial Ōöé ŌööŌöĆ Tubed (more robust against rim damage; cheaper to repair in field) Ōöé ŌööŌöĆ Application = High-Performance / Run-Flat Required ŌööŌöĆ Tubeless with reinforced sidewall (mandatory) ``` ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Failure Modes Comparison | Failure Mode | Tubed Tyre Consequence | Tubeless Tyre Consequence | |---|---|---| | Nail puncture (small) | Tube punctured ŌåÆ **instant deflation** | Nail plugs hole ŌåÆ **slow leak** | | Bead unseat (severe impact) | Tube may burst; rim flange damage | **Bead unseat** ŌåÆ potential rapid deflation | | Rim corrosion | Tube absorbs minor imperfections | Bead seal compromised ŌåÆ **slow leak** | | Valve failure | Replace tube + valve | Replace rim-mounted valve only | | Casing failure (ageing) | Tube retains air short-term | **Inner liner failure** ŌåÆ air permeation rise | | Heat buildup in tube | Tube softens ŌåÆ **blowout risk** | Better dissipation ŌåÆ lower blowout risk | # SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram ŌĆö Tubed Tyre Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OuterLayer["OUTER LAYER"] TreadBlock["TREAD BLOCK
Rubber compound with carbon black
Provides road grip and wear resistance"] end subgraph MidLayer["MIDDLE LAYER"] CasingBlock["CASING PLIES
Nylon or Polyester cords
Load-bearing radial or bias structure"] SidewallBlock["SIDEWALL
Flex-resistant rubber
Carries size markings"] end subgraph InnerAssembly["INNER ASSEMBLY"] TubeBlock["INNER TUBE
Butyl rubber
Holds compressed air at 30-35 psi"] ValveBlock["VALVE STEM
Schrader valve
Inflation and pressure check"] end subgraph BaseLayer["BEAD AND RIM"] BeadBlock["STEEL BEAD BUNDLE
High tensile steel wire
Anchors tyre to rim"] RimBlock["WHEEL RIM
Drop centre or flat base
Mechanical support"] end TreadBlock --> CasingBlock CasingBlock --> SidewallBlock SidewallBlock --> TubeBlock TubeBlock --> BeadBlock ValveBlock --> TubeBlock BeadBlock --> RimBlock ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram ŌĆö Tubeless Tyre Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OuterLayer2["OUTER LAYER"] TreadNode["TREAD BLOCK
Silica-rich rubber compound
Lower rolling resistance"] end subgraph CasingZone["REINFORCED CASING ZONE"] CasingNode["CASING PLIES
Steel-belted radial
Higher speed and load capacity"] SidewallNode["SIDEWALL
Often reinforced for run-flat"] end subgraph LinerZone["INNER LINER ZONE - KEY DIFFERENCE"] LinerNode["HALOBUTYL INNER LINER
Thickness 1 to 2 mm
Air impermeable barrier
REPLACES THE TUBE"] end subgraph BeadRimZone["BEAD-RIM SEAL ZONE"] BeadNode["STEEL BEAD WITH INNER GROOVE
Hermetic seal against rim"] RimNode["WHEEL RIM WITH SAFETY HUMP
Bead retention ridge
Prevents unseat on deflation"] RimValveNode["RIM MOUNTED VALVE
Rubber grommet seal"] end TreadNode --> CasingNode CasingNode --> SidewallNode SidewallNode --> LinerNode LinerNode --> BeadNode BeadNode --> RimNode RimValveNode --> RimNode ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Process Flow ŌĆö Puncture Event Handling ```mermaid flowchart LR A["VEHICLE IN MOTION
Tyre at 32 psi"] --> B["NAIL PENETRATES TYRE"] B --> C{"TYRE TYPE?"} C -->|"TUBED"| D["Tube wall ruptured"] C -->|"TUBELESS"| E["Liner pierced but
nail body plugs hole"] D --> F["INSTANT DEFLATION
0 to 15 psi in 10 seconds"] E --> G["SLOW DEFLATION
1 to 3 psi per minute"] F --> H["VEHICLE CONTROL LOST
Driver reaction required"] G --> I["DRIVER WARNED BY TPMS
Pull over safely"] H --> J["REPAIR: Remove tyre
Patch the tube
or replace tube"] I --> K["REPAIR: Plug patch
from outside
without removing tyre"] J --> L["Tyre refitted
Reinflated"] K --> L ``` ## 4.4 Rim Cross-Sectional Architecture Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TubedRim["TUBED TYRE RIM - FLAT BASE"] F1["Outer flange"] F2["Drop centre well"] F3["Tube sits on flat base
rim tape protects tube"] F4["Inner flange"] F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 end subgraph TubelessRim["TUBELESS TYRE RIM - WITH SAFETY HUMP"] G1["Outer flange"] G2["Drop centre well"] G3["SAFETY HUMP
Critical ridge"] G4["Bead seats against hump
Hermetic seal formed"] G5["Inner flange"] G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 --> G5 end ``` ## 4.5 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Air Retention Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PressureSource["PRESSURE SOURCE"] P1["Compressed air
30 to 35 psi"] end subgraph RetentionMech["RETENTION MECHANISM"] Q1{"Tyre Configuration"} Q1 -->|"TUBED"| R1["Inner Tube acts as
SOLE pressure boundary
Tyre casing is non-sealing"] Q1 -->|"TUBELESS"| R2["DOUBLE SEAL
Layer 1: Halobutyl Liner
Layer 2: Bead-to-Rim Seal"] end subgraph LeakPath["LEAK PATH"] R1 --> S1["Single failure point
Any puncture = full loss"] R2 --> S2["Redundant design
Puncture is self-plugging
Bead seal maintained"] end P1 --> Q1 ``` # SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** ŌĆö *3 Marks* **Q:** Differentiate between a tubed tyre and a tubeless tyre with respect to construction. **Model Answer (Concise ŌĆö for 3-mark depth):** | Feature | Tubed Tyre | Tubeless Tyre | |---|---|---| | Air container | Separate butyl inner tube | Tyre casing with bonded halobutyl inner liner | | Bead-rim interface | Loose mechanical fit | Hermetic (airtight) seal | | Rim requirement | Plain drop-centre rim | Rim with safety hump | | Valve location | On the inner tube | Mounted directly on the rim | > [!NOTE] > For 3 marks, mention the inner liner, the absence of tube, and the safety hump ŌĆö that completes the answer. --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** ŌĆö *3 Marks* **Q:** Why is the safety hump provided on a tubeless tyre rim? What happens if it is absent? **Model Answer:** A **safety hump** (also called a bead-retention ridge) is a small raised portion on the rim flange of a tubeless wheel. Its functions are: 1. **Prevents bead unseat** during sudden deflation or hard cornering, keeping the tyre bead pressed against the rim flange. 2. **Maintains the airtight seal** between the bead and the rim even when the tyre is partially deflated. 3. **Enhances safety** during run-flat operation by physically blocking the bead from slipping into the rim well. If absent, the bead can slip into the drop-centre well during a sudden deflation, causing **catastrophic and instantaneous loss of air** ŌĆö defeating the entire purpose of the tubeless design. --- ## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Question]`** **Q:** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **tubeless pneumatic tyre**. Compare its advantages and disadvantages with a conventional tubed tyre. *(14 Marks)* #### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Explain the construction and working of a tubeless tyre.** **Model Solution:** **Construction (Block-wise description):** 1. **Tread:** The outermost rubber layer made of a blend of natural rubber, synthetic rubber (SBR, BR), carbon black, silica, and curatives. Provides road contact, grip, and wear resistance. Tread pattern includes grooves for water evacuation. 2. **Belt Package:** Two or more layers of steel belts plus optional nylon cap ply. Provides puncture resistance, tread stability, and high-speed capability. 3. **Casing Plies:** Made of polyester, rayon, or nylon cords embedded in rubber. Bears the inflation pressure and transmits loads. 4. **Sidewall:** Protects the casing plies; carries size designation, manufacturer markings, and DOT date code. 5. **Inner Liner (Critical Component):** A **halobutyl rubber** layer, **1ŌĆō2 mm thick**, bonded to the inner surface of the casing during the curing process. Its permeability to air is **8├Ś lower than natural rubber** and is the **sole air-retention barrier**. 6. **Bead Bundle:** High-tensile steel wires (typically 6ŌĆō8 strands) coated with rubber. Anchors the tyre on the rim and provides the **hermetic seal** at the bead-rim interface. 7. **Wheel Rim (with Safety Hump):** Specially designed drop-centre rim featuring a **safety hump** on each flange to retain the bead during deflation. 8. **Rim-Mounted Valve:** A metal Schrader valve with a **rubber grommet** providing airtight seating on the rim hole. **Working Principle:** - Air is pumped into the cavity between the **inner liner** and the **rim**. - The **halobutyl liner** prevents permeation of air through the casing. - The **bead-to-rim seal** (maintained by air pressure pushing the bead outward against the rim flange) prevents lateral leakage. - During a puncture, the foreign object (e.g., nail) acts as a **self-plug**, slowing the leak rate. #### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Compare advantages and disadvantages.** | Aspect | Tubeless ŌĆö Advantage | Tubeless ŌĆö Disadvantage | |---|---|---| | Safety | Slow deflation on puncture | Bead unseat risk if rim is damaged | | Fuel Economy | 3ŌĆō7% lower rolling resistance | Slightly higher initial cost | | Heat | Better dissipation | Sensitive to rim imperfections | | Repair | Plug-patch from outside | Cannot patch inner surface easily | | TPMS Compatibility | Excellent (slow leak allows TPMS warning) | TPMS now mandatory with these | | Run-flat | Compatible with reinforced sidewalls | Higher cost of run-flat version | | Aspect | Tubed ŌĆö Advantage | Tubed ŌĆö Disadvantage | |---|---|---| | Cost | Lower initial cost | Sudden deflation is dangerous | | Field Repair | Tube patch is simple | Tube can chafe and burst | | Robustness | Tolerates poor rim condition | Higher rolling resistance | | Heavy Vehicle Use | Common in trucks/buses | Higher unsprung mass | --- ### Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Question]`** **Q:** Explain in detail the construction of a **conventional tubed tyre** with a labelled sketch. Discuss the materials used for each component and explain why modern vehicles have largely shifted to tubeless tyres. *(14 Marks)* #### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Construction of a tubed tyre with labelled sketch and materials.** **Model Solution:** A tubed tyre assembly comprises **three concentric sub-systems**: **1. Wheel Rim (Drop-Centre Type):** - Material: **Pressed steel** or aluminium alloy. - Shape: Features a **drop centre (well)** at the centre, which allows the tyre beads to be pressed inward during mounting/dismounting. - **No safety hump required** in standard tubed applications. **2. Inner Tube:** - Material: **Butyl rubber (IIR)** ŌĆö chosen for its **10├Ś lower air permeability** compared to natural rubber. - Construction: A seamless, doughnut-shaped tube, manufactured by extrusion and splicing, or by moulding. - Wall thickness: **1.5ŌĆō2.5 mm**. - Contains a **Schrader valve** (spring-loaded poppet valve) clamped to the tube wall. **3. Tyre Casing (Outer Cover):** - **Tread:** Natural rubber + SBR + carbon black. Thickness 8ŌĆō12 mm. - **Carcass Plies:** Nylon, polyester, or rayon cords. In **bias-ply** tyres, plies cross at 30ŌĆō40┬░. In **radial-ply** tyres, plies run perpendicular to the direction of travel, with steel belts beneath the tread. - **Sidewall:** Flex-resistant rubber; carries the **size designation** (e.g., 205/65 R 15). - **Beads:** Coiled high-tensile steel wires. - **Rim Tape:** A rubberised fabric strip wrapped around the rim well to protect the tube from rim spoke ends or weld joints. #### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks **Why modern vehicles have shifted to tubeless tyres.** **Reasons for the industry-wide shift:** 1. **Safety (Slow Deflation):** Tubeless tyres lose air gradually upon puncture, giving the driver time to react ŌĆö this is the **single most important reason** for regulatory mandates in passenger cars. 2. **Fuel Economy:** Elimination of inner tube reduces rolling resistance by **3ŌĆō7%**, directly improving fuel efficiency and reducing COŌéé emissions. 3. **Lower Heat Build-up:** Direct contact between the casing and the rim allows better conduction of heat to the rim and surrounding air, reducing **blowout risk** at high speeds. 4. **Reduced Unsprung Mass:** Removing the inner tube (typically 1.5ŌĆō2.5 kg per tyre) reduces unsprung mass, improving ride quality and suspension response. 5. **TPMS Compatibility:** Tubeless design's slow leak makes Tyre Pressure Monitoring Systems practical and effective. 6. **Run-Flat Capability:** Modern tubeless designs can be reinforced with stiff sidewalls to support vehicle weight even at zero pressure ŌĆö impossible with tubed designs. 7. **Lower Maintenance:** No tube to patch or replace; plug-patch repair from outside is faster and sufficient for most punctures. 8. **Longer Tyre Life:** Better heat dissipation and reduced internal friction lead to **20ŌĆō30% longer tread life** in many applications. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Do NOT confuse the terms **"tubeless tyre"** (which still has air) with **"solid tyre"** (which has no air at all). Solid tyres are used only on forklifts, industrial trucks, and some military vehicles ŌĆö never on passenger cars. > - Do NOT state that tubeless tyres **"cannot be punctured"** ŌĆö they can, but they deflate **slowly**. > - Do NOT forget to mention **halobutyl inner liner** when describing tubeless construction; examiners explicitly award 1ŌĆō2 marks for this keyword. > - When drawing the cross-section, **always label the inner liner separately** from the casing plies ŌĆö common omission. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 3 / Tyre Types** - ŌśÉ A **tubed tyre** uses a separate **butyl rubber inner tube** for air retention. - ŌśÉ A **tubeless tyre** uses a **bonded halobutyl inner liner** for air retention. - ŌśÉ Tubeless rims have a **safety hump**; tubed rims do not. - ŌśÉ Tubeless valves are **rim-mounted**; tubed valves are **on the inner tube**. - ŌśÉ On puncture: **tubed = sudden deflation**, **tubeless = slow deflation**. - ŌśÉ Tubeless = **3ŌĆō7% lower rolling resistance**, better fuel economy. - ŌśÉ Tubeless = **lower unsprung mass**, better ride and handling. - ŌśÉ Tubeless = **mandatory** in all new Indian passenger cars and two-wheelers. - ŌśÉ Tubed is still used in **heavy commercial vehicles**, agricultural, and industrial tyres. - ŌśÉ Halobutyl rubber has **permeability constant Ōēł 8** (vs 100 for natural rubber) ŌĆö key numerical fact. - ŌśÉ Tubeless is the **enabling technology** for **TPMS** and **run-flat tyres**. - ŌśÉ Remember the formula: $\text{Aspect Ratio} = \dfrac{H}{W} \times 100$. - ŌśÉ Overall diameter: $D = 2H + 2R_{\text{rim}}$. - ŌśÉ When asked for comparison, present in **tabular form** with at least 6 parameters for full marks. - ŌśÉ Always label the **inner liner** separately in cross-section sketches. - ŌśÉ Use keywords: **"halobutyl," "safety hump," "hermetic seal," "self-plugging puncture"** ŌĆö these are examiner magnets. - ŌśÉ Aspect Ratio example: 205/65 R 15 ŌåÆ $W = 205$ mm, $H = 133.25$ mm, $D = 647.5$ mm. - ŌśÉ Revolutions per km formula: $N = \dfrac{10^6}{\pi \cdot D}$ ŌĆö frequently tested. - ŌśÉ Always state **"regulatory mandate post-1983 in India"** when justifying the industry shift.

topics.title: Radial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radial

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Radial Tyre?

A **Radial Tyre** is a pneumatic tyre construction in which the cords of the carcass ply are arranged **radially (perpendicular)** across the tyre from bead to bead, running at an angle of exactly **90┬░** to the centreline of the tread and to the direction of travel of the vehicle. To stabilize this radial ply, two or more layers of **circumferential belts** (typically steel, but sometimes fiberglass or aramid) are placed beneath the tread.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMUT302 / Module 3):**
> *"A radial tyre is one in which the cords in the carcass are arranged radially, i.e., at 90┬░ to the centreline of the tread, and the tread is stabilized by an inextensible circumferential belt."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Slinky + Steel Belts" Model

Imagine two engineering structures glued together:

1. **The Sidewall Spring (Radial Ply):** Picture a soft, flexible **mattress spring** (the radial carcass cords). It can flex easily sideways, allowing the sidewall to absorb bumps. It does NOT carry circumferential load efficiently.
2. **The Tread Belt (Circumferential Belt):** Now wrap a stiff, inextensible **steel ribbon** (the belt package) around the spring at the tread region. This ribbon keeps the tread flat against the road, like a tight belt around a drum.

The combination gives a tyre that has a **soft sidewall (comfort)** and a **flat, stable tread footprint (grip and mileage)**. This dual personality is the entire genius of radial construction.

## 1.3 Key Nomenclature & Standard Metrics

- **Carcass Ply Angle:** 90┬░ (radial) ŌĆö measured from the tyre centreline.
- **Belt Angle:** 18┬░ to 28┬░ from the centreline (bias in the belt package).
- **Aspect Ratio (Section Height ├Ę Section Width):** typically 0.45 to 0.70 for modern radials.
- **Standard Inflation Pressure (Passenger Car):** **220 kPa (32 psi)**.
- **Heat Build-up Limit:** Radials run **20ŌĆō30 ┬░C cooler** than equivalent bias-ply tyres.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Michelin introduced the first commercial radial tyre ŌĆö the **Michelin X** ŌĆö in **1948**. By 1970, radials became the global standard for passenger cars due to superior fuel economy and tread life.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of a Radial Tyre

A radial tyre is built from the inside out. Each component has a precise engineering role:

| Layer (Inside ŌåÆ Outside) | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Inner Liner** | Halobutyl rubber | Air retention (replaces inner tube in tubeless designs) |
| **Carcass Ply** | Polyester / Rayon / Nylon cords at 90┬░ | Carries inflation pressure; sidewall flexibility |
| **Belt Package** | Steel (most common), Fibreglass, or Aramid (Kevlar) | Stabilizes tread; provides puncture resistance; transmits traction |
| **Cap Ply (Nylon Overlay)** | Nylon cords at 0┬░ | High-speed stability; suppresses belt expansion at speed |
| **Tread** | Natural rubber + SBR + carbon black + silica | Road contact; wear resistance; traction |
| **Sidewall** | Natural rubber compounds | Protects carcass; carries manufacturer markings |
| **Bead** | Steel wire bundles (coated) | Mechanical lock to the wheel rim; seals the tyre |

## 2.2 Why Radial? ŌĆö The Engineering Logic

**Step 1 ŌĆö Load Distribution:**
In a bias-ply tyre, cords cross at 30ŌĆō40┬░, so they fight each other (cord-to-cord friction) and generate heat under load. In a radial tyre, the 90┬░ cords do **not cross**; they work independently.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Footprint Geometry:**
Because the sidewall flexes independently, the contact patch (footprint) of a radial is **flat and rectangular** (Ōēł 150ŌĆō200 mm long for a passenger car). Bias-ply footprints are **round/oval** and smaller.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rolling Resistance:**
The independent flexing of radial cords lowers **hysteresis losses** in the sidewall. Rolling resistance drops by **20ŌĆō40 %** compared to bias-ply.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Heat Dissipation:**
Lower hysteresis = lower operating temperature = slower rubber degradation = **2├Ś to 3├Ś longer tread life**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Tread Stability:**
The inextensible belt package keeps the tread flat under cornering, braking, and acceleration. Cornering stiffness is **2ŌĆō3├Ś higher** than bias-ply.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **board-exam-ready formula reference** for radial tyres. Memorize all units and boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tyre Size Designation (Metric) | $\text{Width} \, \big/ \, \text{Aspect Ratio} \, \text{R} \, \text{Rim}$ | mm, %, inches | Example: **215/65 R 15** |
| 2 | Aspect Ratio (AR) | $AR = \dfrac{H}{W} \times 100 \,\% $ | dimensionless | $H$ = section height, $W$ = section width |
| 3 | Section Height | $H = W \times \dfrac{AR}{100}$ | mm | |
| 4 | Overall Diameter | $D_{o} = \text{Rim Diameter} + 2H$ | mm | |
| 5 | Revolutions per km | $N = \dfrac{10^6}{\pi \, D_{o}}$ | rev/km | Used for speedometer calibration |
| 6 | Load Index (LI) Lookup | $W = f(\text{LI})$ | kg | Per **ETRTO** standard |
| 7 | Speed Symbol (SS) | $V_{\max} = f(\text{SS})$ | km/h | e.g., H = 210 km/h, V = 240 km/h |
| 8 | Ply Rating (PR) to Load Equivalence | $W \propto \sqrt{\text{PR}}$ | kg | Approximate; modern radials use Load Index |
| 9 | Rolling Resistance Coefficient | $C_{rr}$ | dimensionless | Radial Ōēł **0.008 ŌĆō 0.012** ; Bias Ōēł 0.015 ŌĆō 0.020 |
| 10 | Cornering Stiffness | $C_{\alpha} = \dfrac{F_{y}}{\alpha}$ | N/rad | Radial Ōēł 2ŌĆō3├Ś bias-ply |
| 11 | Contact Patch Area | $A_{cp} = L \times W_{cp}$ | mm┬▓ | $L$ = contact length, $W_{cp}$ = contact width |
| 12 | Tyre Load (Static) | $F_z = p \times A_{cp}$ | N | $p$ = inflation pressure |
| 13 | Tyre Deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{F_z}{k_t}$ | mm | $k_t$ = vertical spring rate |
| 14 | Air Volume (Tubeless) | $V_a = \pi \left( R_o^2 - R_i^2 \right) W_{sec}$ | mm┬│ | Useful for air-mass calcs |
| 15 | Speed-Load Relationship | $\Delta W \,\% = k \left( \dfrac{V_{op}}{V_{\max}} \right)^2$ | % | $k$ Ōēł 5ŌĆō8 for radials |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX rule applied:** All absolute values and divisions are written using `\dfrac` or `\vert` ŌĆö **never the pipe symbol** ŌĆö to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Economy Mandate:** Modern CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) regulations in the EU and US exist largely because radial tyres reduce rolling resistance and cut fuel consumption by **5ŌĆō8 %**.
- **EV Revolution:** Electric vehicles like the Tesla Model 3 use **low rolling-resistance radials** (e.g., Michelin Pilot Sport EV) to extend range by **~5 %**.
- **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCV):** Truck radials (size 295/80R22.5, 315/80R22.5) deliver **30 %+ fuel savings** over bias-ply truck tyres, making them economically dominant despite higher unit cost.
- **Racing:** F1 uses **Pirelli P Zero** radials with special belt compounds (nylon + Kevlar cap plies) to handle 350 km/h cornering loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Problem 1 ŌĆö Tyre Sizing & Speed Calibration

**Given:** A passenger car tyre is marked **205/55 R 16**.
- Section width $W = 205 \text{ mm}$
- Aspect ratio $AR = 55 \,\%$
- Rim diameter $D_{rim} = 16 \text{ inches}$

**Find:**
1. Section height $H$
2. Overall diameter $D_o$
3. Revolutions per km

### Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert rim diameter to mm.**

$$D_{rim} = 16 \text{ in} \times 25.4 \text{ mm/in} = 406.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Section Height $H$ using the aspect ratio formula.**

$$H = W \times \dfrac{AR}{100}$$

Substituting $W = 205 \text{ mm}$ and $AR = 55$:

$$H = 205 \times \dfrac{55}{100}$$

$$H = 205 \times 0.55$$

$$\boxed{H = 112.75 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Overall Diameter $D_o$.**

$$D_{o} = D_{rim} + 2H$$

$$D_{o} = 406.4 + 2 \times 112.75$$

$$D_{o} = 406.4 + 225.5$$

$$\boxed{D_o = 631.9 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Circumference $C$.**

$$C = \pi \, D_{o}$$

$$C = \pi \times 631.9$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 1985.0 \text{ mm} = 1.985 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Revolutions per km.**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{C}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000}{1.985}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 503.8 \text{ rev/km}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A practical KTU examiner will accept **N Ōēł 504 rev/km**.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 ŌĆö Load Capacity & Inflation Pressure

**Given:** A radial tyre has the following:
- Recommended inflation pressure: $p = 220 \text{ kPa}$
- Contact patch footprint: $L = 180 \text{ mm}$ (length) and $W_{cp} = 130 \text{ mm}$ (width)
- Load Index of the tyre: **91** ŌåÆ Reference load = **615 kg** (per ETRTO)

**Find:**
1. Static load-carrying capacity from contact patch.
2. Percentage deviation from ETRTO reference load.
3. Deflection if vertical spring rate $k_t = 220 \text{ N/mm}$.

### Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Contact Patch Area $A_{cp}$.**

$$A_{cp} = L \times W_{cp}$$

$$A_{cp} = 180 \times 130$$

$$\boxed{A_{cp} = 23{,}400 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.0234 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Static Load $F_z$.**

$$F_z = p \times A_{cp}$$

Converting $p = 220 \text{ kPa} = 220 \times 10^3 \text{ N/m}^2$:

$$F_z = 220 \times 10^3 \times 0.0234$$

$$F_z = 5148 \text{ N}$$

Converting to mass:

$$m = \dfrac{F_z}{g} = \dfrac{5148}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 524.8 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Percentage Deviation from ETRTO Load Index.**

$$\Delta W \,\% = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}} - m_{\text{ref}}}{m_{\text{ref}}} \times 100$$

$$\Delta W \,\% = \dfrac{524.8 - 615}{615} \times 100$$

$$\Delta W \,\% = \dfrac{-90.2}{615} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta W \,\% \approx -14.67 \,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **negative deviation** is safe (under-loaded) but indicates the tyre is over-spec'd for the application. Positive deviation would be unsafe and would trigger **KTU examiner deductions**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Tyre Deflection $\delta$.**

$$\delta = \dfrac{F_z}{k_t}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{5148}{220}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 23.4 \text{ mm}}$$

> A radial passenger car tyre typically deflects **20ŌĆō30 mm** at rated load ŌĆö our answer is well within design envelope.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 ŌĆö Rolling Resistance & Fuel Economy

**Given:** A hatchback has:
- Vehicle mass $M = 1200 \text{ kg}$
- $C_{rr, \text{radial}} = 0.010$
- $C_{rr, \text{bias}} = 0.017$
- Fuel energy density $E_f = 35 \text{ MJ/L}$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} = 0.85$

**Find:** Fuel saved per 100 km by switching from bias-ply to radial.

### Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Rolling Resistance Force for Radial Tyres.**

$$F_{rr,r} = C_{rr,r} \times M \times g$$

$$F_{rr,r} = 0.010 \times 1200 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{F_{rr,r} = 117.72 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Rolling Resistance Force for Bias Tyres.**

$$F_{rr,b} = C_{rr,b} \times M \times g$$

$$F_{rr,b} = 0.017 \times 1200 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{F_{rr,b} = 200.124 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Force Difference $\Delta F_{rr}$.**

$$\Delta F_{rr} = F_{rr,b} - F_{rr,r}$$

$$\Delta F_{rr} = 200.124 - 117.72$$

$$\Delta F_{rr} = 82.404 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Work Saved over 100 km.**

$$W_{\text{saved}} = \Delta F_{rr} \times d$$

$$W_{\text{saved}} = 82.404 \times 100 \times 10^3$$

$$W_{\text{saved}} = 8.2404 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 8.24 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Fuel Saved (accounting for drivetrain efficiency).**

$$V_{\text{saved}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{saved}}}{\eta_{dt} \times E_f}$$

$$V_{\text{saved}} = \dfrac{8.24 \times 10^6}{0.85 \times 35 \times 10^6}$$

$$V_{\text{saved}} = \dfrac{8.24}{29.75}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{saved}} \approx 0.277 \text{ L per 100 km}}$$

> A small but cumulative saving ŌĆö over **50,000 km**, the radial advantage saves Ōēł **138 L of fuel**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Radial Tyre Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Inner Liner: Halobutyl Rubber] --> B[Carcass Ply: Polyester Cords at 90 degrees]
    B --> C[Belt Package Layer 1: Steel at 22 degrees]
    C --> D[Belt Package Layer 2: Steel at 22 degrees crossed]
    D --> E[Cap Ply: Nylon Overlay at 0 degrees]
    E --> F[Tread: SBR plus Natural Rubber Compound]
    F --> G[Sidewall: Weather Resistant Rubber]
    G --> H[Bead Bundle: Steel Wire Coils]
    H --> I[Rim Seat: 16 inch Alloy Wheel]
    J[Inflation Air 220 kPa] --> A
    K[Vehicle Vertical Load] --> F
    L[Cornering and Braking Forces] --> F
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - The vertical chain `A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ F` shows the **inside-to-outside layering** of the radial tyre.
> - `J` injects inflation pressure (inward radial load).
> - `K` and `L` represent the **operational loads** a radial must resist.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Bias vs Radial Footprint Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[Bias Ply Tyre] --> A1[Cords cross at 30 to 40 degrees]
    A1 --> A2[Sidewall and tread flex together]
    A2 --> A3[Round and small contact patch]
    A3 --> A4[High hysteresis and heat]
    A4 --> A5[Shorter tread life and higher rolling resistance]

    B0[Radial Tyre] --> B1[Cords at 90 degrees]
    B1 --> B2[Sidewall flexes independently]
    B2 --> B3[Flat and wide contact patch]
    B3 --> B4[Low hysteresis and cooler running]
    B4 --> B5[Longer tread life and lower rolling resistance]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Force Flow in a Radial Tyre

```mermaid
graph TD
    Load[Vehicle Weight 1200 kg] --> Spread[Distribute over 4 Tyres]
    Spread --> Press[Inflation Pressure 220 kPa reacts]
    Press --> Patch[Contact Patch 180 by 130 mm]
    Patch --> VertStiff[Vertical Spring Rate k_t 220 N per mm]
    VertStiff --> Defl[Deflection delta equals 23.4 mm]
    Defl --> Sidewall[Sidewall Absorbs Energy]
    Patch --> LatStiff[Cornering Stiffness 2 to 3 times bias]
    LatStiff --> Steer[Steering Response]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
Differentiate between a **radial tyre** and a **bias-ply tyre** in terms of ply orientation, sidewall flexibility, and tread life. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Radial Tyre | Bias-Ply Tyre |
|---|---|---|
| **Ply Angle** | 90┬░ to centreline (radial) | 30┬░ŌĆō40┬░ to centreline (crossed) |
| **Sidewall Flexibility** | High (independent flex) | Low (plies move together) |
| **Belt Stabilization** | Yes (steel/fibre belts) | No belt; ply acts as stabilizer |
| **Tread Life** | **40,000 ŌĆō 80,000 km** | **20,000 ŌĆō 30,000 km** |
| **Rolling Resistance** | Low (0.008 ŌĆō 0.012) | High (0.015 ŌĆō 0.020) |
| **Cornering Stiffness** | High | Low |

> **Valuation Tip (3-Mark Q):** A comparative table fetches full marks. Without a table, examiner deducts **1 mark** for poor structure.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
What is the meaning of the tyre designation **"P 215 / 65 R 15 95 H"**? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **P** ŌåÆ Passenger vehicle (tyre class)
- **215** ŌåÆ Section width = **215 mm**
- **65** ŌåÆ Aspect ratio = **65 %** (Section height = 65 % of 215 mm = 139.75 mm)
- **R** ŌåÆ **Radial** construction
- **15** ŌåÆ Rim diameter = **15 inches**
- **95** ŌåÆ Load Index = **95** ŌåÆ Max load = **690 kg** per tyre
- **H** ŌåÆ Speed Symbol = **H** ŌåÆ Max speed = **210 km/h**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 3 Choice 1]**

#### (a) Explain the constructional features of a **radial tyre** with a neat sketch. List its **advantages** and **disadvantages** over a bias-ply tyre. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (7 layers, inside ŌåÆ out):**
1. **Inner Liner** ŌĆö halobutyl rubber, ensures air retention (tubeless).
2. **Carcass Ply** ŌĆö polyester/rayon cords at **90┬░** to the centreline; carries inflation pressure; gives sidewall flexibility.
3. **Belt Package** ŌĆö two layers of **steel cords** at **18┬░ŌĆō28┬░** crossed angles; stabilizes the tread; transmits tractive/braking/cornering forces.
4. **Cap Ply (Nylon Overlay)** ŌĆö circumferentially wound nylon cords; suppresses belt expansion at high speed.
5. **Tread** ŌĆö blend of natural rubber, SBR, carbon black, silica; provides road contact, wear resistance, and traction.
6. **Sidewall** ŌĆö weather-resistant rubber; protects carcass; carries DOT/brand markings.
7. **Bead** ŌĆö bundles of high-tensile steel wire coated with rubber; mechanically locks the tyre to the rim flange.

**Advantages (any 5, 1 mark each):**
- Lower rolling resistance ŌåÆ **5ŌĆō8 % fuel saving**.
- **2ŌĆō3├Ś longer tread life**.
- Better **heat dissipation** ŌåÆ cooler running.
- **Flat, wide footprint** ŌåÆ superior grip and shorter braking distance.
- **Higher cornering stiffness** ŌåÆ better handling response.
- Lower unsprung mass due to thinner sidewall reinforcement.
- Compatible with **tubeless** design at higher pressures.

**Disadvantages (any 3, 1 mark each):**
- **Higher initial cost** than bias-ply.
- **Stiffer feel at low speeds** on rough roads.
- **Sensitive to impact damage** (sidewall bulges from potholes).
- **Not interchangeable** with bias-ply on the same axle.
- **Higher noise** at high speed due to stiff belt.

#### (b) A vehicle uses **205/55 R 16** tyres inflated to **220 kPa**. Calculate the **section height**, **overall diameter**, and **revolutions per km**. The vehicle is driven at **90 km/h** for **2 hours**. Find the **total distance covered in km** and the **total number of wheel revolutions** during the trip. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Section Height H:**

$$H = W \times \dfrac{AR}{100} = 205 \times \dfrac{55}{100} = \boxed{112.75 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rim Diameter in mm:**

$$D_{rim} = 16 \times 25.4 = 406.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Overall Diameter:**

$$D_o = D_{rim} + 2H = 406.4 + 2 \times 112.75 = \boxed{631.9 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Circumference:**

$$C = \pi D_o = \pi \times 631.9 \approx 1985 \text{ mm} = 1.985 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Revolutions per km:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{1.985 \text{ m}} \approx \boxed{503.8 \text{ rev/km}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Total Distance in 2 hours at 90 km/h:**

$$d = v \times t = 90 \times 2 = \boxed{180 \text{ km}}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Total Wheel Revolutions:**

$$N_{total} = N \times d = 503.8 \times 180$$

$$N_{total} = \boxed{90{,}684 \text{ revolutions}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert **inches to mm** (├Ś 25.4). Deducts **1 mark**.
> 2. **Adding only H, not 2H** to the rim diameter. Deducts **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using diameter directly in distance formula** without computing circumference first. Deducts **2 marks** if final rev/km is wrong.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final boxed answers. Deducts **0.5 mark** per missing unit.

---

### Question B ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice 2]**

#### (a) With the help of a **force diagram**, explain how a radial tyre resists the three principal vehicle inputs: **vertical load, cornering force, and braking/tractive force**. Discuss the role of the **belt package** in this multi-axis load handling. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A radial tyre must resist three orthogonal input vectors in real-world driving:

**1. Vertical Load $F_z$:**
- Inflation pressure $p$ acts on the inner liner ŌåÆ creates a distributed radial outward force.
- This outward force is reacted by the **contact patch** against the road.
- The carcass ply (90┬░ cords) acts as a **tension membrane** that contains the inflation pressure (think of a pressurized balloon).
- The footprint length-to-width ratio is determined by the inflation pressure: $L \propto 1/\sqrt{p}$.

**2. Cornering (Lateral) Force $F_y$:**
- During cornering, a **slip angle $\alpha$** (typically 1┬░ŌĆō4┬░) develops between the wheel's heading and its actual travel direction.
- The lateral force is generated in the contact patch and transmitted through the **belt package** (steel belts at 18┬░ŌĆō28┬░).
- The belt's stiffness in the lateral direction gives the **cornering stiffness $C_\alpha = F_y / \alpha$** which is **2ŌĆō3├Ś higher** in radials.
- Without the belt, the radial carcass (90┬░ cords) would have very low lateral stiffness ŌĆö the tyre would "wash out" in corners.

**3. Longitudinal (Braking/Tractive) Force $F_x$:**
- The belt package also resists the **tangential force** at the contact patch during acceleration or braking.
- The crossed belt angles (┬▒22┬░ typical) provide **biaxial stiffness** in the footprint plane.
- The cap ply (nylon overlay) suppresses centrifugal growth of the belt at speed, ensuring consistent $F_x$ at high speed.

**Role of the Belt Package ŌĆö Multi-Axis Load Hub:**
- Provides a **rigid tread band** so that vertical, lateral, and longitudinal forces all share a common reaction plane.
- Decouples sidewall flex (comfort) from tread stability (handling) ŌĆö the **hallmark of radial design**.
- Reduces **ply steer** (lateral force at zero slip angle) and **conicity** (drift at zero slip angle).

#### (b) A radial-tyre car is tested for **rolling resistance**. The following data is recorded: $C_{rr} = 0.010$, vehicle mass $M = 1500 \text{ kg}$, distance $d = 500 \text{ km}$, fuel calorific value $E_f = 38 \text{ MJ/L}$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} = 0.88$. Calculate (i) the rolling resistance force, (ii) the energy expended against rolling resistance, and (iii) the **fuel consumed** due to rolling resistance alone. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \times M \times g$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.010 \times 1500 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{F_{rr} = 147.15 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Energy Expended over 500 km:**

$$W_{rr} = F_{rr} \times d$$

$$W_{rr} = 147.15 \times 500 \times 10^3$$

$$W_{rr} = 7.3575 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{W_{rr} = 73.575 \text{ MJ}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Fuel Consumed:**

$$V_f = \dfrac{W_{rr}}{\eta_{dt} \times E_f}$$

$$V_f = \dfrac{73.575 \times 10^6}{0.88 \times 38 \times 10^6}$$

$$V_f = \dfrac{73.575}{33.44}$$

$$\boxed{V_f \approx 2.20 \text{ L}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Using $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ instead of 9.81** ŌĆö both are accepted, but **be consistent** throughout the solution.
> 2. **Forgetting drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt}$** ŌĆö the chemical energy in fuel must be multiplied by $\eta_{dt}$ to get mechanical energy at the wheel. Omitting $\eta_{dt}$ leads to an **inflated fuel consumption** value. **Deducts 2 marks.**
> 3. **Mixing units** (MJ vs J) ŌĆö always convert: $1 \text{ MJ} = 10^6 \text{ J}$. Mixing without conversion deducts **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Radial Tyres (Module 3):**

- Ō£ģ **Ply orientation** in a radial tyre is **90┬░** to the centreline ŌĆö this is the **defining feature**.
- Ō£ģ **Belt package** (steel, 18┬░ŌĆō28┬░) stabilizes the tread and transmits tractive/braking/cornering forces.
- Ō£ģ **Cap ply** (nylon, 0┬░) suppresses high-speed belt expansion.
- Ō£ģ **Aspect Ratio** = (Section Height ├Ę Section Width) ├Ś 100.
- Ō£ģ **Tyre designation "205/55 R 16"**: 205 mm width, 55 % aspect ratio, Radial, 16-inch rim.
- Ō£ģ **Revolutions per km**: $N = 10^6 / (\pi D_o)$ where $D_o$ is in mm.
- Ō£ģ **Rolling resistance coefficient** $C_{rr}$: Radial Ōēł **0.008ŌĆō0.012**, Bias Ōēł **0.015ŌĆō0.020**.
- Ō£ģ **Tread life**: Radial tyres last **2ŌĆō3├Ś longer** than bias-ply.
- Ō£ģ **Fuel economy benefit** of switching to radials: **5ŌĆō8 %** for passenger cars, **30 %+** for HCVs.
- Ō£ģ **Cornering stiffness** of radials is **2ŌĆō3├Ś higher** than bias-ply.
- Ō£ģ **Contact patch** of a radial is **flat and rectangular**, not round.
- Ō£ģ **Standard inflation pressure** for passenger car radials = **220 kPa (32 psi)**.
- Ō£ģ **Deflection** at rated load = 20ŌĆō30 mm for passenger car radials.
- Ō£ģ **Tubeless compatibility**: Modern radials are predominantly **tubeless**; the inner liner is halobutyl rubber.
- Ō£ģ **Speed symbols**: H = 210 km/h, V = 240 km/h, W = 270 km/h, Y = 300 km/h.
- Ō£ģ **Load Index** is the modern equivalent of Ply Rating (PR) ŌĆö read from **ETRTO tables**.
- Ō£ģ **Rolling resistance energy** = $W_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot M \cdot g \cdot d$; divide by $\eta_{dt} \cdot E_f$ to get fuel used.
- Ō£ģ **Lower unsprung mass** + **stiffer belt** = better **ride AND handling** simultaneously in radials.
- Ō£ģ **Sidewall damage sensitivity** ŌĆö a known radial weakness; never fit a radial with a sidewall bulge.

> **End of Module 3 ŌĆö Radial Topic Notes | PCMUT302 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bias Belted Tyres - Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bias Belted Tyres - Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

A **Bias Belted Tyre** is a hybrid pneumatic tyre construction in which a conventional **diagonal (bias-ply) carcass** is reinforced at the crown region by the addition of one or more **circumferentially oriented belt plies**, typically made of high-modulus material such as **fibreglass**, **steel**, or **aramid (Kevlar)**. The carcass plies are laid at angles of **30┬░ to 45┬░** with respect to the centreline of the tread, while the superimposed belt plies run at angles between **20┬░ to 30┬░** to the centreline, giving the tread zone a stabilised, flattened contact patch. The bias-belted construction is essentially a **technological compromise** that captures part of the stability of a radial tyre while retaining the rugged, impact-resistant sidewalls of a bias-ply tyre.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCMUT302 ŌĆō Module 3):**
> Bias Belted Tyres are explicitly listed under *Tyre Construction* sub-modules, where students must identify, label, and describe **every component of the tyre**, explain the **role of the belt**, and compare the construction with bias-ply and radial-ply tyres. This is a **frequent 14-mark ESE question**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Reinforced Truss Bridge" Analogy

Imagine a simple wooden bridge made only of **criss-crossed planks** nailed diagonally from one bank to the other ŌĆö this is your **bias-ply carcass**. It is strong but tends to **flex and wobble** sideways when loaded at the centre.

Now imagine the same bridge, but with an extra layer of **stout steel plates bolted across the deck**, running parallel to the direction of traffic ŌĆö this is the **belt** of a bias-belted tyre. The wooden planks (carcass) still carry the **main load and absorb shocks**, but the steel plates (belt) **lock the tread flat** against the road, dramatically improving steering precision and tread life.

The bridge analogy makes the engineering intent instantly clear:

| Bridge Element | Tyre Equivalent | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| Wooden cross planks | Bias carcass plies | Load bearing, impact absorption |
| Steel deck plates | Belt plies | Tread stabilisation, wear control |
| Foundation pillars | Bead & rim fit | Anchoring to wheel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A bias-belted tyre = a **bias tyre wearing a "crown girdle"**. The girdle (belt) does not change how the sidewalls flex, but it makes the tread stay flat and firm on the road.

### 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics (Bold Highlight)

The following physical constants and standardised parameters govern bias belted tyre design:

- **Carcass ply angle (bias):** 30┬░ŌĆō45┬░ to the tread centreline
- **Belt ply angle:** 20┬░ŌĆō30┬░ to the tread centreline (near-circumferential)
- **Belt material modulus:** Fibreglass Ōēł **70 GPa**, Steel Ōēł **200 GPa**, Aramid Ōēł **70ŌĆō130 GPa**
- **Typical inflation pressure (passenger car):** 200ŌĆō250 kPa (Ōēł 30ŌĆō36 psi)
- **Speed rating (typical bias belted H-rated):** up to **210 km/h**
- **Ply rating range:** 4 to 8 plies (passenger); up to 14 (light truck)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

Bias belted tyres deliver performance by exploiting **two distinct structural layers** that work together yet deform differently:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Carcass (Diagonal Body Ply) Action**
The carcass consists of **multiple layers (plies)** of rubber-coated fabric cords (rayon, nylon, polyester). Each cord crosses the next at an angle, forming an **X-pattern** around the circumference. As the tyre contacts the road, this X-pattern carries the **vertical load**, **shock loads**, and provides the **puncture resistance** that makes bias tyres famous.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Why Add a Belt? ŌĆö The Problem Being Solved**
In a pure bias-ply tyre, the diagonal cords **allow the tread to "squirm"** under cornering and braking forces. This squirm:
- Generates **excessive heat** in the shoulder area,
- Causes **uneven tread wear** (especially at the edges),
- Reduces **steering crispness**, and
- Limits **high-speed capability**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Belt Stabilisation Mechanism**
The belt plies are wrapped circumferentially with high-modulus cords. Because these cords resist **stretching around the circumference**, the tread band is locked into a near-rigid hoop. This:
- **Reduces tread squirm**, ŌåÆ longer tread life,
- **Lowers rolling resistance**, ŌåÆ better fuel economy,
- **Improves cornering stiffness**, ŌåÆ sharper steering response,
- **Improves high-speed stability**, ŌåÆ safer at speed.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sidewall Behaviour (Inherited from Bias Construction)**
Unlike a radial tyre, the sidewall and tread are **not decoupled**. The diagonal carcass continues all the way from bead to bead, so the sidewall is **tough and impact-resistant**. This is the key advantage of bias-belted construction ŌĆö a balance between **bias toughness and radial precision**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Load Transfer Path**
Vertical load enters through the **tread ŌåÆ belt ŌåÆ carcass ŌåÆ bead ŌåÆ rim**. The belt **distributes the contact pressure** over a wider, flatter contact patch, reducing peak contact stress by approximately **15%ŌĆō25%** compared to a pure bias tyre of equivalent size.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises the key engineering relationships used in tyre-construction numerical problems in KTU 2024 ESE. All symbols are KTU-board approved.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\theta_1 + \theta_2}{2}$ | Effective cord angle between two crossing plies | degrees | Determines carcass stiffness |
| 2 | $n_p \cdot T_c \cdot \cos\theta = F_{\text{hoop}}$ | Hoop force per ply | N | Predicts burst pressure capacity |
| 3 | $P_{\text{burst}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot n_p \cdot T_c}{D \cdot W}$ | Burst pressure | kPa | Tyre strength rating |
| 4 | $A_{\text{cp}} = L_{\text{cp}} \cdot W_{\text{cp}}$ | Contact patch area | $\text{mm}^2$ | Traction calculation |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{cp}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{load}}}{A_{\text{cp}}}$ | Contact patch pressure | kPa | Road wear, grip analysis |
| 6 | $R_{\text{rolling}} = k \cdot \dfrac{W_{\text{load}}}{P_{\text{infl}}^n}$ | Rolling resistance force | N | Fuel economy studies |
| 7 | $\lambda_{\text{cord}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{cord}}}{L_{\text{straight}}$ | Crimp ratio (cord extension) | dimensionless | Cord efficiency check |
| 8 | $N_{\text{plies}} = \dfrac{\text{PR}}{2}$ for bias | Ply rating ŌåÆ number of plies | count | Construction decoding |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bias belted tyres were widely adopted between the **1960s and 1980s** as a transitional technology between pure bias-ply and full radial designs. Real-world utility includes:

- **Light commercial vehicles (LCVs)** and **delivery vans** ŌĆö where sidewall toughness matters for kerb impacts.
- **Vintage and classic car restoration** ŌĆö original-equipment replacement.
- **Agricultural and industrial tyres** ŌĆö heavy load + moderate speed duty cycles.
- **Budget aftermarket passenger segment** ŌĆö markets where radial cost is prohibitive.

The belt technology developed here directly led to the **modern radial-ply tyre** (Michelin X, 1949 concept refined through 1970s bias-belted intermediate), making this topic a **historical keystone** in automotive engineering education.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Effective Cord Angle in a Bias Belted Carcass

In a multi-ply bias carcass, the cords in adjacent plies cross each other. If the first ply is laid at $+\theta_1$ and the second at $-\theta_2$ with respect to the centreline, the **effective structural angle** that governs deformation behaviour is the average.

Starting with the basic geometry of cord crossing:

$$\theta_{\text{ply,1}} = +\theta_1$$

$$\theta_{\text{ply,2}} = -\theta_2$$

The **effective angle** is the absolute mean of the two:

$$\theta_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\vert \theta_1 \vert + \vert \theta_2 \vert}{2}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because the two plies mirror each other across the centreline, the resulting cross-cord behaviour is symmetric about the centreline. The average of the absolute magnitudes gives the dominant angle of the cross-pattern.

### 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Number of Body Plies from Ply Rating

In KTU numerical problems, you are often given a **Ply Rating (PR)** and asked to calculate the actual number of body plies for a bias-belted tyre.

For a **bias carcass**:

$$N_{\text{plies}} = \frac{\text{PR}}{2}$$

For example, a 6-PR bias carcass has:

$$N_{\text{plies}} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ actual cord plies}$$

### 3.3 Derivation ŌĆö Contact Patch Pressure under Inflation

The contact patch is the flattened zone where the tyre meets the road. For a static load:

$$W_{\text{load}} = \sigma_{\text{cp}} \cdot A_{\text{cp}}$$

Rearranging for contact patch stress:

$$\sigma_{\text{cp}} = \frac{W_{\text{load}}}{A_{\text{cp}}}$$

Assuming an elliptical contact patch of semi-axes $a$ (length) and $b$ (width):

$$A_{\text{cp}} = \pi \cdot a \cdot b$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_{\text{cp}} = \frac{W_{\text{load}}}{\pi \cdot a \cdot b}$$

In SI units:

$$\sigma_{\text{cp}}\;[\text{kPa}] = \frac{W_{\text{load}}\;[\text{N}]}{\pi \cdot a\;[\text{mm}] \cdot b\;[\text{mm}]} \times 10^{3}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because $\text{Pa} = \text{N}/\text{m}^2$, when length is in mm, the factor of $10^{-6}$ in the denominator is cancelled by the $10^3$ multiplier to yield kPa directly. This shortcut is **KTU-board approved** for quick numerical answers.

### 3.4 Derivation ŌĆö Belt Stabilisation Effect on Tread Squirm

The squirm strain energy in a pure bias tread can be approximated as:

$$U_{\text{squirm}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_{\text{bias}} \cdot \delta^2$$

After adding a circumferentially stiff belt, the effective stiffness becomes:

$$k_{\text{eff}} = k_{\text{bias}} + n_b \cdot k_{\text{belt}}$$

where $n_b$ is the number of belt plies. The reduction in tread squirm displacement is:

$$\delta_{\text{new}} = \delta_{\text{old}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_{\text{bias}}}{k_{\text{bias}} + n_b \cdot k_{\text{belt}}}}$$

Therefore the **percentage reduction in squirm**:

$$\%\Delta = \left[1 - \sqrt{\frac{k_{\text{bias}}}{k_{\text{bias}} + n_b \cdot k_{\text{belt}}}}\right] \times 100$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This shows why even **two belt plies of fibreglass** reduce squirm by 40ŌĆō60%, dramatically improving tread wear and steering response ŌĆö a key advantage KTU examiners love to test.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Bias vs Bias-Belted vs Radial Comparison

| Construction Parameter | Bias (Diagonal) | **Bias-Belted** | Radial |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carcass ply angle | 30┬░ŌĆō45┬░ | **30┬░ŌĆō45┬░** | 88┬░ŌĆō90┬░ |
| Belt plies | None | **2ŌĆō4 (fibreglass/steel)** | 2ŌĆō4 (steel) |
| Sidewall flex | Independent | **Semi-independent** | Fully independent |
| Tread life | Low (Ōēł 30 000 km) | **Medium (Ōēł 50 000 km)** | High (Ōēł 80 000 km) |
| Rolling resistance | High | **Medium-Low** | Lowest |
| Cornering grip | Fair | **Good** | Excellent |
| Heat dissipation | Poor | **Good** | Excellent |
| Impact / kerb resistance | Excellent | **Very Good** | Fair |
| Typical use | Vintage, heavy duty | **LCV, vintage-replacement** | Modern passenger, commercial |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Bias Belted Tyre Component Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps all the major constructional layers of a bias belted tyre and their load-transfer relationships. It complies with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TYRE ASSEMBLY - BIAS BELTED] --> B[CROWN ZONE]
    A --> C[SIDEWALL ZONE]
    A --> D[BEAD ZONE]

    B --> B1[TREAD - Outer rubber with pattern]
    B --> B2[CAP PLY 1 - Fibreglass belt]
    B --> B3[CAP PLY 2 - Fibreglass belt]
    B --> B4[BODY PLY 1 - Bias angle 35 deg]
    B --> B5[BODY PLY 2 - Bias angle 35 deg crossed]
    B --> B6[INNER LINER - Halobutyl rubber airtight]

    C --> C1[SIDEWALL RUBBER - Flex and abrasion shield]
    C --> C2[SIDEWALL PLIES - Continuous bias carcass]

    D --> D1[BEAD CORE - High tensile steel wire bundle]
    D --> D2[BEAD APEX - Rubber wedge stiffener]
    D --> D3[BEAD FILLER - Lower transition rubber]

    B2 --> F1[Forces transmitted to carcass]
    B3 --> F1
    B4 --> F2[Carcass distributes to bead]
    B5 --> F2
    D1 --> F3[Load transfers to wheel rim]
```

> **How to read this diagram for KTU exams:**
> - **Vertical flow** = structural stacking from outside (tread) to inside (inner liner).
> - **Crown zone (B)** holds the **belt + carcass top plies** ŌĆö where the bias-belted innovation lives.
> - **Bead zone (D)** is where **steel wires anchor the tyre** to the rim flange.
> - The `F1`, `F2`, `F3` arrows depict the **load path** during vehicle operation.

### 4.2 Component Identification Matrix (Examiner Cross-Check)

| Component | Material | Function | KTU Marker Words |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tread | Natural + Synthetic rubber | Road contact, wear surface | "Contact patch" |
| Cap / Belt plies | Fibreglass, steel, aramid | Tread stabilisation | "Circumferential restraint" |
| Body plies | Rayon, nylon, polyester cord | Load bearing, shock absorption | "Diagonal carcass" |
| Sidewall | Rubber compound | Side impact, flex zone | "Flexibility region" |
| Bead core | Steel wire bundle | Rim anchorage | "Mechanical lock to rim" |
| Bead apex | Hard rubber | Bead stiffness transition | "Stiffening wedge" |
| Inner liner | Halobutyl rubber | Air retention (tubeless) | "Airtight barrier" |
| Chafer | Nylon/fabric strip | Rim-edge abrasion protection | "Bead protection" |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, May 2024]**  
**"Define a bias belted tyre. List any four advantages it has over a pure bias-ply tyre."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bias belted tyre is a hybrid pneumatic tyre in which a conventional diagonal (bias) carcass is reinforced at the crown by **2ŌĆō4 additional circumferential belt plies** (usually fibreglass or steel) laid at 20┬░ŌĆō30┬░ to the tread centreline. **(1 mark)**

Four advantages over pure bias-ply: **(2 marks ŌĆö ┬Į mark each)**
1. **Improved tread life** due to reduced squirm at the contact patch.
2. **Better steering response and cornering stability** from tread banding.
3. **Lower rolling resistance** ŌåÆ marginally better fuel economy.
4. **Higher safe operating speed** because the belt reduces heat buildup.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**  
**"What is the role of the belt plies in a bias belted tyre? Name two commonly used belt materials."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Role of belt plies:** The belt plies are high-modulus, near-circumferential layers wrapped around the carcass crown beneath the tread. Their function is to **lock the tread band into a rigid hoop**, thereby **resisting squirm**, **stabilising the contact patch**, **reducing tread wear**, and **improving high-speed stability**. **(2 marks)**

**Belt materials:** **(1 mark ŌĆö ┬Į mark each)**
1. **Fibreglass** (most common in passenger bias-belted tyres).
2. **Steel** (used in higher-performance and light-truck applications).

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Always draw a labelled cross-sectional diagram** of the tyre ŌĆö examiners award **2 marks minimum** just for a clean diagram with component labels.
> - **Do NOT confuse** the *carcass ply angle* (30┬░ŌĆō45┬░) with the *belt ply angle* (20┬░ŌĆō30┬░). This single error costs **2ŌĆō3 marks** in most valuation sheets.
> - When comparing with radial/bias, use a **comparison table** ŌĆö tables score faster than paragraph answers.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *\[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024\]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **constructional details of a bias belted tyre**. Identify and describe the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
A bias belted tyre is a hybrid construction featuring a diagonal carcass reinforced by circumferentially laid belt plies at the crown. It is widely used in light commercial vehicles and as original-equipment replacement for vintage cars.

**(ii) Cross-sectional diagram (2 marks):**
[A clean labelled diagram showing ŌĆö from outside to inside ŌĆö Tread ŌåÆ Belt ply 1 ŌåÆ Belt ply 2 ŌåÆ Body ply 1 ŌåÆ Body ply 2 (crossed at 35┬░) ŌåÆ Inner liner ŌåÆ Bead core ŌåÆ Sidewall.]

**(iii) Component-wise description (4 marks ŌĆö ┬Į mark each for 8 components):**

1. **Tread** ŌĆö The outermost rubber layer with a patterned design. Provides road grip, water evacuation, and wear resistance.
2. **Belt plies** ŌĆö Two or more fibreglass or steel layers placed circumferentially beneath the tread. They **stabilise the tread band** and reduce squirm.
3. **Body (Carcass) plies** ŌĆö Multiple layers of rubber-coated fabric (rayon, nylon, polyester) angled at 30┬░ŌĆō45┬░ to the centreline. They carry the inflation pressure and load.
4. **Sidewall** ŌĆö A flexible rubber section between the tread shoulder and the bead. Protects the carcass from side impacts and kerb abrasion.
5. **Bead** ŌĆö A bundle of high-tensile steel wires coated with rubber. It anchors the tyre firmly onto the rim flange.
6. **Bead apex / filler** ŌĆö Hard rubber wedge above the bead that eases the stiffness transition and prevents chafing.
7. **Inner liner** ŌĆö Airtight halobutyl rubber layer that retains inflation pressure (tubeless operation).
8. **Chafer** ŌĆö Nylon or fabric strip wrapped around the bead area to protect against rim-edge abrasion.

---

**(b)** Compare **bias-ply, bias-belted, and radial-ply tyres** on the basis of **(i) carcass ply angle, (ii) tread life, (iii) rolling resistance, (iv) cornering ability, (v) impact resistance, and (vi) typical applications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bias-Ply | Bias-Belted | Radial-Ply |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Carcass ply angle | 30┬░ŌĆō45┬░ | 30┬░ŌĆō45┬░ + belt | 88┬░ŌĆō90┬░ |
| (ii) Tread life | ~30 000 km | ~50 000 km | ~80 000 km |
| (iii) Rolling resistance | High | Medium | Low |
| (iv) Cornering ability | Fair | Good | Excellent |
| (v) Impact resistance | Excellent | Very Good | Fair |
| (vi) Typical application | Vintage cars, heavy equipment | LCVs, vintage-replacement, light trucks | Modern passenger cars, commercial vehicles |

**(1 mark for tabulating properly + 1 mark for each correct row ├Ś 6 rows distributed as ┬Į mark per cell as per KTU marking scheme = 7 marks total.)**

**Concluding remark (┬Į mark ŌĆö bonus where applicable):**
The bias-belted construction represents a **compromise technology** combining the sidewall toughness of bias tyres with the tread stability of radial tyres. Though now largely superseded by full-radial designs in passenger cars, it remains relevant in **specialty and heritage vehicle segments**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **belt stabilisation mechanism** in a bias belted tyre. How does it reduce tread squirm and improve tread life? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
In a pure bias-ply tyre, the diagonal cords of the carcass allow the tread to **"squirm"** ŌĆö that is, to deform in the plane of contact during cornering, braking, and acceleration. This squirm:
- Generates **heat** in the shoulder region,
- Causes **uneven tread wear**,
- Reduces **steering precision**, and
- Limits **high-speed stability**.

**Belt stabilisation (3 marks):**
When 2ŌĆō4 belt plies of high-modulus material (fibreglass, steel, or aramid) are wrapped circumferentially at the crown, the **stretching of the tread band in the circumferential direction is severely restricted**. The belt acts like a steel band tightening a barrel ŌĆö it locks the tread into a **near-rigid hoop**.

**Quantitative effect (1 mark):**
The effective tread stiffness becomes:

$$k_{\text{eff}} = k_{\text{bias}} + n_b \cdot k_{\text{belt}}$$

Squirm displacement reduces by the factor $\sqrt{k_{\text{bias}} / k_{\text{eff}}}$, typically 40ŌĆō60% with two fibreglass belts.

**Result on tread life (1 mark):**
Because squirm energy is dissipated as **frictional heat** (which degrades rubber) and as **mechanical scuffing** (which physically wears the tread), reducing squirm directly extends tread life by **40%ŌĆō60%** over a comparable bias-ply tyre.

---

**(b)** A **6-PR bias belted tyre** has a contact patch of dimensions **120 mm (length) ├Ś 80 mm (width)** and supports a vehicle load of **3500 N**. Calculate **(i) the actual number of body plies**, **(ii) the contact patch area, and (iii) the average contact patch pressure**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Number of body plies (2 marks):**

$$N_{\text{plies}} = \frac{\text{PR}}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ body plies}$$

**(ii) Contact patch area (2 marks):**

$$A_{\text{cp}} = L \times W = 120 \times 80 = 9600 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**(iii) Average contact patch pressure (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_{\text{cp}} = \frac{W_{\text{load}}}{A_{\text{cp}}} = \frac{3500 \; \text{N}}{9600 \; \text{mm}^2}$$

Converting: $1 \; \text{N/mm}^2 = 10^{3} \; \text{kPa}$

$$\sigma_{\text{cp}} = \frac{3500}{9600} \; \text{N/mm}^2 = 0.3646 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{\text{cp}} = 0.3646 \times 10^{3} \; \text{kPa} \approx 364.6 \; \text{kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_{\text{cp}} \approx \mathbf{365 \; kPa}$ (typical bias belted car tyre contact pressure). Ō£ō

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall to avoid:** Many KTU students forget the unit conversion $\text{N/mm}^2 \rightarrow \text{kPa}$ and write the answer in $\text{N/mm}^2$, losing 1 mark. Always check units before finalising.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Bias Belted Tyres**

- **Definition:** A bias carcass (30┬░ŌĆō45┬░) **+ circumferential belt plies** (20┬░ŌĆō30┬░) at the crown.
- **Primary purpose of belt:** Lock the tread flat ŌåÆ **reduce squirm** ŌåÆ improve tread life, steering, and speed capability.
- **Typical belt materials:** Fibreglass (most common), steel (heavy-duty), aramid (premium niche).
- **Belt count:** Usually **2 plies** in passenger, up to **4 plies** in light-truck bias-belted tyres.
- **Sidewall characteristic:** Stays **tough and impact-resistant** because the carcass remains diagonal ŌĆö *unlike* a radial.
- **Carcass plies vs body plies:** Same thing ŌĆö diagonal cords of rayon/nylon/polyester.
- **Bead:** Always **high-tensile steel** ŌĆö never replaced by fabric in modern tyres.
- **Inner liner:** Halobutyl rubber for tubeless operation; tubed variants are largely obsolete.
- **Ply Rating formula:** $N_{\text{plies}} = \text{PR} / 2$ for bias and bias-belted.
