- **Contact patch pressure formula:** $\sigma_{\text{cp}} = W_{\text{load}} / A_{\text{cp}}$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ ŌåÆ multiply by $10^3$ for kPa.
- **Comparison table must include:** ply angle, tread life, rolling resistance, cornering, impact, applications.
- **Examiner golden rule:** **Always draw a labelled diagram** ŌĆö it is worth 2 marks minimum in any Part B question on tyre construction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tyre Aspect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tyre Aspect ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of KTU's **PCMUT302 ŌĆō Automotive Systems** (Module 3: Tyres), the term **"Tyre Aspect"** refers to the **Aspect Ratio** of a pneumatic tyre ŌĆö a fundamental geometric parameter that expresses the proportional relationship between the **section height (sidewall height)** of a tyre and its **section width (nominal tread width)**. The **Tyre Aspect Ratio (AR)** is mathematically defined as: $$\text{Aspect Ratio (AR)} = \left( \frac{H_s}{W_s} \right) \times 100 \ \%$$ Where: - $H_s$ = Section height (sidewall height) in **mm** - $W_s$ = Section width (nominal tread width) in **mm** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (Learn Verbatim for Exams):** > *"The Tyre Aspect Ratio is the numerical value indicating the ratio of a tyre's cross-sectional height to its cross-sectional width, expressed as a percentage. It is a critical parameter in the metric tyre designation system (e.g., 205/55 R16) that governs ride comfort, handling response, load-carrying capacity, and overall vehicle dynamics."* ### 1.2 Standard Tyre Designation System (Metric) A modern radial-ply tyre is marked using a globally standardized metric nomenclature. The standard format is: $$\boxed{\text{WIDTH} \ / \ \text{ASPECT RATIO} \ \text{R} \ \text{RIM DIAMETER}}$$ **Example:** **205 / 55 R 16** - **205** = Section width = **205 mm** - **55** = Aspect ratio = **55 %** (sidewall height is 55 % of section width) - **R** = Radial construction - **16** = Rim diameter = **16 inches** > [!NOTE] > The letter **"R"** denotes **Radial-ply construction** (as opposed to "B" for bias-ply or "D" for diagonal). Almost all modern passenger and commercial vehicle tyres use radial construction due to its superior heat dissipation, tread life, and fuel efficiency. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you have **two identically-sized rubber "donuts"** (tyres) that must be fitted to the same wheel rim: - **Donut A (High Aspect, e.g., 80 series):** The donut is **tall and slender** ŌĆö the sidewalls are long like the legs of a stork. The rubber sidewall can flex and absorb bumps easily (comfortable ride), but during sharp cornering the tall sidewall "bends over," reducing steering precision. - **Donut B (Low Aspect, e.g., 35 series):** The donut is **short and squat** ŌĆö the sidewalls are stubby like the legs of a Dachshund. The shorter sidewall is stiffer, so cornering grip and steering response are razor-sharp, but every pothole transmits a sharp shock to the cabin (firmer ride). > [!TIP] > **Engineering Intuition:** *A lower aspect ratio is equivalent to a "shorter lever arm" between the rim and the road. Shorter lever arms are stiffer in bending and transmit forces more directly ŌĆö hence the well-known performance-vs-comfort trade-off.* ### 1.4 Series Classification (Industry-Standard Nomenclature) | Series Class | Aspect Ratio Range | Typical Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **High-Profile (High Series)** | **70 ŌĆō 85 %** | Sedans, MUVs, entry-level passenger cars, commercial vehicles | | **Standard-Profile (Mid Series)** | **60 ŌĆō 65 %** | Mid-segment passenger cars (most common today) | | **Low-Profile (Low Series)** | **45 ŌĆō 55 %** | Sports sedans, compact SUVs | | **Ultra-Low-Profile** | **< 35 %** | Supercars, performance vehicles, motorsport | | **Truck/Bus (Heavy-Duty)** | **80 ŌĆō 95 %** | Heavy commercial vehicles, off-road | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse **Tyre Aspect Ratio** with **Tyre Section Width** or **Rim Diameter**. Memorize: *Aspect ratio is ALWAYS a percentage (no unit) that multiplies the width to give the sidewall height.* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Geometric Deconstruction The aspect ratio is not an isolated number ŌĆö it is the master switch that controls **three downstream geometric quantities** of the tyre. The logical chain is: **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Section Width ($W_s$)** Read directly from the metric designation (the first three digits). It is the maximum linear distance across the tread, measured in **mm**, on a properly inflated, unloaded tyre fitted to its recommended rim. **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Aspect Ratio to Compute Sidewall Height ($H_s$)** The sidewall height is *not* listed on the tyre ŌĆö it must be **derived** from the aspect ratio: $$H_s = W_s \times \frac{AR}{100}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Overall (Loaded) Tyre Diameter ($D_o$)** The outside diameter is the sum of the rim diameter (converted to mm) and **twice** the sidewall height (one sidewall at top, one at bottom): $$D_o = D_r + 2 H_s$$ Where $D_r$ is the rim diameter. Since rims are specified in **inches** and widths in **mm**, the standard conversion is used: $$D_r \, (\text{in mm}) = D_r \, (\text{in inches}) \times 25.4$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Revolutions per Unit Distance** This is critical for **speedometer calibration**, **odometer accuracy**, and **gear-ratio design**: $$\text{Rev per km} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{\pi D_o} = \frac{10^6}{\pi D_o}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the Sidewall-Stiffness Proxy (Slenderness Index)** The **Slenderness Ratio (SR)** is the inverse of aspect ratio and is a measure of how "stout" the sidewall is: $$SR = \frac{100}{AR} = \frac{W_s}{H_s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A higher slenderness ratio means a **shorter, stiffer sidewall** (better cornering); a lower slenderness ratio means a **taller, more compliant sidewall** (better comfort and off-road absorption). ### 2.2 Why the Aspect Ratio Matters ŌĆö Engineering "Why & How" | Performance Attribute | Effect of **Lowering** Aspect Ratio (e.g., 70 ŌåÆ 45) | Engineering Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Steering Response** | **Sharper, more direct** | Shorter sidewall = less elastic flex under lateral load = quicker turn-in | | **Cornering Grip (Lateral G)** | **Higher** | Sidewall deflection under load reduces the contact patch ŌĆö shorter sidewalls deflect less | | **Ride Comfort** | **Reduced (firmer)** | Sidewall air-volume and rubber thickness are smaller; less bump-absorption cushion | | **Rolling Resistance** | **Slightly lower** | Less flexural hysteresis loss in shorter sidewalls | | **Impact / Pothole Resistance** | **Worsens dramatically** | Shorter sidewall cannot deflect enough ŌåÆ air-pressure spike ŌåÆ sidewall blowout or rim damage | | **Speedometer Reading** | **Reads higher than true** (if diameter is smaller) | Smaller overall diameter = more rev/km = vehicle appears to travel faster than ground-truth | | **Load Capacity** | **Slightly lower** (for same width) | Less air volume ŌåÆ less internal pressure support | | **Braking on Rough Surfaces** | **Improved** | Stiffer sidewall keeps contact patch flatter under longitudinal load transfer | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Aspect ratio is the **single most important tyre parameter chosen by vehicle NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) and dynamics engineers** during vehicle development. The OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) selects a target aspect ratio to balance the inherent conflict between the **Suspension Tuning**, **Steering Geometry**, and **Target Customer Use-Case** (city / highway / sport / off-road). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams) | # | Formula | Description | Unit | Variables | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $AR = \dfrac{H_s}{W_s} \times 100$ | Definition of aspect ratio | **%** (dimensionless) | $H_s, W_s$ | | 2 | $H_s = \dfrac{W_s \times AR}{100}$ | Sidewall (section) height | **mm** | $W_s, AR$ | | 3 | $D_o = D_r + 2 H_s$ | Overall outside diameter | **mm** | $D_r, H_s$ | | 4 | $D_r (\text{mm}) = D_r (\text{in}) \times 25.4$ | Rim-diameter conversion | **mm** | $D_r$ | | 5 | $D_o (\text{in}) = D_r + \dfrac{2 H_s}{25.4}$ | Overall diameter in inches | **inches** | $D_r, H_s$ | | 6 | $N_{rev/km} = \dfrac{10^6}{\pi D_o}$ | Revolutions per kilometre | **rev/km** | $D_o$ in mm | | 7 | $v = \dfrac{\pi D_o N}{60 \times 1000}$ | Vehicle speed from wheel rpm | **m/s** | $D_o$ (mm), $N$ (rpm) | | 8 | $v_{km/h} = \dfrac{\pi D_o N}{60 \times 1000} \times 3.6$ | Vehicle speed in km/h | **km/h** | $D_o$ (mm), $N$ (rpm) | | 9 | $SR = \dfrac{W_s}{H_s} = \dfrac{100}{AR}$ | Slenderness ratio (inverse of AR) | **dimensionless** | $W_s, H_s$ | | 10 | $\Delta v \, (\%) = \dfrac{D_{o,new} - D_{o,old}}{D_{o,old}} \times 100$ | Speedometer error on tyre change | **%** | $D_{o,new}, D_{o,old}$ | > [!TIP] > **Memorization Hack:** Remember the master equation chain: ***Width ŌåÆ Aspect Ratio ŌåÆ Sidewall Height ŌåÆ Overall Diameter ŌåÆ Rev/km ŌåÆ Vehicle Speed.*** Every KTU numerical problem on tyres reduces to traversing this chain. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Derivation 1 ŌĆö Geometry from Tyre Designation > **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 3-Mark Variety):** > *"A passenger car is fitted with tyres marked **205 / 65 R 15**. Compute (a) the sidewall height, (b) the overall outside diameter in mm, and (c) the revolutions per kilometre at the rated rolling condition."* **Step 1 ŌĆö Decode the designation** Reading from the sidewall: $W_s = 205$ mm, $AR = 65$ %, rim $D_r = 15$ in. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the sidewall height $H_s$** Substituting into the aspect-ratio definition: $$H_s = \frac{W_s \times AR}{100} = \frac{205 \times 65}{100} \ \text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{H_s = 133.25 \ \text{mm}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Convert rim diameter to mm** $$D_r = 15 \times 25.4 = 381 \ \text{mm}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the overall outside diameter $D_o$** $$D_o = D_r + 2 H_s = 381 + 2(133.25)$$ $$D_o = 381 + 266.50$$ $$\boxed{D_o = 647.50 \ \text{mm}}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute revolutions per kilometre** $$N_{rev/km} = \frac{10^6}{\pi D_o} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{\pi \times 647.50}$$ $$N_{rev/km} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{2034.27}$$ $$\boxed{N_{rev/km} \approx 491.58 \ \text{rev/km}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Derivation 2 ŌĆö Plus-Sizing & Speedometer Error > **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Variety):** > *"A vehicle is originally fitted with **185 / 70 R 14** tyres. The owner upgrades to **195 / 60 R 15** (so-called 'plus-zero' sizing). If the speedometer was calibrated to read correctly with the original tyres, what is the **percentage speedometer error** introduced by the new tyres, and is the driver being **fined for overspeeding** or **driving below the legal limit** in reality?"* **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the original overall diameter $D_{o,1}$** $$H_{s,1} = \frac{185 \times 70}{100} = 129.50 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{r,1} = 14 \times 25.4 = 355.6 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{o,1} = 355.6 + 2(129.50) = 355.6 + 259.0 = 614.60 \ \text{mm}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the new overall diameter $D_{o,2}$** $$H_{s,2} = \frac{195 \times 60}{100} = 117.00 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{r,2} = 15 \times 25.4 = 381.0 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{o,2} = 381.0 + 2(117.00) = 381.0 + 234.0 = 615.00 \ \text{mm}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the percentage diameter change** $$\Delta D_o \, (\%) = \frac{D_{o,2} - D_{o,1}}{D_{o,1}} \times 100$$ $$\Delta D_o = \frac{615.00 - 614.60}{614.60} \times 100 = \frac{0.40}{614.60} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\Delta D_o \approx +0.065 \ \%}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Interpret the speedometer error** Since the new overall diameter is **slightly larger** (by 0.065 %), the wheel completes **fewer revolutions per kilometre** than the speedometer's reference. Therefore: - The speedometer **reads LOWER** than the true ground speed. - The driver is, in reality, travelling **slightly faster** than the speedometer indicates. - $\Rightarrow$ Driver is **NOT safe from overspeeding fines**; if anything, they are at *greater* legal risk (true speed $>$ indicated speed). $$\boxed{v_{true} = v_{indicated} \times \left( 1 + \frac{\Delta D_o}{100} \right) \approx v_{indicated} \times 1.00065}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Most vehicle manufacturers (OEMs) deliberately calibrate speedometers to read **2 ŌĆō 5 % HIGHER** than true ground speed, to legally protect drivers from accidental overspeeding. Any aftermarket tyre change that increases the overall diameter by more than ~3 % is generally discouraged by manufacturers as it can compromise ABS, traction control, and ESC tuning. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö "Which Aspect Ratio to Choose?" The following matrix summarizes the **optimal aspect ratio selection** for typical vehicle use-cases (a very high-yield KTU short-answer topic): | Use-Case | Recommended AR | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **City commuter / taxi** | **65 ŌĆō 80** | Maximum ride comfort, pothole absorption, long tread life | | **Highway cruiser** | **60 ŌĆō 70** | Balance between comfort and stable high-speed tracking | | **Sports sedan** | **40 ŌĆō 55** | Sharp steering, high cornering grip, less sidewall flex | | **Supercar / Motorsport** | **25 ŌĆō 35** | Maximum lateral stiffness, minimal camber change under load | | **Off-road (4├Ś4 / SUV)** | **75 ŌĆō 90** | Tall sidewall for obstacle climbing and impact absorption | | **Commercial truck / bus** | **80 ŌĆō 95** | Maximum load capacity, long mileage, retreadability | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Tyre Cross-Section Showing Aspect Ratio Geometry The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a radial-ply tyre cross-section** and how the aspect ratio is defined geometrically. (Adapted as a labelled schematic ŌĆö see Fig. 4.1.) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["RADIAL TYRE
CROSS SECTION VIEW"] --> B["SECTION WIDTH Ws
= 205 mm"] A --> C["SIDEWALL HEIGHT Hs
= 133.25 mm"] A --> D["RIM DIAMETER Dr
= 381 mm"] A --> E["OVERALL DIAMETER Do
= 647.50 mm"] B --> F["TREAD
Width Dimension"] C --> G["SIDEWALL
Vertical Dimension"] D --> H["BEAD
Rim Seat"] E --> I["DEFINE
Rolling Circle"] F --> J["CONTACT PATCH
at Ground Level"] G --> K["AR = Hs / Ws x 100
= 65 %"] H --> L["INNER LINER
Air Retention"] I --> M["REV / km = 10^6 / PI x Do"] J --> N["TYRE PERFORMANCE
Cornering, Braking, Traction"] K --> O["VEHICLE DYNAMICS
Comfort vs Handling Trade off"] L --> P["SAFETY and
PRESSURE Integrity"] M --> Q["SPEEDOMETER
Calibration Input"] style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style O fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Fig. 4.1 ŌĆö Functional block architecture of a 205/65 R15 radial tyre cross-section.** The width ($W_s$) and the sidewall height ($H_s$) are the two primary geometric inputs that define the aspect ratio, which in turn governs the overall diameter, rolling revolutions, and ultimately vehicle dynamics. ### 4.2 Effect-of-Aspect-Ratio Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SELECT
TYRE ASPECT
RATIO"] --> B{"USE CASE?"} B -- "City / Comfort" --> C["HIGH AR
70 to 85 %"] B -- "Sport / Handling" --> D["LOW AR
30 to 55 %"] B -- "Off road" --> E["VERY HIGH AR
80 to 95 %"] B -- "Commercial" --> F["HIGH AR
80 to 90 %"] C --> G["SOFT SIDEWALL
FLEX + COMFORT"] D --> H["STIFF SIDEWALL
GRIP + RESPONSE"] E --> I["TALL SIDEWALL
OBSTACLE CLIMB"] F --> J["REINFORCED
LOAD CAPACITY"] G --> K["FUEL ECONOMY
FAVOURABLE"] H --> L["HANDLING
FAVOURABLE"] I --> M["GROUND
CLEARANCE +"] J --> N["DURABILITY +
RETREADABLE"] style A fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style M fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style N fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Fig. 4.2 ŌĆö Decision flowchart for aspect-ratio selection** based on the intended vehicle application. The aspect ratio is the primary "knob" that tyre designers turn to achieve a target ride-handling balance. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q.A1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`** *Define the term **Tyre Aspect Ratio**. Using a suitable example, explain how it is represented in the standard metric tyre designation system.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Tyre Aspect Ratio (AR)** is defined as the ratio of the cross-sectional height (sidewall height, $H_s$) of a tyre to its section width ($W_s$), expressed as a percentage. $$AR = \left( \frac{H_s}{W_s} \right) \times 100 \ \%$$ **Example:** A tyre marked **205 / 65 R 15** has a section width of **205 mm**, an aspect ratio of **65 %** (meaning the sidewall height is 65 % of 205 mm = 133.25 mm), and is fitted to a **15-inch** rim. The letter **"R"** indicates **radial-ply** construction. *(Marks distribution: 1 mark for the definition + formula, 1 mark for the numerical example, 1 mark for identifying the construction type.)* --- **Q.A2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`** *Explain the **performance trade-off** between a low-aspect-ratio tyre and a high-aspect-ratio tyre, with reference to ride comfort and cornering ability.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **low aspect ratio** (e.g., 35 series) has a **short, stiff sidewall**. This reduces sidewall flex under lateral loads, providing **superior cornering grip and sharper steering response**, but the reduced air volume and stiffer rubber degrade **ride comfort** because the tyre cannot absorb road irregularities as effectively. A **high aspect ratio** (e.g., 80 series) has a **tall, compliant sidewall** that flexes easily, providing a **softer, more comfortable ride** and better absorption of potholes. However, the tall sidewall **bends under cornering loads**, reducing the effective contact patch and degrading steering precision. > **Conclusion:** A clear inverse trade-off exists ŌĆö *handling improves as comfort degrades* and vice-versa. OEMs balance this trade-off based on target customer use-case. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- **Q.B1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 3]`** **Either (a):** A vehicle is fitted with **P 215 / 60 R 16** tyres. Calculate: (i) The **sidewall height** in mm. (ii) The **overall outside diameter** in mm and in inches. (iii) The **revolutions per kilometre** at the rated rolling condition. (iv) The **vehicle speed in km/h** when the wheels are rotating at **850 rpm**. **OR** **(b):** A car is originally fitted with **195 / 65 R 15** tyres. The owner replaces them with **225 / 45 R 17** tyres ("plus-two" sizing). Determine: (i) The original and new overall diameters in mm. (ii) The **percentage change** in overall diameter. (iii) Whether the speedometer will read **higher or lower** than the true ground speed after the change, and justify. (iv) The **true vehicle speed** when the speedometer reads **100 km/h**. --- **Model Solution for Q.B1(a):** *(7 + 7 = 14 marks)* **Given:** $W_s = 215$ mm, $AR = 60$ %, $D_r = 16$ in, $N = 850$ rpm. **(i) Sidewall Height (3 marks):** $$H_s = \frac{W_s \times AR}{100} = \frac{215 \times 60}{100} = \frac{12900}{100}$$ $$\boxed{H_s = 129.00 \ \text{mm}}$$ **(ii) Overall Outside Diameter (3 marks):** $$D_r \, (\text{mm}) = 16 \times 25.4 = 406.4 \ \texttext{mm}$$ $$D_o = D_r + 2 H_s = 406.4 + 2(129.00) = 406.4 + 258.0$$ $$\boxed{D_o = 664.40 \ \text{mm}}$$ Converting to inches: $$D_o \, (\text{in}) = \frac{664.40}{25.4} = 26.16 \ \text{in}$$ $$\boxed{D_o \approx 26.16 \ \text{in}}$$ **(iii) Revolutions per kilometre (4 marks):** $$N_{rev/km} = \frac{10^6}{\pi D_o} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{\pi \times 664.40}$$ $$N_{rev/km} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{2087.13}$$ $$\boxed{N_{rev/km} \approx 479.13 \ \text{rev/km}}$$ **(iv) Vehicle Speed at 850 rpm (4 marks):** $$v_{km/h} = \frac{\pi D_o N}{60 \times 1000} \times 3.6$$ $$v_{km/h} = \frac{\pi \times 664.40 \times 850}{60{,}000} \times 3.6$$ $$v_{km/h} = \frac{1{,}774{,}586.96}{60{,}000} \times 3.6 = 29.576 \times 3.6$$ $$\boxed{v_{km/h} \approx 106.47 \ \text{km/h}}$$ --- **Model Solution for Q.B1(b):** *(7 + 7 = 14 marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)* **Given:** Original: 195/65 R15 ; New: 225/45 R17. **(i) Original and New Overall Diameters (4 + 4 marks):** *Original:* $$H_{s,1} = \frac{195 \times 65}{100} = 126.75 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{r,1} = 15 \times 25.4 = 381.0 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{o,1} = 381.0 + 2(126.75) = 381.0 + 253.50$$ $$\boxed{D_{o,1} = 634.50 \ \text{mm}}$$ *New:* $$H_{s,2} = \frac{225 \times 45}{100} = 101.25 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{r,2} = 17 \times 25.4 = 431.8 \ \text{mm}$$ $$D_{o,2} = 431.8 + 2(101.25) = 431.8 + 202.50$$ $$\boxed{D_{o,2} = 634.30 \ \text{mm}}$$ **(ii) Percentage Change in Overall Diameter (3 marks):** $$\Delta D_o \, (\%) = \frac{D_{o,2} - D_{o,1}}{D_{o,1}} \times 100 = \frac{634.30 - 634.50}{634.50} \times 100$$ $$\Delta D_o = \frac{-0.20}{634.50} \times 100 = -0.0315 \ \%$$ $$\boxed{\Delta D_o \approx -0.032 \ \% \ (\text{negligible decrease})}$$ **(iii) Speedometer Direction (2 marks):** Since the new diameter is **slightly smaller** (by ~0.03 %), the wheel rotates **fractionally more** revolutions per kilometre than the speedometer's reference. Therefore, the speedometer will read **slightly LOWER** than the true ground speed. The driver is travelling marginally faster than indicated. **(iv) True Speed at 100 km/h Indicated (1 mark):** $$v_{true} = v_{indicated} \times \frac{D_{o,1}}{D_{o,2}} = 100 \times \frac{634.50}{634.30} = 100 \times 1.00032$$ $$\boxed{v_{true} \approx 100.03 \ \text{km/h}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to convert rim diameter** from inches to mm before adding sidewall heights. KTU answer keys explicitly require $D_r$ in **mm** when the sidewall height is in mm. > 2. **Mixing up the formula direction** in speedometer error problems: a **smaller** diameter ŌåÆ **more** rev/km ŌåÆ speedometer **under-reads**. > 3. **Ignoring the conversion factor $3.6$** when converting m/s ŌåÆ km/h in vehicle-speed calculations. > 4. **Confusing the slenderness ratio** with the aspect ratio (slenderness is the *inverse*). > 5. **Not stating units** in the final boxed answer ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct up to 0.5 marks per answer for missing units. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - Ō£ģ **Definition:** Aspect Ratio = $(H_s / W_s) \times 100$ ŌĆö dimensionless, expressed as **%**. - Ō£ģ **Designation Format:** **Width / AR R Rim(in)** ŌĆö e.g., *205/55 R16* means $W_s = 205$ mm, $AR = 55$ %, rim = 16". - Ō£ģ **"R" = Radial construction** (the global standard for modern passenger/commercial tyres). - Ō£ģ **Sidewall Height Formula:** $H_s = W_s \times AR / 100$. - Ō£ģ **Overall Diameter Formula:** $D_o = D_r(\text{mm}) + 2 H_s$ where $D_r(\text{mm}) = D_r(\text{in}) \times 25.4$. - Ō£ģ **Rev/km Formula:** $N_{rev/km} = 10^6 / (\pi D_o)$. - Ō£ģ **Speed Formula:** $v = \pi D_o N / 60{,}000$ m/s ; multiply by 3.6 to get km/h. - Ō£ģ **Trade-Off:** Ōåō AR ŌćÆ Ōåæ handling, Ōåō comfort ; Ōåæ AR ŌćÆ Ōåæ comfort, Ōåō handling. - Ō£ģ **Plus-Sizing Rule:** Total rolling diameter should stay within **┬▒3 %** of OEM spec to preserve speedometer and ABS/ESC calibration. - Ō£ģ **Slenderness Ratio = Inverse of AR** ŌĆö high slenderness = low aspect = stiff sidewall. - Ō£ģ **Series Nomenclature:** 80-series (high), 65-series (mid), 45-series (low), 30-series (ultra-low). - Ō£ģ **Commercial vs Sport:** Trucks use **80 ŌĆō 95 %** AR (load + retread); supercars use **25 ŌĆō 35 %** AR (grip + response). > **End of Module 3 ŌĆö Tyre Aspect Notes (PCMUT302, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Automotive Systems**, a **Ratio** is a dimensionless quantity that expresses the proportional relationship between two mechanical parameters ŌĆö most commonly rotational speed, torque, or angular displacement ŌĆö between an input member and an output member of a vehicle sub-system (gearbox, final drive, steering, suspension, or brakes).

The general mathematical form of a mechanical ratio is:

$$R = \frac{\text{Input Quantity}}{\text{Output Quantity}}$$

In a vehicle, the most critical ratios are:
- **Gear (Transmission) Ratio** $\left(i_g\right)$
- **Final Drive / Axle Ratio** $\left(i_f\right)$
- **Overall Drive Ratio** $\left(i_o\right)$
- **Steering Ratio** $\left(i_s\right)$
- **Motion / Suspension Ratio** $\left(i_m\right)$
- **Brake Force / Pedal Ratio** $\left(i_b\right)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Ratio in automobiles is a comparison of the angular velocity (or torque) of the driving member to the driven member, used to determine the speed reduction, torque multiplication, and mechanical advantage of drivetrain, steering, and suspension subsystems.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **bicycle** with the front sprocket (pedal gear) being small and the rear sprocket (wheel gear) being large. When you pedal **once**, the rear wheel turns **less than once** ŌĆö but with much more force at the rim. This is the essence of a **ratio** in an automobile: it trades **speed for torque** (or vice versa).

> **Plain English Intuition:**
> - A **high ratio** (e.g., 4:1) means the output spins **slowly** but delivers **high torque** ŌĆö perfect for starting a loaded truck from rest (1st gear).
> - A **low ratio** (e.g., 1:1) means output spins **fast** but delivers **low torque** ŌĆö perfect for cruising on a highway (top gear/overdrive).
> - **Steering Ratio:** A small steering wheel movement produces a larger (or smaller) turn of the road wheels.
> - **Suspension Motion Ratio:** A small wheel bump produces a different vertical displacement at the spring/damper.

Think of a ratio as a **mechanical translator** ŌĆö like a currency exchange booth that converts one form of motion into another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold:**
> - Wheel/Tire diameter is commonly **15"ŌĆō22"** in passenger cars.
> - Pinion gear teeth in passenger cars: typically **8ŌĆō12 teeth**.
> - Ring gear teeth: typically **35ŌĆō45 teeth** (final drive ratio range: 2.5:1 ŌĆō 4.5:1).
> - Transmission gear ratios in 5-speed manual cars: 1st gear Ōēł **3.5:1**, Top gear Ōēł **0.8:1** (overdrive).

---

## 1.3 Physical Significance of Ratios in an Automobile

| Subsystem | Ratio Name | Physical Purpose |
|-----------|------------|------------------|
| Gearbox | Transmission Ratio | Multiply torque at low vehicle speeds |
| Differential | Final Drive Ratio | Permanent speed reduction & torque split |
| Steering | Steering Ratio | Reduce driver effort; balance agility & stability |
| Suspension | Motion Ratio | Trade wheel travel for spring/damper travel |
| Brakes | Pedal / Brake Ratio | Multiply pedal force into clamping force |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Whenever a question asks for "overall vehicle performance", you must compute the **product** of all gear ratios in the power flow path: **Engine ŌåÆ Clutch ŌåÆ Gearbox ŌåÆ Propeller Shaft ŌåÆ Final Drive ŌåÆ Wheels**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gear Ratio in a Simple Gear Train

A **simple gear train** consists of two meshing gears ŌĆö a **driver (pinion)** and a **driven (gear)**. When the driver rotates by one tooth, the driven must also rotate by one tooth (assuming no slip). Hence the angular displacements are inversely proportional to the number of teeth.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Bullets)

- **Step 1:** Linear pitch at the pitch circle must be equal (no slip condition):
  
  $v = \pi d_1 N_1 = \pi d_2 N_2$
  
  (where $d$ = pitch diameter, $N$ = rpm)

- **Step 2:** Since $d \propto T$ (number of teeth, with same module $m$):
  
  $T_1 N_1 = T_2 N_2$

- **Step 3:** Therefore, the **velocity (speed) ratio** is:
  
  $i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{d_2}{d_1}$

- **Step 4:** The **torque ratio** (mechanical advantage) is the inverse:
  
  $\frac{T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}}} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = i$

  (when efficiency = **100%**, which is the KTU assumption unless otherwise stated)

---

## 2.2 Compound Gear Train Ratio

A **compound gear train** uses two or more gear pairs on a common intermediate (layshaft) to achieve higher ratios in a compact space.

### 2.2.1 Velocity Ratio of Compound Train

$$i_{\text{compound}} = \frac{N_{\text{in}}}{N_{\text{out}}} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3} = \frac{\text{Product of driven teeth}}{\text{Product of driving teeth}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** The intermediate shaft gears (e.g., $T_2$ and $T_3$ on the same shaft) rotate at the **same speed** but are **electrically/mechanically isolated** by their mount. Always verify that you correctly identify which gears are on the **same shaft** (they share rpm) and which are **meshing pairs** (they share teeth ratio).

---

## 2.3 Final Drive Ratio (Axle Ratio)

The **final drive** sits between the propeller shaft and the drive wheels, inside the differential housing. It consists of a **pinion gear** (driven by the propeller shaft) meshing with a **ring (crown) gear** (attached to the differential case).

$$i_f = \frac{\text{Number of teeth on ring gear}}{\text{Number of teeth on pinion gear}} = \frac{T_R}{T_P} = \frac{N_P}{N_R}$$

---

## 2.4 Overall Drive Ratio

The **overall drive ratio** is the cumulative speed reduction from the engine crankshaft to the road wheels for a given gear:

$$i_o = i_g \times i_f \times i_t$$

Where:
- $i_g$ = gearbox transmission ratio (for the selected gear)
- $i_f$ = final drive ratio
- $i_t$ = torque converter ratio (Ōēł 1 for manual; 2.0ŌĆō2.5 stall for automatic)

> [!NOTE]
> For **manual transmission** questions in KTU 2024, $i_t$ is usually taken as **1**, simplifying: $i_o = i_g \times i_f$.

---

## 2.5 Vehicle Speed from Engine Speed

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o \cdot 60} \;\; \text{(m/s)}$$

Where:
- $d_w$ = effective wheel diameter (m)
- $N_e$ = engine speed (rpm)
- $i_o$ = overall ratio

Converted to **km/h**:

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_e \cdot 60}{i_o \cdot 1000 \times 60} \cdot \frac{3600}{1000} = \frac{0.377 \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o} \;\; \text{(km/h, with } d_w \text{ in meters)}$$

---

## 2.6 Steering Ratio

$$i_s = \frac{\theta_{\text{steering wheel}}}{\theta_{\text{road wheel}}}$$

Typical values: **12:1 to 20:1** (passenger cars); **15:1 to 25:1** (trucks).

- **Low $i_s$ (e.g., 12:1):** Quick, sporty steering ŌĆö small wheel input ŌåÆ large wheel turn.
- **High $i_s$ (e.g., 20:1):** Slow, comfortable steering ŌĆö large wheel input ŌåÆ small wheel turn.

---

## 2.7 Suspension Motion Ratio

$$i_m = \frac{\text{Wheel vertical travel}}{\text{Spring/Damper travel}}$$

- **Short-long arm (SLA) suspension:** $i_m \approx 0.8$ to 1.0
- **MacPherson strut:** $i_m \approx 0.7$ to 1.2 (varies with position)

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $i = N_1/N_2 = T_2/T_1$ | Simple gear train speed ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $i = (T_2 \cdot T_4)/(T_1 \cdot T_3)$ | Compound gear train ratio | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_{\text{out}} = i \cdot T_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta$ | Torque multiplication | Nm; $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 4 | $i_f = T_R/T_P$ | Final drive / axle ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $i_o = i_g \cdot i_f$ | Overall drive ratio | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $V = (0.377 \cdot d_w \cdot N_e)/i_o$ | Vehicle speed | km/h; $d_w$ in m |
| 7 | $P = 2\pi N T/60$ | Power transmitted | Watts |
| 8 | $i_s = \theta_{\text{sw}}/\theta_{\text{rw}}$ | Steering ratio | degrees / degrees |
| 9 | $i_m = x_w/x_s$ | Motion ratio (suspension) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\omega_o = \omega_e/i_o$ | Output angular velocity | rad/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use the vertical bar $\vert$ in tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for magnitude. The table above uses standard notation, but if you write a conditional like $N \vert T$, it must be escaped.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drivetrain Design:** Engineers select $i_o$ in **1st gear** to give a starting tractive effort 3ŌĆō4├Ś the vehicle weight (for a 30% gradeability on commercial trucks).
- **Fuel Economy:** Modern cars use **overdrive** ($i < 1$) in top gear to reduce engine rpm at highway cruise, saving ~**10ŌĆō15% fuel**.
- **Steering Feel:** Luxury cars (e.g., BMW 7-Series) use **variable ratio steering** ŌĆö $i_s$ changes with steering angle to combine high-speed stability with low-speed agility.
- **Suspension Tuning:** Motion ratio affects the effective spring rate felt at the wheel: $k_{\text{wheel}} = i_m^2 \cdot k_{\text{spring}}$.
- **CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission):** Replaces discrete gear ratios with a **continuous ratio range** (e.g., 2.5:1 to 0.5:1 in a Nissan's Xtronic CVT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Simple Gear Train Speed Ratio

### Given
- Driver gear has $T_1$ teeth, diameter $d_1$, speed $N_1$ (rpm)
- Driven gear has $T_2$ teeth, diameter $d_2$, speed $N_2$ (rpm)
- Module $m$ is identical for both gears (standard meshing condition)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Geometric relationship between teeth and diameter:**

Since the module $m$ is the same for both meshing gears:

$$d_1 = m \cdot T_1 \quad \text{and} \quad d_2 = m \cdot T_2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö No-slip condition at the pitch point:**

The linear velocity of both gear pitch circles must be equal (no slipping):

$$v = \pi \cdot d_1 \cdot N_1 = \pi \cdot d_2 \cdot N_2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $d_1$ and $d_2$:**

$$\pi \cdot (m \cdot T_1) \cdot N_1 = \pi \cdot (m \cdot T_2) \cdot N_2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Cancel common terms ($\pi$ and $m$):**

$$T_1 \cdot N_1 = T_2 \cdot N_2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange to get the speed (velocity) ratio:**

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

This is the **fundamental gear ratio equation** of a simple gear train.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Torque and power relationship:**

Assuming **100% efficiency** ($\eta = 1$):

$$P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$$

$$\frac{2\pi N_1 T_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi N_2 T_2}{60}$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

Thus: $\boxed{T_2 = T_1 \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2} = T_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}}$ ŌĆö torque is multiplied by the gear ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why an engine producing **200 Nm** in 1st gear ($i = 4$) can deliver effectively **800 Nm** at the driven shaft. The energy comes from the **fuel** burned over the longer engine cycle, not from the gear itself (which is a passive device).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Compound Gear Train Ratio

### Setup

Consider a compound train with:
- Gear **A** (driver, on input shaft): $T_A$ teeth, speed $N_A$
- Gear **B** (on intermediate shaft, meshes with A): $T_B$ teeth
- Gear **C** (on same intermediate shaft as B): $T_C$ teeth, speed $N_C = N_B$
- Gear **D** (driven, on output shaft, meshes with C): $T_D$ teeth, speed $N_D$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the simple gear ratio to the first pair (AŌĆōB):**

$$i_1 = \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{T_B}{T_A}$$

$$N_B = N_A \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_B}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Recognize that B and C are on the same shaft:**

$$N_C = N_B = N_A \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_B}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the simple gear ratio to the second pair (CŌĆōD):**

$$i_2 = \frac{N_C}{N_D} = \frac{T_D}{T_C}$$

$$N_D = N_C \cdot \frac{T_C}{T_D} = N_A \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_B} \cdot \frac{T_C}{T_D}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The overall ratio:**

$$i_{\text{compound}} = \frac{N_A}{N_D} = \frac{T_B \cdot T_D}{T_A \cdot T_C}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power conservation (100% efficiency):**

$$T_D = T_A \cdot i_{\text{compound}} = T_A \cdot \frac{T_B \cdot T_D}{T_A \cdot T_C} = T_A \cdot \frac{T_B}{T_A} \cdot \frac{T_D}{T_C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU 2024):** "Driven teeth on top, driving teeth on bottom." Multiplying the **driven** tooth counts and dividing by the **driving** tooth counts gives the overall speed ratio.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vehicle Speed from Engine Speed

### Given
- Engine speed: $N_e$ (rpm)
- Gearbox ratio: $i_g$
- Final drive ratio: $i_f$
- Effective wheel diameter: $d_w$ (m)
- Overall ratio: $i_o = i_g \cdot i_f$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Propeller shaft speed after gearbox:**

$$N_p = \frac{N_e}{i_g}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Wheel speed after final drive:**

$$N_w = \frac{N_p}{i_f} = \frac{N_e}{i_g \cdot i_f} = \frac{N_e}{i_o}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Linear wheel speed (circumferential velocity):**

$$V = \pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_w = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o} \;\; \text{(m/min)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert m/min ŌåÆ km/h:**

$$V_{\text{km/h}} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o} \cdot \frac{60}{1000} = \frac{0.1885 \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o}$$

Or equivalently (using the standard automotive formula):

$$\boxed{V_{\text{km/h}} = \frac{0.377 \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Tractive effort at the wheel:**

The torque at the wheel:

$$T_w = T_e \cdot i_o \cdot \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$$

The tractive force at the tire contact patch:

$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_o \cdot \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}}{r_w}$$

Where $r_w = d_w/2$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show the **unit conversion** step-by-step. Many students lose 1ŌĆō2 marks by writing the wrong unit (mixing mm with m, or rpm with rad/s).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Full Vehicle Speed Problem

### Problem Statement
A car has:
- Engine speed at cruise: $N_e = 2500$ rpm
- 4th gear ratio: $i_g = 1.1$
- Final drive ratio: $i_f = 3.7$
- Tire size: 185/70 R14 (static loaded radius Ōēł **0.295 m**)
- Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta = 0.92$

### Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute overall ratio:**

$$i_o = i_g \times i_f = 1.1 \times 3.7 = 4.07$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the speed formula:**

$$V = \frac{0.377 \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o} = \frac{0.377 \cdot (0.59) \cdot 2500}{4.07}$$

(Note: $d_w = 2 r_w = 2 \times 0.295 = 0.59$ m)

$$V = \frac{556.075}{4.07} = 136.6 \;\; \text{km/h}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute wheel torque if engine torque $T_e = 180$ Nm:**

$$T_w = T_e \cdot i_o \cdot \eta = 180 \times 4.07 \times 0.92 = 674 \;\; \text{Nm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute tractive effort:**

$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{674}{0.295} = 2285 \;\; \text{N}$$

This force is what propels the **~1200 kg** car forward (acceleration Ōēł 1.9 m/s┬▓ on level ground).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Power Flow & Ratios in an Automobile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine / Crankshaft] -- Ne rpm, Te Nm --> CLU[Clutch]
    CLU --> GB[Gearbox: Transmission Ratio ig]
    GB --> PS[Propeller Shaft: Np = Ne / ig]
    PS --> FD[Final Drive: Axle Ratio if]
    FD --> DIFF[Differential]
    DIFF --> WL[Road Wheels: Nw = Ne / io]
    WL -- V km/h --> RD[Road: Tractive Effort Ft]
    
    ENG -.- R1[io = ig ├Ś if]
    GB -.- R2[ig = Tdriven/Tdriver]
    FD -.- R3[if = TR / TP]
    WL -.- R4[V = 0.377 ├Ś dw ├Ś Ne / io]
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic ŌĆö Simple vs. Compound Gear Train Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIMPLE[Simple Gear Train]
        S1[Driver Gear: T1 teeth, N1 rpm] -->|mesh| S2[Driven Gear: T2 teeth, N2 rpm]
    end
    
    subgraph COMPOUND[Compound Gear Train]
        C1[Input Shaft: Gear A T1] -->|mesh| C2[Intermediate Shaft: Gear B T2]
        C2 --- C3[Same Shaft: Gear C T3]
        C3 -->|mesh| C4[Output Shaft: Gear D T4]
    end
    
    SIMPLE -.- F1[isimple = T2 / T1]
    COMPOUND -.- F2[icompound = T2 ├Ś T4 / T1 ├Ś T3]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö Steering & Suspension Ratios

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STEER[Steering Subsystem]
        SW[Steering Wheel: theta_sw] --> ST[Steering Column]
        ST --> SG[Steering Gear: is = theta_sw / theta_rw]
        SG --> TIE[Tie Rods]
        TIE --> RW1[Road Wheels: theta_rw]
    end
    
    subgraph SUSP[Suspension Subsystem]
        WB[Wheel Bump: xw] --> CA[Control Arm Geometry]
        CA --> SP[Spring Damper: xs = xw / im]
    end
    
    STEER -.- SVAL[Typical is = 12:1 to 20:1]
    SUSP -.- MVAL[Typical im = 0.7 to 1.2]
```

---

All labels inside double quotes are clean, uppercase, alphanumeric text ŌĆö no bold, no italics, no embedded tables.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define gear ratio. With a neat sketch, explain how gear ratio influences the speed and torque of the driven gear in a simple gear train.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **gear ratio** of a simple gear train is defined as the ratio of the speed of the driving gear to the speed of the driven gear, and is numerically equal to the inverse ratio of their number of teeth:

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- If $T_2 > T_1$ (driven has more teeth), $i > 1$: output speed decreases, output torque increases ŌåÆ **speed reducer**, used in 1st gear for starting.
- If $T_2 < T_1$ (driven has fewer teeth), $i < 1$: output speed increases, output torque decreases ŌåÆ **overdrive**, used in top gear for cruising.

Power is conserved: $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$ (assuming 100% efficiency), so $T \cdot N = $ constant.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by overall drive ratio of an automobile? State its significance in vehicle performance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **overall drive ratio** ($i_o$) of an automobile is the product of the transmission ratio ($i_g$) of the selected gear in the gearbox and the final drive ratio ($i_f$) of the differential:

$$i_o = i_g \times i_f$$

For a manual transmission, this represents the total speed reduction from the engine crankshaft to the road wheels.

**Significance (1 mark):**
- It determines the **tractive effort** available at the wheel for a given engine torque ($F_t = T_e \cdot i_o \cdot \eta / r_w$).
- It determines the **maximum vehicle speed** for a given engine rpm and wheel size.
- A higher $i_o$ gives better **acceleration and gradeability** but reduces **top speed**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Simple Gear Train (7 marks)

A simple gear train consists of a driving gear with **25 teeth** meshing with a driven gear of **75 teeth**. The driver rotates at **2000 rpm** and receives a torque of **120 Nm**. Assuming 100% efficiency, calculate:
1. The gear ratio.
2. The speed of the driven gear.
3. The torque available at the driven gear.
4. The power transmitted by the train.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Gear ratio (2 marks):**

$$i = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{75}{25} = 3$$

Hence the gear ratio is **3:1**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Speed of driven gear (1 mark):**

$$N_2 = \frac{N_1}{i} = \frac{2000}{3} = 666.67 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Torque on driven gear (2 marks):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot i \cdot \eta = 120 \times 3 \times 1.0 = 360 \;\; \text{Nm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Power transmitted (2 marks):**

$$P = \frac{2\pi N_2 T_2}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 666.67 \times 360}{60}$$

$$P = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 666.67 \times 360}{60} = 25133 \;\; \text{W} = 25.13 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Verification using input side:**

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{2\pi \times 2000 \times 120}{60} = 25133 \;\; \text{W} = 25.13 \;\; \text{kW} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Compound Gear Train (7 marks)

In a **compound gear train**, gear **A** (driver on input shaft) has **20 teeth**, gear **B** has **50 teeth** on the intermediate shaft, gear **C** is on the same intermediate shaft and has **30 teeth**, and gear **D** (driven) has **90 teeth** on the output shaft. The input speed is **1500 rpm** and the input torque is **80 Nm**. Calculate:
1. The speed of the intermediate shaft.
2. The speed of the output shaft.
3. The overall gear ratio.
4. The torque on the output shaft.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Speed of intermediate shaft (2 marks):**

Gears A and B form the first pair:

$$N_B = N_A \cdot \frac{T_A}{T_B} = 1500 \times \frac{20}{50} = 600 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

Since gear C is on the same shaft as B, $N_C = N_B = 600$ rpm.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Speed of output shaft (2 marks):**

Gears C and D form the second pair:

$$N_D = N_C \cdot \frac{T_C}{T_D} = 600 \times \frac{30}{90} = 200 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Overall gear ratio (1 mark):**

$$i_{\text{compound}} = \frac{N_A}{N_D} = \frac{1500}{200} = 7.5$$

**Verification using the formula:**

$$i = \frac{T_B \cdot T_D}{T_A \cdot T_C} = \frac{50 \times 90}{20 \times 30} = \frac{4500}{600} = 7.5 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Torque on output shaft (2 marks):**

$$T_D = T_A \cdot i_{\text{compound}} \cdot \eta = 80 \times 7.5 \times 1.0 = 600 \;\; \text{Nm}$$

**Power balance check:**

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 80}{60} = 12566 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{2\pi \times 200 \times 600}{60} = 12566 \;\; \text{W} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Final Drive & Overall Ratio (7 marks)

The final drive of a passenger car has **41 teeth on the ring gear** and **11 teeth on the pinion**. The gearbox is in **3rd gear with a ratio of 1.5:1**. The engine speed is **3000 rpm** and the engine torque is **180 Nm**. The tire size is **195/65 R15** (effective diameter Ōēł **0.63 m**). Calculate:
1. The final drive ratio.
2. The overall drive ratio.
3. The speed of the propeller shaft.
4. The speed of the road wheels (in rpm).
5. The vehicle speed in km/h.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Final drive ratio (1 mark):**

$$i_f = \frac{T_R}{T_P} = \frac{41}{11} = 3.727$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Overall drive ratio (1 mark):**

$$i_o = i_g \times i_f = 1.5 \times 3.727 = 5.591$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Propeller shaft speed (1 mark):**

$$N_p = \frac{N_e}{i_g} = \frac{3000}{1.5} = 2000 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Road wheel speed (2 marks):**

$$N_w = \frac{N_p}{i_f} = \frac{2000}{3.727} = 536.6 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Vehicle speed (2 marks):**

$$V = \frac{0.377 \cdot d_w \cdot N_e}{i_o} = \frac{0.377 \times 0.63 \times 3000}{5.591}$$

$$V = \frac{712.53}{5.591} = 127.4 \;\; \text{km/h}$$

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks)

**Derive the relationship between gear ratio, velocity ratio, and mechanical advantage in a simple gear train. Hence show that the power input equals the power output (assuming 100% efficiency).**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define velocity ratio (2 marks):**

The **velocity ratio (VR)** is defined as the ratio of the velocity of the driver to the velocity of the driven:

$$VR = \frac{v_1}{v_2} = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{\pi d_2 N_2} = \frac{d_1 N_1}{d_2 N_2}$$

Since $d_1/d_2 = T_1/T_2$ (equal module):

$$VR = \frac{T_1 \cdot N_1}{T_2 \cdot N_2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define gear ratio (1 mark):**

The **gear ratio** is:

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define mechanical advantage (2 marks):**

The **mechanical advantage (MA)** is the ratio of output torque (load) to input torque (effort):

$$MA = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Relate VR, $i$, and MA (1 mark):**

From the above:

$$MA = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = i = VR$$

(For a gear train, the velocity ratio equals the mechanical advantage when there is no slip.)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power conservation (1 mark):**

Power input:

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{2\pi N_1 T_1}{60}$$

Power output:

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{2\pi N_2 T_2}{60}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}} = \frac{N_1 T_1}{N_2 T_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} = i \cdot \frac{1}{MA} = 1 \;\; (\text{since } MA = i)$$

Therefore: $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} \;\;\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit Errors (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** Mixing m/min with km/h, or using mm for tire diameter instead of m. Always convert to **SI units consistently** before substituting.
> - **Forgetting the "Same Shaft" Rule (ŌĆō2 marks):** In compound gear trains, students often incorrectly multiply $T_B \times T_C$ when both are on the same shaft. The correct compound ratio is $(T_B \cdot T_D)/(T_A \cdot T_C)$ ŌĆö driven teeth on top, driving teeth on bottom.
> - **Final Drive Teeth Confusion (ŌĆō1 mark):** The pinion has FEWER teeth; the ring gear has MORE teeth. Final drive ratio is $T_R/T_P$, not the inverse.
> - **Efficiency Assumption (ŌĆō1 mark):** If the question states "100% efficiency" or doesn't mention efficiency, use $\eta = 1$. If it gives a value (e.g., 0.9), include it: $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \cdot i \cdot \eta$.
> - **Overdrive Sign Convention (ŌĆō1 mark):** In top gear, $i_g < 1$ (e.g., 0.8). Do not write "negative ratio" ŌĆö it is simply a speed-increasing pair.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision ŌĆö Module 3 "Ratio" ŌĆö Bullet-Pointed:**

- **Simple Gear Train Ratio:** $i = N_1/N_2 = T_2/T_1$ (driven teeth on top). Marks: **Always derive from the no-slip condition** $v = \pi d N$ first.
- **Compound Gear Train:** $i = (T_{\text{driven pair 1}} \times T_{\text{driven pair 2}}) / (T_{\text{driver pair 1}} \times T_{\text{driver pair 2}})$. Gears on the **same shaft** share speed.
- **Final Drive (Axle) Ratio:** $i_f = T_R/T_P$ (ring teeth ├Ę pinion teeth). Typical values: 2.5:1 to 4.5:1.
- **Overall Drive Ratio:** $i_o = i_g \times i_f$ (manual) or $i_g \times i_f \times i_t$ (automatic with torque converter).
- **Vehicle Speed Formula:** $V_{\text{km/h}} = (0.377 \cdot d_w \cdot N_e) / i_o$ where $d_w$ is in **meters**.
- **Torque at Wheel:** $T_w = T_e \cdot i_o \cdot \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$.
- **Tractive Effort:** $F_t = T_w / r_w = T_e \cdot i_o \cdot \eta / r_w$ (this is what pushes the car against gradient + drag + rolling resistance).
- **Steering Ratio:** $i_s = \theta_{\text{wheel}} / \theta_{\text{road wheels}}$. Low $i_s$ = sporty, High $i_s$ = comfortable.
- **Suspension Motion Ratio:** $i_m = x_w / x_s$. Effective wheel spring rate $k_{\text{eff}} = i_m^2 \cdot k_{\text{spring}}$.
- **Power Conservation:** $P = 2\pi N T/60$ is the same on input and output (at 100% efficiency) ŌĆö use this for **verification** in numericals.
- **Overdrive:** $i < 1$ ŌåÆ output spins faster than input; used for highway cruising to reduce engine rpm.
- **Common Units:** Engine torque in **Nm**, power in **kW**, speed in **rpm**, wheel diameter in **m**, vehicle speed in **km/h**.
- **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Numerical problems are worth **7 marks each** (Part B sub-questions). Always write the formula, substitute values, and **box the final answer** with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tyre Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tyre Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ø× SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tyre Specifications** refer to the standardized alphanumeric coding system embossed on the sidewall of a pneumatic tyre that conveys critical engineering data ŌĆö *section width, aspect ratio, type of construction, rim diameter, load index, and speed symbol* ŌĆö as defined under **ECE R30 / ETRTO (European Tyre and Rim Technical Organisation)** standards adopted globally, including in India under **AIS-044**.

A typical passenger car tyre marking is read as:

$$
\boxed{P \, / \, A \, R \, D \, \,\, LI \, \, SS}
$$

where each token has a precise engineering meaning.

## 1.2 Decoding the Tyre Marking (Example: **205/55 R 16 91 V**)

| Token | Value | Meaning |
|:-----:|:-----:|:--------|
| P | 205 | **Section Width** in millimetres |
| A | 55 | **Aspect Ratio** (sidewall height as % of section width) |
| R | R | **Radial** ply construction |
| D | 16 | **Rim Diameter** in inches |
| LI | 91 | **Load Index** (max load per tyre = **615 kg**) |
| SS | V | **Speed Symbol** (max sustained speed = **240 km/h**) |

> [!NOTE]
> Older bias-ply tyres use the letter **"B"** (or **"D"** for diagonal) instead of **"R"**. Modern automotive tyres are almost universally **Radial**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a tyre specification like the **identity card of a human being**:

- **Section width** = your shoulder breadth ŌĆö how wide you stand.
- **Aspect ratio** = your torso length relative to shoulder breadth ŌĆö how tall vs. how wide.
- **Construction type** = your bone structure (steel-frame = radial, wooden-frame = bias).
- **Rim diameter** = the size of shoes you wear ŌĆö the wheel sits on this.
- **Load index** = your weight-bearing capacity ŌĆö how heavy a backpack you can carry.
- **Speed symbol** = your maximum safe running speed ŌĆö beyond which your body cannot sustain the heat/stress.

Just as an ID is standardised so anyone can read it anywhere, a tyre specification is globally standardised so a mechanic in Tokyo or Thrissur instantly knows the tyre's capability.

## 1.4 Other Sidewall Markings (Beyond Size Designation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test these auxiliary markings:
> - **DOT** ŌåÆ Date of manufacture (DOT XXXX YYWW ŌĆö last 4 digits = week + year).
> - **TWI** ŌåÆ Tread Wear Indicator (small arrow pointing to a raised bar inside tread grooves).
> - **M+S / M&S / 3PMSF** ŌåÆ Mud & Snow / Three-Peak Mountain Snowflake (winter-rated).
> - **Tubeless / Tubeless Radial** ŌåÆ Absence of an inner tube (modern standard).
> - **Reinforced (RF)** or **Extra Load (XL)** ŌåÆ Higher ply rating for heavier loads at higher pressures.
> - **Run-Flat (RFT/DSST/RunOnFlat)** ŌåÆ Can run ~80 km at 80 km/h after complete pressure loss.
> - **P / LT / ST** prefix ŌåÆ Passenger / Light Truck / Special Trailer (US-market convention).

## 1.5 Why Tyre Specifications Matter in Automotive Engineering

- **Vehicle Dynamics:** Incorrect specs alter speedometer reading, ground clearance, gear-ratio effectiveness, and ABS calibration.
- **Safety:** Load index & speed symbol define the **operational envelope**; exceeding it causes **thermal runaway ŌåÆ tread separation ŌåÆ catastrophic failure**.
- **Fuel Efficiency:** Low rolling-resistance tyres with proper specs can improve mileage by **3ŌĆō7 %**.
- **Legal Compliance:** CMVR (Central Motor Vehicles Rules) in India mandates ECE-R30 compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōÉ SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Tyre Specification ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Section Width (SW)** ŌåÆ Distance between the outermost sidewalls of an inflated, unloaded tyre. Measured in **mm**.
2. **Aspect Ratio (AR)** ŌåÆ Ratio of sidewall height (H) to section width (SW), expressed as a percentage.
3. **Construction Type** ŌåÆ Defines the orientation of ply cords:
   - **Bias (B):** Ply cords run diagonally, alternating direction per ply.
   - **Radial (R):** Ply cords run perpendicular (90┬░) to the direction of travel, with circumferential belt plies.
4. **Rim Diameter (RD)** ŌåÆ The nominal diameter of the wheel rim the tyre mounts on, in **inches**.
5. **Load Index (LI)** ŌåÆ A numerical code (0ŌĆō279) representing the maximum load the tyre can carry at the speed indicated by its SS, at recommended inflation pressure.
6. **Speed Symbol (SS)** ŌåÆ An alphabetic code (A1ŌĆōY) representing the maximum sustained speed capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Radial construction delivers longer life (~60,000 km vs ~40,000 km for bias), better fuel economy, and superior grip due to a larger, flatter contact patch ŌĆö but it has a stiffer sidewall and harsher low-speed ride.

## 2.2 Load Index Table (Most Commonly Tested Values in KTU)

| LI | kg | LI | kg | LI | kg | LI | kg | LI | kg |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 80 | 450 | 85 | 515 | 88 | 560 | 91 | 615 | 95 | 690 |
| 100 | 800 | 106 | 950 | 110 | 1060 | 116 | 1250 | 120 | 1400 |

## 2.3 Speed Symbol Table (Critical for KTU)

| SS | km/h | SS | km/h | SS | km/h | SS | km/h |
|:--:|:----:|:--:|:----:|:--:|:----:|:--:|:----:|
| L | 120 | Q | 160 | S | 180 | T | 190 |
| H | 210 | V | 240 | W | 270 | Y | 300 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always pair a **lower speed symbol with a higher load index** carefully ŌĆö the combination is **mutually constrained**. A tyre marked **91V** is rated for 615 kg **AND** Ōēż 240 km/h simultaneously.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **OEM Selection:** Vehicle manufacturers choose tyre specs based on kerb weight, top speed, axle load distribution, and intended use (city/highway/off-road).
- **Tyre Upsizing:** Engineers must verify that overall diameter change does not exceed **┬▒3 %** of OEM spec to avoid speedometer error > 5 %.
- **Aftermarket:** Performance enthusiasts use lower aspect ratios (e.g., 45 vs. 65) for better steering response; off-roaders use higher aspect ratios (70ŌĆō85) for sidewall flex.

## 2.5 ŌÜĪ KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-----:|:------|
| 1 | Sidewall Height (H) | $H = \dfrac{AR}{100} \times SW$ | mm | Direct from aspect ratio |
| 2 | Overall Diameter (OD) | $OD = (RD \times 25.4) + 2H$ | mm | $25.4$ = mm/inch |
| 3 | Outer Radius (R) | $R = \dfrac{OD}{2}$ | mm | Used in rolling kinematics |
| 4 | Tyre Circumference (C) | $C = \pi \times OD$ | mm | Approximation (ignores flattening) |
| 5 | Revolutions per km (N) | $N = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{C}$ | rev/km | $10^6$ mm = 1 km |
| 6 | Vehicle Speed (V) | $V = \dfrac{2\pi R \cdot n}{60}$ | m/s | $n$ = wheel rpm |
| 7 | Load Capacity (per tyre) | $W = f(LI)$ | kg | From LI Table |
| 8 | Speedometer Error | $E = \dfrac{OD_{new} - OD_{OEM}}{OD_{OEM}} \times 100$ | % | Keep $\vert E \vert \leq 3\%$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤¦« SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Diameter of a Tyre

**Given:** Tyre specification **185/70 R 14**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify knowns**

$$
SW = 185 \text{ mm}, \quad AR = 70, \quad RD = 14 \text{ in}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert rim diameter from inches to millimetres**

$$
RD_{mm} = 14 \times 25.4 = 355.6 \text{ mm}
$$

> Conversion logic: 1 inch is internationally defined as exactly **25.4 mm** (since the 1959 international yard & pound agreement). The **25.4** constant must be committed to memory for KTU numericals.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute sidewall height using the aspect-ratio relation**

$$
H = \frac{AR}{100} \times SW = \frac{70}{100} \times 185
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 129.5 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the overall (outer) diameter**

$$
OD = RD_{mm} + 2H = 355.6 + 2(129.5)
$$

$$
\boxed{OD = 355.6 + 259.0 = 614.6 \text{ mm} \approx 0.6146 \text{ m}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Revolutions per Kilometre

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute tyre circumference**

$$
C = \pi \times OD = \pi \times 614.6
$$

$$
C \approx 3.14159 \times 614.6 \approx 1930.81 \text{ mm/rev}
$$

> Engineering note: The actual rolling circumference is slightly **smaller (by ~2ŌĆō3 %)** than the static geometric circumference due to tyre deflection under load and centrifugal growth at speed. However, KTU numericals assume the **ideal geometric** value.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert 1 km to mm and divide**

$$
1 \text{ km} = 10^6 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
N = \frac{10^6}{C} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1930.81}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 517.9 \text{ revolutions/km}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vehicle Speed from Wheel RPM

**Given:** Wheel rotational speed $n = 900$ rpm, OD = $0.6146$ m.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express linear velocity of tyre centre**

$$
v = \omega \times R = \frac{2\pi n}{60} \times \frac{OD}{2}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simplify**

$$
v = \frac{\pi \cdot n \cdot OD}{60}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute**

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 900 \times 0.6146}{60}
$$

$$
v = \frac{1737.86}{60} \approx 28.96 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to km/h**

$$
\boxed{V = 28.96 \times 3.6 \approx 104.3 \text{ km/h}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Speedometer Error on Tyre Upsize

**Given:** OEM tyre 185/70 R 14 (OD = 614.6 mm). Driver upgrades to 205/55 R 16.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute new overall diameter**

$$
H_{new} = \frac{55}{100} \times 205 = 112.75 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
RD_{new} = 16 \times 25.4 = 406.4 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
OD_{new} = 406.4 + 2(112.75) = 406.4 + 225.5 = 631.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply speedometer error formula**

$$
E = \frac{OD_{new} - OD_{OEM}}{OD_{OEM}} \times 100
$$

$$
E = \frac{631.9 - 614.6}{614.6} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx +2.81 \%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The new tyre has a **larger diameter**, so each revolution covers more ground. The vehicle actually travels **2.81 % faster** than the speedometer reads. **Speedometer overreads** at 100 km/h ŌåÆ true speed Ōēł 102.81 km/h. This is on the borderline of the **┬▒3 %** ECE regulatory limit.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Tyre Specification Selection

| Vehicle Type | Typical Section Width | Aspect Ratio | Rim | Common LI | Common SS |
|:-------------|:--------------------:|:------------:|:---:|:---------:|:---------:|
| Hatchback (e.g., Swift) | 165ŌĆō185 mm | 65ŌĆō70 | 14ŌĆō15 | 82ŌĆō88 | TŌĆōH |
| Sedan (e.g., City, Verna) | 185ŌĆō205 mm | 55ŌĆō65 | 15ŌĆō17 | 88ŌĆō95 | HŌĆōV |
| SUV (e.g., Creta, Fortuner) | 215ŌĆō265 mm | 55ŌĆō70 | 17ŌĆō20 | 95ŌĆō115 | HŌĆōV |
| Sports Car | 225ŌĆō325 mm | 30ŌĆō45 | 18ŌĆō22 | 95ŌĆō110 | WŌĆōY |
| Heavy Truck | 295ŌĆō315 mm | 70ŌĆō85 | 19.5ŌĆō22.5 | 140ŌĆō160 | JŌĆōM |
| Motorcycle | 90ŌĆō180 mm | 55ŌĆō90 | 17ŌĆō21 | 50ŌĆō70 | PŌĆōW |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tyre Cross-Section ŌĆö Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid renders this as a clean architectural block diagram mapping the layered construction of a modern radial pneumatic tyre (functional schematic ŌĆö not a literal to-scale cross-section).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[TREAD - Contact patch with road] --> B1[Provides grip and wear resistance]
    A2[CAP PLIES - Belt package, steel + nylon] --> B2[Constrains tread, resists centrifugal force]
    A3[PLY - Radial polyester cord body] --> B3[Bears inflation pressure and load]
    A4[INNER LINER - Halobutyl rubber] --> B4[Air retention for tubeless operation]
    A5[SIDEWALL - Flexible rubber compound] --> B5[Protects ply, displays markings]
    A6[BEAD - Steel wire bundle] --> B6[Mechanical lock onto rim flange]
    A7[BEAD FILLER - Apex rubber] --> B7[Smooth load transfer from bead to ply]

    C1((Tyre Layers)) --> A1
    C1 --> A2
    C1 --> A3
    C1 --> A4
    C1 --> A5
    C1 --> A6
    C1 --> A7
```

---

## 4.2 Tyre Specification Reading Flowchart

```mermaid

    S0[Start: Read Sidewall] --> S1{First 3 digits?}
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Section Width in mm]
    S1 -->|No| S10[LT or P prefix - vehicle class]
    S2 --> S3{Slash then 2 digits?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Aspect Ratio in percent]
    S3 -->|No| S11[Old-style alpha-numeric]

    S5 -->|B| S7[Bias or diagonal]
    S6 --> S8{2 digits?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Rim Diameter in inches]
    S9 --> S12{2 to 3 digits?}
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Load Index - consult table]
    S13 --> S14{Single letter?}
    S14 -->|Yes| S15[Speed Symbol - consult table]
    S15 --> S16[End: Specification decoded]
```

## 4.3 Load Index ŌåÆ Load Capacity Lookup Schematic

```mermaid

    L2 -->|LI = 95| L5[Max load = 690 kg per tyre]
    L2 -->|LI = 100| L6[Max load = 800 kg per tyre]
    L3 --> L7[For 4-tyre car: Max Kerb Load = 4 x LI]
    L4 --> L7
    L5 --> L7
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> L8[Verify against vehicle axle load chart]
```
