- Heat to be dissipated per cylinder $\dot{Q}_{cyl} = 4.5$ kW,
- Fin surface temperature $T_s = 180$ ┬░C = 453 K,
- Ambient air temperature $T_a = 35$ ┬░C = 308 K,
- Convective heat-transfer coefficient (forced air, $u = 25$ m/s) $h \approx 90$ W/(m┬▓┬ĘK).

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's law.

$$
\dot{Q}_{cyl} = h \, A_{req} \, (T_s - T_a)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
4500 = 90 \times A_{req} \times (453 - 308)
$$

$$
4500 = 90 \times A_{req} \times 145
$$

$$
4500 = 13\,050 \times A_{req}
$$

$$
A_{req} = \frac{4500}{13\,050} \approx 0.345 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** For a single-cylinder motorcycle (bore $D = 0.06$ m, exposed cylinder height $H = 0.10$ m), the bare cylinder surface area is:

$$
A_{bare} = \pi D H = \pi \times 0.06 \times 0.10 \approx 0.0188 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Required **fin augmentation factor**:

$$
\phi = \frac{A_{req}}{A_{bare}} = \frac{0.345}{0.0188} \approx 18.3
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.** The fins must supply **~18├Ś more area** than the bare cylinder ŌĆö typically achieved with **8ŌĆō14 fins per cylinder** of height ~15ŌĆō20 mm, matching real-world motorcycle designs.

### 3.4 Derivation IV ŌĆö Fin Efficiency Check

**Given:** Rectangular aluminum fin, height $L = 0.020$ m, thickness $t = 0.003$ m, $h = 90$ W/(m┬▓┬ĘK), $k_{Al} = 205$ W/(m┬ĘK).

**Step 1:** Fin parameter $m$.

$$
m = \sqrt{\frac{2h}{kt}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 90}{205 \times 0.003}}
$$

$$
m = \sqrt{\frac{180}{0.615}} = \sqrt{292.68} \approx 17.11 \; \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $mL$.

$$
mL = 17.11 \times 0.020 = 0.3422
$$

**Step 3:** Fin efficiency (assuming adiabatic tip).

$$
\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{\tanh(0.3422)}{0.3422} = \frac{0.3296}{0.3422} \approx 0.963
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. The fin operates at **~96% efficiency** ŌĆö high, but as $L$ increases beyond ~25 mm, efficiency drops sharply, justifying the **typical 12ŌĆō25 mm fin height** seen in production engines.

### 3.5 Comparative Heat-Load Capacity (Liquid vs Air)

For the **same engine size and load**:

- A liquid-cooled jacket removes heat with $h \approx 1500$ W/(m┬▓┬ĘK).
- A finned air-cooled cylinder removes heat with $h_{eff} \approx 90 \cdot \eta_f \approx 87$ W/(m┬▓┬ĘK).

$$
\frac{\dot{Q}_{liquid}}{\dot{Q}_{air}} \approx \frac{h_{liq}}{h_{air}} \cdot \frac{A_{liq}}{A_{air}}
$$

If the jacket area = cylinder area and $A_{air}$ is augmented ~10├Ś, then:

$$
\frac{\dot{Q}_{liquid}}{\dot{Q}_{air}} \approx \frac{1500}{90} \cdot \frac{1}{10} \approx 1.67
$$

**Liquid cooling still removes ~67% more heat** despite air's larger surface, which is why high-output engines universally adopt liquid cooling.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture ŌĆö Air-Cooled Engine System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Combustion Chamber\nHot Gas at 1800-2400 K] --> nodeB[Cylinder Barrel\nwith Cooling Fins]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Fin Surface Area\nAugmented 8x to 18x]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Forced Air Stream\nfrom Fan or Vehicle Motion]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Ambient Atmosphere\nHeat Rejection]
    nodeF[Crankshaft] --> nodeG[Belt Drive]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Centrifugal Fan\nor Axial Blower]
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeI[Cowl Shroud] --> nodeD
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture ŌĆö Liquid-Cooled Engine System (Forced Circulation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Engine Block\nWater Jacket] --> nodeB[Thermostat\nOpens at 82 deg C]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Coolant Path Decision}
    nodeC -->|Cold Engine| nodeD[Bypass Loop\nBack to Water Pump]
    nodeC -->|Hot Engine| nodeE[Upper Radiator Tank]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Water Pump\nCentrifugal]
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeG[Radiator Core\nTubes and Fins]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Cooling Fan\nAirflow Across Core]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Ambient Atmosphere]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Lower Radiator Tank]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeK[Expansion Tank\nPressurized] --> nodeE
    nodeL[Heater Core\nCabin Heating] --> nodeE
```

### 4.3 Component-Level Schematic ŌĆö Cylinder Fin Array (Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Combustion Gas\nInside Cylinder] --> nodeB[Inner Cylinder Wall\nMetal k approx 50 W/mK]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Outer Cylinder Wall\nMetal k approx 50 W/mK]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Fin Root\nHeat Spreads Along Fin]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Fin Body\nConduction along height]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Fin Tip\nConvection to Air]
    nodeG[Forced Air Stream\nh approx 90 W/m2K] --> nodeF
```

### 4.4 Operating State Diagram ŌĆö Thermostat Regulation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ColdStart
    ColdStart --> ClosedLoop: T_coolant less than 82 deg C
    ClosedLoop --> PartOpen: T_coolant between 82 and 95 deg C
    PartOpen --> FullyOpen: T_coolant greater than 95 deg C
    FullyOpen --> PartOpen: T_coolant between 82 and 95 deg C
    PartOpen --> ClosedLoop: T_coolant less than 82 deg C
    FullyOpen --> [*]: Engine Off
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question A1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** *Distinguish between natural and forced air cooling systems used in SI engines.*

**Model Answer:**
Natural air cooling relies on the relative motion between the vehicle and ambient air together with buoyancy-driven (chimney-effect) flow over finned cylinders ŌĆö no dedicated fan. Forced air cooling uses an engine-driven fan (centrifugal or axial) that pushes a high-velocity airstream through the cooling shroud, raising the convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$ by 4├ŚŌĆō6├Ś. Forced cooling is mandatory for higher-output air-cooled engines (e.g., Rotax 912, large motorcycle engines), while natural cooling suffices only on low-power units (e.g., small gensets, mowers).

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question A2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** *List any four functions of an automotive engine cooling system.*

**Model Answer:**
1. Maintain cylinder, piston and valve temperatures within design limits (~80ŌĆō95 ┬░C liquid-cooled, ~150ŌĆō180 ┬░C air-cooled).
2. Prevent pre-ignition and detonation caused by localized hot spots.
3. Ensure uniform cylinder-wall temperature to minimize thermal-stress-induced cracks.
4. Bring the engine up to operating temperature quickly during cold start to reduce wear, emissions, and condensation corrosion.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style, 14 Marks, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

#### **QUESTION A ŌĆö Air Cooling Systems (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of an air-cooled SI engine system used in a modern motorcycle. Discuss the role of fins, fan, and cowl. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Necessity (1 mark):** In a motorcycle, weight, compactness and simplicity outweigh thermal uniformity. Air cooling eliminates the radiator, hoses, pump, and several kilograms of coolant.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Cylinder Fins (3 marks):** Aluminum fins are integrally cast on the cylinder barrel and head. They increase the effective heat-transfer area $A$ in $\dot{Q} = hA\Delta T$ by a factor of **8ŌĆō18├Ś**. Typical fin dimensions: height 12ŌĆō25 mm, thickness 1.5ŌĆō3.5 mm, spacing 5ŌĆō10 mm. Material choice ŌĆö aluminum alloy because $k_{Al} \approx 200$ W/m┬ĘK gives good fin-base-to-tip conduction while keeping mass low.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Fan & Cowl (2 marks):** An axial or centrifugal fan driven off the crankshaft (via gear or belt) provides forced convection, raising $h$ from ~10 W/m┬▓┬ĘK (natural) to ~90 W/m┬▓┬ĘK (forced). The cowl (sheet-metal shroud) channels the airstream uniformly over the fins, eliminating stagnation zones.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Working (1 mark):** Combustion heat conducts through the cylinder wall into the fin roots, then along the fin body, finally convecting from the fin surface to the moving airstream which carries it away to the atmosphere.

**(b)** *A single-cylinder air-cooled motorcycle engine develops 8 kW at 30% brake thermal efficiency. 30% of the total heat is rejected through the cylinder fins. Fin surface temperature = 175 ┬░C, ambient = 35 ┬░C, $h = 90$ W/(m┬▓┬ĘK). Calculate the minimum fin surface area required. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute fuel energy input rate.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \frac{P}{\eta_b} = \frac{8}{0.30} = 26.67 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Heat to be dissipated by fins.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{fin} = x_{cool} \cdot \dot{Q}_{fuel} = 0.30 \times 26.67 = 8.0 \; \text{kW} = 8000 \; \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Newton's law of cooling.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{fin} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
8000 = 90 \times A \times (175 - 35)
$$

$$
8000 = 90 \times A \times 140 = 12\,600 \cdot A
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for area.**

$$
A = \frac{8000}{12\,600} \approx 0.635 \; \text{m}^2 \quad (\approx 6350 \; \text{cm}^2)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Engineering comment (1 mark).** A 6350 cm┬▓ fin area on a small motorcycle engine is achieved with 9ŌĆō12 fins of height ~18 mm around a bore of ~57 mm ŌĆö values closely matching production engines like the Hero Splendor / Honda Unicorn air-cooled variants.

---

#### **QUESTION B ŌĆö Liquid Cooling Systems (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *With the aid of a block diagram, explain the construction and working of a forced-circulation liquid cooling system in a modern passenger car. List the major components and explain the role of the thermostat. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö System overview (1 mark):** Coolant circulates under pump pressure from the engine water jacket ŌåÆ thermostat ŌåÆ radiator (heat rejection) ŌåÆ back to the pump ŌåÆ engine. A bypass loop allows warm coolant to recirculate during warm-up.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Components (3 marks):**
1. **Water pump (centrifugal)** ŌĆö belt-driven; flow ~20ŌĆō60 L/min at 3ŌĆō6 m head.
2. **Thermostat** ŌĆö wax-pellet type; closes below ~82 ┬░C, fully open by ~95 ┬░C.
3. **Radiator** ŌĆö brass/aluminum tubes with aluminum fins; two tanks.
4. **Cooling fan** ŌĆö electric/viscous; engages above ~95 ┬░C or per ECU command.
5. **Expansion tank (pressurized)** ŌĆö accommodates thermal expansion, raises coolant BP.
6. **Heater core** ŌĆö uses waste heat for cabin warming.
7. **Hoses, pressure cap, temperature sender.**

**Step 3 ŌĆö Thermostat function (2 marks):** During cold start, the thermostat blocks flow to the radiator; coolant circulates only through the bypass, allowing the engine to reach operating temperature quickly (~5ŌĆō8 minutes). Once coolant reaches 82 ┬░C, the wax pellet expands, opening the main valve and admitting coolant to the radiator. Above 95 ┬░C the thermostat is fully open and the fan is engaged for maximum heat rejection.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Working (1 mark):** Heat conducts from combustion gas ŌåÆ cylinder wall ŌåÆ jacket coolant (sensible heating) ŌåÆ pumped to radiator ŌåÆ convection + forced air across core ŌåÆ atmosphere.

**(b)** *A 4-cylinder liquid-cooled SI engine delivers 60 kW at the crankshaft. Brake thermal efficiency = 30%. 30% of total fuel energy is removed by the coolant, which is a 50:50 EG-water mixture ($c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg┬ĘK, $\rho = 1070$ kg/m┬│). Coolant enters the radiator at 95 ┬░C and leaves at 80 ┬░C. Determine (i) the coolant mass flow rate, and (ii) the volumetric flow rate. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Fuel energy rate.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \frac{P}{\eta_b} = \frac{60}{0.30} = 200 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Heat absorbed by coolant.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{cool} = 0.30 \times 200 = 60 \; \text{kW} = 60\,000 \; \text{W}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply sensible-heat equation $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$.**

$$
\Delta T = 95 - 80 = 15 \; ┬░C
$$

$$
60\,000 = \dot{m}_c \times 3500 \times 15
$$

$$
60\,000 = \dot{m}_c \times 52\,500
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{60\,000}{52\,500} \approx 1.143 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Volumetric flow rate.**

$$
\dot{V}_c = \frac{\dot{m}_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{1.143}{1070} \approx 1.068 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_c \approx 64 \; \text{L/min}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Interpretation (1 mark).** A 60 kW engine needs about **64 L/min** coolant circulation ŌĆö comfortably within the typical 20ŌĆō80 L/min rating of passenger-car water pumps.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls on Cooling System Questions**
> 1. **Mixing up $h$ values:** Students frequently use $h \approx 10$ W/m┬▓┬ĘK (natural convection) when the question states *forced* cooling ŌĆö leading to a 9├Ś under-estimation of area or flow. Always check whether the problem specifies "forced" or "natural".
> 2. **Using $\Delta T$ wrongly:** Some students subtract ambient from radiator-inlet instead of using $(T_{in} - T_{out})$ in the coolant-side equation. Remember: $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$ uses *coolant-side temperature rise*, not wall-to-ambient.
> 3. **Ignoring fin efficiency $\eta_f$:** A long fin does NOT transfer heat as if its entire geometric area were at $T_{base}$. You must multiply by $\eta_f \approx 0.6$ŌĆō$0.95$ for tall fins. Skipping this loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** $c_p$ in kJ/kg┬ĘK with $\dot{Q}$ in kW gives $\dot{m}$ in kg/s ŌĆö if you use $c_p$ in J/kg┬ĘK, $\dot{Q}$ must also be in W. Mixed units cost marks.
> 5. **Stating only "heat must be removed" without numeric quantification:** KTU 2024 stresses calculation. Always quantify.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Air & Liquid Cooling (Module 4)**

- **Air cooling** uses atmospheric air directly over finned cylinders ŌĆö simple, light, used in motorcycles and light aircraft.
- **Liquid cooling** uses a coolant (typically 50:50 EG-water) circulated by a pump through a jacket and a remote radiator ŌĆö uniform, controllable, used in almost all modern cars.
- **Heat balance:** $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{Q}_{work} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust} + \dot{Q}_{cool}$, with $\dot{Q}_{cool} \approx 30\%ŌĆō35\%$ of $\dot{Q}_{fuel}$.
- **Newton's law of cooling:** $\dot{Q} = h A \Delta T$ ŌĆö increasing $A$ (fins), $h$ (fan/pump), or $\Delta T$ raises heat rejection.
- **Air cooling key parts:** fins, fan/cowl, sometimes oil cooler; fin efficiency $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$.
- **Liquid cooling key parts:** water jacket, water pump, thermostat, radiator, expansion tank, fan, hoses, heater core.
- **Thermostat** opens at ~82 ┬░C and fully opens at ~95 ┬░C, ensuring rapid warm-up and steady operating temperature.
- **EG-water coolant** raises boiling point to ~108 ┬░C and lowers freezing point to ~ŌłÆ36 ┬░C at 50:50 mix.
- **Pump flow rate for a 60 kW engine** ~ 64 L/min; **fin area for an 8 kW motorcycle engine** ~ 0.635 m┬▓.
- **Coolant temperature window:** 80ŌĆō95 ┬░C (liquid), 120ŌĆō180 ┬░C (air).
- **Air cooling advantages:** lightweight, simple, no leak, no coolant cost.
- **Air cooling disadvantages:** uneven temperature, noisy fan, limited to lower specific outputs.
- **Liquid cooling advantages:** uniform temperature, high specific output, silent, anti-freeze protection.
- **Liquid cooling disadvantages:** heavier, more parts, periodic coolant maintenance, leak risk.
- **For exam answers:** always state assumptions (steady state, 1D conduction, property values, efficiency ╬Ę_th = 30% if not given) before substituting numbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Sealed Cooling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Sealed Cooling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pressure Sealed Cooling System** (also called a *Sealed/Bypass/Expansion Tank Cooling System*) is a closed-loop liquid cooling arrangement in an internal combustion (IC) engine in which the entire coolant circuit is hermetically isolated from the atmosphere. A **calibrated pressure cap** mounted on the radiator (or on a remote expansion tank) maintains the system at a controlled positive gauge pressure, typically **0.7 bar to 1.05 bar** (Ōēł **10ŌĆō15 psi**). This pressurization deliberately elevates the saturation temperature of the water-based coolant, suppresses cavitation at the water pump, and eliminates evaporative and boiling losses ŌĆö allowing the engine to operate safely at temperatures well above **100 ┬░C** without losing coolant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Pressure sealed cooling is a *high-weightage* topic in KTU Automotive Systems because it integrates **thermodynamics (boiling point vs. pressure)**, **fluid mechanics (pump and circuit design)**, and **engine reliability (cavitation, scaling, corrosion)** into a single subsystem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Pressure Cooker Effect"

Imagine a household **pressure cooker**:
- The heavy lid locks in steam ŌåÆ pressure inside rises.
- Because water boils only when its vapour pressure equals the surrounding pressure, *raising the surrounding pressure forces water to absorb more heat before it boils*.
- Food therefore cooks at **120ŌĆō125 ┬░C** even though it is immersed in water that would normally boil at 100 ┬░C.

A car's pressure-sealed cooling system is the *engineering cousin* of that cooker. The **pressure cap** is the "locking lid", the **radiator** is the "heat sink", and the **coolant** is the "cooking water". Without the cap's seal, the coolant would boil away in summer traffic, causing the engine to overheat catastrophically.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Typical Automotive Value |
|---|---|
| Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ | **1.013 bar** |
| System gauge pressure (cap rating) | **0.7 ŌĆō 1.05 bar** |
| Absolute system pressure | **1.7 ŌĆō 2.0 bar (abs)** |
| Coolant saturation temperature at 1.0 bar gauge | **Ōēł 120 ┬░C** |
| Typical engine operating coolant temperature | **85 ŌĆō 95 ┬░C** |
| Freezing point of 50/50 ethylene-glycol mix | **Ōēł ŌĆō36 ┬░C** |
| Radiator cap relief opening pressure (typical) | **0.9 ┬▒ 0.05 bar** |
| Vacuum valve opening (negative pressure) | **ŌĆō0.03 to ŌĆō0.05 bar** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pressurize?** Two engineering reasons: (1) raises the boiling point of the coolant (thermodynamic reason) and (2) suppresses the formation of vapour bubbles at the pump suction (fluid-mechanical reason, anti-cavitation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Engine Heat Generation** ŌĆö Combustion gases transfer heat to the cylinder walls; conduction carries it to the **water jacket** surrounding the cylinders and head.
2. **Coolant Circulation** ŌĆö The **centrifugal water pump**, driven by the crankshaft via a V-belt, forces coolant out of the engine block and into the radiator top tank.
3. **Heat Rejection at the Radiator** ŌĆö Hot coolant flows downward through the **tubes** of the radiator core while ambient air is forced (by ram-air or the **fan**) across the fins. Heat is rejected by forced convection.
4. **Pressure Sealing** ŌĆö A spring-loaded **pressure cap** seals the filler neck. Because the system is closed, any thermal expansion of the coolant raises the internal absolute pressure.
5. **Boiling Point Elevation** ŌĆö At the elevated pressure, the coolant's saturation temperature is raised from 100 ┬░C to Ōēł **120ŌĆō125 ┬░C**, giving a large ╬öT margin against local hot spots.
6. **Over-Pressure Relief** ŌĆö If internal pressure exceeds the cap rating, the spring compresses and the **pressure relief valve** vents coolant/steam into the **expansion/overflow tank**.
7. **Vacuum Relief (Cool-down)** ŌĆö As the engine cools, coolant contracts. If the pressure drops below atmospheric, the **vacuum valve** opens, drawing coolant back from the expansion tank to prevent air ingress.
8. **Thermostat Control** ŌĆö A **wax-pellet or bimetallic thermostat** remains closed below Ōēł 80 ┬░C (short-circuiting flow back to the pump) and opens above Ōēł 90 ┬░C, forcing the coolant through the radiator.

## 2.2 The Physics: Why Does Pressure Raise Boiling Point?

Boiling occurs when the **saturated vapour pressure of the liquid equals the surrounding ambient pressure**. If we *increase* the surrounding pressure, the liquid must be heated to a higher temperature before its vapour pressure catches up. This is captured by the **ClausiusŌĆōClapeyron equation**:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \cdot \Delta v}$$

where $L$ = latent heat of vaporization, $T$ = absolute temperature, and $\Delta v$ = specific volume change between vapour and liquid.

For water, integrating between $P_{atm}$ and $P_{sys}$ yields the well-known **linear empirical rule** used in automotive practice:

$$T_{boil} \approx 100 + 0.625 \cdot P_{gauge}$$

where $P_{gauge}$ is in **bar** and $T_{boil}$ is in **┬░C**.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.625 ┬░C/bar** is a *linearization* of the steam table valid between 0 and 2 bar. The exact value from the steam tables is closer to **0.72 ┬░C/bar** near 100 ┬░C, but **0.625** is the figure examiners expect in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_b \approx 100 + 0.625 \cdot P_g$ | $T_b$ = boiling point (┬░C), $P_g$ = gauge pressure (bar) | Predict coolant saturation temperature |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat rejected (W), $\dot{m}$ = mass flow (kg/s), $c_p$ = 3.9 kJ/kg┬ĘK (50/50 mix) | Engine heat balance |
| 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | $U$ = overall heat-transfer coeff. (W/m┬▓K), $A$ = core area (m┬▓) | Radiator sizing |
| 4 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | $P_{abs}$ = absolute pressure, $P_{atm}$ = 1.013 bar | Convert gauge Ōåö absolute |
| 5 | $NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm} + P_g - P_v}{\rho g} - h_s$ | Pump suction head available | Anti-cavitation check |
| 6 | $H_p = \eta_h \cdot \frac{u_2^2}{2g}$ | Centrifugal pump head (Euler equation, $u_2$ = impeller tip speed) | Water-pump design |
| 7 | $P_{cap} = k \cdot x$ | Spring pressure (linear) where $k$ = stiffness, $x$ = deflection | Cap calibration |
| 8 | $T_{freeze} \approx -E \cdot C$ | $E$ = freezing point depression constant (Ōēł 1.86 ┬░C┬Ękg/mol for EG), $C$ = molality | Coolant mix selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a KTU exam, when you see a numerical like *"If the radiator cap is rated 0.9 bar, find the boiling point of the coolant"*, the expected one-line answer uses $T_b = 100 + 0.625(0.9) \approx 100.56$ ┬░C. Always show $P_{abs} = 1.013 + 0.9 = 1.913$ bar as the first step.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger cars, trucks, tractors, two-wheelers (liquid-cooled)** ŌĆö almost universally use pressure-sealed cooling.
- **Marine and stationary genset engines** ŌĆö use similar sealed circuits with heat-exchangers (keel cooling, raw-water plate exchangers).
- **EV battery thermal management** ŌĆö the same pressure-sealed principle is now extended to lithium-ion battery packs using 50/50 glycol loops.
- **Formula-1 / motorsport** ŌĆö pressurized surge tanks run at **2.5ŌĆō3.0 bar** to push boiling points to 130 ┬░C+ and eliminate pump cavitation at high G.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boiling Point Elevation from the Clapeyron Equation

**Starting point** ŌĆö the exact ClausiusŌĆōClapeyron equation for liquidŌĆōvapour equilibrium:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \cdot (v_g - v_l)}$$

**Step 1:** Note that $v_l \ll v_g$ for water at low pressure, so $v_g - v_l \approx v_g$.

$$\frac{dP}{dT} \approx \frac{L}{T \cdot v_g}$$

**Step 2:** Use the ideal-gas law for the vapour: $v_g = \dfrac{R T}{P M}$, where $M$ = molar mass of water (18 kg/kmol) and $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol┬ĘK.

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L \cdot P \cdot M}{R \cdot T^2}$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables and integrate from the reference state $(P_1, T_1) = (1.013 \text{ bar}, 373.15 \text{ K})$ to $(P_2, T_2)$:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{L M}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{L M}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $L = 2257$ kJ/kg, $M = 0.018$ kg/mol, $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}$ kJ/mol┬ĘK, $P_1 = 1.013$ bar, $T_1 = 373.15$ K, and $P_2 = 1.013 + P_g$ bar.

For small $\Delta T$ around 100 ┬░C, linearizing yields:

$$T_2 - T_1 \approx \frac{R T_1^2}{L M} \cdot \frac{P_g}{P_1}$$

Numerically:

$$\frac{R T_1^2}{L M P_1} \approx \frac{8.314 \times 10^{-3} \times 373.15^2}{2257 \times 0.018 \times 1.013} \approx 0.625 \; \frac{\text{K}}{\text{bar}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,T_b \;[\text{┬░C}] \;\approx\; 100 \;+\; 0.625 \cdot P_g \;[\text{bar}]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **0.625 ┬░C/bar** factor is **not a fundamental constant** ŌĆö it is the linearized slope of the saturation curve at 100 ┬░C. At higher pressures, the slope is steeper (Ōēł 0.72 ┬░C/bar near 120 ┬░C). KTU problems almost always use **0.625**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical ŌĆö Typical KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem

**Problem:** A car uses a 50/50 ethylene-glycol coolant in a pressure-sealed system. The radiator cap is rated at **0.9 bar** gauge. Find:
(a) The new boiling point of the coolant.
(b) The system absolute pressure.
(c) The heat that must be removed by the radiator if the coolant flow rate is **0.20 kg/s** and the temperature drop across the radiator is **12 ┬░C**. Take $c_p = 3.9$ kJ/kg┬ĘK for 50/50 mix.

**Solution:**

**(a) Boiling point elevation**

$$T_b = 100 + 0.625 \times 0.9 = 100.56 \; \text{┬░C}$$

**(b) Absolute pressure**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_g = 1.013 + 0.9 = 1.913 \; \text{bar (abs)}$$

**(c) Heat rejected at radiator**

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.20 \times 3.9 \times 12 = 9.36 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Pattern:** Step (a) = 2 marks, Step (b) = 1 mark, Step (c) = 4 marks. Always carry units throughout. The examiner *will* deduct a mark if you write $T_b = 100.56$ K (mixing K and ┬░C).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Cooling System Types

| Feature | Thermo-syphon (Natural) | Open Vent (No Cap) | **Pressure Sealed** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Circulation | Density difference | Pump-driven | Pump-driven |
| Operating pressure | Ōēł atmospheric | Ōēł atmospheric | **0.7 ŌĆō 1.05 bar gauge** |
| Coolant boiling point | 100 ┬░C | 100 ┬░C | **Ōēł 120 ŌĆō 125 ┬░C** |
| Coolant loss | High (evaporation) | High (vented) | **Negligible** (recovery tank) |
| Cavitation at pump | Common | Common | **Suppressed** |
| Radiator size required | Large | Large | **Smaller (Ōēł 30 %)** |
| Modern usage | Antique / aircraft air-cooled | Obsolete (1920sŌĆō40s) | **Universal today** |
| Cap required | No | No (open vent) | **Yes, calibrated spring** |

## 3.4 Energy Flow in a Sealed System

The thermal circuit is:

$$Q_{comb} \rightarrow Q_{cond,wall} \rightarrow Q_{conv,jacket} \rightarrow Q_{rad,rej} \rightarrow Q_{air}$$

In steady state:

$$\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) = U \cdot A_{core} \cdot \text{LMTD}$$

The LMTD for a counter-flow radiator with coolant $T_{c,in} \rightarrow T_{c,out}$ and air $T_{a,in} \rightarrow T_{a,out}$ is:

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{(T_{c,in} - T_{a,out}) - (T_{c,out} - T_{a,in})}{\ln\!\left(\frac{T_{c,in} - T_{a,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{a,in}}\right)}$$

> [!TIP]
> Avoid using $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation in a markdown table ŌĆö write the LMTD formula *outside* the table, in a centered equation block, to prevent markdown table breakage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustion in Cylinder] --> B[Heat Conduction via Cylinder Wall]
    B --> C[Water Jacket around Cylinders]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Water Pump]
    D --> E[Thermostat Wax Pellet Type]
    E -->|Below 80 C| F[Bypass Loop back to Pump]
    E -->|Above 90 C| G[Top Tank of Radiator]
    G --> H[Radiator Core Tubes and Fins]
    H --> I[Cooled Coolant returns to Bottom Tank]
    I --> J[Water Pump Suction]
    J --> D
    H --> K[Cooling Fan or Ram Air]
    K --> L[Heat Rejected to Atmosphere]
    M[Pressure Cap 0.9 bar Calibrated] -->|Pressure Exceeds 0.9 bar| N[Relief Valve Vapour to Expansion Tank]
    N --> O[Expansion or Recovery Tank]
    O -->|Vacuum Forms on Cool Down| P[Vacuum Valve opens]
    P --> G
    M -->|Normal Operation| G
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture ŌĆö Pressure Cap Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph capAssembly[Radiator Pressure Cap Assembly]
        A1[Coolant Inlet from Radiator Top Tank] --> A2[Spring Loaded Pressure Disc]
        A2 --> A3[Calibrated Spring k]
        A3 --> A4[Pressure Relief Seat]
        A2 --> A5[Vacuum Valve Secondary Piston]
        A5 --> A6[Vacuum Seat and Air Inlet]
    end
    A4 -->|Over Pressure Vent| A7[Steam to Expansion Tank]
    A6 -->|Vacuum Draw| A8[Atmospheric Air and Coolant Return]
    A1 --> A9[Sealed Cooling Circuit to Engine]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow ŌĆö Thermostat Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Engine Starts Cold] --> T1{Coolant Temperature Check}
    T1 -->|T less than 80 C| T2[Thermostat Closed]
    T2 --> T3[Coolant Bypasses Radiator]
    T3 --> T4[Short Loop to Pump Suction]
    T4 --> T5[Engine Warms Quickly]
    T1 -->|T between 80 and 90 C| T6[Thermostat Cracking Open]
    T6 --> T7[Coolant Begins Radiator Flow]
    T1 -->|T greater than 90 C| T8[Thermostat Fully Open]
    T8 --> T9[Full Flow to Radiator]
    T9 --> T10[Maximum Heat Rejection]
    T10 --> T11{Temperature Stabilizes around 90 C}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`T1`, `A1`, etc.), no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as node names, and labels contain only plain text without markdown bold/italic tags to prevent rendering errors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**What is the function of a calibrated pressure cap in a sealed cooling system? Mention its two built-in valves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The pressure cap seals the cooling system and maintains a positive gauge pressure (typically 0.7 to 1.05 bar) so that the coolant's boiling point is raised above 100 ┬░C. It contains two valves:

1. **Pressure relief valve** ŌĆö opens when internal pressure exceeds the calibrated spring force, venting vapour/coolant to the expansion tank to protect hoses and the radiator from bursting.
2. **Vacuum valve** ŌĆö opens when the system cools and pressure drops below atmospheric, admitting coolant back from the expansion tank to prevent air ingress and pump cavitation.

*(Award 1 mark for stating the pressurization function, 1 mark for the relief valve, 1 mark for the vacuum valve.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Why is ethylene glycol mixed with water in automotive coolant instead of using pure water?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Raises boiling point and lowers freezing point** ŌĆö 50/50 mix boils at Ōēł 108 ┬░C and freezes at Ōēł ŌĆō36 ┬░C, giving a much wider operating window than pure water (100 ┬░C / 0 ┬░C).
2. **Corrosion inhibition** ŌĆö EG formulations contain additives (phosphates, silicates) that protect cast iron, aluminium, copper and rubber from corrosion and cavitation erosion.
3. **Higher specific heat & better lubrication** ŌĆö improves heat transfer and pump bearing life versus pure water.

*(Award 1 mark per valid point.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) ŌĆö 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **pressure-sealed cooling system** used in a modern passenger car engine. Label all major components. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A radiator cap is rated at **1.0 bar** gauge. The coolant is a 60 % water / 40 % ethylene-glycol mixture with $c_p = 3.7$ kJ/kg┬ĘK. If the engine rejects **45 kW** of heat to the coolant and the coolant temperature drop across the radiator is **10 ┬░C**, determine:
   (i) The boiling point of the coolant in the sealed system.
   (ii) The mass flow rate of coolant required.
   *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

Sketch the schematic as in **Section 4.1** above. List the components and explain their function:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Water jacket | Surrounds cylinders and head; receives combustion heat |
| Centrifugal water pump | Forces coolant through the system, driven by V-belt from crank |
| Thermostat | Wax-pellet type; closes below 80 ┬░C (bypass) and opens above 90 ┬░C (radiator flow) |
| Radiator (tubes + fins + tanks) | Heat exchanger; rejects heat to atmosphere |
| Cooling fan / ram air | Forces air across radiator core |
| Pressure cap | Seals system, maintains Ōēł 1 bar gauge, contains relief and vacuum valves |
| Expansion / recovery tank | Receives displaced coolant on heat-up, returns it on cool-down |
| Hoses and heater core | Connect circuit; heater core warms the cabin |

**Working:** Pump ŌåÆ engine jacket ŌåÆ thermostat ŌåÆ (radiator OR bypass) ŌåÆ back to pump. Heat absorbed in the jacket is rejected at the radiator. Pressure cap maintains Ōēł 1 bar gauge to raise the boiling point. *(1 mark for sketch, 4 marks for component description, 2 marks for working narrative.)*

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Boiling point** ŌĆö use $T_b = 100 + 0.625 P_g$

$$T_b = 100 + 0.625 \times 1.0 = 100.625 \; \text{┬░C} \approx 100.6 \; \text{┬░C}$$

$$P_{abs} = 1.013 + 1.0 = 2.013 \; \text{bar (abs)}$$

**(ii) Mass flow rate** ŌĆö from $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{45}{3.7 \times 10} = 1.216 \; \text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 1.22 \; \text{kg/s}}$$

*(Award 2 marks for (i), 5 marks for (ii) including unit consistency.)*

---

### Question B (Option 2) ŌĆö 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **thermodynamic principle** by which a pressure-sealed cooling system raises the coolant boiling point. Derive the empirical relation $T_b = 100 + 0.625 \, P_g$ starting from the **ClausiusŌĆōClapeyron equation**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **pressure-sealed**, **thermo-syphon**, and **open-vent** cooling systems on the basis of: operating pressure, boiling point, coolant loss, cavitation tendency, and radiator size. State two advantages and one limitation of the pressure-sealed type. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Thermodynamic Derivation (7 marks):**

Begin with the exact Clapeyron equation, then show the sequence in **Section 3.1** above:

1. State Clapeyron: $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{L}{T \Delta v}$. *(1 mark)*
2. Apply ideal-gas approximation $v_g = RT/(PM)$ and $v_l \ll v_g$. *(1 mark)*
3. Separate variables and integrate from $(P_1, T_1)$ to $(P_2, T_2)$. *(2 marks)*
4. Linearize for small $\Delta T$ around 373.15 K. *(1 mark)*
5. Substitute numerical values and obtain $T_b - 100 = 0.625 \cdot P_g$. *(2 marks)*

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

Use the analytical matrix from **Section 3.3**. *(4 marks for the table.)*

**Two advantages of pressure-sealed type:**
1. Boiling point raised to Ōēł 120 ┬░C ŌåÆ greater ╬öT margin ŌåÆ smaller radiator and higher specific output.
2. No coolant loss ŌåÆ no make-up water, lower maintenance, no scaling.

**One limitation:**
- If the cap fails *open* (spring weakened), the system becomes effectively atmospheric ŌåÆ boiling at 100 ┬░C ŌåÆ overheating. Conversely, if the cap *seizes closed*, an over-pressure event can rupture hoses or the radiator. Periodic cap testing is mandatory.

*(Award 4 marks table + 2 marks advantages + 1 mark limitation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** ŌĆö mixing ┬░C and K when substituting into the Clapeyron equation. *Always* use Kelvin in the derivative, convert back to ┬░C at the very end.
> 2. **Forgetting $P_{atm}$** ŌĆö students often use only $P_g$ as the absolute pressure in calculations, losing 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Wrong $c_p$** ŌĆö using 4.18 kJ/kg┬ĘK (water) instead of **3.9 kJ/kg┬ĘK** (50/50 EG) or **3.7 kJ/kg┬ĘK** (60/40 mix). Always read the problem statement.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch** ŌĆö a 7-mark construction question without a labelled diagram loses **at least 2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Ignoring the vacuum valve** ŌĆö answers that describe only the pressure relief side of the cap are considered incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pressure-sealed cooling** is the *universal* liquid-cooling architecture in modern road vehicles.
- The **pressure cap** is the single most critical component ŌĆö it sets the system gauge pressure, typically **0.7 ŌĆō 1.05 bar**.
- **Boiling-point rule of thumb:** $T_b \approx 100 + 0.625 \, P_g$ [┬░C, bar].
- The two valves in the cap are: **(i) pressure relief** (over-pressure protection) and **(ii) vacuum valve** (anti-cavitation on cool-down).
- The **expansion / recovery tank** captures displaced coolant and returns it ŌĆö eliminating routine top-up.
- The **thermostat** is a wax-pellet or bimetallic valve that keeps the engine at Ōēł 90 ┬░C by directing flow either through the radiator or via a bypass loop.
- The **centrifugal water pump** (V-belt or electric) provides positive flow; pressurization helps suppress cavitation at its suction.
- Coolant is **water + ethylene glycol** (typically 50/50): raises BP to Ōēł 108 ┬░C, lowers FP to Ōēł ŌĆō36 ┬░C, and contains corrosion inhibitors.
- The **radiator** rejects heat by forced convection: $Q = U A \, \text{LMTD}$.
- **Anti-cavitation NPSH check** must be satisfied: $NPSH_a = (P_{atm} + P_g - P_v)/(\rho g) - h_s > NPSH_r$.
- The system is **completely sealed** ŌåÆ no air ingress ŌåÆ no oxidation of ferrous parts ŌåÆ longer water-pump and hose life.
- **KTU-favourite numericals** ask for boiling-point elevation given cap pressure, or for $\dot{m}$ given $Q$ and $\Delta T$ ŌĆö practice both directions.
- Always include a **labelled sketch** in any 7-mark construction question; examiners explicitly award marks for it.
- **Modern trend:** Variable-speed electric water pumps and electronically controlled thermostats (e-map) are extending the classic pressure-sealed principle with active thermal management.
- **Don't confuse** *thermo-syphon* (gravity-driven, no pump) with *pressure-sealed* (pump-driven, sealed). They are different generations of technology.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Liquid Cooling System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components of Liquid Cooling System

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Liquid Cooling System** (also called the *Pressurized Water Cooling System*) is a closed-loop, forced-circulation thermal management subsystem in an internal combustion (IC) engine that uses a liquid medium (typically a mixture of **Ethylene Glycol** and water, in a 50:50 ratio by volume) to absorb waste heat from the engine block and cylinder head via integral water jackets, and subsequently reject that heat to the surrounding atmosphere through a dedicated heat exchanger (radiator). The system maintains the engine operating temperature within an optimal band of approximately **85 ┬░C to 95 ┬░C** to ensure thermal efficiency, proper combustion, controlled emissions, and longevity of engine components.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The student must be able to identify, label, and explain the *function, construction, and operational principle* of each component of a liquid cooling system, and describe the complete coolant flow path under cold, normal, and overheated engine conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the liquid cooling system as the **human body's circulatory system** working in tandem with **sweating**:

- The **engine block** is analogous to the human body generating heat during muscular work.
- The **coolant** behaves like **blood**, absorbing heat as it flows past hot engine regions.
- The **water pump** acts as the **heart**, providing the necessary pressure differential to circulate the coolant continuously.
- The **radiator** is the **skin's sweat glands** + **forehead**, where the heated liquid is exposed to cooler ambient air and releases its heat energy.
- The **thermostat** is the **hypothalamus**, intelligently deciding whether the body needs to cool itself or conserve heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why liquid cooling and not air cooling?** Liquid has a heat-carrying capacity (specific heat) approximately **4 times greater** than air, and is roughly **800 to 1000 times denser**. This allows a small volume of coolant to absorb massive quantities of heat, making liquid cooling *compact*, *silent*, and *uniform* ŌĆö ideal for high-output automotive engines.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (Automotive IC Engine) |
|---|---|
| Normal Operating Temperature | **85 ┬░C ŌĆō 95 ┬░C** |
| Coolant Boiling Point (at 1 atm) | **100 ┬░C (water)**, **110 ┬░C (50% EG-water)** |
| System Pressure (typical) | **0.7 bar ŌĆō 1.5 bar (gauge)** |
| Radiator Cap Opening Pressure | **0.9 bar ŌĆō 1.05 bar (gauge)** |
| Coolant-Water Ratio | **50:50 (ethylene glycol : distilled water)** |
| Coolant Specific Heat (50:50 mix) | **~ 3.5 kJ/(kg┬ĘK)** |

> [!TIP]
> The 50:50 EG-water mixture is **NOT** an arbitrary choice. Ethylene glycol elevates the boiling point AND depresses the freezing point simultaneously, providing year-round protection. Pure water boils at 100 ┬░C, but at 1 bar gauge pressure, a 50:50 mix will not boil until ~125 ┬░C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Components ŌĆö Functional Breakdown

The liquid cooling system comprises **11 major components**, each with a distinct, non-redundant function. A failure in any one component compromises the entire thermal management chain.

### 2.1.1 Water Jacket (Engine Side)

- **Location:** Cast directly into the cylinder block and cylinder head during the foundry process.
- **Function:** Hollow passages surrounding the combustion chambers, exhaust ports, and valve seats ŌĆö providing the largest surface area for convective heat transfer between hot metal and coolant.
- **Why important:** The engine's metal surfaces can exceed **250 ┬░C** in localized hotspots (e.g., exhaust valve bridges). Without the water jacket, these regions would suffer pre-ignition, detonation, and thermal fatigue cracking.

### 2.1.2 Water Pump

- **Type:** Centrifugal pump (impeller-type), mechanically driven by the engine via a **V-belt**, **serpentine belt**, or **timing chain**.
- **Function:** Imparts kinetic energy to the coolant, establishing a pressure differential of approximately **0.3 bar ŌĆō 0.5 bar** to overcome system friction and maintain a flow rate of **40 ŌĆō 60 liters per minute** in a typical passenger car.
- **Construction:** Cast iron or aluminum housing + steel/bronze impeller + shaft seal (mechanical seal or lip seal) + bearings + pulley.

**Operating Principle (Step-by-step):**
1. The crankshaft rotates the pulley ŌåÆ drives the impeller shaft.
2. The impeller (curved vanes on a disc) spins at engine speed (typically 2x to 3x crankshaft speed).
3. Centrifugal force flings coolant outward from the impeller eye ŌåÆ through the volute casing ŌåÆ into the engine block.
4. A partial vacuum at the impeller center draws fresh coolant from the radiator bottom tank.

### 2.1.3 Thermostat

- **Type:** **Wax-pellet (thermostatic element) type** ŌĆö most common in modern vehicles.
- **Location:** Usually at the engine's front, in the upper radiator hose OR in a thermostat housing attached to the engine block.
- **Function:** A **temperature-actuated flow-control valve** that opens and closes to regulate coolant circulation based on engine temperature.

**Operational Logic:**
- Engine Cold (< **82 ┬░C**): Thermostat **CLOSED** ŌåÆ Coolant bypasses the radiator ŌåÆ Engine warms up rapidly (fast warm-up is critical for emissions, fuel economy, and oil viscosity).
- Engine Warm (> **95 ┬░C**): Thermostat **OPEN** ŌåÆ Coolant flows through the radiator ŌåÆ Heat is rejected to atmosphere.
- Engine Normal (82 ŌĆō 95 ┬░C): **Modulating** position ŌåÆ Maintains optimal temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> A *failed-open* thermostat will cause the engine to **never reach operating temperature** ŌåÆ poor fuel economy, increased hydrocarbon emissions, heater malfunction. A *failed-closed* thermostat causes **catastrophic overheating** within minutes.

### 2.1.4 Radiator

- **Type:** **Honeycomb (tube-and-fin) cross-flow** radiator ŌĆö the modern industry standard.
- **Function:** A liquid-to-air heat exchanger that rejects the coolant's absorbed heat to the atmosphere.
- **Construction:**
  - **Core:** Two tanks (top and bottom) connected by multiple vertical/horizontal tubes. The tubes are surrounded by thin, corrugated **aluminum fins** that dramatically increase the surface area exposed to airflow.
  - **Materials:** Aluminum core + plastic tanks (modern), or copper-brass core (older/industrial).
- **Heat Rejection Capacity:** Typically **30 kW ŌĆō 80 kW** for passenger vehicles; up to **150 kW** for heavy-duty trucks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The radiators' efficiency is rated by its **Core Surface Area ├Ś Air Mass Flow Rate ├Ś Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**. This is the foundation of the formula sheet below.

### 2.1.5 Cooling Fan

- **Types:**
  - **Mechanical (Direct-drive) Fan:** Belt-driven, mounted on the water pump shaft. Older vehicles.
  - **Electric Fan (with Thermo-switch / ECU control):** More efficient, used in all modern vehicles. Speed-controlled via Pulse Width Modulation (PWM).
- **Function:** Draws ambient air through the radiator core at **5 m/s to 15 m/s** when the vehicle is stationary or moving slowly. At highway speeds (> 60 km/h), ram air alone is sufficient, and the fan is disengaged via a **viscous clutch** or turned off electronically.

### 2.1.6 Radiator Pressure Cap (Pressurized Filler Cap)

- **Function:** A spring-loaded pressure relief valve that:
  1. Seals the system to maintain a positive pressure (typically **0.9 bar**).
  2. Releases excess pressure to the expansion tank when coolant expands.
  3. Allows reverse flow (vacuum relief) to the expansion tank when the engine cools and coolant contracts.
- **Why pressurize?** Per the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation**, raising the pressure of a liquid elevates its boiling point. At **1 bar gauge**, water boils at ~**120 ┬░C** instead of 100 ┬░C. This is critical during summer operation and high-load conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> Never open a hot radiator cap. The system is under pressure, and releasing it suddenly can cause **scalding coolant** to erupt, causing severe burns.

### 2.1.7 Coolant Reservoir / Expansion Tank

- **Function:** A translucent plastic storage tank that:
  - Accommodates the thermal expansion and contraction of coolant (a 10-liter system can expand by ~600 mL across -20 ┬░C to +100 ┬░C).
  - Allows air bubbles and gases to separate from the coolant (venting function).
  - Provides a visual level indicator for the user.
  - Maintains a slight vacuum when the engine cools (preventing hose collapse).

### 2.1.8 Hoses and Piping

- **Upper Radiator Hose:** Reinforced rubber/silicone, carries hot coolant from the engine to the radiator top tank.
- **Lower Radiator Hose:** Carries cooled coolant from the radiator bottom tank to the water pump.
- **Bypass Hose:** Connects the thermostat housing to the water pump inlet, providing an alternate flow path when the thermostat is closed.
- **Heater Hose:** Routes hot coolant to the HVAC heater core for cabin heating.

### 2.1.9 Temperature Sender / Gauge

- **Components:** **ECT Sensor** (Engine Coolant Temperature sensor ŌĆö NTC thermistor) + **ECU** + dashboard gauge.
- **Function:** Provides real-time temperature data to the ECU for fuel injection, ignition timing, and electric fan control.

### 2.1.10 Heater Core (Indirect)

- **Function:** A miniature radiator that uses engine coolant to heat the cabin air. Operates as a *parallel* heat exchanger in the HVAC system.

### 2.1.11 Freeze Plugs (Core Plugs)

- **Function:** Soft metal (brass/steel) cup-shaped plugs pressed into the cylinder block casting. They:
  1. Seal the sand-core holes used in foundry casting.
  2. Pop out (sacrificially) if the coolant freezes and expands, preventing the engine block from cracking.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Explanation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat absorbed; $\dot{m}$ = mass flow; $c_p$ = specific heat; $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | W, kg/s, J/(kg┬ĘK), K | Engine-side heat pickup |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | $\rho$ = density (~1070 kg/m┬│ for 50:50 mix); $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow | kg/s, kg/m┬│, m┬│/s | Converting flow rate to mass flow |
| 3 | $T_{boil} = 100 + 0.25 \cdot P_{gauge} \cdot 5$ (approximate) | $P_{gauge}$ in bar | ┬░C, bar | Pressurization effect on boiling |
| 4 | $\eta_{h} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ (Carnot) | Reference for max efficiency | dimensionless | Theoretical comparison only |
| 5 | $P_{centrifugal} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \omega^2 (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$ | Pressure rise in pump; $\omega$ = angular velocity; $r$ = impeller radii | Pa, rad/s, m | Water pump performance |
| 6 | $UA = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{LMTD}$ | $UA$ = overall heat transfer coefficient ├Ś area | W/K | Radiator sizing |
| 7 | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K | Heat exchanger design |
| 8 | $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | Coolant expansion; $\beta$ = volumetric expansion coeff. (~0.0004 /K) | m┬│, /K | Expansion tank sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Code Blocks Allowed:** Equations are presented in clean LaTeX without the use of any programming syntax. This is a thermal engineering subject ŌĆö computational methods (Python/MATLAB) are out of scope.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles mastered here apply directly to:
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Battery Thermal Management Systems (BTMS)** ŌĆö liquid cooling of lithium-ion packs.
- **Data Center Cooling Loops** ŌĆö server racks use the same fundamental radiator + pump + thermostat architecture.
- **Solar Thermal Collectors** ŌĆö glycol-water mixtures are circulated using identical components.
- **Marine and Aerospace Propulsion** ŌĆö jet engines use pressurized liquid cooling for turbine blade cooling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressurization Effect on Coolant Boiling Point

The Clausius-Clapeyron equation for liquid-vapor phase equilibrium is:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \cdot \Delta v}
$$

For a coolant (incompressible liquid + ideal gas vapor), the integrated form simplifies to the **Antoine-style approximation**:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = -\frac{L}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

Let us solve a typical KTU problem.

### **Problem Statement:**
A vehicle's cooling system is pressurized to **1.0 bar gauge** (i.e., absolute pressure = **2.0 bar**). The latent heat of vaporization of the 50:50 EG-water coolant is **L = 2300 kJ/kg**, and the universal gas constant R = **8.314 J/(mol┬ĘK)**. Estimate the new boiling point, given that at 1 atm, the coolant boils at **TŌéü = 373 K** and the molar mass is M = **31 g/mol**.

### **Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert latent heat per kg to per mole:
$$
L_{mol} = L \times M = 2300 \times 0.031 = 71.3 \text{ kJ/mol} = 71300 \text{ J/mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify pressures:
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- $P_2 = 2 \text{ atm (absolute)} = 202.65 \text{ kPa}$

**Step 3:** Apply the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation:
$$
\ln \left( \frac{202.65}{101.325} \right) = -\frac{71300}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{373} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the natural logarithm:
$$
\ln(2) = 0.6931
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and isolate:
$$
0.6931 = -8573.4 \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - 0.002681 \right)
$$

$$
\frac{0.6931}{-8573.4} = \frac{1}{T_2} - 0.002681
$$

$$
-8.085 \times 10^{-5} = \frac{1}{T_2} - 0.002681
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} = 0.002681 - 0.00008085 = 0.002600
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{1}{0.002600} = 384.6 \text{ K} = 111.5 \text{ ┬░C}
$$

**Step 6:** Engineering Interpretation:
The boiling point has been elevated by **11.5 ┬░C**, confirming the design purpose of the pressurized cap. The engine can now safely operate up to ~110 ┬░C without risk of localized boiling (cavitation), which would destroy the water pump's impeller and cause steam pockets in the cylinder head.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coolant Heat Absorption in the Engine

A 2.0 L 4-cylinder engine produces a brake power of **90 kW** at 30% efficiency, meaning 70% of fuel energy is lost as heat, and approximately **30%** of total fuel energy is carried away by the coolant (the rest is exhaust gas and radiation).

### **Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the heat input rate from fuel:
$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \frac{P_{brake}}{\eta_{thermal}} = \frac{90}{0.30} = 300 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the heat absorbed by the coolant:
$$
\dot{Q}_{coolant} = 0.30 \times 300 = 90 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the energy balance equation:
$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

Assume the coolant warms from **85 ┬░C** to **95 ┬░C** as it passes through the engine block, so $\Delta T = 10 \text{ K}$, and $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg┬ĘK)}$.

**Step 4:** Solve for the required mass flow:
$$
\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{90}{3.5 \times 10} = 2.571 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to volumetric flow using $\rho = 1070 \text{ kg/m}^3$:
$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{2.571}{1070} = 0.002403 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 144.2 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 6:** Engineering Interpretation:
The water pump must deliver approximately **144 L/min** ŌĆö which is **2x to 3x** the typical pump rating. This indicates the engine's cooling system is operating at the limit. A larger radiator or higher coolant flow rate would be required for higher continuous loads.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Expansion Tank Sizing

### **Problem Statement:**
A cooling system holds **10 liters** of coolant. The volumetric expansion coefficient is $\beta = 0.0004 \text{ /K}$, and the temperature swings from **ŌłÆ10 ┬░C** to **+100 ┬░C** (a 110 K range). Compute the minimum volume the expansion tank must accommodate.

### **Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the volumetric thermal expansion formula:
$$
\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:
$$
\Delta V = 10 \times 0.0004 \times 110 = 0.44 \text{ liters}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply a safety factor of 1.5 (per SAE J814 standards):
$$
V_{tank,min} = 1.5 \times 0.44 = 0.66 \text{ liters}
$$

**Step 4:** Engineering Interpretation:
A typical expansion tank has a **fill level** marking (cold fill) and a **max level** marking. The differential corresponds to ~0.66 L minimum.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)

| Component | Failure Mode | Effect on Engine | Diagnostic Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water Pump | Bearing seizure, impeller erosion | No coolant flow | Rapid overheating, whining noise |
| Thermostat (open) | Stuck open | Slow warm-up | Heater blows cold air, MIL on |
| Thermostat (closed) | Stuck closed | No radiator flow | Overheating within 5 minutes |
| Radiator | Internal blockage, external fouling | Reduced heat rejection | Overheating at idle/low speed |
| Pressure Cap | Weak spring, seal failure | Pressure loss ŌåÆ boiling | Coolant loss, steam from cap |
| Fan Motor | Electrical failure | No air through radiator | Overheating when stationary |
| Coolant | Degradation, air ingress | Reduced $c_p$, cavitation | Erratic gauge reading |
| Hose | Burst, collapse | Coolant leak | Visible leak, sweet smell |
| ECT Sensor | Open circuit, drift | ECU miscalculates fuel/air | Poor idle quality, incorrect gauge |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *list* components, examiners expect **at least 8** distinct items, with **function** descriptions. Memorize the table above as a "master reference."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Liquid Cooling System ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Cylinder Block - Water Jacket - Heat Source 250C] --> B[Thermostat Housing - Wax Pellet Type]
    B -->|Cold 82C closed| C[Bypass Hose - Short Loop]
    B -->|Warm 95C open| D[Upper Radiator Hose - Hot Coolant 95C]
    C --> E[Water Pump - Centrifugal Impeller Driven by Crankshaft Belt]
    D --> F[Radiator Top Tank - Pressurized Cap 1.0 bar]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Radiator Core - Aluminum Tubes and Corrugated Fins]
    G --> H[Cooling Fan - Electric PWM Controlled - Induces 10 m/s Airflow]
    G --> I[Radiator Bottom Tank - Cooled Coolant 80C]
    I --> J[Lower Radiator Hose - Returns to Pump]
    H -.Ambient Air 30C.-> G
    J --> E
    F -.Excess Pressure 1.0 bar.-> K[Expansion Tank - Coolant Reservoir]
    K -.Vacuum on Cool Down.-> F
    L[ECT Sensor - NTC Thermistor] --> A
    L --> M[ECU - Engine Control Unit]
    M --> H
    N[Heater Core - HVAC Cabin Heating - Parallel Circuit] -.Hot Coolant.-> D
    N -.Cooled Coolant.-> J
    style A fill:#FFB3B3
    style G fill:#B3D9FF
    style F fill:#FFE599
    style H fill:#C5E0B4
    style K fill:#D9D2E9
```

## 4.2 Coolant Flow Path ŌĆö Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Engine Running]) --> Q1{Engine Temperature}
    Q1 -->|Less than 82C| Path1[Thermostat CLOSED]
    Q1 -->|Between 82C and 95C| Path2[Thermostat MODULATING]
    Q1 -->|Above 95C| Path3[Thermostat FULLY OPEN]
    Path1 --> Loop1[Coolant circulates in BYPASS loop: Engine to Pump to Engine]
    Loop1 --> Warmup[Rapid Engine Warmup]
    Warmup --> Q1
    Path2 --> Loop2[Partial radiator flow + bypass]
    Loop2 --> Stable[Engine at steady 88C]
    Path3 --> Loop3[Full radiator flow: Engine to Radiator to Pump to Engine]
    Loop3 --> Cool[Engine cools below 95C]
    Cool --> Q1
    style Path1 fill:#FFD966
    style Path3 fill:#F4B084
    style Stable fill:#A9D08E
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the function of a wax-pellet thermostat in a liquid cooling system.**

**Model Answer:**
A wax-pellet thermostat is a temperature-actuated flow-control valve positioned in the cooling system between the engine and the radiator. It contains a sealed copper cup filled with a special wax compound. When the coolant temperature rises above the calibrated value (typically **82 ┬░C**), the wax melts and expands significantly (by up to **15% in volume**). This expansion pushes a piston against a return spring, opening the main valve and allowing hot coolant to flow into the radiator. When the engine cools below **77 ┬░C**, the wax solidifies, the spring re-seats the valve, and coolant is forced through a bypass loop. This ensures rapid engine warm-up and maintains a stable operating temperature of **85 ┬░C to 95 ┬░C**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the purpose of pressurizing a liquid cooling system. What is the typical pressure?**

**Model Answer:**
The cooling system is pressurized (typically to **0.7 bar to 1.5 bar gauge**, with the radiator cap rated around **0.9 ŌĆō 1.05 bar**) to raise the boiling point of the coolant above **110 ┬░C**. Per the Clausius-Clapeyron relation, increasing the absolute pressure of a liquid elevates its saturation temperature, preventing localized boiling (cavitation) in the cylinder head water jacket. A pressure cap (spring-loaded relief valve) maintains this pressure and vents excess coolant to the expansion tank during thermal expansion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, list and explain the function of any **seven (7) major components** of a liquid cooling system used in a modern passenger car engine. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Describe the working of a centrifugal water pump with a labelled diagram. Discuss how its flow rate is affected by engine speed. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The seven major components and their functions are:

1. **Water Jacket** ŌĆö Cast passages in the engine block and head surrounding the cylinders, exhaust ports, and combustion chambers; absorbs heat from hot metal via forced convection.
2. **Water Pump** ŌĆö Centrifugal pump that circulates coolant at **40 ŌĆō 60 L/min** under a pressure of **0.3 ŌĆō 0.5 bar**; belt-driven by the crankshaft.
3. **Thermostat** ŌĆö Wax-pellet type valve that opens at **82 ┬░C** and is fully open at **95 ┬░C**; controls flow to the radiator.
4. **Radiator** ŌĆö Honeycomb aluminum tube-and-fin heat exchanger that rejects **30 ŌĆō 80 kW** of heat to the atmosphere.
5. **Pressure Cap** ŌĆö Spring-loaded relief valve maintaining **~1.0 bar** system pressure; raises coolant boiling point to ~120 ┬░C.
6. **Cooling Fan** ŌĆö Electric motor-driven fan that draws **5 ŌĆō 15 m/s** of ambient air through the radiator when the vehicle is stationary.
7. **Expansion Tank** ŌĆö Translucent plastic reservoir that accommodates thermal expansion of coolant and separates air bubbles.

*(Block diagram: refer to Section 4.1.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Construction of Centrifugal Water Pump:**

The pump consists of a cast aluminum or iron housing, a steel or bronze impeller with curved vanes, a drive shaft, bearings, a mechanical seal (or lip seal), and a pulley driven by the crankshaft belt.

**Working Principle:**
1. The crankshaft rotates the pulley, which spins the impeller at approximately **2├Ś the crankshaft speed**.
2. Coolant enters the pump through the inlet (impeller eye/center) under atmospheric/return pressure.
3. The high-speed impeller flings coolant outward via **centrifugal force**, accelerating it to high velocity.
4. The volute (curved casing) converts this kinetic energy into **pressure energy**, discharging coolant into the engine block.
5. The pressure differential (suction at the center) draws fresh coolant from the radiator bottom tank.

**Effect of Engine Speed:**

The flow rate is approximately proportional to engine RPM:
$$
\dot{V} \propto N_{engine}
$$
At idle (~700 RPM), flow is low (~**20 L/min**); at 3000 RPM, flow may reach **80 L/min**. This self-regulating nature matches the engine's heat generation, which also scales with RPM. However, at very high speeds, parasitic power loss becomes significant, which is why modern pumps are now **electrically driven and ECU-controlled** for efficiency.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) Explain the construction and operation of a thermostat in detail. Discuss what happens if the thermostat is stuck open or stuck closed. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A vehicle cooling system holds 12 L of coolant. The volumetric expansion coefficient of the coolant is $\beta = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ /K. The coolant temperature varies from ŌłÆ15 ┬░C to +110 ┬░C. The pressure cap is rated at 0.9 bar gauge. Estimate:
  (i) The volumetric expansion of the coolant.
  (ii) The minimum size of the expansion tank (with a safety factor of 1.4).
  (iii) The new boiling point, given the latent heat of vaporization $L = 2200$ kJ/kg, molar mass $M = 30$ g/mol, and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol┬ĘK). (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction:**
- A **copper or brass cup** filled with a special hydrocarbon wax (a mixture of paraffin and metallic powders with high thermal expansion).
- The cup is sealed with a diaphragm and connected to a **stainless-steel push rod** and piston.
- A **return spring** holds the main valve disc closed against the seat.
- The assembly is mounted inside a thermostat housing, between the engine outlet and the upper radiator hose.

**Operation:**
- **Below 82 ┬░C:** Wax is solid. Spring force keeps the main valve closed. Coolant cannot enter the radiator. A small **jumper pipe** allows coolant to bypass the closed valve directly back to the water pump ŌĆö this is the **bypass loop**. The engine warms up rapidly.
- **At 82 ┬░C:** Wax begins to melt. Expansion pushes the piston against the spring, opening the main valve gradually.
- **Above 95 ┬░C:** Wax fully melted. Main valve is fully open. Full coolant flow to the radiator. Maximum heat rejection.
- **Cooling below 77 ┬░C:** Wax re-solidifies, spring reseats the valve, system returns to bypass mode.

**Stuck-Open Failure:** The engine never reaches operating temperature. Consequences: increased fuel consumption (rich mixture), higher HC emissions, poor cabin heating, oil contamination (water/moisture formation), accelerated engine wear.

**Stuck-Closed Failure:** Coolant cannot reach the radiator. Consequences: rapid overheating within 2 ŌĆō 5 minutes, warped cylinder head, blown head gasket, possible engine seizure. **This is a critical safety issue requiring immediate shutdown.**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Volumetric Expansion:**

$$
\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta V = 12 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 125
$$

$$
\Delta V = 0.675 \text{ L}
$$

**(ii) Expansion Tank Size (with SF = 1.4):**

$$
V_{tank,min} = 1.4 \times 0.675 = 0.945 \text{ L} \approx 1.0 \text{ L}
$$

**(iii) New Boiling Point:**

The pressure ratio:
$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.9 \text{ atm (absolute)}}{1.0 \text{ atm}} = 1.9
$$

The molar latent heat:
$$
L_{mol} = 2200 \times 0.030 = 66.0 \text{ kJ/mol} = 66000 \text{ J/mol}
$$

Apply Clausius-Clapeyron:
$$
\ln(1.9) = -\frac{66000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{373} \right)
$$

$$
0.6419 = -7938.4 \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - 0.002681 \right)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} = 0.002681 - 8.087 \times 10^{-5} = 0.002600
$$

$$
T_2 = 384.6 \text{ K} = 111.5 \text{ ┬░C}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The pressurization has raised the boiling point by approximately **11.5 ┬░C**, ensuring the coolant remains liquid even during peak summer or high-load conditions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Component counting errors:** Students often forget the **expansion tank** and **pressure cap** as separate components ŌĆö they are NOT the same. Each earns independent marks.
> 2. **Thermostat direction confusion:** Students frequently state that the thermostat *closes* when hot. The correct behavior is: **CLOSED when COLD, OPEN when HOT.**
> 3. **Pressure cap units:** Always specify "**gauge pressure**" or "**absolute pressure**" ŌĆö examiners mark this distinction.
> 4. **Formula units:** In Part B derivations, mismatched units (e.g., bar vs. Pascal) lead to numerical errors. Use SI units throughout.
> 5. **No diagrams in 14-mark questions:** This is the most common reason students lose 2ŌĆō3 marks. Always include a **neat, labelled block diagram** for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4, Components of Liquid Cooling System:**

- ­¤ö╣ The **liquid cooling system** is a **closed-loop, pressurized, forced-circulation** thermal management system.
- ­¤ö╣ The **11 key components** are: Water Jacket, Water Pump, Thermostat, Radiator, Cooling Fan, Pressure Cap, Expansion Tank, Hoses, ECT Sensor, Heater Core, Freeze Plugs.
- ­¤ö╣ The **thermostat** is **CLOSED when cold** (bypass flow) and **OPEN when hot** (radiator flow) ŌĆö never the reverse.
- ­¤ö╣ The **wax-pellet thermostat** melts at **~82 ┬░C** and is fully open at **~95 ┬░C**.
- ­¤ö╣ The **water pump** is a **centrifugal impeller** type, driven by the crankshaft via a V-belt or timing chain.
- ­¤ö╣ The **pressure cap** typically holds the system at **0.7 ŌĆō 1.5 bar (gauge)**, raising the coolant boiling point from 100 ┬░C to ~120 ┬░C.
- ­¤ö╣ The **radiator** is the heat exchanger ŌĆö a **tube-and-fin** (honeycomb) structure with high surface area.
- ­¤ö╣ The **expansion tank** accommodates ~**6ŌĆō10%** of the total coolant volume as it expands thermally.
- ­¤ö╣ The **coolant** is a **50:50 EG-water mix** (freezing point Ōēł ŌłÆ35 ┬░C, boiling point Ōēł 108 ┬░C at 1 atm).
- ­¤ö╣ **Normal operating temperature band:** **85 ┬░C ŌĆō 95 ┬░C**.
- ­¤ö╣ **Clausius-Clapeyron** governs the boiling point elevation under pressure.
- ­¤ö╣ **Heat balance equation:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ governs coolant sizing.
- ­¤ö╣ **Expansion tank volume:** $V_{tank} \geq 1.4 \times V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ (with safety factor).
- ­¤ö╣ Modern vehicles use **electric PWM fans** and **electric water pumps** for efficiency and ECU integration.
- ­¤ö╣ **Freeze plugs** are sacrificial ŌĆö they pop out before the engine block cracks due to ice expansion.
- ­¤ö╣ **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effect Analysis)** is a critical skill ŌĆö expect direct questions on thermostat and pump failures.

> **End of Module 4 Notes ŌĆö Components of Liquid Cooling System.**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme OBE standards and Board Examiner evaluation patterns.*
