topics.title: Coolants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coolants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coolant:** A heat-transfer fluidŌĆömost commonly a mixture of water and ethylene glycol (with performance additives)ŌĆöcirculated through an internal combustion engine to absorb waste heat from the cylinder head, block, and exhaust manifold, transporting it to the radiator where it is rejected to the atmosphere.

In KTU 2024 Scheme automotive terminology, the term **engine coolant** refers specifically to the *ready-to-use* liquid in the cooling loop (radiator + engine + heater core), while the concentrated additive that is *diluted with water* to prepare the coolant is called **antifreeze**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - *Antifreeze* = Concentrate (e.g., pure ethylene glycol).
> - *Coolant* = Antifreeze + Water + Additives (the working fluid in the system).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of coolant as the **"blood of the engine."** Just as human blood:
- **Carries heat away** from active muscles (engine combustion chambers).
- **Releases that heat** in the lungs (radiator).
- **Contains protective agents** (coolant additives = white blood cells) that prevent corrosion, cavitation, and scale formation in the "arteries" (hoses, water passages).

If the engine ran on plain water:
- Water freezes at **0 ┬░C**, cracking the block in winter.
- Water boils at **100 ┬░C**, causing vapor lock and overheating.
- Water is corrosive to iron, aluminium, and copper.
- Water has no lubrication for the water pump seal.

This is why a properly formulated coolant is **non-negotiable** in modern automotive systems.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of a Coolant

A high-quality automotive coolant performs **six simultaneous duties**:

1. **Heat Absorption** ŌĆö High specific heat capacity to soak up combustion heat.
2. **Freeze Protection** ŌĆö Lowers the freezing point to below ambient winter temperatures.
3. **Boil-over Protection** ŌĆö Raises boiling point under radiator-cap pressure.
4. **Corrosion Inhibition** ŌĆö Chemical buffers protect ferrous and non-ferrous metals.
5. **Cavitation Prevention** ŌĆö Supplemental Coolant Additives (SCAs) protect the water-pump impeller and liner walls.
6. **Lubrication** ŌĆö Mild boundary lubrication for the water-pump shaft seal and thermostat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants for Engine Coolants:**
> - Specific heat of water: **4.18 kJ/kg┬ĘK**
> - Specific heat of ethylene glycol: **2.30 kJ/kg┬ĘK**
> - Freezing point of pure water: **0 ┬░C (273.15 K)**
> - Boiling point of pure water at 1 atm: **100 ┬░C (373.15 K)**
> - Density of ethylene glycol: **1.113 g/cm┬│ at 20 ┬░C**

## 1.4 Why Not Just Use Oil for Cooling?

Engine oil *does* absorb heat (about **15ŌĆō20 %** of total heat rejection in a liquid-cooled engine), but it:
- Has **lower specific heat** than water (~2.0 kJ/kg┬ĘK vs 4.18).
- **Degrades** at temperatures above 130 ┬░C.
- Cannot be permitted to mix with combustion gases in the combustion chamber.

Hence, a **dedicated aqueous coolant loop** is mandatory for high-output engines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engine Coolants

Coolants are classified by **additive technology**, which determines their colour, service life, and metallurgical compatibility.

### 2.1.1 Inorganic Additive Technology (IAT)
- **Chemistry:** Silicates and/or phosphates (inorganic salts).
- **Colour:** Traditional **green**.
- **Service Life:** **2 years / 50,000 km**.
- **Application:** Older passenger cars, light commercial vehicles.
- **Drawback:** Silicate gel can plug heater cores and radiator tubes if neglected.

### 2.1.2 Organic Acid Technology (OAT)
- **Chemistry:** Organic acids (e.g., 2-Ethylhexanoic acid, sebacic acid).
- **Colour:** **Orange, red, pink, dark green**.
- **Service Life:** **5 years / 250,000 km** (extended-life).
- **Application:** Modern GM, Ford, VW, Chrysler vehicles.
- **Advantage:** Long-life, low depletion rate, excellent aluminium compatibility.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid OAT (HOAT)
- **Chemistry:** OAT + a small amount of silicate or phosphate.
- **Colour:** **Yellow, turquoise**.
- **Service Life:** **3ŌĆō5 years / 150,000 km**.
- **Application:** European OEMs (Mercedes-Benz, Ford Europe).

### 2.1.4 Silicate-Hybrid OAT (Si-OAT)
- **Chemistry:** OAT + siloxane-stabilised silicate.
- **Colour:** **Purple, pink**.
- **Service Life:** **5+ years / 250,000 km**.
- **Application:** Asian OEMs (Toyota, Honda, Hyundai) ŌĆō aluminium-intensive engines.

### 2.1.5 Phosphate-Hybrid OAT (P-OAT)
- **Chemistry:** OAT + phosphate.
- **Colour:** **Blue, red**.
- **Service Life:** **5+ years**.
- **Application:** Asian and European vehicles with mixed-metal radiators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Colour is NOT a reliable industry standard.** Two manufacturers may use the *same colour* for *incompatible chemistries*. Always refer to the **OEM specification** (e.g., G12++, G13, DEX-COOL, G11).

## 2.2 Glycol-Based vs Glycerol-Based Coolants

| Property | Ethylene Glycol (EG) | Propylene Glycol (PG) | Glycerol |
|----------|---------------------:|---------------------:|---------:|
| Molar mass (g/mol) | 62.07 | 76.09 | 92.09 |
| Density (g/cm┬│) | 1.113 | 1.036 | 1.261 |
| Boiling point (┬░C) | 197 | 188 | 290 |
| Toxicity | High (sweet taste, lethal) | Low (food-grade) | Low |
| Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| KTU exam frequency | Very High | High | Low |

> **Real-world utility:** Propylene glycol is used in **food-delivery vehicles, buses with potable-water systems, and aircraft de-icing fluid formulations** where accidental ingestion is a risk.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation, constant, and unit a KTU 2024 student must memorise for the ESE on this topic.

| S.No | Formula / Constant | Symbol / Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|:----:|:------------------:|:-----------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by coolant | Q in kJ, m in kg, c in kJ/kg┬ĘK, ╬öT in K |
| 2 | $q_v = \rho \cdot c$ | Volumetric heat capacity | kJ/(m┬│┬ĘK) |
| 3 | $\dot Q = \dot m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T = \dot V \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Rate of heat transfer | kW |
| 4 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | Freezing point depression | ╬öT_f in K, K_f = 1.86 K┬Ękg/mol |
| 5 | $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i$ | Boiling point elevation | K_b = 0.512 K┬Ękg/mol |
| 6 | $T_{bp,system} = T_{bp,water} + \Delta T_b$ | Effective boiling point | ┬░C |
| 7 | $P_{cap} = P_{atm} + \Delta P$ | Radiator cap pressure (gauge) | bar or psi |
| 8 | $V_{EG} / V_{total} = x$ | Glycol concentration (vol/vol) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_f$(water) | Cryoscopic constant | **1.86 K┬Ękg/mol** |
| 10 | $K_b$(water) | Ebullioscopic constant | **0.512 K┬Ękg/mol** |
| 11 | $M_{EG}$ | Molar mass ethylene glycol | **62.07 g/mol** |
| 12 | $i$ (EG in water) | Van't Hoff factor | **1** (non-electrolyte) |
| 13 | $\rho_{EG}$ | Density ethylene glycol | **1.113 g/cm┬│** |
| 14 | $\rho_{H_2O}$ | Density water | **1.000 g/cm┬│** |

## 2.4 Mechanism of Heat Absorption (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Combustion** in the cylinder produces ~30ŌĆō40 % of fuel energy as waste heat.
2. Heat conducts through the **cylinder wall** (cast iron / aluminium).
3. Coolant flowing through the **water jacket** receives this heat by **forced convection**.
4. The water pump maintains turbulent flow (Reynolds number > 4000) to maximise the convective heat-transfer coefficient.
5. Hot coolant travels to the **radiator**, where it gives up heat to air (either ram-air or fan-driven).
6. The cooled coolant returns to the pump and the loop repeats ŌĆö about **5 to 10 times per minute** at full load.

> **Why convection matters:** A coolant that flows laminarly would overheat the engine. Turbulent flow (Re > 4000) increases the convective coefficient $h$ by an order of magnitude, allowing compact radiators to dissipate the required thermal load.

## 2.5 Additive Package Breakdown

A finished coolant is approximately:
- **90ŌĆō95 %** ŌĆö Water + Glycol base.
- **5ŌĆō10 %** ŌĆö Additive package, which itself contains:

| Additive Class | Function | Typical Chemicals |
|----------------|----------|-------------------|
| Corrosion inhibitors (anodic) | Protect aluminium, iron | Nitrites, molybdates, silicates |
| Corrosion inhibitors (cathodic) | Protect copper, brass | Triazoles, tolyltriazoles |
| pH buffers | Maintain pH 7.5ŌĆō11 | Borates, phosphates |
| Anti-foam agents | Prevent cavitation | Silicone defoamers |
| Dyes | Leak detection, type ID | Fluorescein, anthraquinone |
| Bitterants (EG coolants) | Prevent accidental ingestion | Denatonium benzoate |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are worked out **exhaustively**, with every algebraic transition explicitly shown, as required for full KTU ESE credit.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Freezing Point of a 50:50 Ethylene GlycolŌĆōWater Mixture

**Given:**
- Volume of mixture: $V_{mix} = 1.0 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$
- Concentration: 50 % EG by volume ŌćÆ $V_{EG} = V_{water} = 500 \text{ cm}^3$
- Density of EG: $\rho_{EG} = 1.113 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Density of water: $\rho_{water} = 1.000 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Molar mass of EG: $M_{EG} = 62.07 \text{ g/mol}$
- Cryoscopic constant of water: $K_f = 1.86 \text{ K┬Ękg/mol}$
- Van't Hoff factor: $i = 1$ (EG is a non-electrolyte)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Mass of ethylene glycol:**

$$
m_{EG} = V_{EG} \times \rho_{EG} = 500 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.113 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}
$$

$$
m_{EG} = 556.5 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Moles of ethylene glycol:**

$$
n_{EG} = \frac{m_{EG}}{M_{EG}} = \frac{556.5 \text{ g}}{62.07 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

$$
n_{EG} = 8.966 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Mass of water (the solvent):**

$$
m_{water} = 500 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.000 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3} = 500 \text{ g} = 0.500 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Molality of the solution:**

$$
m = \frac{n_{EG}}{m_{water}} = \frac{8.966 \text{ mol}}{0.500 \text{ kg}}
$$

$$
m = 17.932 \text{ mol/kg}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Freezing point depression (colligative property):**

$$
\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i = 1.86 \times 17.932 \times 1
$$

$$
\Delta T_f = 33.35 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final freezing point of the mixture:**

$$
T_{f,mixture} = 0 \text{ ┬░C} - 33.35 \text{ ┬░C}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{f,mixture} = -33.35 \text{ ┬░C}}
$$

This matches the **published industry value of approximately ŌĆō35 ┬░C** for 50:50 EG mixtures, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Boiling Point Elevation for the Same 50:50 Mixture

**Given:** Ebullioscopic constant of water $K_b = 0.512 \text{ K┬Ękg/mol}$, molality from above $m = 17.932 \text{ mol/kg}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the boiling point elevation equation:**

$$
\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i = 0.512 \times 17.932 \times 1
$$

$$
\Delta T_b = 9.181 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Atmospheric boiling point of the mixture:**

$$
T_{b,mixture} = 100 \text{ ┬░C} + 9.18 \text{ ┬░C} = 109.18 \text{ ┬░C}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effect of a pressurised radiator cap (1.0 bar / 14.7 psi):**

Using the ClausiusŌĆōClapeyron relation in a simplified engineering form, every 1 bar (14.7 psi) of cap pressure raises the boiling point of the coolant by approximately **20 ┬░C**.

$$
T_{b,system} = 109.18 \text{ ┬░C} + 20 \text{ ┬░C}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{b,system} \approx 129 \text{ ┬░C}}
$$

This is why engines can run at **coolant temperatures of 105ŌĆō115 ┬░C** without boilingŌĆöa critical enabler for high specific-output engines.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Coolant Flow Rate for a Given Heat Load

**Given:**
- Heat rejected by engine: $\dot Q = 50 \text{ kW}$
- Coolant: 50:50 EG-water, density $\rho_{mix} \approx 1075 \text{ kg/m}^3$, specific heat $c_{mix} \approx 3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg┬ĘK)}$
- Allowable temperature rise: $\Delta T = 10 \text{ K}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rearrange the heat-transfer equation for mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot Q = \dot m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\dot m = \frac{\dot Q}{c \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{50 \text{ kW}}{3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg┬ĘK)} \times 10 \text{ K}}
$$

$$
\dot m = 1.4286 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to volumetric flow rate:**

$$
\dot V = \frac{\dot m}{\rho} = \frac{1.4286 \text{ kg/s}}{1075 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

$$
\dot V = 1.329 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express in litres per minute (a useful engineering unit):**

$$
\dot V = 1.329 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \text{ L/s} \times 60 \text{ s/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot V \approx 79.7 \text{ L/min}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A typical 2.0 L passenger-car engine therefore requires a coolant pump that delivers **~80 L/min** at full thermal load.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Total Mass of Coolant in a Radiator

**Problem Statement:**
A radiator holds 12 L of coolant. The mixture is 50 % ethylene glycol and 50 % water by volume. Compute the total mass of coolant.

*Density of ethylene glycol = 1.113 g/cm┬│, density of water = 1.000 g/cm┬│.*

**Step 1 ŌĆö Volume split:**

$$
V_{EG} = V_{water} = 0.5 \times 12 \text{ L} = 6.0 \text{ L} = 6000 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Mass of ethylene glycol:**

$$
m_{EG} = 6000 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.113 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3} = 6678 \text{ g} = 6.678 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Mass of water:**

$$
m_{water} = 6000 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.000 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3} = 6000 \text{ g} = 6.000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total mass:**

$$
m_{total} = 6.678 + 6.000
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{total} = 12.678 \text{ kg}}
$$

> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A very common "trap" question is to assume the total density is 1.056 g/cm┬│ (simple average) and use that to get 12.67 kg, but a more careful student computes masses *individually* and then sums them. Either method gives the same answer to three significant figures, but the second method reveals the underlying physics and earns full method marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are rendered as **Mermaid** flowcharts. Physical drawings of an engine cross-section are not feasible in Mermaid; therefore, a **block-level functional architecture** is presented that captures every interaction of the coolant loop.

---

## 4.1 Coolant Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    engBlock[Engine Block and Cylinder Head] -->|Hot Coolant Leaves| statOut[Thermostat Housing]
    statOut -->|If T greater than 88C| radTop[Radiator Top Tank]
    statOut -->|Bypass Flow| bypassPipe[Bypass Hose]
    bypassPipe --> pumpIn[Water Pump Inlet]
    radTop --> radCore[Radiator Core Tubes and Fins]
    radCore --> radBot[Radiator Bottom Tank]
    radBot --> pumpIn
    pumpIn --> pumpImp[Water Pump Impeller]
    pumpImp -->|Pressurised Coolant| engBlock
    fanMotor[Electric or Belt Driven Fan] -->|Forced Air| radCore
    resTank[Expansion Reserve Tank] -->|Make Up Coolant| radBot
    radTop -->|Overflow| resTank
    engBlock -->|Diverted Flow| heatCore[Heater Core]
    heatCore -->|Return| pumpIn
    capVlv[Radiator Pressure Cap] -->|Vents at Rated Pressure| resTank
    radTop --> capVlv
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Coolant absorbs heat in the **engine block** and exits hot.
- The **thermostat** decides whether to route coolant through the radiator or via the bypass.
- Heat is shed at the **radiator core** aided by the **cooling fan**.
- The **pressure cap** vents excess coolant/steam to the **reserve tank** during thermal expansion.
- A small bleed to the **heater core** warms the cabin.
- The **water pump** drives the entire loop.

---

## 4.2 Coolant-Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TB
    root[Automotive Engine Coolants]
    root --> iat[IAT - Inorganic Additive Technology]
    root --> oat[OAT - Organic Acid Technology]
    root --> hoat[HOAT - Hybrid Organic Acid Technology]
    root --> sioat[Si-OAT - Silicate Stabilised OAT]
    root --> poat[P-OAT - Phosphate Hybrid OAT]
    iat --> iatNote[Silicates and Phosphates. Green. 2 yr or 50000 km. Legacy vehicles.]
    oat --> oatNote[Organic Acids. Orange Red. 5 yr or 250000 km. Modern aluminium engines.]
    hoat --> hoatNote[OAT plus small dose silicate or phosphate. Yellow. European OEMs.]
    sioat --> sioatNote[Siloxane plus OAT. Purple Pink. Asian aluminium intensive engines.]
    poat --> poatNote[Phosphate plus OAT. Blue Red. Mixed metal radiators.]
```

---

## 4.3 Property-Composition Influence Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    egConc[Ethylene Glycol Concentration] --> fp[Freezing Point Decreases]
    egConc --> bp[Boiling Point Increases]
    egConc --> shc[Specific Heat Decreases]
    egConc --> visc[Viscosity Increases]
    egConc --> dpth[Density Increases]
    shc --> pump[Greater Volume Flow Needed]
    visc --> pump
    pump --> wpLoad[Higher Water Pump Load]
    fp --> prot[Winter Protection]
    bp --> protHot[Summer Protection]
```

**Interpretation for KTU exams:** This is a *qualitative* map showing why **50:50 is the universal compromise** ŌĆö beyond 60 % EG, the freezing point *rises* again (an unusual colligative effect known as the **eutectic point** of EG-water, near ŌĆō69 ┬░C at 70 % EG, but the viscosity penalty becomes unacceptable).

---

## 4.4 Additive Function Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    addPack[Coolant Additive Package]
    addPack --> ci[Corrosion Inhibitors]
    addPack --> buf[pH Buffers]
    addPack --> af[Anti Foam Agents]
    addPack --> d[Dyes]
    addPack --> sc[SCA - Supplemental Coolant Additive]
    addPack --> bit[Bitterants]
    ci --> an[Anodic for Al and Fe]
    ci --> ca[Cathodic for Cu and Brass]
    buf --> range[Maintains pH between 7.5 and 11]
    af --> cav[Prevents cavitation in water pump]
    sc --> liner[Protects wet cylinder liners in heavy duty diesels]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Never write "coolant" and "antifreeze" interchangeably** in a 14-mark answer ŌĆö examiners dock 1 mark for this terminological slip.
> 2. **Do NOT mix IAT and OAT coolants** in a real-world recommendation; this causes gel formation and heater-core blockage. State the OEM spec explicitly.
> 3. **Show all unit conversions** in numerical problems (cm┬│ ŌåÆ L ŌåÆ m┬│) ŌĆö silent unit errors account for 30 % of "method-only" partial credit losses.
> 4. **Do NOT confuse freezing-point depression (K_f = 1.86) with boiling-point elevation (K_b = 0.512).** Mixing them up is the single most common fatal error in derivations.
> 5. **Always state the Van't Hoff factor $i$ explicitly.** For ethylene glycol, $i = 1$ ŌĆö students often write $i = 2$ or 3 by reflex.

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper Set A]
**Define an engine coolant. List any four desirable properties it must possess.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **engine coolant** is a liquidŌĆöusually a mixture of water and ethylene glycol with chemical additivesŌĆöcirculated through an engine's cooling jacket to absorb heat and transfer it to the radiator for rejection to the atmosphere.

*Four desirable properties:*
1. **High specific heat capacity** to absorb maximum heat per unit mass.
2. **Low freezing point** and **high boiling point** to remain liquid over operating temperatures.
3. **Chemical inertness** ŌĆö non-corrosive to cast iron, aluminium, copper, and rubber.
4. **Good thermal conductivity and low viscosity** at all operating temperatures to allow turbulent flow.

*(Any 4 of: high flash point, non-foaming, non-toxic where required, low cost, long service life, lubricant for water-pump seal.)* **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between IAT and OAT coolants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | IAT | OAT |
|------:|-----|-----|
| Additive chemistry | Inorganic (silicates, phosphates) | Organic acids |
| Typical colour | Green | Orange / red / dark green |
| Service life | 2 yr / 50 000 km | 5 yr / 250 000 km |
| Metallurgical focus | Iron / copper | Aluminium |
| Replenishment | Frequent | Long-life, low depletion |

IATs deplete quickly because silicates are consumed as they protect; OATs are consumed far more slowly because the organic acids react only at active corrosion sites. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024, Model]

**(a) Explain, with a neat block diagram, the engine cooling system and the role of coolant in each component. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Engine block (cylinder head) water jacket:** Combustion heat (Ōēł 30ŌĆō35 % of fuel energy) conducts through cylinder walls; coolant absorbs this heat by forced convection. *Marks: 1*
2. **Water pump:** A centrifugal pump driven by the timing belt (or electric motor) maintains turbulent flow (Re > 4000) in the jacket to maximise the convective heat-transfer coefficient. *Marks: 1*
3. **Thermostat (wax-pellet type):** A temperature-sensitive valve. Below ~88 ┬░C it closes, forcing coolant through the bypass (fast warm-up). Above ~88 ┬░C it opens, allowing flow to the radiator. *Marks: 1*
4. **Radiator:** A cross-flow or down-flow heat exchanger; hot coolant enters the top tank, descends through finned tubes, and is forced-cooled by ram-air or fan air. *Marks: 1*
5. **Cooling fan:** Either belt-driven (mechanical) or electric (ECU-controlled); maintains airflow when the vehicle is idling. *Marks: 1*
6. **Pressure cap & expansion tank:** The cap is typically rated 0.9ŌĆō1.5 bar; it raises the coolant boiling point and vents excess coolant/steam to the reserve tank during thermal expansion. *Marks: 1*
7. **Heater core:** A small radiator inside the cabin HVAC box; hot coolant passes through to deliver cabin heat. *Marks: 1*

**Block diagram** (essential to draw/reproduce in exam ŌĆö refer to Section 4.1 of these notes): **Coolant path = Engine ŌåÆ Thermostat ŌåÆ Radiator ŌåÆ Pump ŌåÆ Engine**, with parallel path through Heater Core and Reserve Tank overflow. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b) Discuss the properties of an ideal coolant and explain how a 50:50 ethylene-glycol/water blend meets these requirements. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Desirable properties of an ideal coolant:**

1. **High specific heat** ŌĆö water (4.18 kJ/kg┬ĘK) is unmatched; 50:50 EG-water Ōēł 3.5 kJ/kg┬ĘK ŌĆö *acceptable.*
2. **High thermal conductivity** ŌĆö water 0.6 W/m┬ĘK, EG 0.25 W/m┬ĘK; mixture Ōēł 0.4 W/m┬ĘK.
3. **Low freezing point** ŌĆö pure water 0 ┬░C, 50:50 mix Ōēł ŌĆō35 ┬░C ŌĆö *meets cold-climate specs.*
4. **High boiling point** ŌĆö pure water 100 ┬░C, 50:50 mix Ōēł 109 ┬░C; with 1 bar cap pressure Ōēł 129 ┬░C ŌĆö *meets hot-climate specs.*
5. **Low viscosity** at operating temperature ŌĆö water is best, EG raises viscosity, but the mixture remains pumpable up to 110 ┬░C.
6. **Chemical compatibility** with iron, aluminium, copper, and elastomer hoses ŌĆö EG alone is mildly corrosive; the additive package (phosphates, silicates, organic acids) buffers pH 7.5ŌĆō11 and passivates metal surfaces.
7. **Non-foaming, non-toxic (where required), low cost, long service life.**

**Conclusion:** Although pure water has the highest specific heat, its freezing and boiling points are inadequate. The 50:50 EG-water blend is the **engineering optimum** that balances thermal performance, freeze protection, and pumpability ŌĆö explaining why virtually every global OEM specifies this dilution ratio in the ownerŌĆÖs manual. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jul 2024, Model]

**(a) A radiator has a coolant capacity of 12 litres. The mixture is 50 % ethylene glycol and 50 % water by volume. Given $\rho_{EG} = 1.113 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_{water} = 1.0 \text{ g/cm}^3$, calculate:
   (i) The total mass of coolant. (3 marks)
   (ii) The molality of the solution. (2 marks)
   (iii) The freezing point of the mixture. (2 marks) (7 marks total)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total mass (3 marks):**
- $V_{EG} = V_{water} = 6 \text{ L} = 6000 \text{ cm}^3$
- $m_{EG} = 6000 \times 1.113 = 6678 \text{ g} = 6.678 \text{ kg}$ ... *(1 mark)*
- $m_{water} = 6000 \times 1.000 = 6000 \text{ g} = 6.000 \text{ kg}$ ... *(1 mark)*
- $\boxed{m_{total} = 12.678 \text{ kg}}$ ... *(1 mark)*

**(ii) Molality (2 marks):**
- $n_{EG} = 6678 \text{ g} / 62.07 \text{ g/mol} = 107.59 \text{ mol}$ ... *(1 mark)*
- $\boxed{m = 107.59 / 6.0 = 17.93 \text{ mol/kg}}$ ... *(1 mark)*

**(iii) Freezing point (2 marks):**
- $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i = 1.86 \times 17.93 \times 1 = 33.35 \text{ K}$ ... *(1 mark)*
- $\boxed{T_f = 0 - 33.35 = -33.35 \text{ ┬░C}}$ ... *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) An engine produces 50 kW of heat that must be rejected by the coolant. Using a 50:50 EG-water mixture (specific heat 3.5 kJ/kg┬ĘK, density 1075 kg/m┬│) and a permitted coolant temperature rise of 10 K, calculate:
   (i) The required mass flow rate of coolant. (3 marks)
   (ii) The required volumetric flow rate in L/min. (2 marks)
   (iii) State two practical consequences if the water pump cannot deliver this flow. (2 marks) (7 marks total)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mass flow rate (3 marks):**
- $\dot m = \dot Q / (c \cdot \Delta T) = 50 / (3.5 \times 10)$ ... *(1 mark)*
- $\dot m = 1.429 \text{ kg/s}$ ... *(1 mark)*
- **Final answer boxed** ... *(1 mark)*

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate (2 marks):**
- $\dot V = \dot m / \rho = 1.429 / 1075 = 1.329 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ ... *(1 mark)*
- $\dot V = 1.329 \times 10^{-3} \times 60\,000 = 79.7 \text{ L/min}$ ... *(1 mark)*

**(iii) Practical consequences (any 2) (2 marks):**
1. Coolant will overheat ŌĆö local hotspots in cylinder head lead to **pre-ignition and detonation**.
2. Laminar flow reduces convective coefficient $h$ ŌĆö the **water jacket may experience nucleate boiling**, forming vapour bubbles that insulate the metal (also called *vapour lock* in the cooling system).
3. Loss of cabin heating ŌĆö heater core receives insufficient flow.
4. Accelerated degradation of additive package due to localised high temperatures.

**(Any two well-stated consequences = 2 marks)** **[7 marks total]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU ESE 2024, Module 4, Coolants**

- **Coolant = water + glycol + additives.** Antifreeze is the *concentrate*; coolant is the *working fluid*.
- **Ethylene glycol (M = 62.07 g/mol, Žü = 1.113 g/cm┬│) is the industry standard**; propylene glycol is the low-toxicity alternative.
- **50:50 mix** is the universal OEM default ŌĆö gives freezing point Ōēł ŌĆō35 ┬░C, boiling point Ōēł 109 ┬░C (Ōēł 129 ┬░C under 1 bar cap).
- **Specific heat values to memorise:** Water 4.18, EG 2.30, 50:50 mix 3.5 kJ/kg┬ĘK.
- **Colligative constants:** $K_f$ (water) = **1.86 K┬Ękg/mol**; $K_b$ (water) = **0.512 K┬Ękg/mol**.
- **Van't Hoff factor $i = 1$ for EG** (it is a non-electrolyte ŌĆö *do not* write 2 or 3).
- **Coolant types by chemistry:** IAT (green, short life), OAT (orange, long life), HOAT (yellow), Si-OAT (purple), P-OAT (blue).
- **Colour is a guide, NOT a specification.** Always quote the OEM standard (G11, G12, G13, DEX-COOL).
- **Heat balance equation:** $Q = m c \Delta T$ and $\dot Q = \dot m c \Delta T = \dot V \rho c \Delta T$.
- **Pressure cap raises boiling point** ~20 ┬░C per bar of cap pressure.
- **Turbulent flow (Re > 4000) in the water jacket is essential** for adequate convective heat transfer.
- **Six functions of coolant:** Heat absorption, freeze protection, boil-over protection, corrosion inhibition, cavitation prevention, water-pump lubrication.
- **Coolant additives (5ŌĆō10 % by volume):** corrosion inhibitors (anodic + cathodic), pH buffers, anti-foam agents, dyes, SCAs, bitterants.
- **Typical 2.0 L engine needs Ōēł 80 L/min coolant flow** at full thermal load.
- **Always convert units** explicitly in numerical problems (cm┬│ ŌåÆ L ŌåÆ m┬│).
- **Never mix IAT and OAT** coolants ŌĆö gel formation and heater-core blockage result.
- **KTU marking tip:** When asked to "list properties," always pair each property with a *consequence* (e.g., "high specific heat ŌåÆ absorbs more heat per unit mass ŌåÆ smaller radiator possible").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Antifreeze Solution, Engine Lubricating System - Requirements of Lubrication Systems, Types of Lubrication System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Antifreeze Solution, Engine Lubricating System - Requirements of Lubrication Systems, Types of Lubrication System # ­¤ö¦ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Antifreeze Solution ### ­¤ōś Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > **Antifreeze** is a chemical additive (typically Ethylene Glycol $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}_2$ or Propylene Glycol) mixed with water in a 50:50 ratio and added to the engine cooling system to **lower the freezing point** and **raise the boiling point** of the coolant, thereby preventing freeze-up in sub-zero climates and boil-over under high thermal loads. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The standard automotive coolant is a mixture of **Ethylene Glycol (EG) + Water + Corrosion Inhibitors**. Pure water boils at **100┬░C** and freezes at **0┬░C**, while a 50:50 EG-water mixture has a freezing point of approximately **ŌłÆ37┬░C** and a boiling point of around **106┬░C** (at atmospheric pressure, elevated further under pressurized radiator caps of **0.9ŌĆō1.05 bar**). ### ­¤¦Ā Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Sweet Spot Salty Water" Trick Imagine you are trying to make ice cubes in winter, but you want them to *not freeze* even when the temperature drops below 0┬░C. If you dissolve salt in water, the freezing point drops. Antifreeze works on the **exact same colligative principle** ŌĆö adding a solute (glycol) to a solvent (water) disrupts the crystal lattice formation of ice, requiring much colder temperatures to solidify. Similarly, it disrupts the vapor pressure, raising the boil point. > [!NOTE] > **Did You Know?** > - The freezing point depression is governed by **Raoult's Law** and depends on the *molar concentration* of the solute. > - Pure Ethylene Glycol freezes at **ŌłÆ12.9┬░C** ŌĆö which is *higher* than the 50:50 mixture! This is the **Eutectic Point** phenomenon. --- ## 1.2 Engine Lubricating System ### ­¤ōś Formal Definition > The **Engine Lubricating System** is a network of mechanical and hydraulic components (oil pump, sump, galleries, filters, cooler, and pressure regulating valve) designed to deliver lubricating oil under controlled pressure to all **moving parts** of the engine, ensuring **friction reduction, wear prevention, cooling, sealing, and corrosion protection**. ### ­¤¦Ā Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Bloodstream of the Engine Think of engine oil as the **blood of the engine**: - The **oil sump (pan)** = the *heart reservoir* storing oil. - The **oil pump** = the *heart* pumping oil. - The **oil galleries** = *arteries* carrying oil to organs. - The **bearings & journals** = *vital organs* receiving oil. - The **oil return paths** = *veins* returning oil to the sump. Without this "blood circulation," the engine's moving parts would weld themselves together within seconds due to metal-to-metal contact. ### ­¤ōŗ Requirements of a Lubrication System (KTU 2024 High-Weight Topic) > [!IMPORTANT] > A modern engine lubrication system **MUST** satisfy the following KTU-listed functional requirements: | # | Requirement | Engineering Rationale | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Adequate Supply of Oil** | All bearing surfaces must receive continuous oil film, even during cold starts and high-RPM operation. | | 2 | **Correct Pressure** | Oil pressure must be sufficient to overcome bearing clearances (typically **1.5ŌĆō4.0 bar** at idle, **3.0ŌĆō5.5 bar** at cruising). | | 3 | **Filtration** | Oil must be cleaned of wear debris, combustion by-products (soot, acids), and contaminants to **5ŌĆō25 microns** (typical filter rating). | | 4 | **Cooling of Oil** | Oil absorbs heat from bearings and pistons; oil cooler maintains oil temp between **80┬░CŌĆō110┬░C**. | | 5 | **Adequate Viscosity at Operating Temperature** | Oil must be fluid at cold start yet retain film strength at **120┬░C+**. | | 6 | **Anti-foaming and Anti-oxidation** | Prevents air entrainment and oil degradation; antioxidants (ZDDP) extend service life. | | 7 | **Sealing & Cushioning** | Oil film must seal piston rings against cylinder walls and dampen vibration in bearings. | | 8 | **Self-Regulation (Relief Valve)** | Pressure relief valve opens at ~**4.5 bar** to prevent pump/line damage. | ### ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Functions of Lubricating Oil (Mnemonic: **"FLWSC"**) - **F** ŌĆō Friction Reduction - **L** ŌĆō Lubrication (load-bearing film) - **W** ŌĆō Washing (carries debris to filter) - **S** ŌĆō Sealing (rings, valves) - **C** ŌĆō Cooling (heat transfer) # ­¤ö¼ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Antifreeze ŌĆö Theoretical Analysis ### ­¤¦¬ Composition of Modern Antifreeze ``` Antifreeze = Ethylene Glycol (EG) + Water + Inhibitor Package ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ Base ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ Additives ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ ``` **Typical Inhibitor Package (IAT ŌĆö Inorganic Additive Technology):** - **Sodium Silicate** ŌĆō aluminum protection - **Sodium Nitrate / Nitrite** ŌĆō cavitation & corrosion inhibitor - **Phosphates** ŌĆō iron & steel protection - **Borax** ŌĆō pH buffer (maintains pH 8ŌĆō10) - **Tolyltriazole** ŌĆō copper & brass protection ### ­¤ōŖ Properties Required of an Ideal Coolant | Property | KTU Standard Value | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | **Freezing Point** | Ōēż ŌłÆ34┬░C (50:50 mix) | Prevents coolant solidification | | **Boiling Point** | Ōēź 106┬░C (at 1 atm) | Prevents vapor lock / boil-over | | **Specific Heat** | ~3.6 kJ/kg┬ĘK | Heat carrying capacity | | **Viscosity** | Low (kinematic) | Pumpability at cold start | | **pH** | 8.0 ŌĆō 10.5 | Prevents acid corrosion | | **Reserve Alkalinity (RA)** | Ōēź 5 mL (0.1N HCl) | Neutralizes combustion acids | | **Foaming Tendency** | Ōēż 50 mL/10 min | Avoids air pockets in system | ### ­¤ōÉ Antifreeze Concentration vs. Freezing Point (KTU Important Data) | EG Concentration (% by volume) | Freezing Point (┬░C) | Boiling Point (┬░C) | |---|---|---| | 0% (Pure Water) | 0 | 100 | | 20% | ŌłÆ7 | 101 | | 30% | ŌłÆ13 | 103 | | **50% (Recommended)** | **ŌłÆ37** | **106** | | 60% | ŌłÆ50 | 110 | | 70% (Eutectic) | **ŌłÆ68 (Eutectic Point)** | 113 | | 100% (Pure EG) | ŌłÆ13 | 197 | > [!NOTE] > **Eutectic Point:** The concentration (Ōēł 68% EG / 32% Water) at which the freezing point is the *lowest*. Beyond this, adding more EG *raises* the freezing point ŌĆö a classic KTU viva question! ### ­¤¦« Antifreeze ŌĆö Key Formula Sheet | Formula | Description | |---|---| | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | Freezing point depression (Raoult's Law colligative) | | $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i$ | Boiling point elevation | | $P_{mix} = X_{solvent} \cdot P^0_{solvent}$ | Vapor pressure reduction (Raoult) | | $C = \dfrac{m_{EG}}{m_{EG} + m_{water}} \times 100$ | Concentration by mass % | Where: - $K_f$ for water = **1.86 ┬░C┬Ękg/mol** - $K_b$ for water = **0.512 ┬░C┬Ękg/mol** - $m$ = molality - $i$ = van't Hoff factor (= 1 for non-electrolyte EG) --- ## 2.2 Lubrication System ŌĆö Theoretical Analysis ### ­¤øó’ĖÅ Lubrication Regimes (Foundation Concept) The **Stribeck Curve** defines three lubrication regimes based on the dimensionless **Hersey number** ($H$): $$H = \dfrac{\mu \cdot N}{P}$$ Where: - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of oil - $N$ = rotational speed - $P$ = load per unit projected area | Regime | Condition | Film Thickness | Friction | |---|---|---|---| | **Boundary Lubrication** | Very low H, cold start | < 0.1 ┬Ąm (asperity contact) | High, wear-prone | | **Mixed Lubrication** | Moderate H | 0.1ŌĆō1 ┬Ąm (partial film) | Moderate | | **Hydrodynamic (Full-Film) Lubrication** | High H, normal running | > 1 ┬Ąm (complete separation) | Low, no wear | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept:** Engine bearings are designed to operate in **hydrodynamic regime** under normal running conditions. Boundary regime is only acceptable during cold start (and even then, anti-wear additives like **ZDDP** protect surfaces). ### ­¤¦« Lubrication ŌĆö KTU Formula Sheet | Formula | Use | |---|---| | $H = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | Hersey number (regime identification) | | $h_{min} = 0.023 \cdot \mu N \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{P}\right)$ | Minimum film thickness (Reynolds) | | $h_0 = R \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | Central film thickness (Hamrock-Dowson approx) | | $h = \dfrac{C}{2} - \Delta r$ | Actual film thickness in journal bearing | | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot \eta}{t}$ | Oil flow rate (galleys) | | $P_{rel} = P_{set} + 0.5$ bar | Pressure relief valve setting | | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity (shear stress) | | $W = \tau \cdot A \cdot v$ | Friction power loss | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In any markdown table, the absolute value `|x|` is rendered as `\vert x \vert` to prevent markdown table breakage. ### ­¤Åü Properties of Lubricating Oil (KTU 2024 Syllabus) | Property | Test Method | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | **Viscosity (Kinematic)** | ASTM D445 | 20 cSt @ 40┬░C for 20W-40 | | **Viscosity Index (VI)** | ASTM D2270 | 95ŌĆō110 (multigrade) | | **Flash Point** | ASTM D92 | Ōēź 200┬░C | | **Pour Point** | ASTM D97 | Ōēż ŌłÆ30┬░C (multigrade) | | **TBN (Total Base Number)** | ASTM D2896 | 6ŌĆō10 mg KOH/g | | **Foaming Tendency** | ASTM D892 | Ōēż 50/0 mL (Seq I/II/III) | ### ­¤¦Ā SAE Viscosity Grading ŌĆö The KTU Must-Know ``` SAE Viscosity Classification (J300 Standard): Winter Grade: 0W, 5W, 10W, 15W, 20W, 25W (cold cranking & pumping) Summer Grade: 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 (hot running viscosity) Multigrade: 5W-30, 10W-40, 15W-50, 20W-50 Example: 10W-40 ŌåÆ "10W" = winter grade; max cranking viscosity = 7000 mPa┬Ęs at ŌłÆ20┬░C ŌåÆ "40" = hot grade; kinematic viscosity 12.5ŌĆō16.3 cSt at 100┬░C ``` # ­¤¦« Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Freezing Point Depression Calculation > **Problem:** A coolant mixture contains **40% by mass** of Ethylene Glycol (Molar Mass = **62 g/mol**) in **1 kg of water** (Molar Mass = **18 g/mol**, $K_f = 1.86$ ┬░C┬Ękg/mol). Calculate the freezing point of the mixture. ### Step 1: Identify Given Data - Mass of water: $m_{water} = 1$ kg - Mass of EG: $m_{EG} = 0.4$ kg (40% of 1 kg solvent basis ŌĆö we use 1 kg water as solvent, so solute = 0.4 kg) - Molar mass of EG: $M_{EG} = 62$ g/mol = **0.062 kg/mol** - $K_f$ (water) = **1.86 ┬░C┬Ękg/mol** - $i = 1$ (EG is a non-electrolyte) - Freezing point of pure water: $T_f^0 = 0$┬░C ### Step 2: Compute Moles of EG (Solute) $$n_{EG} = \dfrac{m_{EG}}{M_{EG}} = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ kg}}{0.062 \text{ kg/mol}} = 6.452 \text{ mol}$$ ### Step 3: Compute Molality ($m$) $$m = \dfrac{n_{EG}}{\text{mass of water (kg)}} = \dfrac{6.452 \text{ mol}}{1 \text{ kg}} = 6.452 \text{ mol/kg}$$ ### Step 4: Apply Freezing Point Depression Formula $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$$ $$\Delta T_f = 1.86 \times 6.452 \times 1$$ $$\Delta T_f = 11.99 \text{ ┬░C} \approx 12 \text{ ┬░C}$$ ### Step 5: Determine New Freezing Point $$T_f = T_f^0 - \Delta T_f = 0 - 12 = -12 \text{ ┬░C}$$ ### Ō£ģ Final Answer: $$\boxed{T_f = -12 \text{ ┬░C}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** The theoretical value (ŌłÆ12┬░C) is less negative than the actual measured value (~ŌłÆ22┬░C) because real antifreezes also contain **synergistic inhibitors** and exhibit non-ideal behavior. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Boiling Point Elevation > **Problem:** Same mixture as above. Calculate the **boiling point elevation** using $K_b = 0.512$ ┬░C┬Ękg/mol for water. ### Step 1: Apply Boiling Point Elevation Formula $$\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m \cdot i$$ $$\Delta T_b = 0.512 \times 6.452 \times 1 = 3.30 \text{ ┬░C}$$ ### Step 2: Compute New Boiling Point $$T_b = T_b^0 + \Delta T_b = 100 + 3.30 = 103.3 \text{ ┬░C}$$ ### Ō£ģ Final Answer: $$\boxed{T_b = 103.3 \text{ ┬░C at 1 atm}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > With a **pressurized** radiator cap (typically 0.9 bar gauge), the boiling point of coolant in a closed system rises to **~125┬░C**, further preventing boil-over ŌĆö a key KTU design concept. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Hersey Number (Lubrication Regime) > **Problem:** A main bearing has the following operating parameters: $\mu = 0.01$ Pa┬Ęs, $N = 3000$ RPM, $P = 5$ MPa. Determine the lubrication regime. ### Step 1: Convert N to rad/s $$N_{rad/s} = 3000 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Hersey Number $$H = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} = \dfrac{0.01 \times 314.16}{5 \times 10^6}$$ $$H = 6.28 \times 10^{-7}$$ ### Step 3: Compare with Stribeck Thresholds (KTU Rule of Thumb) | H Range | Regime | |---|---| | $H < 10^{-7}$ | **Boundary** | | $10^{-7} \leq H \leq 10^{-4}$ | **Mixed** | | $H > 10^{-4}$ | **Hydrodynamic** | Our value $H = 6.28 \times 10^{-7}$ lies in the **Mixed Lubrication** zone. ### Ō£ģ Final Answer: $$\boxed{\text{Mixed Lubrication Regime}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Antifreeze vs Coolant vs Water | Property | Pure Water | 50:50 EG-Water | Pure Ethylene Glycol | |---|---|---|---| | Freezing Point (┬░C) | 0 | ŌłÆ37 | ŌłÆ12.9 | | Boiling Point (┬░C) | 100 | 106 | 197 | | Specific Heat (kJ/kg┬ĘK) | 4.18 | 3.6 | 2.4 | | Density (kg/m┬│) | 1000 | 1070 | 1113 | | Viscosity @ 40┬░C (cSt) | 0.65 | 4.5 | 19 | | Corrosion to Iron | High (rust) | Low (inhibited) | Moderate | | Cost (INR/L, approx) | 0.5 | 250ŌĆō500 | 350ŌĆō600 | --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Lubrication System Types (Preview for Section 5) | Parameter | Splash System | Pressure System | Combination (Mist) | |---|---|---|---| | Oil Delivery | Dipper splashes oil | Pump-driven | Pump + splash | | Pressure (bar) | Atmospheric | 3ŌĆō5 | 3ŌĆō5 | | Oil Quantity | Large (4ŌĆō8 L) | Small (3ŌĆō5 L) | Moderate (3.5ŌĆō6 L) | | Wear Control | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | | Cooling | Limited | Good (via cooler) | Moderate | | Modern Use | Obsolete | High-performance engines | **Most common in cars** | | Typical Application | Old tractors, small 2-strokes | Trucks, racing | Passenger cars, SUVs | # ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Engine Lubrication System Architecture (Full-Flow Forced-Feed) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Oil Sump / Pan

[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] **List the functions of lubricating oil in an IC engine. State any three properties of a good lubricant.** #### Ō£ģ Model Answer: **Functions of Lubricating Oil:** 1. **Friction reduction** ŌĆō forms hydrodynamic film between moving parts. 2. **Cooling** ŌĆō absorbs heat from bearings, pistons, and dissipates via cooler. 3. **Sealing** ŌĆō oil film seals piston rings against cylinder walls. 4. **Cleaning** ŌĆō suspends contaminants; carries them to the filter. 5. **Corrosion protection** ŌĆō neutralizes acids; forms protective film. 6. **Vibration damping** ŌĆō cushions bearing impacts. 7. **Power transmission** ŌĆō in hydraulic tappets, VVT systems. **Properties of a Good Lubricant:** 1. **Adequate Viscosity** at operating temperature. 2. **High Viscosity Index (VI)** ŌĆō minimal viscosity change with temperature. 3. **Low Pour Point** ŌĆō flows at low temperatures. 4. **High Flash Point** ŌĆō resists vaporization. 5. **Chemical Stability** ŌĆō resists oxidation. 6. **Anti-foam and Anti-corrosion additives.** > **Valuation Tip (2/3 marks):** Mentioning 4ŌĆō5 functions + 3 properties = full 3 marks. Vague 2-liners get only 2 marks. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **What is antifreeze? Why is ethylene glycol preferred over methanol as an automotive coolant?** #### Ō£ģ Model Answer: **Antifreeze:** A chemical fluid (typically Ethylene Glycol mixed with water) added to the engine cooling system to **lower the freezing point** and **raise the boiling point**, preventing coolant from freezing in cold weather or boiling over in hot conditions. **Why Ethylene Glycol is preferred over Methanol:** | Property | Ethylene Glycol | Methanol | |---|---|---| | Boiling Point | 197┬░C (high) | 65┬░C (low) | | Freezing Point Depression | ŌłÆ37┬░C (50:50) | ŌłÆ25┬░C | | Flash Point | 111┬░C (safer) | 12┬░C (highly flammable) | | Vapor Pressure | Low | High (evaporates fast) | | Toxicity | Moderate | High (more dangerous) | | Stability | High | Low (oxidizes to acids) | > **Conclusion:** EG has a **higher boiling point**, **higher flash point** (safer), **lower vapor loss**, and provides **broader temperature protection**, making it the industry standard. --- ## ­¤ōī PART B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice) --- ### ­¤öĄ QUESTION A ŌĆö (14 Marks) [Comprehensive Option] **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Expected Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **various types of lubricating systems** used in IC engines with neat sketches. State their **advantages, disadvantages, and applications**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **requirements of a good lubrication system** and explain with a block diagram the **forced-feed (pressure) lubrication system** in a modern multi-cylinder engine. **(7 marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution: ### (a) Types of Lubricating Systems (7 marks) There are **four** principal types: **1. Splash Lubrication System** - **Construction:** Oil stored in sump. Connecting rod big-end carries a **dipper (scoop)** that dips into oil on each rotation, splashing oil onto cylinder walls and bearings. - **Used in:** Old single-cylinder engines, small tractors, vintage motorcycles. - **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, no pump required. - **Disadvantages:** No pressure control; inadequate for high-speed engines; excessive oil churning causes foaming and aeration. - **Sketch:** Big-end with dipper dipping into sump. **2. Pressure (Forced-Feed) Lubrication System** - **Construction:** Oil pump (gear/rotor type) draws oil from sump ŌåÆ filter ŌåÆ cooler ŌåÆ galleries ŌåÆ all bearings under pressure (3ŌĆō5 bar). - **Used in:** Trucks, heavy-duty engines, racing applications. - **Advantages:** Precise oil delivery; suitable for high speeds; works in inclined positions; reliable lubrication. - **Disadvantages:** Costly; if pump fails, catastrophic engine damage; complex plumbing. - **Sketch:** Pump ŌåÆ Filter ŌåÆ Cooler ŌåÆ Main gallery ŌåÆ Bearings. **3. Combination (Splash + Pressure) System ŌĆö "Mist Lubrication"** - **Construction:** Oil pump supplies oil under pressure to **main bearings and camshaft**. Connecting rod bearings are pressure-fed, and **excess oil** is allowed to escape through clearances, which is then **splashed** onto cylinder walls and piston pins. - **Used in:** **Most modern passenger cars** (Maruti, Honda, Toyota engines). - **Advantages:** Best of both ŌĆö controlled pressure + effective cylinder wall lubrication; oil quantity can be reduced; sump can be smaller (semi-wet or dry sump variants). - **Disadvantages:** Slightly complex; requires careful design of splash jets. - **Sketch:** Pressure line to crank + splash to cylinders. **4. Petroil (Premix) Lubrication System** - **Construction:** Oil is **mixed with fuel** in a 2ŌĆō3% ratio (e.g., 25 mL oil per 1 L petrol) and supplied via carburettor. - **Used in:** Small 2-stroke engines ŌĆö mopeds, chainsaws, lawn mowers, outboard motors. - **Advantages:** Extremely simple; no separate oil system; light weight. - **Disadvantages:** Combustion by-products contaminate oil; high oil consumption; smoky exhaust; not suitable for 4-stroke. > **Mark Split (7):** 2 types ├Ś 1.5 marks each = 3 + 0.5 each advantages/disadvantages = 4. Add neat sketch = 3 extra. --- ### (b) Requirements of Lubrication System + Forced-Feed Diagram (7 marks) **Requirements (4 marks):** (Refer to the table in Section 1.2) The KTU-mandated 8 requirements are: 1. Adequate and continuous supply of oil to all moving parts. 2. Correct and regulated oil pressure (with relief valve at 4.5 bar). 3. Efficient filtration (10ŌĆō25 ┬Ąm). 4. Cooling of oil (oil cooler maintains 80ŌĆō110┬░C). 5. Viscosity stability across temperature range. 6. Corrosion resistance and chemical stability. 7. No foaming and aeration. 8. Foolproof operation (warning light at low pressure < 0.5 bar). **Forced-Feed (Pressure) Lubrication System ŌĆö Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` OIL SUMP | STRAINER | OIL PUMP (Gear/Rotor) | PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE | OIL FILTER (Full Flow) | COOLER BYPASS VALVE | +-------+-------+-------+--------+ | | | | | MAIN CAM VALVE TIMING PISTON BRG BRG TRAIN CHAIN COOLING | | | | | +-------+-------+-------+--------+ | RETURN TO SUMP ``` **Working:** Oil pump draws oil through strainer ŌåÆ pressure relief valve protects against overpressure ŌåÆ full-flow filter removes contaminants ŌåÆ cooler bypass routes oil to cooler if temperature exceeds 90┬░C ŌåÆ oil enters main oil gallery ŌåÆ distributed to all bearing points ŌåÆ excess oil drains back to sump. --- ### ­¤¤ó QUESTION B ŌĆö (14 Marks) [Alternative Option] **(a)** What is **antifreeze**? Explain its **properties and composition**. State why a **50:50 EG-water mixture** is recommended for tropical and cold climates. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable **sketch, explain the working of a forced-feed lubrication system** of a multi-cylinder engine. Discuss the role of **pressure relief valve, filter, and oil cooler** in detail. **(7 marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution: ### (a) Antifreeze ŌĆö Properties, Composition, and 50:50 Mix Rationale (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Antifreeze is a chemical (chiefly Ethylene Glycol) added to cooling water to lower its freezing point and raise its boiling point. **Composition (2 marks):** - **Base fluid:** Ethylene Glycol (CŌééHŌéåOŌéé) ŌĆö 47ŌĆō50% by volume - **Water:** Deionized/distilled water ŌĆö 47ŌĆō50% by volume - **Inhibitor Package (IAT ŌĆö 2ŌĆō3%):** - Silicates (aluminum protection) - Nitrates/Nitrites (cavitation inhibitor) - Phosphates (iron protection) - Tolyltriazole (copper protection) - Borax (pH buffer) **Properties (2 marks):** 1. Freezing point: Ōēż ŌłÆ34┬░C (50:50 mix) 2. Boiling point: Ōēź 106┬░C (at 1 atm); Ōēź 125┬░C with 1 bar pressurized cap 3. Specific heat: ~3.6 kJ/kg┬ĘK 4. Density: ~1070 kg/m┬│ 5. pH: 8.0ŌĆō10.5 6. Reserve Alkalinity: Ōēź 5 mL (0.1N HCl) 7. Low foaming tendency 8. Compatible with rubber hoses and aluminum **Why 50:50 EG-Water Mix? (2 marks):** - **Tropical (India, Middle East):** Boiling point rises to **106┬░C** (vs 100┬░C pure water), preventing boil-over in summer. Cooling capacity remains high (specific heat 3.6 kJ/kg┬ĘK vs 2.4 of pure EG). - **Cold Climates (Himalayas, Europe):** Freezing point drops to **ŌłÆ37┬░C**, preventing coolant solidification. - **Balanced viscosity** ŌĆö pumpable at cold start, adequate film strength at 100┬░C+ operation. - **Optimal corrosion protection** ŌĆö inhibitors are most effective at this dilution. - The mix is near the **eutectic range**, providing the **broadest operating window** of any concentration. > **Mark Distribution (7):** 1 def + 2 composition + 2 properties + 2 reasoning = 7. --- ### (b) Forced-Feed Lubrication System ŌĆö Detailed Explanation (7 marks) **Working Principle (2 marks):** Oil is drawn from sump, pressurized by pump, filtered, cooled (if required), and distributed through drilled passages to all bearings and moving parts. **Components and Their Role (5 marks):** **1. Oil Sump/Pan (0.5 mark):** - Reservoir for oil (3ŌĆō5 L capacity). - Sump plug with magnet to capture ferrous wear particles. - Baffled to prevent oil surge during cornering. **2. Oil Pump (0.5 mark):** - Usually **gear-type** (external/internal) or **rotor-type** (Gerotor). - Driven by camshaft via gear/distributor shaft. - Flow rate: 30ŌĆō60 L/min at 3000 RPM. **3. Pressure Relief Valve (1 mark):** - **Spring-loaded ball/piston valve** in the pump body or filter housing. - Opens at **4.0ŌĆō4.5 bar**, dumps excess oil back to sump. - Protects pump, lines, and filter from rupture. - Maintains constant gallery pressure regardless of engine RPM. **4. Oil Filter (1.5 marks):** - **Full-flow type** ŌĆö all oil passes through it before reaching bearings. - Filter media: **pleated paper** with center steel tube. - **Bypass valve** ŌĆö opens at ~2.5 bar differential if filter clogs (cold start or dirty filter), allowing unfiltered oil to pass (prevents oil starvation). - **Anti-drainback valve** ŌĆö rubber flap prevents oil from draining back to sump when engine stops (faster lubrication on next start). - Filtration rating: **10ŌĆō25 microns** (Ōēł 1/4 the width of a human hair). **5. Oil Cooler (1.5 marks):** - **Engine-Oil-Cooler (EOC)** ŌĆö heat exchanger mounted in coolant expansion tank or front of radiator. - **Bypass thermostatic valve** ŌĆö at oil temp < 90┬░C, oil bypasses cooler (warms up faster); at > 90┬░C, oil flows through cooler. - Maintains oil temperature at **80ŌĆō110┬░C** for optimal viscosity. - Essential in turbocharged engines and high-performance applications. **Sketch (2 marks, included in total):** ``` SUMP --> STRAINER --> PUMP --> RELIEF --> FILTER --> COOLER BYPASS | MAIN GALLERY / | \ MAIN CAM TIMING BRG BRG CHAIN | | | +-----+------+ | SUMP (return) ``` --- ## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes ŌĆö Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Splash and Petroil Systems** ŌĆö Petroil is ONLY for 2-stroke. Splash uses a **dipper on the big-end**, not a separate oil pump. > 2. **Skipping the Eutectic Point** ŌĆö If the question says "concentration beyond which freezing point rises," the answer is the **eutectic point (68% EG)**. Missing this loses 2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the Pressure Cap's Role** ŌĆö Many students forget that the radiator cap at **0.9ŌĆō1.05 bar** raises coolant boiling point to **~125┬░C**. > 4. **No mention of Distilled Water** ŌĆö Anti-freeze mixed with hard water causes **scale formation**. Always mention **distilled/deionized water**. > 5. **Oil Filter Bypass Valve** ŌĆö A common 1-mark trap. Students forget to mention the bypass that opens at **2.5 bar ╬öP**. > 6. **Methanol vs EG** ŌĆö Do not write "EG is cheaper" ŌĆö it's actually costlier. The correct answer is **safety (high flash point) and low vapor pressure**. > 7. **SAE Grades** ŌĆö Don't confuse **W** (Winter) with **Weight**. "10W-40" means cold cranking at ŌłÆ20┬░C and hot viscosity at 100┬░C. --- ## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > Ō£ģ **Antifreeze (Coolant):** > - Composition = **EG (47ŌĆō50%) + Water (47ŌĆō50%) + Inhibitors (2ŌĆō3%)** > - Pure water: FP = 0┬░C, BP = 100┬░C ŌåÆ **Inadequate** for engines > - 50:50 Mix: FP = **ŌłÆ37┬░C**, BP = **106┬░C** (at 1 atm) > - Eutectic Point = **68% EG** (FP = ŌłÆ68┬░C, lowest) > - Pressurized cap (**0.9ŌĆō1.05 bar**) ŌåÆ BP rises to **~125┬░C** > - **Always use distilled water** to prevent scale > - Key formula: $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ (Raoult's Law) > - Inhibitor types: **Silicates, Nitrates, Phosphates, Tolyltriazole** > - pH maintained at **8.0ŌĆō10.5** (alkaline) > Ō£ģ **Lubrication System Requirements:** > - 8 Requirements (Adequate Supply, Pressure, Filtration, Cooling, Viscosity, Anti-foam, Sealing, Self-Regulation) > - Oil pressure: **3ŌĆō5 bar** (relief valve at 4.5 bar) > - Oil temperature: **80ŌĆō110┬░C** > - Filter rating: **10ŌĆō25 ┬Ąm**, Bypass at **2.5 bar ╬öP** > - Oil capacity: **3ŌĆō5 L** (typical passenger car) > - Oil pump: **Gear / Rotor / Gerotor** type > Ō£ģ **Types of Lubrication Systems:** > - **Splash** ŌĆö Dipper on big-end; obsolete; tractors/vintage bikes > - **Pressure (Forced-Feed)** ŌĆö Pump-driven; trucks, racing > - **Combination (Mist)** ŌĆö Pressure to crank + splash to cylinders; **most modern cars** > - **Petroil** ŌĆö 2-stroke only; oil mixed in fuel (2ŌĆō3%) > Ō£ģ **Lubrication Regimes (Stribeck):** > - **Boundary** ($H < 10^{-7}$): Cold start, asperity contact > - **Mixed** ($10^{-7} \leq H \leq 10^{-4}$): Transition regime > - **Hydrodynamic** ($H > 10^{-4}$): Full film, no wear ŌĆö desired state > Ō£ģ **SAE Viscosity Grading (J300):** > - **W grades** (0W, 5W, 10W, 15W, 20W) ŌåÆ Winter, cold cranking > - **Hot grades** (20, 30, 40, 50, 60) ŌåÆ 100┬░C viscosity > - **Multigrade** (e.g., 10W-40) ŌåÆ year-round use > Ō£ģ **Functions of Lubricating Oil (FLWSC):** > **F**riction Ōåō, **L**ubrication, **W**ashing, **S**ealing, **C**ooling > Ō£ģ **Properties of Lubricating Oil:** > - Viscosity (cSt at 40┬░C and 100┬░C) > - Viscosity Index (VI) Ōēź 95 for multigrade > - Flash Point Ōēź 200┬░C > - Pour Point Ōēż ŌłÆ30┬░C > - TBN (Total Base Number) = 6ŌĆō10 mg KOH/g > - Foaming Tendency Ōēż 50 mL/10 min > **­¤öü Cross-Reference: Always link these topics to Module 1 (Engine Cycles) and Module 2 (Fuel System) for KTU 2024's outcome-based integration questions.**

topics.title: Mist
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMARTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mist

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MIST LUBRICATION SYSTEM (MLS) ŌĆö KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A lubrication arrangement, predominantly employed in small-capacity **two-stroke spark-ignition (SI) engines**, in which the lubricating oil is pre-mixed with the fuel in a predetermined ratio. The resulting fuelŌĆōoil blend is inducted into the **crankcase**, where volatile fractions vaporize, generating a heterogeneous aerosol (an oilŌĆōfuel *mist*) that contacts and lubricates critical tribo-pairs (cylinder wall, piston skirt, crank-pin, main bearings, and wrist-pin) before the charge is compressed and transferred to the combustion chamber.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Mist"?** The name is derived from the physical state of the lubricant as it enters the engine ŌĆö it is no longer a bulk liquid pool, but a **finely atomized spray (aerosol/mist)** suspended in fuel vapor, capable of reaching conventionally inaccessible components (e.g., the underside of the piston crown).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **spraying a fine perfume mist on a mirror** versus **pouring water on it**.
- Pouring water = traditional wet-sump lubrication in 4-stroke engines (bulk oil bath).
- Perfume mist = mist lubrication in 2-stroke engines (oil atoms travel everywhere as a cloud).

Just as the perfume mist reaches every corner of the glass uniformly and leaves no puddle, the oil mist in a 2-stroke coats the cylinder, piston, and bearings in a thin, evenly-distributed film ŌĆö even though the engine has no oil pan, oil pump, or sump.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Petrol : Oil mixing ratio** | **20 : 1** (old 2-stroke) to **50 : 1** (modern 2-stroke) |
| **Density of engine oil (SAE 40)** | **Ōēł 0.88 g/cm┬│ at 15 ┬░C** |
| **Density of petrol** | **Ōēł 0.74 g/cm┬│ at 15 ┬░C** |
| **Crankcase compression ratio (2-stroke)** | **6 : 1 to 8 : 1** (typical) |
| **Oil droplet size in mist** | **5 ┬Ąm ŌĆō 50 ┬Ąm** (atomized) |
| **Engine speed range (small 2-S) ** | **3000 ŌĆō 9000 rpm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step

The mist lubrication cycle is governed by **crankcase compression**, which differentiates it fundamentally from a wet-sump loop. The logic chain is as follows:

1. **Metering Stage** ŌĆö Lubricant is blended with fuel either:
   - **Manually (pre-mix)** in the fuel tank, *or*
   - **Automatically** via an *oil injection pump* (e.g., Yamaha Autolube, Honda Separate Lubrication) that injects oil into the intake manifold.
2. **Induction Stage** ŌĆö The fuelŌĆōoil blend enters the **sealed crankcase** as the piston moves up during the **scavenging/exhaust stroke**.
3. **Atomization Stage** ŌĆö Volatile petrol fractions vaporize; the residual oil droplets remain suspended, forming the **aerosol mist**.
4. **Migration Stage** ŌĆö The mist contacts hot engine components:
   - **Cylinder walls** ŌåÆ film lubrication.
   - **Crankshaft & main bearings** ŌåÆ impingement lubrication.
   - **Wrist-pin & small-end** ŌåÆ splash from crankcase vortex.
5. **Combustion Co-stage** ŌĆö The oil burns along with fuel, producing a characteristic **blue exhaust smoke** ŌĆö an inherent signature of 2-stroke engines.
6. **Top-up Stage** ŌĆö The process is cyclic and continuous, since the entire oil charge is consumed in the combustion process.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the System

| Why Mist Lubrication? | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| No sump ŌåÆ no oil pump | Crankcase is used as the **compression chamber** (no space for a sump). |
| Lighter engine | 2-stroke engines are 2├Ś more powerful per kg; mist enables compactness. |
| No oil change required | Oil is burnt, not circulated ŌåÆ zero oil-change intervals. |
| Lower manufacturing cost | Absence of oil pump, filter, sump, galleries. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET ŌĆö MEMORIZE THESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oil volume per litre of fuel | $V_{oil} = \dfrac{1000}{R}$ | mL / L |
| 2 | Oil-to-fuel mass ratio | $m_{oil} = V_{oil} \cdot \rho_{oil}$ | grams / litre |
| 3 | Oil consumption rate | $\dot{m}_{oil} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{fuel}}{R}$ | L / h |
| 4 | % oil in mixture (by volume) | $\%_{oil} = \dfrac{1}{R+1} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Crankcase compression pressure | $P_{cc} = P_{atm} \cdot r_c^{n}$ | bar |
| 6 | Mist droplet settling velocity (Stokes) | $v_s = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_{oil}-\rho_{air}) \cdot g}{18\mu}$ | m/s |
| 7 | Specific fuel consumption (oil) | $sfc_{oil} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{oil}}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh |

Where $R$ = mixing ratio (fuel:oil), $r_c$ = crankcase compression ratio, $n$ = polytropic index.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Small mobility**: Scooters (Honda Activa 2-stroke legacy), mopeds, motorcycles (older Rajdoot, Yezdi).
- **Power tools**: Chainsaws (Stihl MS 250), trimmers, leaf blowers.
- **Marine outboards**: Mercury, Yamaha outboard 2-stroke models.
- **Aeromodelling & UAVs**: Glow engines (fuel-oil-nitromethane mixtures).
- **Lawn & garden**: Lawnmowers, hedge trimmers.

The principle also forms the conceptual basis for modern **direct-injection stratified-charge lubrication** research in 4-stroke motorcycles aimed at reducing frictional losses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö Oil Volume Required per Litre of Fuel

> **Problem Statement:** A 2-stroke engine is rated for a fuel-to-oil mixing ratio of $R = 25:1$. Determine (a) the volume of oil required per litre of petrol, and (b) the percentage of oil in the total mixture by volume.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the ratio definition.**

By definition of the mixing ratio $R$:

$$
\text{Fuel : Oil} = R : 1
$$

This means for every $R$ parts of fuel, $1$ part of oil is added.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express oil per unit fuel volume.**

If the total mixture volume is normalised to 1 litre, then:

$$
V_{fuel} + V_{oil} = 1000 \text{ mL}
$$

From the ratio $V_{fuel} / V_{oil} = R$:

$$
V_{fuel} = R \cdot V_{oil}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $V_{oil}$.**

Substituting into the total volume equation:

$$
R \cdot V_{oil} + V_{oil} = 1000
$$

$$
V_{oil} (R + 1) = 1000
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{oil} = \dfrac{1000}{R+1} \text{ mL per litre of mixture}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the oil per litre of *pure fuel*.**

Per litre of fuel (1000 mL), oil required is:

$$
\boxed{V_{oil/fuel} = \dfrac{1000}{R} \text{ mL}}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate for $R = 25$.**

$$
V_{oil/fuel} = \dfrac{1000}{25} = 40 \text{ mL per litre of petrol}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Percentage of oil in the mixture.**

$$
\%_{oil} = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{V_{fuel} + V_{oil}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{R+1} \times 100
$$

$$
\%_{oil} = \dfrac{1}{26} \times 100 \approx 3.85\%
$$

## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö Hourly Oil Consumption

> **Problem Statement:** A 2-stroke scooter engine consumes $F = 2.5$ L/h of fuel at a mixing ratio of $R = 30:1$. Find the (a) hourly oil consumption in mL, and (b) the mass flow rate of oil in g/h. (Use $\rho_{oil} = 0.88$ g/cm┬│.)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the consumption formula.**

$$
\dot{V}_{oil} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{fuel}}{R}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute values.**

$$
\dot{V}_{oil} = \dfrac{2.5 \text{ L/h}}{30} = 0.0833 \text{ L/h}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to mL/h.**

$$
\dot{V}_{oil} = 83.33 \text{ mL/h}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = \dot{V}_{oil} \cdot \rho_{oil}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 83.33 \text{ cm}^3/\text{h} \times 0.88 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{oil} = 73.33 \text{ g/h}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö Mist Droplet Terminal Velocity (Stokes' Law)

> **Derivation of the terminal settling velocity of an oil droplet in still air inside the crankcase.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Force balance on a falling droplet.**

At terminal velocity, drag force equals gravitational minus buoyancy force:

$$
F_{drag} = F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute Stokes drag ($F_D = 3\pi\mu d v$).**

$$
3\pi \mu d v_s = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_{oil} - \rho_{air}) g
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $v_s$.**

$$
v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_{oil} - \rho_{air}) g}{18 \mu}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation for $d = 20\ \mu\text{m}$.**

Given $d = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_{oil} = 880$ kg/m┬│, $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m┬│, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa┬Ęs, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓:

$$
v_s = \dfrac{(20\times 10^{-6})^2 \times (880-1.2) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
v_s = \dfrac{4\times 10^{-10} \times 878.8 \times 9.81}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_s \approx 1.06 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s} = 1.06 \text{ cm/s}}
$$

This low terminal velocity justifies why oil droplets **remain suspended as a mist** long enough to coat engine surfaces before draining.

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Sample Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixing ratio | $R$ | Fuel : Oil | 25 : 1 |
| Oil per litre of fuel | $V_{oil/fuel}$ | $1000/R$ | 40 mL |
| % oil in mixture | $\%_{oil}$ | $100/(R+1)$ | 3.85 % |
| Hourly oil volume | $\dot{V}_{oil}$ | $\dot{V}_{fuel}/R$ | 83.33 mL/h |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}_{oil}$ | $\dot{V}_{oil}\rho_{oil}$ | 73.33 g/h |
| Settling velocity | $v_s$ | $d^2\Delta\rho g/18\mu$ | 1.06 cm/s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Mist Lubrication Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional flow of a mist lubrication system, from oil metering to final combustion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Tank with Oil Reservoir or Separate Autolube Pump] --> B[Mixing Chamber and Fuel Oil Manifold]
    B --> C[Carburettor Venturi]
    C --> D[Inlet Port to Crankcase]
    D --> E[Crankcase Compression Chamber]
    E --> F[Oil Mist Formation and Aerosol]
    F --> G[Lubrication Targets Cylinder Wall Piston Skirt Crankshaft]
    G --> H[Transfer Port to Combustion Chamber]
    H --> I[Combustion of Oil and Fuel]
    I --> J[Exhaust Port with Blue Smoke Signature]
    J --> K[Fresh Charge Entry via Inlet Port Recursive Loop to D]
    G --> L[Drain Back to Crankcase Sump]
    L --> E
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-by-block functional interpretation (use this for KTU 14-mark answers):**

| Block | Engineering Function |
|---|---|
| **A** | Houses fuel; if Autolube, oil pump is servo-driven by crankshaft pulses. |
| **B** | Homogenises fuel and oil; critical for uniform droplet size. |
| **C** | Venturi draws mixture via pressure differential into the engine. |
| **D** | Reed or rotary valve admits charge; prevents reverse flow. |
| **E** | Crankcase acts as a pump; compression stroke pressurises the charge. |
| **F** | Atomisation; vapourŌĆōliquid equilibrium sets droplet size distribution. |
| **G** | Tribo-pair film formation; this is the **primary lubrication zone**. |
| **H** | Transfer port opens near BDC; charge enters combustion chamber. |
| **I** | Oil + fuel co-combust; partial oil burnout releases energy. |
| **J** | Exhaust shows bluish hue (signature of burning oil). |
| **L** | Excess oil drains back; reused for splash lubrication. |

## 4.3 Schematic ŌĆö Manual Pre-Mix vs. Automatic Oil Injection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYS1[Manual Pre Mix Method]
        A1[Fuel Tank] --> B1[Pre Measured Oil Added Manually]
        B1 --> C1[Shake to Mix]
        C1 --> D1[Carburettor Intake]
    end
    subgraph SYS2[Automatic Oil Injection Autolube]
        A2[Separate Oil Tank] --> B2[Oil Pump Driven by Crankshaft]
        B2 --> C2[Intake Manifold Injection Point]
        D2[Carburettor Fuel Only] --> C2
        C2 --> E2[Mixing in Inlet Manifold]
    end
    D1 --> ENG((2 Stroke Engine))
    E2 --> ENG
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to **compare** the two methods, always state that automatic injection **eliminates pre-mix errors**, allows **dynamic ratio adjustment with throttle position**, and prevents engine seizure if the oil tank is forgotten ŌĆö a common 2-stroke failure mode in field operations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Q: Define mist lubrication. Why is it preferred in two-stroke engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mist lubrication is a lubrication technique in which lubricating oil is pre-mixed with fuel in a defined ratio and inducted into the engine. As the volatile fuel vaporises in the crankcase, the oil forms a fine aerosol (mist) that lubricates cylinder walls, piston, and bearings. **(1 mark)**

It is preferred in two-stroke engines because the crankcase is used as a **compression chamber** and hence cannot accommodate a separate oil sump or oil pump. Mist lubrication eliminates the need for a sump, oil galleries, and pressure pump, making the engine **lighter, simpler, and lower in cost**. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Q: State any three advantages and two disadvantages of mist lubrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages (1┬Į marks):**
1. Engine is compact and lightweight due to absence of sump.
2. No oil pump or filter required ŌĆö lower manufacturing and maintenance cost.
3. Uniform lubrication of cylinder walls and piston due to aerosol distribution.

**Disadvantages (1┬Į marks):**
1. Oil is burnt and lost ŌĆö high oil consumption and visible blue exhaust smoke.
2. Carbon deposits on piston crown, spark plug, and exhaust port; risk of pre-ignition.
3. Mixing ratio must be precisely maintained; incorrect ratio causes seizure (lean) or fouling (rich).

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **confuse mist lubrication with splash lubrication**. They are fundamentally different: mist uses **fuelŌĆōoil pre-mix + crankcase atomisation**, while splash uses a **dedicated oil dipper on the crankshaft** striking an oil bath. Mixing these in your answer is a guaranteed 3ŌĆō4 mark deduction.
> - Another common error: **forgetting to state the role of crankcase compression**. Without it, mist formation is impossible ŌĆö examiners look for this phrase specifically.
> - Always **write the numerical ratio example** (e.g., 25:1 ŌåÆ 40 mL/L). Examiners award a full mark for a worked example.
> - In diagrams, mark **all ports (intake, transfer, exhaust)**. A diagram showing only the crankcase without ports invites 2-mark penalty.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **mist lubrication system** used in a two-stroke petrol engine. Discuss its working principle and clearly differentiate it from the splash lubrication method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-stroke scooter engine uses a **fuelŌĆōoil ratio of 32:1**. If the fuel consumption is **3.2 litres per hour**, calculate:
- (i) The volume of oil required per litre of petrol.
- (ii) The hourly oil consumption in mL.
- (iii) The percentage of oil in the mixture.
- (iv) The mass flow rate of oil in g/h, given oil density = **0.88 g/cm┬│**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

Mist lubrication in a two-stroke engine operates on the principle of **crankcase compression and aerosol formation**. The lubrication oil is pre-mixed with petrol either manually in the fuel tank or automatically via an *oil injection pump* (e.g., Yamaha Autolube) that injects oil into the intake manifold. The blended mixture enters the crankcase through the inlet port during the upward stroke of the piston. As the piston compresses the charge, volatile petrol vaporises and the residual oil droplets remain suspended, forming a fine **aerosol (mist)**. **(2 marks)**

This mist migrates to and deposits on critical components ŌĆö the **cylinder wall, piston skirt, crankpin, main bearings, and wrist pin** ŌĆö providing a thin, uniform lubricating film. As the piston descends, the pressurised charge transfers to the combustion chamber via the transfer port, where it ignites. The oil, being heavier, partially burns and partially exhausts. **(2 marks)**

**Differentiation from splash lubrication (3 marks):**

| Feature | Mist Lubrication | Splash Lubrication |
|---|---|---|
| Oil storage | Mixed with fuel | Separate oil sump in crankcase |
| Delivery mechanism | Aerosol/mist cloud | Dipper on big-end splashes oil |
| Typical engine | Two-stroke SI | Four-stroke SI / CI |
| Oil consumption | High (oil burnt) | Low (oil recirculated) |
| Lubricant recirculation | None (consumed) | Continuous loop |

**Neat sketch** (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram for the flow; the student is expected to draw a labelled 2-stroke engine cross-section showing fuel-oil entry, crankcase mist, transfer port, and cylinder).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R = 32$, $\dot{V}_{fuel} = 3.2$ L/h, $\rho_{oil} = 0.88$ g/cm┬│

**(i) Oil volume per litre of petrol:**

$$
V_{oil/fuel} = \dfrac{1000}{R} = \dfrac{1000}{32} = 31.25 \text{ mL}
$$

**(1┬Į marks)**

**(ii) Hourly oil consumption:**

$$
\dot{V}_{oil} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{fuel}}{R} = \dfrac{3.2}{32} = 0.1 \text{ L/h} = 100 \text{ mL/h}
$$

**(1┬Į marks)**

**(iii) Percentage of oil in mixture:**

$$
\%_{oil} = \dfrac{1}{R+1} \times 100 = \dfrac{100}{33} \approx 3.03\%
$$

**(1┬Į marks)**

**(iv) Mass flow rate of oil:**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = \dot{V}_{oil} \cdot \rho_{oil} = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{h} \times 0.88 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 88 \text{ g/h}
$$

**(2 marks ŌĆö including unit consistency and final statement)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Alternative Module Choice]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the working of an **automatic oil injection system (Autolube type)** used in modern two-stroke engines. List its **advantages over manual pre-mix lubrication**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **terminal settling velocity of an oil droplet in crankcase air** using Stokes' law. A droplet of diameter **30 ┬Ąm** is suspended in crankcase air at **60 ┬░C**. Given: $\rho_{oil} = 870$ kg/m┬│, $\rho_{air} = 1.06$ kg/m┬│, dynamic viscosity of air $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa┬Ęs. Compute the terminal velocity and comment on whether such droplets will remain suspended long enough to form a stable mist. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Autolube System Description (7 marks):**

The Autolube (or oil-injection) system decouples the oil and fuel reservoirs. Fuel is stored in the conventional fuel tank, while lubricating oil is stored in a **separate small oil tank**. A **mechanically driven oil pump**, geared to the crankshaft, delivers a precisely metered quantity of oil into the **intake manifold** at a point just downstream of the carburettor. The pump discharge is proportional to engine RPM, ensuring that more oil is injected at higher speeds. **(3 marks ŌĆö working)**

**Labelled diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 (SYS2 branch). The student should sketch: oil tank ŌåÆ oil pump (cable/BT-driven) ŌåÆ injection nozzle ŌåÆ intake manifold ŌåÆ carburettor.

**Advantages over manual pre-mix (4 marks):**
1. **No pre-mixing error** ŌĆö eliminates the human error of measuring oil in the tank.
2. **Dynamic ratio** ŌĆö oil quantity varies with RPM and load, optimizing lubrication efficiency.
3. **No risk of fuel-oil separation** during long storage (pre-mix can stratify).
4. **Lower visible smoke** at part throttle since rich oil mixture is avoided at low loads.
5. **Engine seizure prevention** ŌĆö if oil tank runs dry, engine stops (safety interlock in some designs).

---

**(b) Terminal Velocity Derivation & Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

At terminal velocity $v_s$, the droplet is in dynamic equilibrium under three forces: gravity (down), buoyancy (up), and viscous drag (up).

Force balance:

$$
F_g = F_b + F_D
$$

Substituting standard expressions:

$$
\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_{oil} g = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_{air} g + 3 \pi \mu d v_s
$$

Rearranging:

$$
3 \pi \mu d v_s = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_{oil} - \rho_{air}) g
$$

$$
\boxed{v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_{oil} - \rho_{air}) g}{18 \mu}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $d = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_{oil} = 870$ kg/m┬│, $\rho_{air} = 1.06$ kg/m┬│, $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa┬Ęs, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓.

$$
v_s = \dfrac{(30\times 10^{-6})^2 \times (870-1.06) \times 9.81}{18 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
v_s = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-10} \times 868.94 \times 9.81}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
v_s = \dfrac{7.67 \times 10^{-6}}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
v_s \approx 0.0213 \text{ m/s} = 2.13 \text{ cm/s}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**

Since the terminal velocity is only **~2 cm/s**, and the crankcase internal dimensions are typically of the order of **10ŌĆō20 cm**, the droplet takes several seconds to settle. In an engine running at **3000+ rpm**, the time between induction and transfer is only a few milliseconds, so droplets **remain suspended long enough to form a stable aerosol mist** and effectively coat engine surfaces.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Mist Lubrication (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Oil pre-mixed with fuel ŌåÆ atomised in crankcase ŌåÆ lubricates as aerosol.
- **Used in:** Two-stroke SI engines (scooters, chainsaws, marine outboards, glow engines).
- **Why mist?** Crankcase compression rules out a sump; mist is the only practical solution.
- **Mixing ratio range:** 20:1 (old) ŌåÆ 50:1 (modern synthetic 2-stroke oils).
