- **Volume formula:** $V_{oil/fuel} = 1000/R$ mL per litre of fuel.
- **% oil formula:** $\%_{oil} = 100/(R+1)$.
- **Hourly oil use:** $\dot{V}_{oil} = \dot{V}_{fuel}/R$.
- **Autolube system:** Mechanical oil pump, RPM-servoed, separate oil tank.
- **Pre-mix system:** Manual mixing in fuel tank; simple but error-prone.
- **Signature exhaust:** Bluish smoke ŌĆö caused by burnt oil.
- **Failure modes:** Lean mix ŌåÆ seizure; rich mix ŌåÆ carbon deposits & plug fouling.
- **Stokes settling velocity:** $v_s = d^2 \Delta\rho g / 18\mu$ ŌåÆ low for 5ŌĆō50 ┬Ąm droplets ŌåÆ stable mist.
- **Key contrast:** Mist ŌēĀ Splash. Mist is aerosol-based; splash is dipper-and-sump-based.
- **KTU must-words:** *Crankcase compression*, *aerosol*, *Autolube*, *blue smoke*, *ratio*.
- **Modern trend:** Migrating to 4-stroke for emission compliance; mist lubrication being phased out in road vehicles but retained in handheld tools and marine applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Splash
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Splash

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Splash Lubrication System:** A type of forced-feed (or partially gravity-fed) engine lubrication system in which the lubricating oil, stored in the **crankcase oil sump**, is picked up by a **dipper**, **scoop**, or the **big-end of the connecting rod** attached to the rotating crankshaft, and is flung centrifugally onto the engine components (cylinder walls, piston pins, camshaft, bearings) that require lubrication.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance for the *Automotive Systems* course, "Splash" falls under the **Engine Auxiliary Systems ŌĆō Lubrication** sub-module and is contrasted with the **Pressure (Forced-Feed) Lubrication System**. It is the **simplest, lowest-cost, and most historically primitive** form of internal-combustion-engine lubrication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Rain Maker Inside Your Engine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a child running on a wet playground with a stick dipped into a puddle.**
> Every time the stick (your *connecting-rod dipper*) dips into the puddle (your *oil sump*) and swings up, droplets of water (your *lubricating oil*) fly in every direction ŌĆö onto nearby objects (your *cylinder walls, piston, bearings*). The faster the child runs (your *crankshaft RPM*), the further the droplets travel.

This is precisely how splash lubrication works ŌĆö a **mechanical rotating member acts as the "oil pump"**, using centrifugal force instead of a positive-displacement pump.

## 1.3 Where is Splash Lubrication Used?

| Application Sector | Typical Engine | Why Splash? |
|---|---|---|
| Two-wheelers (motorcycles) | Single-cylinder 100ŌĆō250 cc | Compact, lightweight, low cost |
| Small portable engines | Lawn mowers, gensets | No pump failure risk |
| Scooters / Mopeds | 2-stroke & 4-stroke small cc | Compact crankcase design |
| Vintage cars | Pre-1940s engines | Period-correct technology |
| Air-cooled engines | Small industrial engines | Matches low oil demand |

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants / Reference Values

- **Engine Oil Viscosity Grade (Common):** SAE 20W-40, SAE 10W-30 (multigrade)
- **Kinematic Viscosity Range:** $\nu = 9.3 \text{ to } 16.3 \text{ cSt at } 100^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Operating Oil Sump Temperature:** **80 ┬░C ŌĆō 110 ┬░C** (steady state)
- **Specific Gravity of Engine Oil:** $S_o \approx 0.88 \text{ to } 0.92$ (at 15 ┬░C)
- **Recommended Splash Oil Capacity (small single-cylinder engine):** **0.6 L ŌĆō 1.5 L**
- **Minimum Splash Crankshaft Speed:** typically **Ōēź 600 RPM** to generate sufficient oil throw.

## 1.5 Classification of Splash Systems (KTU Nomenclature)

1. **Pure Splash System** ŌĆö No oil pump at all; rod dipper does all the work.
2. **Splash + Pressure (Combined) System** ŌĆö A small gear pump feeds the crankshaft/main bearings under pressure, *while* the rod dipper continues to splash oil onto the upper cylinder region.
3. **Scoop-and-Dipper Type** ŌĆö A stationary scoop (on the crankcase wall) catches oil at high RPM and routes it through an internal drilling to the camshaft / valve gear.
4. **Crankshaft Throws Type** ŌĆö Counterweights on the crank act as slingers to throw oil outwards onto the cylinder walls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Oil Storage:** Lubricating oil resides in the **crankcase sump** at the bottom of the engine block.
2. **Pick-Up:** As the **crankshaft rotates**, the **big-end of the connecting rod** (or a separate **dipper attached to the big-end cap**) dips into the oil pool.
3. **Centrifugal Acceleration:** The rotating mass of oil is flung outward by centrifugal force, $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$.
4. **Atomisation:** The oil sheet breaks up into a **fine mist / spray** that coats nearby surfaces.
5. **Coating of Surfaces:** The cylinder liner, piston skirt, gudgeon pin, and (in some layouts) the camshaft lobes are continuously bathed in oil.
6. **Gravity Drainage:** Used oil drains back down into the sump ŌåÆ circulation cycle repeats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the oil reach the cylinder walls and not just fly straight out?**
> The crankcase interior is enclosed; oil droplets collide with the cylinder walls, piston underside, and the crankcase walls (which are often **ribbed or shaped** to redirect the spray upward).

## 2.2 Component-wise Role

| Component | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| Oil Sump (Pan) | Reservoir ŌĆö holds 0.6 L to 1.5 L of oil |
| Dipper / Scoop | Attached to connecting-rod big-end cap; dips into oil |
| Crankcase | Enclosed chamber to contain and direct oil spray |
| Cylinder Liner | Receives splash for piston-ring lubrication |
| Piston & Rings | Distribute oil film; scrap excess back to sump |
| Breather / PCV | Vents oil-mist-laden crankcase gases (prevents pressurisation) |
| Oil Sight Glass / Dipstick | Level verification during maintenance |

## 2.3 Advantages & Limitations (KTU Favourite Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Splash System | Pressure System |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | **Very Low** | High (pump + galleries) |
| Weight | **Light** | Heavier |
| Reliability | **High** (no pump) | Depends on pump health |
| Oil Pressure at Bearings | Near atmospheric | 2 ŌĆō 5 bar typical |
| Suitability for High RPM | **Poor** (oil starvation risk) | Excellent |
| Heat Dissipation Capacity | Moderate | High (cooled in sump + cooler) |
| Application | Small low-RPM engines | All modern multi-cylinder automotive engines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Splash lubrication is **centrifugal**; hence its effectiveness rises with $\omega^2$. Above ~4000 RPM, the spray pattern becomes excessive and oil may be lost through the breather. Below ~400 RPM, oil is not thrown high enough, leading to **upper-cylinder lubrication failure**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Centrifugal force on oil droplet | N |
| 2 | $v_{tip} = \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | Tangential velocity of dipper tip | m/s |
| 3 | $h_{splash} \approx \dfrac{v_{tip}^{\,2}}{2g}$ | Theoretical maximum splash height (free-flight) | m |
| 4 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,h}{r^2}}$ | Minimum crankshaft RPM for splash height $h$ | RPM |
| 5 | $P_{oil} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000}$ | Pumping power (only for combined systems) | kW |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p,\,oil} \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removed by oil per second | W |
| 7 | $H_{total} = \mu V + \rho g z + p$ | Bernoulli head (oil flow in galleries) | m |
| 8 | $T_{oil} = T_{ambient} + \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{friction}}{\dot{m}_{oil}\,c_{p}}$ | Steady-state oil temperature | ┬░C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** When writing the centrifugal force formula, $r$ is the **dipper-tip radius** (NOT the crank radius), and $m$ is the **effective mass of oil clinging to the dipper at the instant of release**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Two-wheeler OEMs (Bajaj, Hero, Honda, TVS)** still use splash + pressure hybrid systems in entry-level commuter motorcycles.
- **Small gensets (Honda GX, Briggs & Stratton)** use pure splash because they rarely exceed 3600 RPM.
- **Vintage automobile restoration**: Maintaining a vintage flathead Ford V8 requires understanding splash geometry because there are **no oil galleries** in the block.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Crankshaft Speed for Adequate Splash Height

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the relationship between crankshaft RPM ($N$) and the maximum height $h$ that an oil droplet, released from the dipper tip, can reach on the cylinder wall.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Relate dipper-tip radius to crank geometry

Let the crank throw radius (half of stroke) be $r$ metres. The dipper is mounted on the connecting-rod big-end; for simplicity, we treat the dipper-tip radius as equal to the crank throw radius $r$ (this is the standard textbook assumption for KTU-level problems).

$$r_{dipper} = r$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Tangential velocity of the dipper tip

When the crankshaft rotates at $N$ RPM, its angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

The linear (tangential) velocity at the dipper tip is therefore:

$$v_{tip} = \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{2\pi N r}{60} = \dfrac{\pi N r}{30} \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Convert kinetic energy into potential energy (energy balance)

At the instant the oil droplet leaves the dipper, all of its kinetic energy is converted into potential energy at the peak of its trajectory (assuming zero air drag ŌĆö a standard textbook idealisation for KTU problems).

$$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{tip}^{\,2} = m g h_{max}$$

Dividing both sides by $m$ (the mass cancels ŌĆö a critical observation in this derivation):

$$\dfrac{v_{tip}^{\,2}}{2} = g h_{max}$$

Solving for $h_{max}$:

$$h_{max} = \dfrac{v_{tip}^{\,2}}{2g}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $v_{tip}$ in terms of $N$

$$h_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2g}\left(\dfrac{\pi N r}{30}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} N^{2} r^{2}}{1800\,g}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Invert to find required $N$ for a given desired splash height $h$

Re-arranging the previous equation for $N$:

$$N^{2} = \dfrac{1800\,g\,h}{\pi^{2} r^{2}}$$

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{30}{\pi r}\sqrt{2 g h} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi r}\sqrt{2 g h}$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{min} = \dfrac{30}{\pi r}\sqrt{2 g h} \quad [\text{RPM}]\,}$$

### Step 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has a stroke of $L = 70$ mm. Determine the minimum crankshaft speed required for the splash from the connecting-rod dipper to reach a height of **80 mm** on the cylinder wall. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.

**Given:**
- Stroke $L = 70$ mm $= 0.070$ m
- Desired splash height $h = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m
- Crank throw radius $r = L/2 = 0.035$ m
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$

**Solution:**

Using the boxed formula:

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{30}{\pi \cdot 0.035}\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080}$$

Compute the term inside the square root:

$$2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080 = 1.5696$$

$$\sqrt{1.5696} = 1.2529$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\pi \times 0.035 = 0.10996$$

Compute the quotient:

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{30 \times 1.2529}{0.10996} = \dfrac{37.586}{0.10996}$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{min} \approx 341.8 \text{ RPM}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Below ~342 RPM, the splash oil will not reach the upper cylinder region ŌĆö meaning at idling speeds typical of small engines (~700ŌĆō900 RPM), splash is safely achieved with margin.

### Step 3.3 Derivation: Oil Mass Circulation Rate (Theoretical)

Let $n_d$ be the number of dip-pickup events per revolution (typically $n_d = 1$ for single-cylinder). The mass of oil carried per dip depends on dipper geometry and oil viscosity; we denote it $m_{dip}$ (kg).

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = \dfrac{n_d \cdot m_{dip} \cdot N}{60} \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

This is the **theoretical oil-feed rate**. In practice, only a fraction $\eta_s \approx 0.4 \text{ ŌĆō } 0.6$ of this oil effectively reaches the surfaces (the rest falls back directly into the sump).

$$\dot{m}_{useful} = \eta_s \cdot \dfrac{m_{dip} \cdot N}{60}$$

### Step 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö "When to Use Splash?"

| Engine Parameter | Favourable for Splash | Unfavourable for Splash |
|---|---|---|
| Crankshaft Speed | < 3000 RPM | > 4000 RPM |
| Cylinder Count | Single cylinder | 4 or more cylinders |
| Specific Output | < 25 kW/L | > 50 kW/L |
| Bearing Load | Low-to-moderate | High (turbo, diesel) |
| Cooling Demand | < 8 kW total | > 15 kW total |
| Oil Capacity | 0.5 ŌĆō 2.0 L | > 4 L (splash geometry fails) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Splash Lubrication System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of oil through a splash lubrication system, showing how mechanical rotation translates into lubrication of various engine subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Crankcase Oil Sump - Oil Reservoir 0.6 to 1.5 L]
    nodeB[Crankshaft - Rotating Prime Mover]
    nodeC[Connecting Rod Big End with Integrated Dipper]
    nodeD[Centrifugal Throw Zone - Radius r, Speed omega]
    nodeE[Oil Mist Cloud - Atomised Lubricant]
    nodeF1[Cylinder Liner Wall - Piston Ring Lubrication]
    nodeF2[Gudgeon Pin / Piston Pin Bushing]
    nodeF3[Camshaft Lobes and Tappets - Upper Lubrication]
    nodeF4[Timing Chain and Sprockets]
    nodeG[Crankcase Inner Walls - Ribbed Guide Surfaces]
    nodeH[Gravity Drain Back to Sump]
    nodeI[PCV Breather - Vents Oil Mist to Inlet]
    nodeJ[Oil Level Dipstick / Sight Glass]
    nodeK[Drain Plug - Service Access]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF1
    nodeE --> nodeF2
    nodeE --> nodeF3
    nodeE --> nodeF4
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF1 --> nodeH
    nodeF2 --> nodeH
    nodeF3 --> nodeH
    nodeF4 --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeA --> nodeJ
    nodeA --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram ŌĆö Oil Pick-Up and Throw Cycle (One Revolution)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Oil Sump
    participant D as Dipper on Big-End
    participant W as Cylinder Wall
    participant P as Piston Rings

    Note over D: TDC to 90 deg - Dipper in air
    D->>S: At 90 deg BTDC - Dipper enters oil pool
    S-->>D: Oil clings to dipper surface
    Note over D: 90 deg BTDC to 270 deg BTDC - Dipper carries oil upward
    D->>W: At 270 deg BTDC - Centrifugal throw, oil released
    W->>P: Oil film coats liner and piston
    P->>S: Excess oil scraped back to sump
    Note over D,S: Cycle repeats every revolution - Two throws per rev for V-engines
```

## 4.3 Component Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputPower[Input Power Source]
        nodeP1[Combustion Pressure on Piston]
    end

    subgraph MechanicalTransmission[Mechanical Transmission]
        nodeP1 --> nodeP2[Piston]
        nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Connecting Rod]
        nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Crankshaft]
    end

    subgraph SplashMechanism[Splash Lubrication Mechanism]
        nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Big-End Dipper]
        nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Centrifugal Oil Throw]
        nodeP6 --> nodeP7[Oil Spray to Upper Components]
    end

    subgraph LubricatedParts[Lubricated Components]
        nodeP7 --> nodeL1[Piston Pin]
        nodeP7 --> nodeL2[Cylinder Wall]
        nodeP7 --> nodeL3[Camshaft]
        nodeP7 --> nodeL4[Valve Gear]
    end

    subgraph OilReturn[Oil Return Path]
        nodeL2 --> nodeP8[Gravity Drainage]
        nodeL3 --> nodeP8
        nodeL4 --> nodeP8
        nodeP8 --> nodeP4
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define splash lubrication system. Mention any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Splash lubrication is a method of engine lubrication in which lubricating oil stored in the crankcase sump is picked up by a dipper or scoop fitted on the big-end of the connecting rod, and is flung by centrifugal force onto the engine components requiring lubrication.

**Limitations (1.5 marks ŌĆö any two):**
1. Inadequate lubrication of bearings at high RPM due to insufficient oil pressure.
2. Oil may be lost through the crankcase breather at very high speeds due to excessive atomisation.
3. Not suitable for multi-cylinder high-output engines.
4. Oil temperature control is poor because no oil cooler can be effectively integrated.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between splash lubrication and pressure lubrication systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Splash System | Pressure System |
|---|---|---|
| Oil Pickup Mechanism | Dipper / big-end scoop | Positive-displacement oil pump |
| Oil Pressure at Bearings | Near atmospheric (Ōēł 0 bar) | 2 ŌĆō 5 bar (or higher) |
| Suitability | Small, low-RPM engines | Modern multi-cylinder engines |
| Cost & Complexity | Low, simple | Higher, requires galleries & pump |
| Heat Removal Capacity | Limited | High (via oil cooler) |

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a splash lubrication system used in a single-cylinder petrol engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. Lubricating oil is stored in the crankcase sump at the bottom of the engine.
2. As the crankshaft rotates, the big-end of the connecting rod (or a dipper attached to it) dips into the oil at every revolution.
3. Oil carried by the dipper is thrown outwards by centrifugal force, atomising it into a fine spray.
4. This spray coats the cylinder walls, piston pin, and upper engine components.
5. The oil drains back to the sump by gravity, completing the circulation cycle.

**Sketch Description (2 marks):** *(Examiner accepts any clear schematic showing the sump, dipper on connecting-rod big-end, cylinder, piston, and arrows indicating oil throw direction.)*

**Components Mentioned (2 marks):** Oil sump, dipper, connecting rod, crankshaft, cylinder liner, breather, dipstick.

---

**(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has a crank radius of 40 mm. Determine the **theoretical splash height** achieved when the engine runs at 1800 RPM. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Crank radius $r = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
- Speed $N = 1800$ RPM
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times \pi \times 1800}{60} = 60\pi \approx 188.50 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Tangential velocity at dipper tip:**

$$v_{tip} = \omega \cdot r = 188.50 \times 0.040 = 7.540 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Theoretical splash height:**

$$h_{max} = \dfrac{v_{tip}^{\,2}}{2g} = \dfrac{(7.540)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{max} = \dfrac{56.85}{19.62} \approx 2.898 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $h_{max} \approx 2.90$ m *(theoretical ŌĆö in practice this is severely reduced by air drag and crankcase geometry; effective height is roughly 10ŌĆō20% of this value, i.e., a few centimetres, which is sufficient for cylinder lubrication.)*

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]`**

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of a splash lubrication system. List the **advantages and disadvantages** of the system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Constructional Features (4 marks):**
1. **Oil Sump:** Sheet-metal or cast-aluminium pan at the bottom of the crankcase.
2. **Dipper:** Steel blade bolted to the connecting-rod big-end cap, sized to dip into oil at BDC.
3. **Dipstick / Sight Glass:** To verify oil level.
4. **Drain Plug:** Threaded bolt at the sump's lowest point for oil change.
5. **Crankcase Breather:** Vents crankcase gases to prevent pressure build-up.
6. **Internal Ribbing:** On the crankcase walls to redirect splash upward.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Simple, low cost, lightweight, no pump to fail, suitable for small engines.

**Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
- Poor lubrication at low RPM; oil starvation possible at high RPM; oil loss via breather; not suitable for high-output multi-cylinder engines.

---

**(b)** A splash-lubricated engine runs at 2400 RPM. The crank radius is 35 mm. **Find the minimum RPM** at which the splash can still reach a height of 50 mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $h = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m
- $r = 35$ mm $= 0.035$ m
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$

**Formula:**

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{30}{\pi r}\sqrt{2 g h}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inside the square root:**

$$2 g h = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.050 = 0.981$$

$$\sqrt{0.981} = 0.9905$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Denominator:**

$$\pi r = \pi \times 0.035 = 0.10996$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $N_{min}$:**

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{30 \times 0.9905}{0.10996} = \dfrac{29.715}{0.10996} \approx 270.2 \text{ RPM}$$

**Result:** $N_{min} \approx 270$ RPM.

**Practical Note:** Since the engine runs at 2400 RPM, the actual splash height at this operating speed is **far higher** than 50 mm ŌĆö confirming that splash lubrication is **more than adequate** at this operating speed (the limiting factor is excessive atomisation at very high RPM, not insufficient reach).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Splash-Lubrication Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Always convert crank radius from **mm to m** before substituting into $v_{tip} = \omega r$. Writing $r = 40$ instead of $0.040$ inflates the answer by **a factor of 100** and loses 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing crank radius with stroke:** Stroke $L = 2r$. If the problem gives **stroke**, divide by 2 to get the crank radius before applying the splash-height formula.
>
> 3. **Wrong angular-velocity formula:** Some students write $\omega = \pi N / 60$ instead of $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. This is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the energy-balance assumption:** The formula $h_{max} = v_{tip}^{2}/(2g)$ assumes **zero air drag**. Examiners expect a brief comment that real splash heights are much smaller due to drag and crankcase wall interference.
>
> 5. **Drawing the wrong direction of oil throw:** In a sketch, oil must be thrown **upwards and outward** from the dipper to reach the cylinder wall ŌĆö not horizontally. A wrongly-directed arrow loses 1 mark in the sketch portion.
>
> 6. **Confusing splash with pressure system:** Examiners specifically test the **distinction** ŌĆö students who claim that splash provides "oil pressure to main bearings" lose marks immediately.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Splash lubrication = mechanical centrifugal pickup** ŌĆö no dedicated oil pump required in its pure form.
- Ō£ģ **Key equation to memorise:** $\boxed{h_{max} = \dfrac{v_{tip}^{\,2}}{2g}}$ and $\boxed{N_{min} = \dfrac{30}{\pi r}\sqrt{2gh}}$.
- Ō£ģ **Convert all lengths to metres** before substituting ŌĆö most common error in numericals.
- Ō£ģ **Splash height increases with $\omega^{2}$** ŌĆö i.e., quadrupling RPM doubles the theoretical splash height.
- Ō£ģ **Applications:** Single-cylinder motorcycles, scooters, gensets, small industrial engines.
- Ō£ģ **Limit:** Inadequate for high-RPM, multi-cylinder, high-output engines ŌåÆ leads to **pressure lubrication**.
- Ō£ģ **Crankcase breather** is essential in splash systems to prevent pressurisation due to oil mist.
- Ō£ģ **Effective splash height** Ōēł 10ŌĆō20% of theoretical $h_{max}$ due to air drag and crankcase geometry.
- Ō£ģ **Combined splash + pressure** is the standard arrangement in modern commuter motorcycles.
- Ō£ģ **Oil quantity** typically 0.6 ŌĆō 1.5 L for single-cylinder engines; overfilling causes oil loss through the breather.
- Ō£ģ **Examiners love comparisons:** Memorise the Splash vs Pressure table for full marks in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wet Sump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT302 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wet Sump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wet Sump Lubrication System** is a pressurized engine lubrication architecture in which the entire working charge of lubricating oil is continuously stored in a shallow reservoir ŌĆö the **oil pan (sump)** ŌĆö that is bolted directly beneath the engine crankcase and forms an integral part of the engine block assembly. A mechanically driven **oil pump** draws oil from this sump through a submerged pickup tube and strainer, pressurises it, routes it through a full-flow **oil filter**, and distributes it via internal **oil galleries** to the principal bearing surfaces (main bearings, big-end bearings, camshaft bearings, valve train, and timing chain). After performing its lubricating and cooling duty, the oil drains back into the same sump by gravity, where it is reused in a closed loop. Because the oil reservoir remains "wet" (i.e., permanently filled with oil) throughout engine operation, the architecture derives its name.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 ŌĆö Engine Auxiliary Systems):**
> The Wet Sump is the *default* lubrication topology specified for all mass-production petrol and light-duty diesel passenger vehicles. The student is expected to (a) identify every component on a sectional cut-away, (b) trace the oil circuit, and (c) justify why a wet sump is preferred over a dry sump for a given application.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Office Water Cooler"

Imagine a small office pantry that contains a **5-gallon water jar at the bottom (the sump)**. A small electric pump (the oil pump) sits beside the jar, sucks water up through a straw with a mesh strainer (pickup tube + strainer), pushes it through a tiny carbon candle filter (oil filter), and then sends it up through a network of pipes (oil galleries) to dispense coolers on every floor (bearings and valve train). After people drink, the unused water drips back down into the same jar by gravity. The jar is always full ŌĆö hence *wet*. The system is simple, compact, cheap, and perfectly adequate for a regular office ŌĆö but if you start shaking the building violently (high-G cornering in a race car) the water sloshes and the straw can gulp air, which is why racing cars switch to a *dry sump* system (a separate external tank).

## 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Standard sump oil capacity (passenger car petrol engine):** 3.5 L ŌĆō 5.5 L
- **Operating oil pressure (gallery pressure):** 2.5 bar ŌĆō 4.5 bar (Ōēł 250 kPa ŌĆō 450 kPa) at idle-to-cruise
- **Pressure relief valve cracking pressure:** 4.0 bar ŌĆō 5.5 bar
- **Oil temperature window (normal operation):** 90 ┬░C ŌĆō 110 ┬░C
- **Viscosity classification:** SAE 5W-30, 10W-40 (multi-grade) ŌĆö defined by **SAE J300**
- **API service classification:** API SP (petrol), API CK-4 (diesel)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Wet" vs "Dry" matters:** In a **wet sump** the reservoir is integral to the engine; in a **dry sump** the reservoir is a remote external tank and the crankcase is scavenged by a dedicated scavenging pump. Wet sump is the cost-effective choice; dry sump is the high-performance choice. Do not confuse the two during valuation.

## 1.4 Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 ŌĆö Module 4)

| Sub-topic in Module 4 | Coverage in these notes |
|---|---|
| Engine lubrication ŌĆö types | Ō£ō Definition + classification |
| Components of wet sump | Ō£ō Listed in Section 2.1 |
| Oil circuit / flow path | Ō£ō Section 4 (Mermaid diagram) |
| Comparison with dry sump | Ō£ō Section 5 (Question bank) |
| Design considerations & failures | Ō£ō Section 2 & 5 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Component Inventory

The wet sump is a *closed-loop, gravity-return, pressure-fed* lubrication system. Its working sub-assemblies are:

1. **Oil Pan / Sump** ŌĆö Stamped steel or aluminium reservoir bolted to the block with a one-piece gasket. Often fitted with a **windage tray / baffle plate** to prevent oil from climbing the crankshaft and foaming.
2. **Pickup Tube with Strainer** ŌĆö A rigid tube projecting into the sump, terminated with a fine mesh strainer that filters large particulates before the oil reaches the pump.
3. **Oil Pump** ŌĆö Almost universally a **gerotor (rotor) pump** or **external spur-gear pump**, driven directly off the crankshaft (1:1 ratio) or via a chain from the camshaft. Generates the pressure differential.
4. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** ŌĆö A spring-loaded ball or poppet valve that opens when gallery pressure exceeds the safe limit, dumping excess oil back to the sump. Protects the filter from bursting and bearings from over-pressure.
5. **Full-Flow Oil Filter** ŌĆö Spin-on canister containing pleated paper media. Filters particulates down to **Ōēł 25 ┬Ąm**. Bypasses internally at Ōēł 0.7 bar ╬öp if clogged.
6. **Oil Galleries** ŌĆö Cast or drilled internal passages in the block and head delivering pressurised oil to bearings, camshaft journals, hydraulic tappets, and timing chain tensioners.
7. **Dipstick / Oil Level Sensor** ŌĆö Manual or electronic measurement of sump oil level.
8. **Oil Pressure Switch / Gauge** ŌĆö A Bourdon-tube gauge or piezoresistive sender monitoring gallery pressure.
9. **Oil Cooler (optional)** ŌĆö Air-oil or water-oil heat exchanger fitted on turbocharged or heavy-duty engines.
10. **Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) System** ŌĆö Breather hose and PCV valve; routes blow-by gases back to the intake and prevents moisture build-up in the sump.

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Suction:** At engine start, the oil pump (driven by the crankshaft via gear/chain) creates a partial vacuum in the sump. Atmospheric pressure on the oil surface plus the pump's suction head pushes oil up the pickup tube.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Pressurisation:** The gerotor/gear pump traps oil between its rotors and forces it out at elevated pressure (typically 3 ŌĆō 5 bar).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Filtration:** Oil is forced through the full-flow filter. If the filter element is clean, all oil passes through it. If clogged, an internal bypass valve opens at ~0.7 bar ╬öp to maintain flow at the cost of filtration.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Distribution:** Filtered oil enters the **main oil gallery**, a longitudinal passage running the length of the block. Branch passages lead to each main bearing, then via internal drillings in the crankshaft to the connecting rod big-end bearings.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Secondary distribution:** Cross-drillings or separate galleries feed the **camshaft bearings**, **hydraulic valve lifters**, **timing chain tensioner**, and (in some engines) **piston cooling jets** that squirt oil onto the underside of the pistons.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Drainage:** After lubricating, oil falls by gravity through internal clearances and returns to the sump, often through dedicated drain-back holes in the cylinder head.
- **Step 7 ŌĆö Pressure regulation:** The PRV continuously bleeds off excess flow to maintain gallery pressure within design limits, irrespective of engine RPM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a windage tray matters:** At high RPM the spinning crankshaft whips the air-oil mixture at the sump surface, creating **aerated oil (foam)**. Foam has very low effective viscosity and can collapse the hydrodynamic film in bearings, causing wear. The windage tray breaks up the surface and prevents crankshaft churn.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical pump displacement (external gear) | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L \cdot Z$ | $D_o$ = outer gear PD (m), $D_i$ = inner gear PD (m), $L$ = gear face width (m), $Z$ = number of teeth | Sizing the pump for a required flow rate |
| 2 | Theoretical volumetric flow rate | $Q_{th} = V_d \cdot N$ | $V_d$ in m┬│/rev, $N$ in rev/s | Maximum oil delivery at a given speed |
| 3 | Actual flow rate (with volumetric efficiency) | $Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N$ | $\eta_v$ = 0.85 ŌĆō 0.95 for gerotor/gear pump | Real flow accounting for leakage |
| 4 | Pressure relief valve cracking pressure | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{4 F_s}{\pi d^2}$ | $F_s$ = spring preload (N), $d$ = valve seat dia (m) | Setting the PRV spring |
| 5 | Sump oil capacity (rule of thumb) | $C_o \approx 0.05 \cdot V_{disp}$ | $V_{disp}$ = engine swept volume (L) | Initial sizing of oil pan |
| 6 | Oil Reynolds number (bearing film) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \omega \cdot r \cdot c}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = oil density (kg/m┬│), $\omega$ = shaft rad/s, $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa┬Ęs) | Determines whether the bearing is in laminar (hydrodynamic) regime |
| 7 | Petroff's bearing friction torque | $T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$ | $N$ in rev/s, $L$ = bearing length | Friction loss in a plain bearing |
| 8 | Oil temperature rise (energy balance) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{fric}}{\dot{m} \cdot c_{p,oil}}$ | $\dot{Q}_{fric}$ = frictional heat (W), $\dot{m}$ = oil mass flow (kg/s), $c_{p,oil} \approx 1900$ J/(kg┬ĘK) | Sizing oil cooler |
| 9 | Stokes' settling velocity (contaminant) | $v_s = \dfrac{(\rho_p - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ | $d_p$ = particle dia, $\rho_p,\rho_o$ = particle & oil densities | Predicts how fast dust settles in the sump |
| 10 | SAE J300 dynamic viscosity at 100 ┬░C | $\mu_{100} \in [9.3, 12.5]$ mm┬▓/s for 30-grade | kinematic viscosity in cSt | Viscosity classification check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical rule for tables (KTU Examiner tip):** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of a bare pipe `|` whenever a mathematical expression contains an absolute value or a conditional delimiter. This prevents the markdown table parser from breaking your answer sheet.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wet sump systems dominate every mass-market passenger vehicle (Maruti, Hyundai, Tata, Toyota, Honda, etc.) because they are the *cheapest*, *lightest*, and most *compact* solution for engines producing less than ~400 N┬Ęm of torque and operating below ~6500 rpm. Beyond this envelope ŌĆö supercars, NASCAR, Formula 1, rally cars, aircraft piston engines ŌĆö designers migrate to a **dry sump** because the remote reservoir eliminates oil-starvation in sustained high-G cornering, allows the engine to be mounted lower (improving CoG), and reduces parasitic drag on the crankshaft. Hence, the wet sump is not just a textbook topic but the workhorse of the global automotive industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A ŌĆö Minimum Required Oil Pump Displacement

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 8-mark sub-part):**
A 4-cylinder petrol engine has a swept volume of 1.5 L per cylinder, runs at a maximum speed of 6000 rpm, and uses a wet sump lubrication system. The engine designer requires that **every bearing receives at least 0.5 L/min of oil** at maximum speed. There are **5 main bearings, 4 connecting rod big-end bearings, 4 camshaft bearings, and 4 piston cooling jets**. The oil pump volumetric efficiency is 90 % and it must also supply 30 % extra oil to compensate for leakage past seals and for cooling. Determine the required theoretical pump displacement in cm┬│/rev.

**Given data:**
- Bearing oil demand (each): $q_b = 0.5$ L/min = $0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ / 60 m┬│/s
- Number of bearings/jets: 5 + 4 + 4 + 4 = **17 lubrication points**
- Cooling/leakage factor: 30 % extra
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_v = 0.90$
- Engine speed: $N = 6000$ rpm = $100$ rev/s

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total required actual flow rate:**

$$
Q_{req} = n_{pts} \cdot q_b \cdot (1 + f_{leak})
$$

where $n_{pts} = 17$, $q_b = 0.5$ L/min, $f_{leak} = 0.30$.

$$
Q_{req} = 17 \times 0.5 \times 1.30
$$

$$
Q_{req} = 11.05 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to m┬│/s for SI consistency:**

$$
Q_{req} = 11.05 \times \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60} = 1.8417 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply volumetric efficiency to obtain theoretical flow:**

The actual delivered flow is $Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th}$, and we need $Q_{act} = Q_{req}$ (the pump must deliver the demanded flow *at the bearings*). Therefore:

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{\eta_v}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{1.8417 \times 10^{-4}}{0.90}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 2.0463 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert theoretical flow per second to flow per revolution:**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{N}
$$

$$
V_d = \dfrac{2.0463 \times 10^{-4}}{100}
$$

$$
V_d = 2.0463 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Express in cm┬│/rev (the industry-standard unit):**

$$
V_d = 2.0463 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_d \approx 2.05 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A small external gear pump of about 2 cm┬│/rev displacement ŌĆö physically a gear of roughly 30 mm OD and 15 mm face width ŌĆö is sufficient. This matches the oil pumps fitted on typical 1.5 L ŌĆō 2.0 L multi-utility vehicles.

## 3.2 Derivation B ŌĆö Pressure Relief Valve Spring Pre-Load

**Problem Statement:**
Design the spring preload on the pressure relief valve of a wet sump system that must crack at **4.5 bar** gallery pressure. The valve is a poppet with a seat diameter of 12 mm.

**Given data:**
- Cracking pressure: $P_{cr} = 4.5 \text{ bar} = 4.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- Seat diameter: $d = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012$ m

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the static equilibrium at the moment of cracking:**

At the instant the valve begins to lift, the hydraulic force equals the spring preload.

$$
F_{spring} = P_{cr} \cdot A_{seat}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the seat area:**

$$
A_{seat} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2
$$

$$
A_{seat} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.012)^2
$$

$$
A_{seat} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 1.44 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
A_{seat} = 1.13097 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the required spring force:**

$$
F_{spring} = 4.5 \times 10^{5} \cdot 1.13097 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_{spring} = 50.89 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to convenient engineering units:**

$$
\boxed{F_{spring} \approx 51 \text{ N} \approx 5.2 \text{ kgf}}
$$

This is the force a moderately stiff compression spring must apply at its installed (free + preload) length. In practice, designers add a further 10 ŌĆō 15 % margin to ensure the valve reseats crisply at lower pressures.

## 3.3 Derivation C ŌĆö Petroff Friction Loss in a Main Bearing

**Problem Statement:**
Estimate the frictional power loss in one main journal bearing of the above engine at maximum speed. Journal diameter $D = 60$ mm, length $L = 25$ mm, radial clearance $c = 0.05$ mm, oil dynamic viscosity at operating temperature $\mu = 0.012$ Pa┬Ęs.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Petroff's equation for friction torque:**

$$
T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}
$$

with $N$ in rev/s, $r = D/2$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Insert numerical values:**

- $r = 0.030$ m
- $N = 100$ rev/s
- $L = 0.025$ m
- $c = 0.00005$ m
- $\mu = 0.012$ Pa┬Ęs

$$
T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 100 \cdot (0.030)^3 \cdot 0.025}{0.00005}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate numerator and denominator separately:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.012 \times 100 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.025
$$

$$
= 3.198 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N┬Ęm┬Ęs}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_f = \dfrac{3.198 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.06396 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Frictional power loss in this bearing:**

$$
P_f = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot 2 \pi N
$$

$$
P_f = 0.06396 \times 2 \pi \times 100
$$

$$
P_f = 40.18 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Sum over all 5 main bearings:**

$$
\boxed{P_{f,total} \approx 5 \times 40.18 = 201 \text{ W} \approx 0.20 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The wet sump system must dissipate roughly 0.2 kW just from main-bearing friction. Add the connecting rods, camshaft, valve train and oil-pump drive, and total lubricated-friction loss rises to 1 ŌĆō 2 kW, which is why an oil cooler is fitted on turbocharged engines.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Wet Sump vs Dry Sump

| Parameter | Wet Sump | Dry Sump |
|---|---|---|
| Oil reservoir location | Integral oil pan under crankcase | Remote external tank |
| Crankcase oil inventory | Full | Shallow (only residual film) |
| Pump count | One pressure pump | One pressure + one or more scavenge pumps |
| Oil starvation under hard cornering | High risk | Negligible (multi-stage scavenging) |
| Cost, weight, complexity | Low | High |
| Crankcase windage losses | Higher (oil bath around crank) | Lower (less oil in crankcase) |
| Centre of gravity | Higher (oil slung high in pan) | Lower (engine can be mounted lower) |
| Typical applications | Passenger cars, LCVs | Supercars, F1, NASCAR, aircraft engines |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall trap (frequently tested):** Students often answer *"dry sump stores no oil"* ŌĆö this is wrong. A dry sump **does store oil**, but in a *remote tank* outside the engine, not in the crankcase. The crankcase is *scavenged* (emptied continuously) by the scavenge pump, hence the name.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram traces the oil circuit through every functional block of a wet sump system. Node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SUMP OIL RESERVOIR] --> A2[PICKUP TUBE STRAINER]
    A2 --> A3[OIL PUMP GEROTOR]
    A3 --> A4[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE BYPASS]
    A3 --> A5[FULL FLOW OIL FILTER]
    A4 --> A1
    A5 --> A6[MAIN OIL GALLERY]
    A6 --> A7[CRANKSHAFT MAIN BEARINGS]
    A6 --> A8[CONNECTING ROD BIG END BEARINGS]
    A6 --> A9[CAMSHAFT BEARINGS]
    A6 --> A10[HYDRAULIC TAPPETS]
    A6 --> A11[PISTON COOLING JETS]
    A7 --> A12[OIL DRAIN BACK TO SUMP]
    A8 --> A12
    A9 --> A12
    A10 --> A12
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A1
    A5 -.BLOCKED.-> A13[INTERNAL BYPASS VALVE OPENS]
    A13 --> A6
```

## 4.2 Pressure / Flow Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[ENGINE START IDLE 800 RPM] --> B2[LOW FLOW LOW PRESSURE]
    B2 --> B3[CRUISE 2500 RPM]
    B3 --> B4[DESIGN PRESSURE 3 TO 4 BAR]
    B4 --> B5[REDLINE 6000 RPM]
    B5 --> B6[PRV BLEEDS EXCESS FLOW]
    B6 --> B1
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (Verbal, for the Answer Sheet)

When you cannot draw in the exam, describe the wet sump circuit using a numbered textual schematic. The standard KTU marker expects this exact ordering:

1. Oil stored in **sump (oil pan)**.
2. Drawn up through **pickup tube + strainer** by **oil pump**.
3. Pumped through **pressure relief valve** (parallel branch) and through **oil filter** (main branch).
4. Filtered oil enters **main oil gallery**.
5. Distributes to **main bearings ŌåÆ connecting rod bearings ŌåÆ camshaft bearings ŌåÆ valve train ŌåÆ piston cooling jets**.
6. Oil drains back into the sump by gravity ŌåÆ reused.

## 4.4 Component Location Diagram (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[ENGINE CYLINDER HEAD] --> C2[CAMSHAFT OIL FEED]
    C1 --> C3[VALVE TRAIN OIL FEED]
    C4[ENGINE BLOCK] --> C5[MAIN OIL GALLERY]
    C4 --> C6[CRANKSHAFT]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[OIL PAN WET SUMP]
    C7 --> C8[WINDAGE TRAY]
    C7 --> C9[OIL PICKUP TUBE]
    C9 --> C10[OIL PUMP]
    C10 --> C5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]** Define "wet sump" lubrication system. Why is it called "wet"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A wet sump lubrication system is one in which the lubricating oil is stored in a reservoir (oil pan/sump) that forms an integral part of the engine, located directly beneath the crankcase. A pump draws oil from this sump, pressurises it, circulates it through the engine's moving parts, and the oil drains back into the same sump by gravity for reuse. It is called "wet" because the sump remains **permanently filled with oil** throughout engine operation, in contrast to a dry sump where the crankcase is scavenged empty and oil is stored in a remote tank. (1 mark ŌĆö definition, 1 mark ŌĆö reason for "wet" name, 1 mark ŌĆö closed-loop concept.)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** List any six components of a wet sump lubrication system.

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö ┬Į mark each):**

1. Oil pan / sump
2. Pickup tube with strainer
3. Oil pump (gerotor / external gear)
4. Oil filter (full-flow)
5. Pressure relief valve
6. Oil galleries (main + branch)
7. Dipstick / oil level sensor
8. Oil pressure gauge / switch
9. Oil cooler (optional)
10. PCV / breather system

(Any six listed correctly ŌĆö full 3 marks.)

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 4 choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, explain the construction and working of a **wet sump lubrication system** used in a modern multi-cylinder petrol engine. Trace the complete oil flow path from the sump to the sump.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **wet sump and dry sump** lubrication systems in terms of (i) construction, (ii) oil capacity, (iii) weight, (iv) oil starvation tendency, (v) cost, and (vi) typical application. State two situations where a dry sump is preferred.

**Model Solution A(a):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A wet sump system comprises an **oil pan (sump)** bolted beneath the engine crankcase, a **pickup tube with strainer** dipped into the oil, a **gear / gerotor oil pump** driven off the crankshaft, a **pressure relief valve** in parallel with the pump discharge, a **full-flow spin-on oil filter**, a **main oil gallery** drilled in the block, and branch passages to each bearing and the valve train. A **dipstick** measures oil level; a **windage tray** between the crankshaft and the sump oil surface prevents aeration; a **PCV breather** removes crankcase gases.

**Working (3 marks):**
At engine start, the oil pump draws oil from the sump, pressurises it, and forces it through the filter. Cleaned oil enters the main gallery and is distributed to the main bearings, then via internal drillings in the crankshaft to the connecting rod big-end bearings, and to the camshaft bearings, valve lifters, and timing chain. After lubrication, oil drains by gravity back into the sump through dedicated drain-back holes. The pressure relief valve bleeds any excess flow back to the sump to cap the gallery pressure at the design limit (typically 3 ŌĆō 5 bar).

**Sketch (out of the 7 marks, ~1 mark reserved for diagram):**
The student should draw a block schematic showing the sump at the bottom, pickup tube, pump, filter, pressure relief valve (as a parallel branch), main gallery, and the various bearings, with arrows indicating flow direction. The verbal schematic given in Section 4.3 of these notes is an acceptable alternative.

**Model Solution A(b):**

| Parameter | Wet Sump | Dry Sump |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Construction | Single pump; sump integral with engine | Multiple (1 pressure + 1ŌĆō3 scavenge) pumps; remote external tank |
| (ii) Oil capacity | Limited (3.5ŌĆō5.5 L) | Large (8ŌĆō15 L remote tank) |
| (iii) Weight | Lighter | Heavier (extra tank + pumps) |
| (iv) Oil starvation | High under sustained high-G | Negligible ŌĆö multi-stage scavenging |
| (v) Cost | Low | High (3ŌĆō5├Ś) |
| (vi) Typical application | Passenger cars, LCVs | Supercars, F1, NASCAR, aircraft engines |

(6 marks ŌĆö ┬Į mark per parameter row + ┬Į mark for contrast explanation. Reserve ┬Į mark for neatness.)

**Two situations where dry sump is preferred (1 mark ŌĆö ┬Į mark each):**
1. **Sustained high-G cornering** in racing, where wet-sump oil would slosh away from the pickup, causing momentary starvation.
2. **Low vehicle bonnet line / centre-of-gravity requirement** in supercars, since a dry sump allows the engine to be mounted lower in the chassis.

### Question B (14 marks) **[Alternative Module 4 choice ŌĆö KTU pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the function of the **oil pump, pressure relief valve, and oil filter** in a wet sump lubrication system. Describe the construction of a **gerotor pump** with a neat sketch.

**(b) [7 marks]** A four-cylinder, 1.5 L displacement petrol engine runs at 5500 rpm. The oil pump delivers 18 L/min at this speed with a volumetric efficiency of 88 %. The pressure relief valve cracks at 4.2 bar and seat diameter is 10 mm. Calculate:
(i) the theoretical pump displacement in cm┬│/rev,
(ii) the spring preload on the pressure relief valve, and
(iii) the total frictional power loss in five main bearings if each journal is 55 mm dia, 22 mm long, with radial clearance 0.045 mm, and oil dynamic viscosity at operating temperature is 0.011 Pa┬Ęs.

**Model Solution B(a):**

**Functions (3 marks ŌĆö 1 each):**
- **Oil pump:** Converts the rotary motion of the crankshaft into hydraulic energy, generating the pressure differential needed to deliver oil at the required rate to all bearing surfaces against leakage and flow resistance.
- **Pressure relief valve:** A spring-loaded valve in parallel with the pump discharge. When gallery pressure exceeds a safe limit (cracking pressure), it opens and dumps excess oil back to the sump, protecting the filter and bearings from over-pressure. When pressure falls, it reseats automatically.
- **Oil filter:** A full-flow pleated-paper element that removes wear debris, soot, and combustion by-products down to ~25 ┬Ąm, prolonging bearing life. An internal bypass valve opens at ~0.7 bar ╬öp if the filter is clogged, preserving oil flow at the cost of filtration.

**Gerotor pump construction (4 marks ŌĆö 2 for sketch, 2 for description):**
A gerotor pump consists of an **inner rotor** with $n$ external teeth and an **outer rotor** with $n+1$ internal teeth, mounted eccentrically inside it. As the inner rotor is driven, the outer rotor follows, and the volume between successive teeth *expands* on the inlet side (drawing oil in) and *contracts* on the outlet side (pushing oil out). A kidney-shaped port plate separates suction and delivery chambers. Advantages: compact, self-priming, quiet, high volumetric efficiency (~95 %).

**Model Solution B(b):**

**Given:**
- $N = 5500$ rpm $= 91.67$ rev/s
- $Q_{act} = 18$ L/min $= 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m┬│/s
- $\eta_v = 0.88$

**(i) Theoretical pump displacement (3 marks):**

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{\eta_v}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.88} = 3.4091 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
V_d = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{N} = \dfrac{3.4091 \times 10^{-4}}{91.67} = 3.719 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_d = 3.72 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}}
$$

**(ii) PRV spring preload (2 marks):**

Seat area:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.010)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

Spring force:

$$
F_s = P_{cr} \cdot A = 4.2 \times 10^{5} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
F_s = 32.99 \text{ N} \approx 33 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_s \approx 33 \text{ N} \; (\approx 3.4 \text{ kgf})}
$$

**(iii) Frictional power loss in 5 main bearings (2 marks):**

Petroff torque per bearing:

$$
T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}
$$

with $\mu = 0.011$ Pa┬Ęs, $r = 0.0275$ m, $L = 0.022$ m, $c = 0.000045$ m, $N = 91.67$ rev/s.

$$
T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \cdot 0.011 \cdot 91.67 \cdot (0.0275)^3 \cdot 0.022}{0.000045}
$$

$$
T_f = \dfrac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.011 \times 91.67 \times 2.078 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.022}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
= 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.011 \times 91.67 \times 2.078 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.022
$$

$$
= 1.732 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N┬Ęm┬Ęs (approx)}
$$

Denominator: $4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ s.

$$
T_f = \dfrac{1.732 \times 10^{-4}}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.85 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

Power per bearing:

$$
P_f = T_f \cdot 2 \pi N = 3.85 \times 2 \pi \times 91.67
$$

$$
P_f = 3.85 \times 575.96 = 2217 \text{ W} \approx 2.22 \text{ kW}
$$

Total for 5 main bearings:

$$
\boxed{P_{f,total} \approx 5 \times 2.22 = 11.1 \text{ kW}}
$$

(Note: this high value reflects Petroff's worst-case assumption of a fully flooded, lightly loaded journal. In a real engine the hydrodynamic friction is ~5ŌĆō10├Ś lower, which is why Petroff is used only as a conservative upper bound.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Read Before You Write:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Petroff with the actual hydrodynamic friction.** Petroff's equation ignores load and assumes a concentric, fully flooded journal; the result is therefore an *upper bound*. Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark if you present it as the "true" friction loss without qualification.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors are the #1 reason for lost marks in PCMUT302 numericals.** Always convert L/min to m┬│/s *before* dividing by rev/s, and always convert bar to Pa *before* multiplying by m┬▓.
> 3. **Sketch orientation:** When drawing the gerotor pump, the inner rotor must be shown with *one fewer* tooth than the outer rotor. Drawing them with equal teeth loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Wet vs Dry Sump confusion:** A common answer written by students is *"dry sump has no oil"* ŌĆö this is wrong. The dry sump has *more* oil capacity but stores it externally.
> 5. **PRV location:** Mark the PRV as a *parallel* branch to the filter, not in series. If drawn in series, the marker will deduct 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wet sump** = oil stored in a pan *integral* with the engine; oil drains back to the *same* pan by gravity.
- **Dry sump** = oil stored in a *remote* external tank; crankcase is *scavenged* continuously.
- **Standard oil pressure window:** 2.5 ŌĆō 4.5 bar at gallery; PRV cracks at 4.0 ŌĆō 5.5 bar.
- **Standard oil capacity:** ~0.05 ├Ś engine displacement in litres (rule of thumb).
- **Filtration grade:** ~25 ┬Ąm for full-flow filters; bypass opens at ~0.7 bar ╬öp.
- **Windage tray** is mandatory on high-RPM engines to suppress foam.
- **PCV** (positive crankcase ventilation) is essential to prevent moisture & blow-by accumulation.
- **Pump type:** External gear (older) or **gerotor** (modern, ~95 % $\eta_v$).
- **Viscosity classification:** Always use **SAE J300** grades; 5W-30 is the most common multi-grade.
- **Oil cooler** is mandatory on turbocharged and heavy-duty engines to limit oil temperature below ~130 ┬░C.
- **Failure modes to remember for viva:** oil starvation (low level), oil aeration (foam), filter clogging (╬öpŌåæ), PRV stuck open (low pressure), oil pump wear (low pressure at high RPM).
- **Quick recall formula trio:**
  1. $Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N$
  2. $F_{spring} = P_{cr} \cdot A_{seat}$
  3. Petroff: $T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
